When the government announced plans to subdivide and sell Bastion Point for luxury housing, a group led by Joe Hawke established an occupation on the hill on the fifth of January, nineteen seventy seven. They built huts. They cooked on open fires. They refused to leave. Five hundred and six days later, on the twenty fifth of May, nineteen seventy eight, six hundred police and army personnel arrived at dawn and arrested two hundred and twenty two occupiers in one of the largest police operations in New Zealand history. The occupation had not won, but the cause had. Ten years later, after a Waitangi Tribunal hearing and years of negotiation, Bastion Point was returned to Ngāti Whātua. A memorial near the top carries the names of those who died during the occupation, including Joe Hawke''s five year old niece Joannee. Visit with respect.
review_summary: Visitors describe Takaparawhā / Bastion Point as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Ngāti Whātua Ōrākei occupied their ancestral land for 506 days in 1977–78 — a landmark of the modern Māori rights movement. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Takapuna Beach & Fossil Forest
short: North Shore's favourite city beach — with a 180,000-year-old fossil forest exposed at low tide.
commentary: Takapuna is the North Shore's main city beach — 1.5 km of sheltered east-facing white sand, safe for swimming, with cafés along the foreshore. At the northern end, at very low tide, you can walk across a fossil forest: petrified tōtara and rātā stumps embedded in volcanic tuff, buried when Lake Pupuke's eruption around 180,000 years ago drowned a kauri forest. The best viewing is on a spring low tide. The walk from Takapuna to Milford at low tide takes about an hour.
review_summary: Takapuna Beach & Fossil Forest draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. North Shore's favourite city beach — with a 180,000-year-old fossil forest exposed at low tide. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Takarunga / Mount Victoria, Devonport
short: An 81m volcano above Devonport with a quick walk to the best harbour view on the North Shore.
commentary: Takarunga is the highest volcano on the North Shore. Significant Māori pā site (Te Hau-rāwhiti) and a coastal defence battery from the 1880s.
audio_story: You are standing on top of Takarunga, the highest volcano on Auckland's North Shore, and you have an entire harbour at your feet. The hill rises eighty one metres above Devonport, and the walk from the village takes only fifteen minutes. Below you the harbour opens up, the city skyline spreads west, and Rangitoto sits on the water to the east, almost too symmetrical to be real. Like every cone on the Auckland isthmus, Takarunga was a Māori pā. The terraces you can still trace on the slopes were the working levels of a fortified settlement called Te Hau-rāwhiti. In the eighteen eighties the British added a coastal defence battery on top, and you can still walk around the gun emplacements and the old observation post. The two are layered onto each other like a palimpsest. From the summit, look down the harbour and you can pick out the route the first European ships took into Auckland in eighteen forty. The wind up here is honest. Bring a jacket. And take the long way back down through the Devonport streets, past the wooden villas and the bookshop and the chocolate factory, before catching the ferry home.
review_summary: Takarunga / Mount Victoria, Devonport draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. An 81m volcano above Devonport with a quick walk to the best harbour view on the North Shore. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Tamaki Drive Coastal Route
short: Auckland's iconic waterfront drive from the city to Mission Bay and beyond.
commentary: Tamaki Drive runs 7.5 km along the Waitemata Harbour from Mechanics Bay to St Heliers. The route passes Okahu Bay, Mission Bay, and Kohimarama, with views of Rangitoto Island dominating the horizon. It is one of Auckland's most popular cycling and walking routes.
audio_story: You are cruising along Tamaki Drive with the Waitemata Harbour sparkling on your left. Rangitoto Island, that perfectly symmetrical volcanic cone, sits on the horizon — it erupted just 600 years ago, making it Auckland's youngest volcano. You pass the heritage boatsheds at Okahu Bay, their bright colours reflected in the water. Ahead, Mission Bay's crescent beach comes into view, where Aucklanders have been swimming and picnicking since the 1920s. The pohutukawa trees lining the road will blaze crimson if you are here in December. Keep going past Kohimarama to St Heliers for fish and chips on the green — you have earned it.
review_summary: The Tamaki Drive Coastal Route route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Auckland's iconic waterfront drive from the city to Mission Bay and beyond. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Tantalus Estate
short: Waiheke estate combining a cellar door, craft brewery (Alibi) and seasonal restaurant.
commentary: Tantalus is Waiheke's most complete food-and-drink destination — the estate combines a cellar door for its premium wines, the Alibi brewery for craft beer, and a restaurant that uses produce from the estate garden. It's the smart move for groups where not everyone is a wine drinker. The Syrah is their best wine, while Alibi's rotating beer taps are consistently creative.
review_summary: The wine-plus-brewery concept is praised for its inclusiveness. The restaurant scores well for seasonal food. Reservations are essential — it's become one of Waiheke's busiest destinations.

name: Te Ahi-kā-a-Rakataura / Mount Albert
short: Named for the fires of the navigator Rakataura who claimed the isthmus with signal fires from the Tainui waka.
commentary: The name means 'the fires of Rakataura', for the navigator who arrived on the Tainui waka and lit signal fires across the isthmus to claim the land.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Ahi-kā-a-Rakataura / Mount Albert as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Named for the fires of the navigator Rakataura who claimed the isthmus with signal fires from the Tainui waka. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Pane o Mataoho / Māngere Mountain
short: A spectacular terraced pā on one of Auckland's southern volcanoes — over 200 food storage pits still visible.
commentary: Te Pane o Mataoho (Māngere Mountain) is one of Auckland's finest volcanic pā sites. The 106m cone was heavily terraced for settlement, with over 200 storage pits and house platforms still visible.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Pane o Mataoho / Māngere Mountain as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A spectacular terraced pā on one of Auckland's southern volcanoes — over 200 food storage pits still visible. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Pūkaki Tapu o Poutūkeka
short: A sacred volcanic crater with a mauri stone at its heart — a wāhi tapu of deep spiritual significance.
commentary: The crater lake and surrounding area are wāhi tapu. A mauri stone placed in the crater represents the life force of the land and the connection between the physical and spiritual worlds.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Pūkaki Tapu o Poutūkeka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A sacred volcanic crater with a mauri stone at its heart — a wāhi tapu of deep spiritual significance. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Tō Waka / Ōtāhuhu Portage
short: The ancient waka portage where Tāmaki iwi hauled their canoes between the Waitematā and Manukau harbours.
commentary: At Ōtāhuhu only 1km separates the Waitematā and Manukau harbours. For centuries iwi dragged waka between the two on a portage called Te Tō Waka.
audio_story: You are on the narrowest point of the Auckland isthmus, where only one kilometre of land separates the Waitematā Harbour on the east coast from the Manukau Harbour on the west. This strip, called Te Tō Waka, the waka dragging place, was for centuries one of the most important transport links in the whole country. Rather than paddle around the exposed North Cape, travelling waka coming from the Bay of Islands in the north bound for the Waikato in the south would enter the Waitematā, be hauled overland across this flat neck, and relaunched into the Manukau on the other side. The physical work was significant but the saving in distance was enormous. The same portage shaped the settlement pattern of Tāmaki Makaurau itself. Control of the portage was one of the reasons the isthmus was so densely populated before colonisation, and one of the reasons it was contested. Today the portage is an unremarkable flat stretch of Great South Road between Ōtāhuhu and Māngere. An interpretive sign on Portage Road marks the centre line. Stand for a moment with a map in your head. Imagine a fifteen metre waka, its crew straining at the hauling lines, sliding across what is now a traffic island. The city forgets, but the landscape does not.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Tō Waka / Ōtāhuhu Portage as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The ancient waka portage where Tāmaki iwi hauled their canoes between the Waitematā and Manukau harbours. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: The Fed
short: Auckland's best deli-restaurant — Jewish-inspired food, house-cured meats and legendary Reuben sandwiches.
commentary: The Fed in the CBD brings New York deli culture to Auckland with house-cured pastrami, smoked salmon, bagels and what many consider the best Reuben sandwich in the country. The cabinet is packed with salads, pastries and deli fare, and the coffee is excellent. Breakfast and lunch are the peak times. It's become a go-to for Auckland's hospitality industry on their days off — always a good sign.
review_summary: The pastrami and Reuben are outstanding. The deli cabinet is impressive. Can be packed at lunch — arrive early for a table.

name: Tiritiri Matangi — Sacred Island Sanctuary
short: An open wildlife sanctuary on an island with Māori settlement sites — where endangered birds thrive among archaeological remains.
commentary: Tiritiri Matangi is an open wildlife sanctuary in the Hauraki Gulf, home to some of NZ's rarest birds. The island also contains significant Māori archaeological sites — pā terraces, middens, and storage pits speak to centuries of occupation.
review_summary: Visitors describe Tiritiri Matangi — Sacred Island Sanctuary as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. An open wildlife sanctuary on an island with Māori settlement sites — where endangered birds thrive among archaeological remains. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tiritiri Matangi Island
short: A 220-hectare open bird sanctuary — predator-free, replanted by volunteers, home to takahē, kōkako, and hihi.
commentary: Tiritiri Matangi was farmland until 1980. Then volunteers began planting native trees — over 280,000 in a decade. By 1993 predators had been eradicated, and the island was restocked with endangered native birds. Today you can walk open bush tracks among takahē (fewer than 500 exist), kōkako, saddleback, hihi/stitchbird, whitehead, bellbird, tūī and tīeke. Ferry from downtown Auckland or Gulf Harbour. Day visit only; guided walks available. One of the most successful ecological restorations on Earth.
review_summary: Visitors call Tiritiri Matangi one of New Zealand's great conservation success stories. The island is alive with birdsong — kōkako, takahē, little spotted kiwi — and reviewers say it's the best place in the country to encounter native species up close. Book the ferry well ahead; day trips sell out fast.

name: Viaduct Harbour & Wynyard Quarter
short: The working harbour that hosted two America's Cup defences — now all superyachts and waterfront restaurants.
commentary: Before 1999 the Viaduct was an industrial fishing basin; the America's Cup regeneration transformed it into a waterfront quarter. Wynyard Quarter, across the Wynyard Crossing lifting bridge, took over the tank-farm zone in 2011 — silos and gasworks kept, context changed. Today: restaurants, the Maritime Museum, and North Wharf's fish-and-chip shops.
review_summary: The Viaduct is Auckland's social heart — visitors love the working waterfront atmosphere, the superyachts, and the concentration of good restaurants and bars. Sunset drinks here draw consistent praise. It can feel expensive and touristy, but the harbour views and easy walkability win reviewers over.

name: Waiheke Island
short: 40-minute ferry from downtown — 30 vineyards, a dozen beaches, and the best olive oil in the country.
commentary: Waiheke's microclimate — drier and warmer than the mainland — makes it one of New Zealand's premier Bordeaux-style wine regions. Mudbrick, Cable Bay and Te Motu are the big three for dining-with-a-view. The 360° car-free walkway circles the whole island (three to four days). Onetangi and Palm Beach are the swimming picks. Ferries run every half-hour from Auckland's Downtown terminal.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waiheke as Auckland's best day trip — 35 minutes by ferry and you're in a different world. The combination of boutique wineries, olive estates, and pristine beaches earns it near-universal five-star reviews. Book wine tours ahead in summer; the island gets very busy December through February.

name: Waiheke Island — Pā Sites & Heritage
short: Waiheke's Māori heritage extends far beyond the vineyards — pā sites, middens, and gardening terraces across the island.
commentary: Waiheke Island has an extensive Māori heritage. Ngāi Tai ki Tāmaki are the mana whenua, and the island contains numerous pā sites, shell middens, and gardening terraces.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waiheke Island — Pā Sites & Heritage as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Waiheke's Māori heritage extends far beyond the vineyards — pā sites, middens, and gardening terraces across the island. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waiheke Island Sea Kayaking
short: Paddle the sheltered bays of Waiheke Island with vineyard-studded hills as your backdrop.
commentary: Sea kayaking around Waiheke Island combines coastal exploration with stunning island scenery. Guided tours depart from Matiatia Bay and explore hidden coves, sandy beaches, and rocky headlands. Some tours include a vineyard visit or beach picnic.
audio_story: You have arrived at Matiatia Bay on Waiheke Island, the launching point for one of Auckland's most scenic kayaking experiences. As you glide away from shore, the sheltered waters of the Hauraki Gulf stretch out before you, emerald-green and remarkably calm in the lee of the island. Your route takes you past golden sand beaches tucked between headlands, through clear shallows where you might spot eagle rays gliding below, and along a coastline backed by olive groves and vineyards. Waiheke has over 30 boutique wineries, and several guided kayak tours include a tasting stop. The paddling is gentle enough for beginners, but the scenery is world-class. Keep your eyes open for little blue penguins, they nest along these shores.
review_summary: The Waiheke Island Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddle the sheltered bays of Waiheke Island with vineyard-studded hills as your backdrop. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Whatipu Beach
short: The wild south end of Auckland's west coast — huge dunes, sea caves, and the mouth of the Manukau Harbour.
commentary: Whatipu sits at the southern end of Auckland's west coast, where the Manukau Harbour meets the Tasman. It's a 45-minute drive from Titirangi down one of the steeper gravel roads in the region. The reward: a massive black-sand flat (created by sediment outwash during the 20th century), extensive dune wetlands, and the ballroom-sized Whatipu Caves — used for dances by early 1900s loggers. The Manukau Bar just offshore has one of NZ's worst maritime histories: HMS *Orpheus* sank here in 1863 with 189 dead. No swimming.
review_summary: Whatipu Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The wild south end of Auckland's west coast — huge dunes, sea caves, and the mouth of the Manukau Harbour. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Woodhill Mountain Bike Park
short: Ride 100+ km of sandy singletrack at Auckland's largest mountain bike park.
commentary: Woodhill Mountain Bike Park in the Woodhill Forest northwest of Auckland offers over 100 km of trails through pine forest on sandy soil. The sand base means trails drain instantly after rain. Features include a skills park, pump track, and trails from Grade 1 to Grade 5.
audio_story: You have arrived at Woodhill Mountain Bike Park, Auckland's largest riding destination with over 100 kilometres of trails through the pine forest northwest of the city. What makes Woodhill unique is the soil, or rather the sand. Built on old coastal sand dunes, the trails here drain almost instantly after rain, making this one of the few places in Auckland where you can ride in any weather without feeling guilty about trail damage. The sandy surface has a distinctive feel, slightly slower than clay-based trails but incredibly grippy in corners. The trail network caters to everyone, from families on the gentle Grade 1 loops to experts hucking drops on the Grade 5 lines. The skills park and pump track are ideal for improving your technique, and the jumps range from tabletops for learning to massive gap jumps for the bold. There is a small entry fee that goes directly to trail maintenance, and the on-site shop offers bike hire and repairs. Come prepared for sand in your drivetrain.
review_summary: The Woodhill Mountain Bike Park adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride 100+ km of sandy singletrack at Auckland's largest mountain bike park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Alpino Restaurant & Bar
short: Mount Maunganui's award-winning Italian — premium ingredients, ocean views and outstanding hospitality.
commentary: Alpino has won multiple hospitality awards for its approach to Italian-inspired cooking using premium local ingredients. The Pacific Avenue location captures sea views, and the menu ranges from exceptional pasta to seasonal seafood. The bar offers craft cocktails and a curated wine list. Both the café (daytime) and restaurant (evening) experiences are excellent. A cut above the typical beach-town restaurant.
review_summary: Consistently rated Mount Maunganui's best restaurant. The Italian-NZ fusion works beautifully. Book ahead for dinner — it fills fast, especially in summer.

name: Astrolabe Brew Bar
short: Mount Maunganui gastropub — craft beers, wood-fired pizzas, burgers and a buzzy atmosphere.
commentary: Astrolabe is the Mount's go-to for craft beer and pub food. The tap selection covers local and NZ craft breweries, and the wood-fired pizzas, burgers and seafood are consistently good. The outdoor area catches the afternoon sun, making it popular for post-beach gatherings. Regular live music and events add to the atmosphere. Family-friendly during the day, livelier at night.
review_summary: Solid beer selection and good pub food. The atmosphere is fun and social. Can get noisy and crowded on weekend evenings.

name: Bobby's Fresh Fish Market
short: Tauranga wharf-side fish market — the freshest catch straight off the boats, plus fish 'n' chips.
commentary: Bobby's sits on the Tauranga wharf where fishing boats unload their catch. The fish display showcases whatever's come in that day — snapper, tarakihi, hapuku, John Dory — and you can buy it fresh to cook or have it battered and fried on the spot. The fish and chips, made with hours-fresh fish, are outstanding. Seating is basic (wharf benches), but the freshness makes up for any lack of ambiance.
review_summary: Fish doesn't get fresher than this. The fish and chips are excellent. Basic setup — it's about the seafood, not the décor.

name: Comvita Visitor Centre
short: World-famous Mānuka honey at the source — tastings, education and the Comvita story in Paengaroa.
commentary: Comvita is one of the world's most recognised Mānuka honey brands, and their visitor centre in Paengaroa (near Te Puke) tells the story of this remarkable NZ product. Interactive displays explain the science behind UMF ratings, and you can taste different grades of Mānuka honey. The gift shop sells products at better prices than airports. A unique food experience that connects you to one of NZ's most valuable exports.
review_summary: Educational and interesting for honey enthusiasts. The tastings help understand the UMF system. Some find it commercial, but the product quality is undeniable.

name: Dickeys Flat & Waihī Gorge
short: The Bay of Plenty approach to the Karangahake Gorge — old gold mines, a one kilometre tunnel, and a dramatic river walk.
commentary: The Karangahake Gorge was one of the richest gold fields in New Zealand from the 1870s to 1950s. The main walking loop passes through a restored 1-km railway tunnel and below several processing batteries.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Karangahake Gorge, the dramatic cleft between the Coromandel and Kaimai ranges that carries the Ohinemuri River from Waihī down to the Hauraki Plains. For about seventy years, from the eighteen seventies to the nineteen fifties, this gorge was one of the richest gold mining landscapes in the country. Thousands of men worked here. Whole towns sprung up and vanished. The gorge itself is now a conservation area, and DOC has done an outstanding job of turning the old mine workings into a network of walking tracks. The classic loop is the Windows Walk and Tunnel, about an hour of easy walking. You start on the old railway line, cross a swing bridge, walk through the dark of a one kilometre railway tunnel lit only by your torch, look down on the ruined concrete footings of the Crown and Talisman batteries, and come out above the roaring river. On a hot day it is pleasantly cool in the tunnel and down by the water. Bring a torch and sensible shoes. Waihī itself, at the southern end, has an excellent gold mining museum and a working open pit gold mine you can view from the Pit Rim Walkway.
review_summary: Dickeys Flat & Waihī Gorge draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The Bay of Plenty approach to the Karangahake Gorge — old gold mines, a one kilometre tunnel, and a dramatic river walk. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: East Cape Road – SH35
short: Drive SH35 around the East Cape, one of NZ's most remote and culturally rich coastal highways.
commentary: State Highway 35 runs 334 km from Opotiki to Gisborne around the East Cape. The road passes through remote Maori communities, past ornate carved meeting houses, and along deserted surf beaches. The East Cape Lighthouse is the first place on mainland New Zealand to see the sunrise each day.
audio_story: You are turning onto State Highway 35 at Opotiki, beginning a 334-kilometre coastal drive around the East Cape — one of New Zealand's most remote and culturally significant journeys. This is deep Te Whanau-a-Apanui and Ngati Porou country, and beautifully carved marae and meeting houses line the road. The pace slows here; the two-lane road winds past pohutukawa-shaded bays where you might be the only visitor. At Te Araroa, a short detour leads to the East Cape Lighthouse — 700 steps up to the first point on mainland New Zealand to see the sunrise each morning. The road passes through Tikitiki, where St Mary's Church contains some of the finest Maori carving and tukutuku panel work in the country. Take at least two days for this drive. Fill up your fuel tank at every opportunity — petrol stations are scarce. And slow down: the journey itself is the destination.
review_summary: The East Cape Road – SH35 route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive SH35 around the East Cape, one of NZ's most remote and culturally rich coastal highways. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Fife Lane Kitchen & Bar
short: Mount Maunganui izakaya blending Māori and Japanese culinary traditions — creative small plates and sake.
commentary: Fife Lane is one of the most original restaurants in the Bay of Plenty, blending Māori and Japanese food cultures in an izakaya ('stay and drink') format. Expect small plates that riff on both traditions — think hangi-smoked meats with Japanese seasonings, local seafood with wasabi, and creative cocktails. The atmosphere is convivial and the concept is genuinely unique in New Zealand.
review_summary: The Māori-Japanese concept is praised as genuinely creative. The small plates are perfect for sharing. Some find the portions small for the price.

name: Gate Pā / Pukehinahina Battle Site
short: Where 230 Māori warriors defeated 1,700 British troops in 1864 — the worst British loss of the NZ Wars.
commentary: On 29 April 1864, British troops stormed Gate Pā and were repulsed with devastating losses — around 100 killed and wounded. The Māori commander Rawiri Puhirake showed extraordinary chivalry, giving water to wounded British soldiers.
review_summary: Visitors describe Gate Pā / Pukehinahina Battle Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where 230 Māori warriors defeated 1,700 British troops in 1864 — the worst British loss of the NZ Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Kaimai Mamaku Forest Walk
short: Tramp through the Kaimai Mamaku ranges between Waikato and Bay of Plenty on old pack-horse tracks.
commentary: The Kaimai Mamaku Forest Park stretches along the range separating the Waikato and Bay of Plenty regions. Multiple tracks cross the range, many following old Maori trails and colonial pack-horse routes. The bush includes magnificent podocarp forest and numerous waterfalls.
audio_story: You are heading into the Kaimai Mamaku ranges, a forested spine that divides the Waikato from the Bay of Plenty. These trails follow routes used by Maori for centuries, later widened by colonial-era pack-horse teams carrying supplies between the coast and the Waikato interior. The bush is dense and wet — annual rainfall here tops 2,500 millimetres — and the result is a lush, dripping forest of rimu, rata, and tree ferns. Waterfalls are everywhere: the Elsa's, Wairere, and Kaiate Falls are each worth a visit. The birdlife is rich, with tui, bellbirds, and native pigeons common. If you are crossing the range, the descent on the Bay of Plenty side is steep and rewarding, with views opening out to the coast through gaps in the canopy. Be prepared for mud and changing conditions — this range generates its own weather.
review_summary: The Kaimai Mamaku Forest Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Tramp through the Kaimai Mamaku ranges between Waikato and Bay of Plenty on old pack-horse tracks. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Kaimai Ranges
short: The bush-clad spine dividing Waikato and Bay of Plenty — home to the Wairere Falls and the wreckage of a 1963 plane crash.
commentary: The Kaimai Range runs 80 km north–south between the Waikato and the Bay of Plenty. The Wairere Falls, off SH29 above Matamata, are the highest falls on the North Island (153 metres). On 3 July 1963 the NAC Douglas DC-3 *Hastings* crashed into the southern Kaimai in bad weather, killing all 23 aboard; it was the worst aviation disaster in New Zealand at the time, and the wreckage is still strewn across the remote ridge. The Kaimai Mamaku Forest Park offers day walks from both sides (Waikato side via Waitawheta, BoP side via Aongatete).
review_summary: Kaimai Ranges draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The bush-clad spine dividing Waikato and Bay of Plenty — home to the Wairere Falls and the wreckage of a 1963 plane crash. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Kaituna River & Tutea Falls
short: The Kaituna River drops over the seven-metre Tutea Falls — the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world.
commentary: The Kaituna River, the outlet from Lake Rotoiti, drops over Tutea Falls (7 m) — the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world. The 50-minute rafting trip includes three waterfalls.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Kaituna River, a short stretch of whitewater where the Kaituna, the outlet from Lake Rotoiti, tumbles down to the Bay of Plenty coast. The Kaituna holds a peculiar world record. At the centre of the run lies Tutea Falls, a seven metre sheer waterfall that is the highest commercially rafted waterfall anywhere in the world. Every day, weather permitting, several operators run hundreds of visitors down this river. The trip is brief, about fifty minutes in the water, but intense. You pull on a helmet and a wetsuit, climb into a rubber raft with six or seven strangers and a guide, and paddle through the preliminary warm-up rapids. Then you reach the lip of Tutea Falls. The guide calls get down, the raft tips, the river disappears, and for a long second you are in free fall inside a thunderous white cloud. Almost every raft comes through upright. Some do not. Either way, you come up laughing or coughing or both. The rest of the run is a gentler float through fern clad canyon. You are out of the water before you have fully processed the experience. Dry clothes, hot drink, and a solid story to take home.
review_summary: The Kaituna River & Tutea Falls adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. The Kaituna River drops over the seven-metre Tutea Falls — the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Katikati — Mural Town
short: A whole small town turned into an open-air gallery — over 70 murals telling the stories of its Ulster founders.
commentary: Katikati was founded as a special Ulster settlement in the 1870s. Since 1991 the town has commissioned more than seventy outdoor murals along its main street.
audio_story: You are walking down the main street of Katikati and you are walking through what may be the largest outdoor art gallery in New Zealand. Since nineteen ninety one the town has commissioned over seventy murals on the walls of its shops, halls and walkways, each telling part of its unusual founding story. Katikati was settled in the eighteen seventies by Ulster Protestants brought out by George Vesey Stewart in one of the few formally planned ethnic settlements in the country. The murals along the street record their faces, their farms, their first orchards, and the Ngāi Te Rangi people whose land they joined. Newer panels add stories from the kiwifruit industry, school children, and local events. There is a self-guided walking map at the information centre, and you can see most of the murals in around an hour. While you are in town, follow the Haiku Pathway down beside the Uretara Stream. It is a short, beautifully landscaped walk lined with stones engraved with haiku poems written by New Zealand and international poets, the only one of its kind in the southern hemisphere. Katikati turns out to be a small town with two surprising obsessions, art and poetry, and it does both with quiet conviction.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Katikati — Mural Town is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A whole small town turned into an open-air gallery — over 70 murals telling the stories of its Ulster founders. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Lake Rotoiti Hot Pools
short: Natural geothermal hot pools on the edge of Lake Rotoiti, accessible only by boat — one of the great low-key experiences of the Bay of Plenty.
commentary: Manupirua Springs Hot Pools on the southern shore of Lake Rotoiti are accessible only by boat — a 15-minute ride from the Okere or Kawaha Point ramps. Five open-air pools at graduated temperatures.
audio_story: You have arrived at Lake Rotoiti, one of a string of lakes east of Rotorua that were formed when the Ōkataina caldera collapsed many thousands of years ago. Rotoiti is quieter than Rotorua itself, ringed by bush covered hills with only a handful of small settlements on its shores. Hidden on the southern shore, accessible only by boat, is one of the small great pleasures of the Bay of Plenty. The Manupirua Springs Hot Pools sit in a small bay reachable in about fifteen minutes from the Okere boat ramp or a bit longer from Kawaha Point. If you have your own boat or kayak, you can tie up at the jetty. If you do not, the local water taxi will take you. Five natural geothermal hot pools step down the bank to the lake edge at graduated temperatures from a very warm forty degrees to a pleasantly hot thirty six. You sit in the pool, watch the cool lake water lap only metres below, and hear nothing but cicadas and the distant putt of an outboard motor. There is no electricity, no cafe, no real infrastructure at all. Bring a towel, bring a drink, bring a friend. Allow two hours minimum.
review_summary: Lake Rotoiti Hot Pools draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Natural geothermal hot pools on the edge of Lake Rotoiti, accessible only by boat — one of the great low-key experiences of the Bay of Plenty. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Maketū — Te Arawa Landing Place
short: The landing place of Te Arawa waka around 1350 — a small seaside settlement of profound significance to Te Arawa iwi.
commentary: Maketū is the landing place of the Te Arawa waka under its captain Tama-te-kapua. A stone monument on the foreshore marks the spot. The settlement retains deep significance for all Te Arawa descendants.
audio_story: You have arrived at Maketū, a small seaside village at the mouth of the Kaituna River that holds a place of profound significance in the history of Aotearoa. Around the middle of the fourteenth century, the Te Arawa waka, one of the great migration canoes, made landfall here under its captain Tama-te-kapua. From Maketū Tama-te-kapua's people spread inland to the thermal country around Rotorua, establishing the great confederation of tribes known collectively as Te Arawa. Every iwi that traces its whakapapa to Te Arawa, and there are many, traces it back to this bay. A stone monument on the foreshore marks the landing place. Maketū itself is a quiet village of a few hundred people, most of them Māori, wrapped around a dramatic estuary of the Kaituna River that is a favourite spot for fishing and whitebaiting in season. The Maketū pies are locally famous and well worth the stop. Walk on the beach, watch for kuaka, the bar-tailed godwit, which has flown eleven thousand kilometres non stop from Alaska to spend the southern summer here, and remember that for Te Arawa this is a beginning place. Treat the foreshore with the respect that place deserves.
sources_to_check: Western Bay of Plenty District Council
review_summary: Visitors describe Maketū — Te Arawa Landing Place as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The landing place of Te Arawa waka around 1350 — a small seaside settlement of profound significance to Te Arawa iwi. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Maketū — Te Arawa Waka Landing
short: Where the Te Arawa waka made its final landfall from Hawaiki — the ancestral landing place of the Te Arawa confederation.
commentary: Maketū is one of the most significant waka landing sites in Aotearoa. The Te Arawa waka, captained by Tamatekapua, arrived here from Hawaiki. The area is rich in pā sites and wāhi tapu.
review_summary: Visitors describe Maketū — Te Arawa Waka Landing as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where the Te Arawa waka made its final landfall from Hawaiki — the ancestral landing place of the Te Arawa confederation. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mataatua Waka — Whakatāne Heads
short: Where the Mataatua waka landed around 1350 — and where Wairaka cried the words that named the town.
commentary: Mataatua landed at Whakatāne Heads. With the men ashore and the waka drifting, Wairaka took up a paddle. Her cry 'E kia whakatāne ake au i a au' — 'I will act the part of a man' — gave Whakatāne its name.
audio_story: You are at Whakatāne Heads, the river mouth at the northern edge of the town, and you are looking at the place where one of the great ancestral waka of the Mātaatua federation landed around the year thirteen fifty. The Mataatua waka, captained by Toroa, brought the ancestors of Ngāti Awa, Tūhoe, Whakatōhea and Te Whānau a Apanui to these shores. The story told of the landing is one of the most famous in Māori tradition. The men went ashore to explore. The tide turned and began to pull the waka, with the women still aboard, back out to sea. As senior woman on the canoe the young rangatira Wairaka grasped a paddle. Women in her time did not traditionally paddle waka, and for her to do so was to risk breaching tapu. She called out e kia whakatāne ake au i a au, I will act the part of a man, and drove the waka back to shore. Her words gave the whole district its name, Whakatāne. A bronze statue of Wairaka stands today on Turuturu Rock at the river mouth. Look out across the bar. This is where Ngāti Awa began.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mataatua Waka — Whakatāne Heads as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where the Mataatua waka landed around 1350 — and where Wairaka cried the words that named the town. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mataatua Wharenui, Whakatāne
short: A magnificent carved meeting house returned home after 128 years overseas.
commentary: Mataatua wharenui was built by Ngāti Awa in the 1870s. The government seized it in 1879 and sent it to exhibitions and museums. It was finally returned in 2011 and now stands in a purpose-built gallery in Whakatāne.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mataatua Wharenui, Whakatāne as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A magnificent carved meeting house returned home after 128 years overseas. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Matakana Island
short: A 24 km sand-bar island at the mouth of Tauranga Harbour — predominantly Māori, mostly off-grid, no tourism.
commentary: Matakana Island is a 24 km sand-bar island forming the outer edge of Tauranga Harbour. Home to about 250 people — mostly Māori (Ngāi Te Rangi) — it's a working community of farming, forestry and fishing, not a tourist destination. There's no bridge; a small vehicle barge runs from Omokoroa. The ocean-side beach is 20 km of untouched white sand, regularly empty, and very exposed to surf. Visit with care and respect: this is a lived-in place, not an attraction. Many of the beach-side roads are private.
review_summary: Visitors describe Matakana Island as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A 24 km sand-bar island at the mouth of Tauranga Harbour — predominantly Māori, mostly off-grid, no tourism. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Matatā Wildlife Refuge
short: The largest wetland on the Bay of Plenty coast — one of the best remaining refuges for the critically endangered Australasian bittern.
commentary: Matatā Wildlife Refuge is a 143-hectare coastal wetland at the mouth of the Tarawera River. It supports the highest known density of Australasian bittern in New Zealand.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Matatā Wildlife Refuge, a hundred and forty three hectare coastal wetland tucked between the village of Matatā and the mouth of the Tarawera River. Most people driving State Highway Two pass this place without ever realising what is in there. The reeds are high. The boardwalk is short. The birds are famously hard to see. Matatā is one of the best remaining refuges in New Zealand for matuku, the Australasian bittern, a large cryptically streaked wading bird that is critically endangered and almost invisible in its preferred habitat. On a still evening between September and December you may hear its distinctive deep booming call, which sounds remarkably like someone blowing gently across the top of an empty bottle and is audible for over a kilometre. Fewer than a thousand of these birds remain in New Zealand. Beyond the bittern, the wetland is also one of the last strongholds of the black mudfish, and the surrounding sand dunes hold breeding colonies of variable oystercatcher and New Zealand dotterel. Take the boardwalk, sit quietly at the viewing platform, and above all, listen. Waterproof shoes recommended. This is not a place for dogs or for a hurry.
review_summary: The wildlife encounters at Matatā Wildlife Refuge earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. The largest wetland on the Bay of Plenty coast — one of the best remaining refuges for the critically endangered Australasian bittern. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Mauao (Mount Maunganui) Base Track
short: Loop the base of Mauao, the iconic volcanic headland at the entrance to Tauranga Harbour.
commentary: Mauao (Mount Maunganui) is a 232-metre volcanic cone at the tip of a narrow peninsula between the ocean beach and Tauranga Harbour. The base track is a 3.4 km loop with stunning coastal views. According to Maori legend, Mauao was a nameless hill who asked the patupaiarehe (fairy folk) to drag him to the sea to drown after being rejected in love.
audio_story: You are walking the base track around Mauao, the 232-metre volcanic cone that anchors the Mount Maunganui peninsula like a sentinel. On one side, surf rolls onto the main beach; on the other, Tauranga Harbour stretches to the city. The 3.4-kilometre loop is flat and easy, winding between pohutukawa trees and rocky shoreline. In Maori legend, this hill was once an unnamed, lovesick mountain who asked the fairy folk to drag him to the sea — but they ran out of darkness before reaching the water, and he was stranded here, forever gazing at the ocean. If you have the energy, take the summit track instead — the 20-minute climb rewards you with a panorama that stretches from White Island to the Coromandel. At the base, the hot salt water pools at the foot of the mount are the perfect post-walk reward.
review_summary: The Mauao (Mount Maunganui) Base Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Loop the base of Mauao, the iconic volcanic headland at the entrance to Tauranga Harbour. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mauao / Mount Maunganui
short: A 232 m volcanic cone guarding Tauranga Harbour — sacred to iwi, a one-hour summit walk for visitors.
commentary: Mauao — 'caught by the dawn' — is sacred to Ngāi Te Rangi, Ngāti Ranginui and Ngāti Pūkenga. Tradition holds that it was once a nameless mountain inland, in love with Pūwhenua. When rejected, he asked the patupaiarehe (forest beings) to drag him to the sea so he could drown — but the sun rose before they finished, freezing him in place at the harbour entrance. The one-hour summit loop is the classic 'Mount' walk. A 3.4 km base track also works well. The town at the base, Mount Maunganui, has one of New Zealand's best beaches.
review_summary: Mauao / Mount Maunganui draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 232 m volcanic cone guarding Tauranga Harbour — sacred to iwi, a one-hour summit walk for visitors. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Mauao / Mount Maunganui — The Mountain Caught by the Dawn
short: In Māori tradition, Mauao was a lonely mountain who tried to drown himself — but was caught by the dawn and turned to stone.
commentary: Mauao was once called Otiti. Unloved by the other mountains, he asked the patupaiarehe (fairy folk) to drag him to the sea. But dawn came before he reached the water and he was turned to stone where he stands today. The mountain was a major pā site and is tapu. The summit walk offers extraordinary views.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mauao / Mount Maunganui — The Mountain Caught by the Dawn as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. In Māori tradition, Mauao was a lonely mountain who tried to drown himself — but was caught by the dawn and turned to stone. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mauao Summit Track (Pā History)
short: The iconic Mount at the end of the peninsula — a heavily terraced pā and one of the most important maunga of Tauranga Moana.
commentary: Mauao rises 232m at the tip of the Mount Maunganui peninsula. An intensively terraced pā of Ngāi Te Rangi. Summit track loops in 1 hour.
audio_story: You are climbing Mauao, the dramatic two hundred and thirty two metre cone that rises at the tip of the Mount Maunganui peninsula, and one of the best known landmarks on the whole Bay of Plenty coast. The story told by Ngāi Te Rangi is that Mauao was once a nameless inland hill who fell in love with Pūwhenua, a much more beautiful mountain. When Pūwhenua did not return his love, the patupaiarehe, the forest spirits, took pity on him and agreed to haul him to the coast and drown him in the sea. They dragged the mountain across the land overnight but the work was incomplete when the sun rose. Unable to continue in daylight, they abandoned him at the sea''s edge, where he stands to this day. His full name, Mauao, means caught by the dawn. The summit track is around a kilometre and can be walked in an hour return. Every terrace you cross on the climb was once part of a pā occupied by Ngāi Te Rangi. Defensive ditches, kūmara storage pits, house platforms, all are visible if you know how to look. The summit view over Tauranga, the harbour, Matakana Island, and the long surf beach below, is among the finest on the coast. Do walk it.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mauao Summit Track (Pā History) as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The iconic Mount at the end of the peninsula — a heavily terraced pā and one of the most important maunga of Tauranga Moana. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: McLaren Falls Glow Worm Kayak
short: Paddle through a galaxy of glow worms on a night kayak at McLaren Falls.
commentary: McLaren Falls Park hosts one of the Bay of Plenty's most magical experiences, a nighttime kayak through glow worm-lit waterways. Guided tours paddle along the lake and stream edges after dark, where thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa light up the overhanging banks. The park is 20 minutes from Tauranga.
audio_story: You have arrived at McLaren Falls Park as the last light fades from the sky, which is exactly when the magic begins. Your guide will launch your kayak onto the still water and lead you quietly along the bush-fringed edges of the lake. As darkness settles, the overhanging banks come alive with thousands of glow worms, tiny Arachnocampa luminosa larvae casting their bioluminescent fishing lines. The effect is like floating through a galaxy. These are the same species found in Waitomo, but here you experience them from the open air, under a canopy of native bush with the Southern Cross overhead. Your guide will explain their fascinating and rather gruesome life cycle, each glowing dot is a hungry larva luring prey into sticky threads. Silence and slow paddling are rewarded with the brightest displays.
review_summary: The McLaren Falls Glow Worm Kayak adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddle through a galaxy of glow worms on a night kayak at McLaren Falls. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: McLaren Falls Park
short: A 190ha riverside park west of Tauranga — short walks, a botanical collection, and a free glowworm canyon at night.
commentary: Council park beside the Wairoa River. Multiple short walks, a small arboretum, and the popular glowworm canyon at the far end of the park.
audio_story: You have arrived at McLaren Falls Park, a one hundred and ninety hectare riverside reserve only twenty minutes drive west of Tauranga and yet quietly unknown even to many locals. The park stretches along the dammed lake of the Wairoa River and has, on a single afternoon, almost everything you might want from a short visit, lakeside picnic spots, a peaceful botanical arboretum, gentle walking tracks, and a campground popular with families. There are two attractions worth planning your day around. The first is the namesake McLaren Falls themselves, fed by water released from the Lloyd Mandeno power scheme upstream and only flowing to full power on certain days, when the local kayak community gathers below for one of the best whitewater runs in the North Island. The second is the glowworm canyon at the far end of the park. Bring a torch, leave it off, walk in quietly after dark, and look up at the rock walls of the canyon. The walls flicker with the soft blue green light of thousands of glowworms, free to anyone willing to make the short walk. Tauranga families often pack a barbecue and stay until the lights come on.
review_summary: McLaren Falls Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 190ha riverside park west of Tauranga — short walks, a botanical collection, and a free glowworm canyon at night. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: McLaren Falls Park Walk
short: Walk through McLaren Falls Park for waterfalls by day and wild glowworms by night — no cave required.
commentary: McLaren Falls Park is a 190-hectare park near Tauranga featuring walking trails, a lake, and a waterfall. The park is renowned for its outdoor glowworm displays along the stream banks, visible on dark nights without needing to enter a cave. The collection of trees includes one of New Zealand's finest exotic arboreta.
audio_story: You are entering McLaren Falls Park, a 190-hectare gem tucked away near Tauranga that many Bay of Plenty visitors overlook entirely. By day, the walking trails wind past waterfalls, through exotic and native plantings, and around a peaceful lake stocked with trout. The park contains one of the finest arboreta in the country, with mature specimens from around the world. But return after dark and the park reveals its most magical feature — wild glowworms lining the stream banks like tiny constellations. No cave required: on a dark, damp night, thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa larvae glow blue-green from the overhanging banks, reflecting in the water below. Bring a torch to navigate the paths but switch it off at the viewing points to let your eyes adjust. The effect is mesmerising — the living light of these tiny creatures turning a suburban park into something from a fairy tale. This is one of the best outdoor glowworm displays in the North Island.
sources_to_check: Western Bay of Plenty District Council
review_summary: The McLaren Falls Park Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through McLaren Falls Park for waterfalls by day and wild glowworms by night — no cave required. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mills Reef Winery & Restaurant
short: BOP's premier winery — cellar door tastings, vineyard restaurant and award-winning Elspeth wines.
commentary: Mills Reef is the Bay of Plenty's standout winery, producing wines from both local and Hawke's Bay grapes. The Elspeth range — particularly the Syrah and Chardonnay — consistently wins gold medals. The vineyard restaurant serves seasonal local produce in an elegant setting, and the cellar door offers comprehensive tastings. It's a short drive from Tauranga and makes a great lunch destination.
review_summary: The restaurant and cellar door combination is well-executed. The Elspeth wines impress. Some note it's not technically a vineyard at the site, as grapes come from Hawke's Bay.

name: Motiti Island
short: A low-lying island 20 km off Tauranga — deeply significant to local iwi and the site of the 2011 Rena grounding.
commentary: Motiti is a 15 sq km island with a population of around 30 permanent residents, mostly descendants of the mana whenua, Ngāi Te Hapū. In October 2011 the container ship Rena ran aground on the Astrolabe Reef just north of Motiti.
audio_story: You are looking at Motiti Island, twenty kilometres offshore from Tauranga, a low green island of some fifteen square kilometres rising only a hundred metres above the sea. Motiti is home to a tiny permanent population of around thirty people, mostly descendants of the mana whenua iwi Ngāi Te Hapū, who have held the island for at least six hundred years. There is no wharf, no shop, and no regular ferry. Access is by private boat or light aircraft. In October twenty eleven, Motiti was briefly the most photographed place in New Zealand. The container ship Rena, carrying three hundred and fifty tonnes of heavy fuel oil, ran aground on the Astrolabe Reef just north of the island at full cruising speed. The ship broke up over several months, spilling oil that blackened the beaches of the Bay of Plenty and killing more than two thousand seabirds. It was the worst maritime environmental disaster in New Zealand history. For Motiti iwi the grounding of Rena on their reef was a profound breach of their mana whenua over these waters, and the legal and environmental recovery has taken more than a decade. Look out across the bay and remember that a green island can hold a whole epic.
review_summary: Visitors describe Motiti Island as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A low-lying island 20 km off Tauranga — deeply significant to local iwi and the site of the 2011 Rena grounding. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mount Brewing Co
short: Mount Maunganui craft brewery with a relaxed taproom — hop-forward ales and beach-town vibes.
commentary: Mount Brewing Co captures the laid-back Mount Maunganui lifestyle in liquid form. The taproom pours a rotating selection of craft beers — IPAs, pale ales and sessionable lagers — alongside local guest taps. The outdoor seating area is perfect for a post-beach beer, and the brewery is a short walk from the Main Beach. Tasting paddles are good value for sampling the range.
review_summary: Good beer in a great location. The relaxed vibe matches the Mount lifestyle. Some find the range limited compared to bigger breweries.

name: Mount Maunganui Surf Beach
short: Catch consistent beach breaks at one of NZ's most popular surf spots.
commentary: The Main Beach at Mount Maunganui is a long, consistent beach break that works on all tides. The northern end near Moturiki Island offers more shelter for learners, while the open beach picks up more swell for experienced surfers. Multiple surf schools operate from the beachfront.
audio_story: You have arrived at Mount Maunganui Main Beach, consistently rated one of the best surf beaches in New Zealand. The sand-bottom break stretches for over a kilometre, offering waves for every ability level. If you are learning, head to the northern end near Moturiki Island, where the rocky point provides shelter and the waves are smaller and more predictable. Experienced surfers should check the open beach, which picks up swell from the north and east and can produce head-high waves on a solid swell. The water here is noticeably warmer than much of New Zealand thanks to the East Auckland Current. After your session, the base track around Mauao, the extinct volcanic cone at the end of the beach, is a must-do walk with panoramic views of the coastline.
review_summary: The Mount Maunganui Surf Beach adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Catch consistent beach breaks at one of NZ's most popular surf spots. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Ngā Potiki Cultural Walk, Tauranga
short: A guided cultural walk through the Tauranga landscape — seeing the city through Māori eyes.
commentary: Ngā Potiki a Tamapahore Trust offers guided walks that reveal the Māori cultural landscape beneath modern Tauranga — pā sites, place names, and stories that connect the present to the past.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ngā Potiki Cultural Walk, Tauranga as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A guided cultural walk through the Tauranga landscape — seeing the city through Māori eyes. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōhiwa Harbour
short: A pristine tidal harbour rich in Māori history — wāhi tapu sites and shellfish beds.
commentary: Ōhiwa Harbour is one of NZ's most ecologically intact tidal estuaries and of immense cultural significance. It was a critical mahinga kai — the shellfish beds sustained communities for centuries.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ōhiwa Harbour as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A pristine tidal harbour rich in Māori history — wāhi tapu sites and shellfish beds. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōhiwa Harbour — Te Kooti''s Final Pā
short: The sheltered harbour where Te Kooti lived his final years — and died peacefully in 1893.
commentary: After his 1883 pardon Te Kooti made his home at Ōhiwa with permission of Whakatōhea and other local hapū. He died there in April 1893.
audio_story: You are at Ōhiwa Harbour, a beautiful sheltered tidal estuary between Whakatāne and Ōpōtiki, ringed with native forest and small settlements. After receiving his Crown pardon in eighteen eighty three Te Kooti Arikirangi, the founder of the Ringatū faith, was allowed by the government to return from exile but not to his home at Tūranga near Gisborne, where the memory of the Matawhero raid was still too raw. Instead he was given refuge by Whakatōhea and by local hapū on the shore of Ōhiwa. He built a small settlement here. He preached. He received visitors from across the country, many travelling for days to seek his counsel or his blessing. He married and fathered children. He composed waiata and moteatea, several of which are still sung in Ringatū services to this day. In April eighteen ninety three Te Kooti died peacefully at Ōhiwa in his early sixties. His burial place on the harbour is not a public site, and its exact location is known only to the Ringatū church. If you drive around the harbour today you are passing through the landscape in which the most controversial and the most creative Māori figure of the nineteenth century ended his days in a measure of peace.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ōhiwa Harbour — Te Kooti''s Final Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The sheltered harbour where Te Kooti lived his final years — and died peacefully in 1893. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōhope Beach
short: Voted 'New Zealand's best-loved beach' repeatedly — eleven kilometres of safe swimming between Whakatāne and Ōhiwa Harbour.
commentary: Ōhope Beach is eleven kilometres of east-facing golden sand between Ōtarawairere Bay (west) and the Ōhiwa Harbour entrance (east). The surf is gentler than the Coromandel beaches to the north and Gisborne to the east, and the sand is shallow a long way out — which is why it's consistently voted NZ's 'best-loved' beach in various polls. West End has cafés and a surf club; East End is quieter. The Ōhiwa Harbour at the east end is a wildlife reserve and a sacred kaimoana (seafood) gathering place for Ngāti Awa, Whakatōhea and Upokorehe.
review_summary: Ōhope Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Voted 'New Zealand's best-loved beach' repeatedly — eleven kilometres of safe swimming between Whakatāne and Ōhiwa Harbour. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ohope Beach Coasteering
short: Scramble, swim, and leap off cliffs along the volcanic coastline at Ohope.
commentary: Coasteering at Ohope involves scrambling along volcanic rocks, swimming through surges, and jumping from cliff platforms into deep water. The volcanic coastline near Whakatane provides natural platforms, channels, and deep pools ideal for this adventure sport.
audio_story: You have arrived at Ohope, where the volcanic coastline creates a natural adventure playground for coasteering, the sport of scrambling, swimming, and jumping your way along a rocky shore. Your guide will kit you out with a wetsuit and helmet, then lead you along a route that weaves between rocky channels, surge pools, and cliff faces. The volcanic rock here has been eroded into platforms at various heights, providing natural jumping spots from 2 metres up to 8 metres for the truly committed. Between jumps, you will swim through rock channels where the ocean surges around you, scramble up seaweed-slicked rocks, and explore caves and blow holes carved into the headland. Coasteering is relatively new to New Zealand but has been hugely popular in the UK and Europe for years, and the Bay of Plenty's volcanic coastline is perfectly suited to it. No climbing or swimming expertise is needed, just a willingness to get wet and push your comfort zone.
review_summary: The Ohope Beach Coasteering adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Scramble, swim, and leap off cliffs along the volcanic coastline at Ohope. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Ōhope Beach Seafood
short: Ōhope's best fish and chips — fresh local catch on one of New Zealand's most beautiful beaches.
commentary: Ōhope Beach is regularly voted one of New Zealand's best beaches, and the fish and chip shops here use the freshest local catch — snapper, blue cod, tarakihi. Grab a portion, find a spot on the sand, and eat while watching the sunset over White Island. It's the quintessential New Zealand beach experience. Simple, unpretentious, and better than it has any right to be.
review_summary: Fresh fish and a beautiful beach — hard to go wrong. Nothing fancy, just excellent fish and chips. Best visited on a fine evening for the full experience.

name: Ohope Beach to Ohiwa Harbour Walk
short: Walk 11 km of golden sand from Ohope Beach to the ecologically rich Ohiwa Harbour.
commentary: Ohope Beach is an 11 km stretch of golden sand regularly voted New Zealand's favourite beach. The eastern end meets Ohiwa Harbour, a significant ecological area home to dotterel, oystercatcher, and shorebirds. The harbour is one of the least modified in the North Island.
audio_story: You are walking the length of Ohope Beach, 11 kilometres of golden sand that has been voted New Zealand's most loved beach multiple times. The beach stretches in a gentle arc, backed by dunes and pohutukawa, with the distant outline of Whakaari White Island on the horizon. At the eastern end, the beach gives way to the Ohiwa Harbour, one of the least modified harbours in the North Island. At low tide, the harbour reveals vast tidal flats where New Zealand dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and godwits feed. The harbour is a nursery for juvenile fish and home to extensive seagrass beds. Walk out onto the sand spit where harbour meets ocean and watch the currents swirl. If the tide is rising, keep to the marked track — the channels fill quickly. This is the Eastern Bay of Plenty at its tranquil best.
review_summary: The Ohope Beach to Ohiwa Harbour Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk 11 km of golden sand from Ohope Beach to the ecologically rich Ohiwa Harbour. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Ōpōtiki & Hiona St Stephen the Martyr
short: The eastern gateway town to the East Cape — a small river-mouth settlement with a heavy nineteenth century history and a beautifully simple timber church.
commentary: Ōpōtiki is the service town at the junction of the Waioeka and Ōtara rivers. Hiona St Stephen the Martyr Church (1864) is where missionary Carl Volkner was killed in 1865 during the Pai Marire conflict — a story with long unresolved shadows for Whakatōhea iwi.
audio_story: You have arrived in Ōpōtiki, a small town of about four thousand people at the junction of two rivers on the eastern edge of the Bay of Plenty. This is the gateway to the East Cape and, depending on which way you are driving, either the last decent coffee stop for three hours or the first. Ōpōtiki has a quiet, weathered character that rewards slowing down. The white timber church of Hiona St Stephen the Martyr on Church Street is one of the oldest mission churches in the country, dating to eighteen sixty four. It is also the site of a dark and long unresolved story. In March eighteen sixty five, the German born Lutheran missionary Carl Volkner was killed by followers of the Pai Marire movement during the confused violence of the Land Wars. The local iwi, Whakatōhea, were punished collectively by colonial confiscation of a quarter of a million acres of their land. In twenty twenty three a Treaty of Waitangi settlement finally returned some of that land and offered a formal Crown apology. The church itself is a place of quiet reflection. Sit for a moment. The eastern Bay of Plenty carries more history than most New Zealand towns its size.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ōpōtiki & Hiona St Stephen the Martyr as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The eastern gateway town to the East Cape — a small river-mouth settlement with a heavy nineteenth century history and a beautifully simple timber church. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Papamoa Beach Stand-Up Paddleboarding
short: Glide along the calm waters of Papamoa Beach on a stand-up paddleboard.
commentary: Papamoa Beach offers flat, sheltered conditions ideal for stand-up paddleboarding, especially on calm mornings. Board hire is available from beachfront operators. The 16-kilometre stretch of golden sand provides plenty of room to explore.
audio_story: You have arrived at Papamoa Beach, where 16 kilometres of golden sand and typically gentle surf create ideal conditions for stand-up paddleboarding. Early mornings here are sublime, the ocean goes glassy, the sun rises behind Mauao to the northwest, and you can paddle in solitude along one of New Zealand's longest uninterrupted beaches. SUP is deceptively great exercise, engaging your core with every stroke while you enjoy the view from an elevated vantage point above the water. You might spot stingrays in the shallows or dolphins cruising the outer break. Board hire is straightforward from operators along the beachfront, and first-timers will find the calm conditions here much more forgiving than ocean swells elsewhere.
review_summary: The Papamoa Beach Stand-Up Paddleboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Glide along the calm waters of Papamoa Beach on a stand-up paddleboard. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Park
short: A network of terraced pā sites in the hills above Papamoa — a regional park showcasing Māori earthworks.
commentary: The park preserves a network of pā sites, terracing, and storage pits with clear defensive features. Walking tracks wind through with interpretation panels.
review_summary: Visitors describe Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Park as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A network of terraced pā sites in the hills above Papamoa — a regional park showcasing Māori earthworks. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pāpāmoa Hills Regional Park
short: A regional park of ridge-top pā sites above Pāpāmoa, with sweeping views from Mauao along the coast to Whakaari.
commentary: The Pāpāmoa Hills hold an extraordinary concentration of pre-European Māori pā, with earthworks, terraces, and kumara storage pits still visible. A two-hour loop walk climbs to the summit of Karangaumu.
audio_story: You are standing in Pāpāmoa Hills Regional Park, a ridge of low green hills that rises directly from the Bay of Plenty coast behind the suburbs of Pāpāmoa. These hills are one of the finest concentrations of pre-European Māori pā sites in the North Island. The ridges above you have been shaped by hand. Terraces, ditches, kumara storage pits, and the postholes of long vanished whare are still clearly visible in the contours of the land. At various times over the last six hundred years these hills were home to several thousand people living in fortified villages that commanded the harbour approaches in every direction. The two hour loop walk climbs gently to the summit of Karangaumu. From there, on a clear day, you can see Mauao above Mount Maunganui to the north, the whole long stretch of Pāpāmoa and Pukehina beaches curving away to the east, and Whakaari, White Island, smoking fifty kilometres out to sea. The park is a wāhi tapu, a sacred place. Please walk only on the marked tracks. Do not picnic on the pā sites. And take a moment, from the top, to imagine the conch shell alarm call ringing out from ridge to ridge.
review_summary: Pāpāmoa Hills Regional Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A regional park of ridge-top pā sites above Pāpāmoa, with sweeping views from Mauao along the coast to Whakaari. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Picnicka
short: Tauranga CBD sharing-plates restaurant — seasonal BOP produce, house-made everything, and a buzzing atmosphere.
commentary: Picnicka sits in Tauranga's revitalised CBD and has quickly become the city's most exciting restaurant. The sharing-plates format showcases Bay of Plenty produce — expect local seafood, seasonal vegetables and house-made everything from bread to ice cream. The natural wine list and cocktail programme are equally creative. A bright spot in a city whose dining scene is rapidly improving.
review_summary: The most exciting restaurant in Tauranga's CBD. The sharing format works well. Some find the small-plates pricing adds up quickly.

name: Pukehina Beach
short: A long, quiet, white-sand beach between Maketū and Matatā — the locals' beach that Tauranga drivers miss.
commentary: Pukehina Beach is a 7-km barrier spit between the Pongakawa and Waitahanui Streams, with a tiny permanent population and a growing collection of holiday homes.
audio_story: You have arrived at Pukehina Beach, a seven kilometre barrier spit on the Bay of Plenty coast between Maketū and Matatā. For most of its length Pukehina is exactly what a New Zealand beach is supposed to be. White sand, low dunes, good surf, almost no infrastructure, and at the height of summer still room to find a patch of beach no one else has claimed. The permanent population is small, just a few hundred people. In the school holidays that number multiplies many times over as Waikato and Tauranga families fill a modest but growing collection of holiday homes strung along the sand dunes. Behind the spit lies the Waihī estuary, rich feeding country for wading birds and whitebait in season. The surf can be large in a northeasterly, so swim between the flags during the patrolled season. At the northern end is the Pukehina boat ramp, which in the right weather is one of the most productive fishing launches on the Bay coast for snapper and kahawai. If you are driving State Highway Two between Tauranga and Ōpōtiki, Pukehina is a five minute detour off the highway. The locals would rather you did not mention they sent you.
review_summary: Pukehina Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A long, quiet, white-sand beach between Maketū and Matatā — the locals' beach that Tauranga drivers miss. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Pukehinahina / Gate Pā
short: Where Ngāi Te Rangi defeated a larger British force in 1864 — the most famous Māori victory of the NZ Wars.
commentary: On 29 April 1864, 230 Ngāi Te Rangi warriors under Rāwiri Puhirake defeated 1,700 British troops at Pukehinahina (Gate Pā). 31 British killed; Colonel Booth mortally wounded.
audio_story: You are at Pukehinahina, also known as Gate Pā, the battlefield of one of the most famous engagements of the New Zealand Wars. On the twenty ninth of April, eighteen sixty four, the British forces of the invasion of Tauranga, some seventeen hundred men, attacked a small fortified pā held by around two hundred and thirty Ngāi Te Rangi warriors under the rangatira Rāwiri Puhirake. The British opened a day long artillery bombardment that reduced the pā to rubble, then launched a mass assault expecting easy victory. Instead, the assault column walked straight into concealed rifle pits and was slaughtered. Thirty one British soldiers were killed and many more wounded, including the commanding officer Colonel Booth, who died of his wounds in the pā overnight. During that night, Heni Te Kiri Karamū, a kuia, was said to have given water to the wounded and dying colonial soldiers on both sides, an act of mercy long remembered. Ngāi Te Rangi withdrew under cover of darkness. The British occupied the empty pā the next morning and claimed a hollow victory. The modern reserve includes a well interpreted walking loop, replica earthworks, and a chapel commemorating the dead of both sides. A small, essential pause on any journey through Tauranga.
review_summary: Visitors describe Pukehinahina / Gate Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Ngāi Te Rangi defeated a larger British force in 1864 — the most famous Māori victory of the NZ Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pūtauaki / Mount Edgecumbe
short: A sacred volcanic peak — the ancestral mountain of Ngāti Awa.
commentary: Pūtauaki (821m) is the maunga of Ngāti Awa and is visible from much of the eastern Bay of Plenty. The mountain holds deep spiritual significance and features in many tribal traditions.
review_summary: Visitors describe Pūtauaki / Mount Edgecumbe as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A sacred volcanic peak — the ancestral mountain of Ngāti Awa. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rotoehu & Rotomā Lakes
short: Two of the quieter Rotorua lakes, on SH30 between Tauranga and Rotorua — crystal-clear swimming, no crowds.
commentary: Lake Rotoehu and Lake Rotomā sit on SH30, between Tauranga and Rotorua, and are among the clearest freshwater lakes in the country — visibility of 15–20 metres in places. Rotomā has white-sand swimming beaches; Rotoehu is best approached via the Soda Springs bush walk. Hongi's Track, between the two lakes, was the path the Ngāpuhi chief Hongi Hika used in 1823 when he dragged his war canoes from Lake Rotoehu to Lake Rotorua, sparking the 'Musket War' attacks on Te Arawa.
