The destination is Cathedral Cove, where a massive natural archway connects two pristine beaches, but the journey is equally spectacular. Your guide will point out volcanic rock formations, explain how the white cliffs were formed from ancient volcanic ash, and keep watch for marine life. Snapper and kingfish patrol these waters, and between December and March, orca have been known to cruise through the reserve. You will land on the beach beneath the arch, a view that needs no filter.
review_summary: The Cathedral Cove Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddle through sea caves and arches to the iconic Cathedral Cove from the water. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Colville
short: The last shop on the far north Coromandel — a ramshackle general store in a valley that has been a hippie refuge since the 1970s.
commentary: Colville is the most northern settlement on the Coromandel with shop services. The general store is famous and has supplied generations of back-to-the-land communities.
audio_story: You have arrived at Colville, the last real shop on the far north Coromandel. Colville is thirty kilometres north of Coromandel Town on a road that has been paved only fairly recently. Beyond Colville the road turns to gravel and climbs over ridges to reach the end of the peninsula at Port Jackson and Fletcher Bay. The village itself is tiny. A general store, a community hall, a cafe, a handful of houses. But Colville is the beating heart of one of the more distinctive alternative communities in New Zealand. From the early nineteen seventies, inspired by the commune movement and the cheap land of this remote corner, people moved here to farm, to build by hand, to live outside the mainstream. The valley remains full of them. The general store has been stocked for nearly a century with the things you actually need at the end of the road. Fuel, gumboots, flour in bulk, mosquito coils, and a fairly remarkable selection of organic produce. The cafe does excellent pies. If you are heading out to the tip, top up here. If you are not, Colville is a fine place to turn around and head back. Either way, the drive here is the real point.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Colville is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The last shop on the far north Coromandel — a ramshackle general store in a valley that has been a hippie refuge since the 1970s. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Cook's Transit at Mercury Bay
audio_story: You are in Whitianga, on the shore of what Captain Cook named Mercury Bay. In November seventeen sixty nine Cook anchored his ship Endeavour here to observe the transit of the planet Mercury across the sun, a vital astronomical observation that helped him calculate longitude. He and his naturalist Joseph Banks landed and met Ngāti Hei, with whom they traded and spoke through the Tahitian navigator Tupaia who had sailed south with the expedition. Cook erected a pole, raised the British flag, and formally claimed possession, though Ngāti Hei had not consented to any such thing. A stone memorial at Cooks Beach on the south side of the bay commemorates his landing. The Whitianga foreshore now holds a replica flagpole and a heritage panel. Think carefully as you stand here. A transit of a planet, a meeting of peoples, and a colonial claim all happened within a few days on this beach. Mercury Bay was a beginning of something that has not yet finished being worked out.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Cook's Transit at Mercury Bay — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Coromandel Goldmine Experience
short: Explore historic gold mine tunnels and try your luck panning for gold.
commentary: The Coromandel Goldmine Experience takes visitors into the historic Doolan Brothers mine tunnels, dating from the 1860s gold rush era. After exploring the underground workings, guests can try gold panning in the creek. The Coromandel Peninsula produced significant gold during the 19th century.
audio_story: You have arrived at the entrance to the Doolan Brothers gold mine, hand-dug into these hills during the Coromandel gold rush that began in 1852 when Charles Ring discovered gold in Driving Creek. Step inside and the temperature drops immediately as you enter tunnels carved by hand with picks and shovels over 160 years ago. Your guide will explain how miners followed quartz veins through the rock, crushing the ore to extract fine gold particles. The Coromandel Peninsula was New Zealand's first major goldfield, and at its peak, thousands of miners swarmed these hills. After exploring the underground workings, you will head to the creek outside to try gold panning yourself. Real alluvial gold still washes through this stream, and with the right technique, you might just find a speck to take home.
review_summary: The Coromandel Goldmine Experience adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Explore historic gold mine tunnels and try your luck panning for gold. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Coromandel Oyster Company
short: Fresh oysters from the source — buy directly from the Coromandel Harbour oyster farm.
commentary: The Coromandel Oyster Company grows Pacific oysters in the clean waters of Coromandel Harbour, and you can buy them fresh from the source. The experience of standing beside the harbour eating oysters that were in the water hours ago is hard to beat. They also sell smoked oysters, mussels and other shellfish. Bring lemon and a shucking knife, or buy them pre-shucked. A quintessential Coromandel food moment.
review_summary: Oysters don't get fresher than this. Great value compared to restaurant prices. The setup is basic — bring your own lemon and condiments.

name: Coromandel Town
short: Where New Zealand's first gold rush began in 1852 — a small historic town with outsized character.
commentary: Gold was found in Driving Creek in late 1852 by sawyer Charles Ring — the country's first gold rush, a decade before Otago. It didn't last; the ore was too fragmented. But the town survived, then boomed again when kauri milling and later proper gold mining restarted in the 1860s–80s. Today Coromandel Town is a small, quirky, independent-minded place. The Coromandel Smoking Company sells excellent smoked mussels and fish. Tāne Tinorau's descendants still own Driving Creek. Visit on a weekday.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Coromandel Town — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where New Zealand's first gold rush began in 1852 — a small historic town with outsized character. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Driving Creek Café
short: Quirky garden café near the famous Driving Creek Railway — pottery, plants and great cabinet food.
commentary: Located near the entrance to the Driving Creek Railway, this garden café captures the creative spirit of Coromandel's arts community. The cabinet is stocked with excellent homemade pies, cakes and slices, and the coffee is good. The garden is filled with pottery, plants and art, making it a pleasant spot to linger before or after a railway ride. Everything feels handmade and heartfelt.
review_summary: Charming and quirky — captures the Coromandel spirit. The homemade food is good. Hours can be limited outside peak season.

name: Driving Creek Railway
short: A quirky 2.75 km hand-built narrow-gauge mountain railway — begun by potter Barry Brickell in 1974 as a way to get clay down the hill.
commentary: Potter and conservationist Barry Brickell bought a worked-out kauri block above Coromandel town in 1973 and began hand-building a narrow-gauge railway to haul clay out of the hills. He never stopped. When he died in 2016 the Driving Creek Railway was 2.75 km long, with two spiral tunnels, five reversing switchbacks, three zig-zag sections, and a terminus called the Eyefull Tower. Operated daily by volunteers. The ride takes about an hour. The hills Barry began replanting in 1973 are now mature native bush — that's the real point of the place.
review_summary: Driving Creek Railway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A quirky 2. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Driving Creek Zipline & Canopy Tour
short: Zipline through a regenerating kauri forest at a unique artist-built railway sanctuary.
commentary: The Driving Creek zipline experience combines native bush canopy exploration with the conservation vision of the late Barry Brickell. The course features multiple ziplines through regenerating kauri and native podocarp forest. The site also includes the famous Driving Creek Railway, a narrow-gauge line built by hand.
audio_story: You have arrived at Driving Creek, the extraordinary legacy of artist and conservationist Barry Brickell, who spent decades planting over 30,000 native trees to regenerate this valley. Your zipline adventure takes you through the canopy of this regenerating forest, soaring between platforms built among kauri, rimu, and totara trees. The lines vary in length and height, with the longest spanning over 200 metres above the valley floor. Between zips, your guide will explain the remarkable conservation story of this place and point out young kauri trees that Brickell planted by hand. The site is also home to the famous Driving Creek Railway, a narrow-gauge line that Brickell built himself to carry clay from the valley to his pottery studio. This is adventure with purpose, every visit supports the ongoing reforestation project.
review_summary: The Driving Creek Zipline & Canopy Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Zipline through a regenerating kauri forest at a unique artist-built railway sanctuary. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Hahei Pā (Te Pare Point)
audio_story: You are at Te Pare Point, the headland at the southern end of Hahei Beach. The terraces and pits visible here mark an old Ngāti Hei pā, one of the defended settlements of the Mercury Bay coast. From this ridge, sentries could see far out into the Pacific and signal any approaching waka. Ngāti Hei trace descent from Hei, an ancestor who travelled on the Te Arawa waka and whose descendants settled this coastline. When Captain Cook anchored off here in seventeen sixty nine during a transit of Mercury to calibrate his longitude, Ngāti Hei observed the strange visitors with cautious diplomacy. The pā was abandoned in the musket wars of the eighteen twenties. Today the walking track loops around the headland with panoramic views to Cathedral Cove and the Te Whanganui o Hei marine reserve below. Stay on the path, respect the terraces, and pause at the viewpoint. This quiet headland was a hub of observation, defence, and welcome long before the beach holidaymakers arrived.
review_summary: Visitors describe Hahei Pā (Te Pare Point) as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Hot Water Beach
short: Dig a hole in the sand at low tide and you'll hit water hot enough to cook in.
commentary: Hot Water Beach has a geothermal spring that rises through the sand at one particular spot, heating the beach to around 64°C just below the surface. Two hours either side of low tide you can dig a hole with a spade (rental available from the store opposite) and make your own natural spa pool. Timing matters — arrive at low tide or the sea covers the hot spot. The beach is beautiful but the surf is dangerous; the rip is one of the worst on the east coast. Swim between the flags only. No digging at high tide.
review_summary: Hot Water Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Dig a hole in the sand at low tide and you'll hit water hot enough to cook in. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Hot Water Beach Coastal Walk
short: Dig your own hot pool in the sand at this geothermally heated beach on the Coromandel coast.
commentary: Hot Water Beach has two underground hot springs that rise to the surface through the sand, reaching temperatures up to 64 degrees Celsius. The thermal area is accessible for about two hours either side of low tide. The beach is also a significant surfing spot and has dangerous rip currents.
audio_story: You are arriving at Hot Water Beach with a spade in hand, ready to dig your own personal spa pool in the sand. Two underground hot springs push water at up to 64 degrees Celsius through fissures in the rock beneath this beach, heating the sand above them. For about two hours either side of low tide, you can excavate a pool, mix the scalding spring water with cool seawater, and soak in your own creation while waves crash nearby. The thermal area is small — just 10 metres across — so it gets crowded at peak times. Come early or late for the best experience. Be careful: the water directly above the vents is hot enough to scald. Outside the thermal area, this is also a good surf beach, though the rip currents can be treacherous. It is one of those uniquely New Zealand experiences where geothermal meets coastal in the most wonderful way.
review_summary: The Hot Water Beach Coastal Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Dig your own hot pool in the sand at this geothermally heated beach on the Coromandel coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Hot Water Beach Thermal Dig
short: Dig your own hot pool in the sand where geothermal water meets the Pacific.
commentary: At Hot Water Beach, geothermal springs push water at up to 64 degrees Celsius through the sand at low tide. Visitors dig their own hot pools with spades, creating a unique natural spa on the beach. The thermal area is accessible for about two hours either side of low tide.
audio_story: You have arrived at Hot Water Beach, one of the Coromandel's most unusual natural attractions. Beneath the sand here, two geothermal vents push mineral-rich water to the surface at temperatures reaching 64 degrees Celsius. Your mission is simple: grab a spade, find a spot between the rock outcrops at the southern end of the beach, and start digging. Within minutes, hot water will seep into your handmade pool, and you can mix it with cool seawater to find your perfect temperature. The effect is surreal, sitting in a steaming pool on an open beach with Pacific waves breaking metres away. Timing is everything, the thermal area is only accessible for about two hours either side of low tide. If you do not have a spade, you can hire one from the shop on the hill above the beach.
review_summary: The Hot Water Beach Thermal Dig adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dig your own hot pool in the sand where geothermal water meets the Pacific. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Kapanga Gold Mine Coromandel Town
audio_story: You are in Coromandel Town, where the first payable gold in New Zealand was discovered in eighteen fifty two by Charles Ring at nearby Driving Creek. The Kapanga Mine operated for decades above the township, its battery stamping ore late into each night. The gold never made Coromandel as rich as Thames or the later Ōhinemuri fields, but the mining years shaped the town forever, a tight street plan along the harbour, a scatter of miners' cottages, and the old school bell that still rings. Nearby, the Driving Creek Railway, built by the eccentric potter Barry Brickell from nineteen seventy five onwards, climbs up the reforested hills in narrow gauge loops, showing how quickly native bush can recover when given a chance. The Coromandel Mining and Historic Museum holds period photographs of bearded miners and their weather-beaten wives. Stand in the main street, count the old verandahs, and imagine this sleepy seaside town as a hammering, shouting place full of men chasing their fortune in quartz.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Kapanga Gold Mine Coromandel Town — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Karaka Bird Hide, Miranda
short: The Firth of Thames wader roost — one of the most important wader sites in the southern hemisphere, and the endpoint of the 11,000 km godwit migration.
commentary: The Miranda shell bank on the western Firth of Thames hosts up to 30,000 migratory wading birds each year. The Pūkorokoro Miranda Shorebird Centre has a staffed hide and visitor centre.
audio_story: You have arrived at Pūkorokoro Miranda on the western shore of the Firth of Thames, and you are standing at one of the most important bird sites in the southern hemisphere. The long shell banks that front the Firth here are the high tide roost for thousands of migratory wading birds that spend the northern summer in Siberia and Alaska and the southern summer here. Every year between thirty and forty thousand birds pass through. The star of the show is the kuaka, or bar-tailed godwit, which flies every autumn in a single non-stop flight from western Alaska to the Firth of Thames. The flight is around eleven thousand kilometres, takes nine days, and ranks as the longest continuous bird migration ever recorded. Other regulars include the red knot, the wrybill which is endemic to New Zealand, and the South Island pied oystercatcher. The Pūkorokoro Miranda Shorebird Centre has a free staffed visitor centre with telescopes, and a short boardwalk takes you to an excellent bird hide overlooking the main roost. The best viewing is on a rising tide, about two hours before high water. Bring binoculars, or borrow them at the centre. This is understated, slightly damp, utterly world class birdwatching.
review_summary: The wildlife encounters at Karaka Bird Hide, Miranda earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. The Firth of Thames wader roost — one of the most important wader sites in the southern hemisphere, and the endpoint of the 11,000 km godwit migration. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Karangahake Windows Walk
short: A gentle riverside walk through gold-mining tunnels with windows cut into the gorge wall.
commentary: Easy 2.5km loop through the Karangahake Gorge passing the historic Crown Mine workings, including a long pedestrian rail tunnel.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Karangahake Gorge, which sounds like a small thing but is actually one of the most engaging short walks anywhere in the North Island. The gorge cuts through the southern end of the Coromandel Range, and from the eighteen seventies until the First World War it was the centre of one of the richest gold mining districts in the country. The mines have long since closed but the workings are still here, and a beautifully developed network of paths links them together. The Windows Walk is the classic loop. You cross a swing bridge over the Ohinemuri River, follow a track along a narrow ledge, and then enter the rock itself. The path runs through old mining tunnels with windows cut through the cliff wall to give the miners light. You can lean out and look straight down into the gorge. Bring a torch. The longer route also passes through the eight hundred metre pedestrian rail tunnel from the abandoned Paeroa to Waihi line, an unforgettable experience for kids of any age. Above all, the gorge itself is beautiful, deep green, river-loud, and shaped by human ambition that has now mostly given way to bush.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Karangahake Windows Walk — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A gentle riverside walk through gold-mining tunnels with windows cut into the gorge wall. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Kauaeranga Valley – The Pinnacles Walk
short: Climb to The Pinnacles for panoramic views of the Coromandel from a dramatic rocky summit at 759 m.
commentary: The Pinnacles Walk in the Kauaeranga Valley is a popular overnight tramp reaching 759 metres. The track passes through regenerating bush and historic kauri dam sites. The Pinnacles Hut accommodates 80 people, and the final climb to the summit uses fixed ladders and chain handholds through rocky terrain.
audio_story: You are heading up the Kauaeranga Valley towards The Pinnacles, the Coromandel's most popular overnight tramp. The track rises through regenerating bush — this valley was heavily logged for kauri timber in the 1800s, and along the way you pass the remains of massive kauri dams that were used to flush logs downstream in artificial floods. The bush has recovered remarkably, and the birdlife is rich. After about three hours, you reach Pinnacles Hut at 759 metres, an 80-bunk hut perched on a ridge. From here, the summit track gets serious — fixed ladders and chain handholds help you scramble up the final rocky pinnacles. From the top, the views are extraordinary: the entire Coromandel Peninsula spread out below you, the Pacific Ocean to the east, the Firth of Thames to the west. Try to time your ascent for sunrise — watching dawn light up the peninsula from this vantage point is unforgettable.
review_summary: The Kauaeranga Valley – The Pinnacles Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Climb to The Pinnacles for panoramic views of the Coromandel from a dramatic rocky summit at 759 m. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Kauaeranga Valley DOC Visitor Centre
short: The DOC centre that tells the story of kauri logging in the Kauaeranga Valley — the single most productive kauri-logging basin in New Zealand.
commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley, behind Thames, produced approximately 200 million board feet of kauri between 1871 and 1928 via driving dams and bush tramways. The DOC centre tells that story before you set off for the Pinnacles.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Kauaeranga Valley Visitor Centre, fifteen kilometres up the valley from Thames on the Coromandel Peninsula. Most people who stop here are on their way to the Pinnacles, the most popular overnight tramp in the upper North Island. Before you go, give the small DOC interpretation centre twenty minutes. The story it tells is the story of how an entire forest vanished. Between eighteen seventy one and nineteen twenty eight, the Kauaeranga Valley produced around two hundred million board feet of kauri timber. At its peak several thousand men worked these hillsides. The logs were moved down the steep tributary streams using kauri driving dams, ingenious wooden barriers that would be packed with logs and then blown apart in a single explosive release, sending a wall of water and timber hurtling downstream to the tidal flats at Thames. At least fifty of these dams were built in this one valley. Today, not a single mature kauri remains in the main basin. The regenerating forest you will walk through on the Pinnacles track is all second growth. Understanding what was lost, and how quickly it was lost, gives the climb to the Pinnacles a different flavour.
review_summary: Kauaeranga Valley DOC Visitor Centre draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The DOC centre that tells the story of kauri logging in the Kauaeranga Valley — the single most productive kauri-logging basin in New Zealand. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Kauri Logging Heritage
audio_story: You are in the forested hills of Coromandel, where for more than a century the giant kauri were felled, dragged, and driven down steep creeks to the sea. From the eighteen thirties kauri timber was the backbone of the colonial economy, prized for its long straight grain and used for ships' masts, house framing, and furniture worldwide. Loggers built elaborate timber dams high in the bush, filling them with logs, then tripping the gates so that the sudden surge of water flushed the logs down to the harbour. Many of these driving dams survive in the ranges as dark pools and broken stonework. The scale of destruction was immense. The original kauri forests of the peninsula, which had stood for over a thousand years in places, were reduced to fragments. Today kauri are under another threat from dieback disease. Stay on tracks, clean your boots at every station, and when you see a surviving giant, slow down and be quiet. The absence of the old forest is itself one of the Coromandel's main stories.
review_summary: Kauri Logging Heritage draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Luke's Kitchen at Kūaotunu
short: Beachside restaurant in a tiny Coromandel village — wood-fired pizzas, seafood and summer live music.
commentary: Luke's Kitchen in the tiny village of Kūaotunu is a quintessential Coromandel experience. The wood-fired pizzas are excellent, the seafood is local, and the coffee is organic. In summer, live music nights draw crowds from across the peninsula. The rustic, beachside setting — think bare feet, salty hair and cold wine — captures everything people love about the Coromandel lifestyle. Off-season hours vary.
review_summary: The relaxed beachside atmosphere is perfect for Coromandel. The pizzas are surprisingly good. Very seasonal — check hours outside summer.

name: Martha Mine Waihi
audio_story: You are at the edge of the Martha Mine at Waihi, the largest goldmine in New Zealand's history. Gold was discovered here in eighteen seventy eight, and the Martha lode proved astonishingly rich. By the early twentieth century, Waihi was one of the country's biggest mining towns, with a stamp battery visible from kilometres away. A long labour dispute, the Waihi strike of nineteen twelve, became a landmark event in New Zealand's industrial history. Hundreds of miners walked off the job against the new arbitration laws, and the strike was eventually broken with violence after one striker, Fred Evans, was killed in a clash. The original underground mine closed in nineteen fifty two, but modern open pit mining reopened the Martha in nineteen eighty seven and continues today. The vast pit cuts into the town's centre, a strange mirror of the tunnels that once spread beneath it. Walk the lookout, see the scale, and consider both the wealth this rock has generated and the hard decades of miners' lives it has taken.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Martha Mine Waihi — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: New Chums Beach / Wainuiototo
short: One of the last completely undeveloped white-sand beaches in the North Island — no road, 30 minutes walk in.
commentary: New Chums (Wainuiototo) is reached only on foot — park at the end of Whangapoua Beach, wade across the stream at low tide, walk over a bush-clad headland for 30 minutes, and you arrive at a kilometre of curved, completely undeveloped white sand backed by pōhutukawa forest. No road, no buildings, no toilets, no rubbish bins, no shops. Take your rubbish out. Don't go in rain (stream crossings). This is how the Coromandel coast used to look in the 1940s; there is sustained local effort to keep it that way.
review_summary: New Chums Beach / Wainuiototo draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. One of the last completely undeveloped white-sand beaches in the North Island — no road, 30 minutes walk in. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Opito Bay
short: A glorious 3 km white-sand beach on the eastern Coromandel — pohutukawa-fringed, holiday-only, and stunningly quiet in winter.
commentary: Opito Bay is a 3-km east-coast beach north of Kuaotunu. Around 200 baches and permanent houses. No shop, no services.
audio_story: You have arrived at Opito Bay, three kilometres of white sand that is for many Coromandel regulars the beach against which all others are measured. Opito is reached by a narrow sealed road off the Kuaotunu Road and does not sit on any through route. You arrive at Opito Bay because you have chosen to. The settlement is around two hundred houses strung along the beach road, most of them bach rather than permanent home, and there is no shop, no cafe, and no fuel. There is, however, almost three kilometres of gently shelving white sand, fringed by old pohutukawa, with clean sea water that is safe for swimming almost all year round. The bay faces east which means it is protected from the prevailing westerly and catches the morning sun. At low tide, vast sand flats open up, good for sand castles and for gathering cockles. At the northern end a little headland shelters a rocky snorkelling bay. Walk the length of the beach at sunrise. Stop at the small historic pā site on the northern hill. Come back to your bach for breakfast. If you have been lucky enough to be invited to Opito, you will understand immediately why New Zealanders guard these places jealously.
review_summary: Opito Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A glorious 3 km white-sand beach on the eastern Coromandel — pohutukawa-fringed, holiday-only, and stunningly quiet in winter. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ōpito Bay — Mercury Bay Pā Sites
short: A beautiful bay with prominent pā sites — part of the Mercury Bay area where Cook observed the Transit of Mercury in 1769.
commentary: Ōpito Bay is surrounded by Māori archaeological sites. The wider Mercury Bay area was home to Ngāti Hei, who interacted with Cook when he anchored here in November 1769.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ōpito Bay — Mercury Bay Pā Sites as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A beautiful bay with prominent pā sites — part of the Mercury Bay area where Cook observed the Transit of Mercury in 1769. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Otama Beach
short: One of the most under-developed beaches on the Coromandel east coast — a long curve of white sand with no shops and very few houses.
commentary: Otama is a largely undeveloped beach between Whangapoua and Kuaotunu. Significant New Zealand dotterel nesting grounds in the dunes.
audio_story: You have arrived at Otama Beach, which may be the quietest of all the good east coast Coromandel beaches. Getting here is half the reason it is quiet. The road in from Kuaotunu climbs over a steep ridge and then drops down a sharp gravel descent to the long curve of white sand. There is no shop. There is no cafe. There is not even much of a settlement. A scattering of fifty or sixty baches, some modest, some flash, lies back in the dune country. The rest is farm, bush, and beach. Otama is one of the most important New Zealand dotterel nesting sites on the peninsula. From September to February please keep well above the high tide line and keep dogs on leads. The dotterels nest in shallow scrapes in the sand, and a single careless step can destroy a nest. At the northern end of the beach the Otama Stream meets the sea in a small tidal lagoon that is perfect for children. The southern end has the best surf. There are no lifeguards. Swim sensibly. Pack in everything you need, pack out everything you brought. And then, back in the car, keep Otama to yourself.
review_summary: Otama Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. One of the most under-developed beaches on the Coromandel east coast — a long curve of white sand with no shops and very few houses. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Pacific Coast Highway – Coromandel Section
short: Drive the Pacific Coast Highway around the Coromandel Peninsula's dramatic coastline.
commentary: The Pacific Coast Highway traces the Coromandel Peninsula coastline via SH25, linking Thames, Coromandel town, Whitianga, and Hot Water Beach. The 170 km route passes through the Coromandel Ranges with views across the Hauraki Gulf. The peninsula's gold mining history, pohutukawa-fringed beaches, and dramatic coastal scenery make it a classic New Zealand drive.
audio_story: You are setting out along the Pacific Coast Highway as it hugs the western coast of the Coromandel Peninsula. The road clings to the hillside above the Firth of Thames, with views across the water to the Hauraki Plains. This peninsula was once the epicentre of New Zealand's gold rush — the Coromandel goldfield was opened in 1852, years before the more famous Otago strikes. As you round the top of the peninsula, the landscape shifts to native bush, pohutukawa-lined coves, and turquoise bays. Mercury Bay, where you reach Whitianga, is where Captain Cook observed the transit of Mercury in 1769 — giving both the bay and the planet's name to the map. Continue south to Hot Water Beach, where thermal springs beneath the sand let you dig your own personal spa pool at low tide. The 170-kilometre drive takes about four hours without stops, but you will want to stop constantly.
review_summary: The Pacific Coast Highway – Coromandel Section route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the Pacific Coast Highway around the Coromandel Peninsula's dramatic coastline. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Paeroa and the Ohinemuri Goldfields
audio_story: You are in Paeroa, the gateway town to the Ohinemuri goldfield and the Karangahake Gorge. From eighteen seventy five gold was worked through the gorge, and the town of Paeroa grew as the administrative and transport hub. Paddle steamers from Auckland brought in supplies up the Waihou River to wharves that no longer exist, and a railway linked the gorge to the sea. Many of Paeroa's Edwardian brick buildings date from these boom years. The town is still famous for another liquid, the lemon and paeroa soft drink invented here in nineteen oh seven, and a giant L and P bottle statue on the main road marks the claim. Just upstream, the gorge holds the concrete ruins of stamp batteries and the old railway tunnel now walked as the Karangahake heritage trail. Paeroa today is a small town of antique shops and cafes, but its Edwardian buildings and the nearby gorge remain a powerful reminder of the gold and silver that made this quiet corner of the country briefly world famous.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Paeroa and the Ohinemuri Goldfields — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Paku Hill Pā, Tairua
short: A terraced volcanic pā commanding views over Tairua Harbour and the Pacific.
commentary: Paku Hill rises steeply between Tairua Harbour and the ocean. The summit was a formidable pā site with terracing still clearly visible. A walking track climbs to the summit (about 20 minutes) and rewards with stunning panoramic views.
review_summary: Visitors describe Paku Hill Pā, Tairua as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A terraced volcanic pā commanding views over Tairua Harbour and the Pacific. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pauanui Beach
short: A three kilometre white-sand beach on a holiday peninsula designed from the ground up in the 1960s as New Zealand's first planned beach resort.
commentary: Pauanui is a planned beach resort community developed from the late 1960s on a sand spit at the southern entrance to Tairua Harbour. It has a 3 km surf beach, a grass airstrip, and a large marina.
audio_story: You have arrived at Pauanui, a carefully planned beach resort on a sand spit at the southern entrance to Tairua Harbour. Pauanui is unusual. Most small beach towns in New Zealand grew organically from a fishing village or a pub on the coast road. Pauanui was designed from the beginning. In nineteen sixty eight a syndicate of developers purchased the entire peninsula, laid out a street grid with a grass airstrip at its centre, and began selling sections to Auckland and Waikato families looking for a holiday home. The result, fifty years later, is one of the tidiest and most affluent beach communities in the country. The three kilometre surf beach is superb, the safest swimming in Tairua Harbour is on the inside of the spit, and the nine hole golf course is surrounded by kahikatea forest. In January the grass airstrip is used by pilots flying themselves up from Auckland for the weekend. In winter the town almost completely empties out. Drive the streets, admire the gardens, swim, play minigolf in the central reserve, and treat yourself to fish and chips on the pier. Pauanui has a pleasantly unreal quality. It is exactly the beach holiday its designers imagined in nineteen sixty eight.
review_summary: Pauanui Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A three kilometre white-sand beach on a holiday peninsula designed from the ground up in the 1960s as New Zealand's first planned beach resort. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley
short: A classic overnight hike to the 759 m Pinnacles — through 19th-century kauri-driving country and up a spectacular rock summit.
commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley was one of the biggest kauri-logging operations in New Zealand from the 1870s to the 1920s; remains of the dams that 'drove' logs down the river are still visible along the track. The main walk climbs from the Kauaeranga Valley Road to the Pinnacles Hut (80 bunks, book ahead — this is DOC's most popular hut) then onto the Pinnacles Summit itself (759 m) via a series of wooden ladders. The summit view on a clear day spans both coasts of the peninsula. Allow two days. Not a walk to underestimate.
review_summary: Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A classic overnight hike to the 759 m Pinnacles — through 19th-century kauri-driving country and up a spectacular rock summit. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Port Jackson & Fletcher Bay
short: The very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — a remote unsealed drive ending at one of the most beautiful campgrounds in the North Island.
commentary: From Coromandel Town a long unsealed road climbs over Te Hikuwai before descending to the white sand beaches of Port Jackson and Fletcher Bay. Start of the Coromandel Coastal Walkway.
audio_story: You have committed to driving to the very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula and you should set aside half a day for the privilege. From Coromandel Town the road heads north along the Hauraki Gulf coast, through old gold mining country, and within an hour the seal runs out. You will spend the next forty kilometres on a winding unsealed road that climbs over headlands, dips into bays, and rewards every cautious driver with another lookout. At the end of the road, separated from each other by a steep ridge, are two of the most beautiful Department of Conservation campgrounds in the North Island. Port Jackson sits behind a long white sand beach with views across to Great Barrier Island. Fletcher Bay sits at the very tip, looking out over a clear blue strait. Between them runs the Coromandel Coastal Walkway, a spectacular three hour one way coastal track that many visitors complete with a shuttle pickup at the other end. Up here there is no shop, no fuel station, no mobile reception. Bring everything you need, including patience for the road. You will be rewarded with one of the quietest stretches of coastline within easy reach of Auckland.
review_summary: Port Jackson & Fletcher Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — a remote unsealed drive ending at one of the most beautiful campgrounds in the North Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Salt Restaurant & Bar
short: Whitianga waterfront restaurant — Coromandel mussels, oysters and fresh local seafood.
commentary: Salt sits on the Whitianga waterfront with views across Mercury Bay. The menu showcases Coromandel seafood — green-lipped mussels, local oysters, fresh fish — alongside NZ wine and craft beer. The Te Ika Mata (raw fish Māori-style) and tuna ahi poke are standouts. The waterfront terrace is the place to be on a warm evening, watching fishing boats return to the harbour.
review_summary: Fresh seafood and harbour views make this a Whitianga highlight. The Coromandel mussels are excellent. Service can slow down when busy in summer.

name: Shakespeare Cliff Lookout
short: Ten minutes above Cooks Beach — one of the great Coromandel lookouts, looking directly across to Cathedral Cove and beyond to the Mercury Islands.
commentary: Shakespeare Cliff sits above the southern end of Cooks Beach. A 15-minute walk from the car park gives a clifftop view across Mercury Bay.
audio_story: You have arrived at Shakespeare Cliff, a modest promontory rising above the southern end of Cooks Beach on the eastern Coromandel coast. The name comes from a nineteenth century surveyor who thought the cliff resembled the silhouette of William Shakespeare, which with enough squinting you can just about make out. The walk up takes about fifteen minutes on a well formed track from the car park, and the pay off is one of the best views on the Coromandel Peninsula. From the top you look directly across Mercury Bay to the white cliffs of Cathedral Cove, south to Hahei Beach with its pohutukawa fringed curve, north to Shakespeare Cliff's neighbour Te Whanganui-a-Hei, and on a clear day out to the Mercury Islands themselves, twenty kilometres offshore. It was in these waters, in November seventeen sixty nine, that James Cook anchored the Endeavour to observe the transit of Mercury across the sun, giving him the first reliable longitude reading for New Zealand and giving Mercury Bay its name. Stand on the cliff, imagine the Endeavour at anchor below, and remember that a single event of eighteenth century astronomy put this quiet corner of the Coromandel on the world's charts.
review_summary: Shakespeare Cliff Lookout draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Ten minutes above Cooks Beach — one of the great Coromandel lookouts, looking directly across to Cathedral Cove and beyond to the Mercury Islands. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Stony Bay & Coromandel Coastal Walkway
short: DOC campground at the far northern end of the Coromandel — trailhead for the spectacular Coromandel Coastal Walkway.
commentary: Stony Bay is a DOC campground at the end of the eastern gravel road around the far north Coromandel. The Coromandel Coastal Walkway (7 km) runs between Stony Bay and Fletcher Bay.
audio_story: You have arrived at Stony Bay, which is near the end of the known road on the Coromandel Peninsula. To get here you have driven forty kilometres of winding gravel north from Coromandel Town, past the turnoff for Port Jackson, around headlands with no safety barriers and no phone reception, and you have probably wondered more than once whether you should have turned back. The DOC campground here is simple. Long drop toilets, flat grass, a tidal stream, a small stony beach, and the constant murmur of the Hauraki Gulf. Most people come to Stony Bay for one particular reason. This is the eastern end of the Coromandel Coastal Walkway, a seven kilometre one way track that runs across the very tip of the peninsula to Fletcher Bay. The walk takes three hours each way. You climb above white cliffs, cross sheep paddocks, pass above hidden bays that are accessible only by kayak, and finish at one of the wildest, least visited beaches in the upper North Island. A small shuttle service runs between the two bays if you do not want to double back. Pack everything. Bring water. Leave time. This is some of the quietest country on the Coromandel.
review_summary: Stony Bay & Coromandel Coastal Walkway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. DOC campground at the far northern end of the Coromandel — trailhead for the spectacular Coromandel Coastal Walkway. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Tairua Harbour & Paku Summit
short: The tidal harbour town on the eastern Coromandel coast, crowned by the twin peaks of Paku — a ten-minute summit walk for one of the great Coromandel views.
commentary: Tairua sits on the eastern Coromandel coast, separated from its neighbour Pauanui by the harbour. Paku, a twin-peaked volcanic plug of 178 m, offers a short walk to a spectacular viewpoint.
audio_story: You have arrived at Tairua, a small town wrapped around a tidal harbour on the eastern Coromandel coast. Tairua is the quieter older sibling to Pauanui, its manicured neighbour on the far side of the harbour. The defining feature of the town is Paku, a twin peaked volcanic plug rising directly out of the sea at the northern head of the harbour. At a hundred and seventy eight metres Paku is small by mountain standards but its position makes it magnificent. A steep sealed road climbs most of the way up and a ten minute walking track takes you to the summit. From the top you look directly down on the entrance to Tairua Harbour, across to Shoe Island and the Alderman Islands offshore, north to Slipper Island, and south along the white crescent of Pauanui Beach. The climb is short enough to do before breakfast and spectacular enough to be remembered long after. Down in the town itself, the main street has a small and excellent bakery, a handful of good cafes, and a tidal estuary full of stingrays in summer that will nose right up to the edge of the pontoons. Tairua is one of those Coromandel towns that has changed very little in thirty years. That is, by any measure, its charm.
review_summary: Tairua Harbour & Paku Summit draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The tidal harbour town on the eastern Coromandel coast, crowned by the twin peaks of Paku — a ten-minute summit walk for one of the great Coromandel views. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Thames Coast Road – SH25
short: Drive the dramatic coast road from Thames north along the Firth of Thames, carved into cliffs above the sea.
commentary: SH25 north of Thames follows the western coast of the Coromandel Peninsula along the Firth of Thames. The road is carved into coastal cliffs with pohutukawa trees overhead and the Firth below. The section passes through small settlements including Tapu, Te Puru, and Thornton Bay.
audio_story: You are driving north from Thames on State Highway 25, and the road has been carved into the cliff face above the Firth of Thames. This is one of the most scenic coastal drives in the North Island, with pohutukawa trees arching overhead and the calm waters of the Firth stretching across to the Hauraki Plains. The road is narrow, winding, and occasionally closes after storms when slips block the way — a reminder that this coast is geologically active. Small communities cling to the narrow strip between the hills and the sea: Tapu, Te Puru, Thornton Bay — each with a few houses, a boat ramp, and not much else. At low tide, the Firth reveals extensive mudflats that are important feeding grounds for migratory shorebirds — godwits, knots, and oystercatchers that travel from as far as Alaska. The Thames coastline is also the northern edge of the Miranda shorebird reserve, one of the most important wetlands in the country. Drive slowly and enjoy the rhythm of the coast.
review_summary: The Thames Coast Road – SH25 route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the dramatic coast road from Thames north along the Firth of Thames, carved into cliffs above the sea. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Thames Goldfields Heritage
audio_story: You are in Thames, once the richest gold town in the southern hemisphere. In eighteen sixty seven the Kuranui Creek was opened to mining after negotiation with Ngāti Maru, and within two years the town had a population of around eighteen thousand people, larger than Auckland. The main streets were lined with hotels, a hundred and twelve at one count, and the hills above were laced with tunnels, stamp batteries, and aerial tramways. Thames produced gold for decades, and some of its Victorian shopfronts and miners' cottages still stand today. A short drive takes you to the Goldmine Experience on the Tararu hill, where you can walk into a real mine tunnel. Remember that the land was never willingly surrendered. Ngāti Maru and their whanaunga were squeezed out of the economic benefits as the town boomed, and their claims over mining rights still echo in Treaty settlements today. Walk the main street, look up at the flat-topped hills behind, and imagine a stream of men and women chasing gold in weather that made them swear.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Thames Goldfields Heritage — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Thames Historical Town
short: Once the largest town in NZ — the Thames goldfield boomed to 18,000 people, twice the size of Auckland in 1870.
commentary: Thames was founded as a gold rush settlement in 1867. At its 1870 peak it was the largest town in New Zealand. The School of Mines, opened in 1886, is now a museum.
audio_story: You are walking through Thames, and you should know that for a brief and astonishing time, this was the largest town in New Zealand. Gold was found here in eighteen sixty seven, and within three years more than eighteen thousand people had crowded into the new settlement around the Hauraki gulf. By eighteen seventy Thames had more residents than Auckland. The pubs ran twenty four hours, the harbour was full of supply ships, and a hundred and fifty stamper batteries pounded gold-bearing quartz day and night. The boom did not last. By the nineteen hundreds most of the easy gold was gone, and the population shrank to a tenth of its peak. What remained, and what you walk through today, is an unusually intact Victorian commercial town. The wide main street still has many of its original brick and timber buildings, and several have been beautifully restored as cafes and small shops. Visit the Thames School of Mines, opened in eighteen eighty six and now a museum, where you can see the original assay ovens and rock collections. The whole town breathes its history, gently and without exaggeration. You are walking through the ghost of a gold rush.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Thames Historical Town — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Once the largest town in NZ — the Thames goldfield boomed to 18,000 people, twice the size of Auckland in 1870. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: The 309 Road
short: A 22km gravel back road across the Coromandel Range — kauri grove, waterfall, and the Waterworks museum.
commentary: Connects Coromandel Town with Whitianga via a winding unsealed road through native bush. Highlights include the Waiau Kauri Grove, Waiau Falls, and the quirky Waterworks attraction.
audio_story: You are about to drive the three hundred and nine, a twenty two kilometre gravel road that crosses the spine of the Coromandel Range. The locals say it was named for the time, in horse and cart days, that it took to travel between Coromandel Town and Whitianga, three hours and nine minutes. The road today is unsealed but in good condition, suitable for any rental car driven slowly. The drive itself takes about an hour, and the joy is in the stopping. Pull in at the Waiau Kauri Grove and take the boardwalk. The trees here are not the giants of Waipoua, but they are real and they are old, and the silence under their canopy is very deep. Continue a few minutes east to Waiau Falls, where you can swim under a small clear waterfall in summer. Further along, the Waterworks museum is exactly the kind of mad creative hand-built oddity that the Coromandel produces. It is an outdoor sculpture park of water-driven contraptions made from kitchen utensils and farm scrap. Children love it. Allow a couple of hours for the whole drive, and let yourself be late.
review_summary: The 309 Road draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 22km gravel back road across the Coromandel Range — kauri grove, waterfall, and the Waterworks museum. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: The Church at Hahei
short: Fine dining in a converted church near Cathedral Cove — seasonal Coromandel seafood and local wines.
commentary: The Church at Hahei is a beautifully converted heritage church that now serves some of the Coromandel's finest food. The seasonal menu showcases local seafood — the snapper and green-lipped mussels are both excellent — alongside NZ wines and craft cocktails. It's a short walk from Hahei Beach and Cathedral Cove, making it the perfect end to a day of exploring. Booking essential in summer.
review_summary: The converted church setting is special. The seafood is fresh and well-prepared. Very seasonal — check opening hours outside summer.

name: The Pepper Tree Restaurant
short: Coromandel Town's best restaurant — local oysters, mussels and organic produce in a garden setting.
commentary: The Pepper Tree is the culinary heart of Coromandel Town, serving locally harvested oysters, green-lipped mussels and organic produce in a charming garden restaurant. The menu changes with the seasons, and the seafood is as fresh as it gets — sourced from the Firth of Thames and Coromandel coastline. The garden seating among subtropical plants creates a relaxed, almost tropical atmosphere.
review_summary: The fresh seafood is consistently praised. The garden setting is charming. Some find Coromandel Town very quiet outside summer.

name: The Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley Hike
short: Climb to the dramatic rock spires of The Pinnacles for panoramic Coromandel views.
commentary: The Pinnacles Track in the Kauaeranga Valley is the Coromandel's premier hike. The track climbs through regenerating bush to the Pinnacles Hut, then a final scramble up metal stairways leads to the 759-metre summit. Views extend across the peninsula to both coasts. Can be done as a long day trip or overnight.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Kauaeranga Valley road end, the starting point for the Coromandel's most iconic hike. The Pinnacles Track climbs steadily through native bush that was heavily logged for kauri in the 19th century but is now regenerating beautifully. Along the way, you will pass the remains of old kauri dams, engineering marvels that loggers used to flush timber downstream in artificial floods. The Pinnacles Hut sits at the treeline, a comfortable base if you are staying overnight. From there, the final push to the 759-metre summit involves metal stairways bolted into the rock face, climbing through gaps in the dramatic volcanic spires that give this place its name. At the top, the view is staggering, you can see both the Pacific Ocean and the Hauraki Gulf, with the entire peninsula laid out below. Start early if doing it as a day trip, the return takes seven to eight hours.
review_summary: The The Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley Hike adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb to the dramatic rock spires of The Pinnacles for panoramic Coromandel views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: The Smoking Keg BBQ
short: Whitianga BBQ joint — low-and-slow smoked meats, craft beers and a laid-back Coromandel atmosphere.
commentary: The Smoking Keg brings low-and-slow American BBQ to the Coromandel with brisket, pulled pork and ribs smoked over local wood. The craft beer selection is well-chosen, and the outdoor area captures the Whitianga sun. It's the perfect casual dinner after a day at the beach or Hot Water Beach. The portions are generous and the prices are reasonable for a tourist town.
review_summary: The smoked meats are surprisingly authentic. Good craft beer selection. Casual and unpretentious — perfect for the Coromandel vibe.

name: The Waterworks
short: An outdoor playground of water-powered sculptures built from old kitchen scraps and farm machinery — only on the 309 Road could this exist.
commentary: A privately operated park along the 309 Road of dozens of water-powered sculptures built from recycled materials. Family-friendly.
audio_story: You have stopped at The Waterworks, and you have done the right thing. Hidden in the bush along the 309 Road, halfway between Coromandel Town and Whitianga, this is one of the more delightfully odd attractions in the country. The park is the lifelong project of one family, built over decades from recycled materials, and it is exactly what the name suggests. The Waiau Stream runs through the property and powers dozens of moving sculptures, almost all of them constructed from kitchen utensils, farm scrap, old bicycle parts, and rusty machinery. Pots beat tunes on bells. A line of toilet bowls flush themselves in sequence. A bicycle wheels itself in slow circles. Children can spend hours here, working their way around the property, pulling levers, climbing on the larger pieces, and squealing at the next absurd contraption. Adults will spend the whole visit smiling at the patience and humour that built the place. There is an old fashioned cafe at the entrance. There is no electricity in the park, just water and gravity. The whole thing is unmistakably handmade, and unmistakably Coromandel. Allow at least an hour, more if you have kids.
review_summary: The cultural experience at The Waterworks is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. An outdoor playground of water-powered sculptures built from old kitchen scraps and farm machinery — only on the 309 Road could this exist. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Waihi — Māori Gold Country
short: Gold country that was Ngāti Tamatera territory — the mines sit on ancestral Māori land.
commentary: The Waihi goldfield, one of the richest in NZ, sits on ancestral Ngāti Tamatera land. Māori were initially involved in prospecting and mining before being sidelined as industrial operations expanded. Treaty claims relating to the gold resource are ongoing.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waihi — Māori Gold Country as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Gold country that was Ngāti Tamatera territory — the mines sit on ancestral Māori land. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waihī Beach
short: An 11 km surf beach at the southern end of the Coromandel — linked by the Orokawa Bay coastal walk to a hidden family-perfect swim beach.
commentary: Waihī Beach is an 11-km surf beach that marks the southern end of the Coromandel east coast. The Orokawa Bay walkway (1.5 hours return) leads to a sheltered northern cove.
audio_story: You have arrived at Waihī Beach, which is where the Coromandel east coast effectively ends and the Bay of Plenty begins. The beach itself is eleven kilometres of open surf, stretching south from the Orokawa headland to the entrance of Tauranga Harbour at Bowentown. Waihī Beach is the biggest town on this stretch of coast, a permanent population of a few thousand, and a summer population many times that. The surf is steady. The swimming is patrolled between the flags in summer. Most visitors stay on the main beach, and that is fine. The genuinely special walk is from the northern end of the town. The Orokawa Bay Walkway climbs over the headland from Waihī Beach itself, takes about forty minutes each way, and delivers you to Orokawa Bay, a small sheltered cove of golden sand, fringed by pohutukawa, inaccessible by road and therefore almost always quiet. In summer the pohutukawa bloom red. In autumn the track is empty. Swim, picnic, walk back. If you want to keep going, the track continues beyond Orokawa to William Wright Falls, a small waterfall in the bush another thirty minutes further on. Pack water. Pack a towel. Allow three hours.
sources_to_check: Western Bay of Plenty District Council
review_summary: Waihī Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. An 11 km surf beach at the southern end of the Coromandel — linked by the Orokawa Bay coastal walk to a hidden family-perfect swim beach. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Waiomu Kauri Grove
short: A quiet two-hour bush walk up the Waiomu Stream to a grove of surviving mature kauri — one of the last on the western Coromandel.
commentary: The Waiomu Kauri Grove is reached by a 2-hour return walk up the Waiomu Stream on the western Coromandel coast. Several kauri of around 1000 years old stand at the head of the track.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Waiomu Kauri Grove track, one of the quieter kauri walks on the Coromandel Peninsula. The track begins ten minutes north of Thames on the coast road, turns inland up the Waiomu Stream, and follows the stream back into the Coromandel Range. It is two hours return at a relaxed pace. For most of its length the track is flat and sheltered, with the stream on one side and regenerating native bush on the other. The point of the walk is the grove at the turn around. Several mature kauri stand here together, the oldest of them perhaps a thousand years old, their great silver grey trunks rising fifty metres before the first branch. The Coromandel lost perhaps ninety eight percent of its original kauri forest to the saws of the nineteenth century, and the small surviving remnants feel like the last pages of a much longer book. Before you enter the grove, use the boot cleaning station at the trailhead. Kauri dieback disease is carried in a single grain of soil and has already killed many of the peninsula's surviving trees. Scrub hard, walk only on the boardwalk, and give these last giants the respect they have earned by standing long.
review_summary: Waiomu Kauri Grove draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A quiet two-hour bush walk up the Waiomu Stream to a grove of surviving mature kauri — one of the last on the western Coromandel. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Whangamata Bar Surf Break
short: Ride the legendary left-hand bar break at Whangamata, one of NZ's best waves.
commentary: The Whangamata bar break is a left-hand river mouth wave considered one of the best in New Zealand. It breaks over the sandbar at the harbour entrance, producing long, hollow lefts on a solid easterly swell. The wave is best suited to experienced surfers due to strong currents.
audio_story: You have arrived at Whangamata, home to one of New Zealand's most celebrated surf breaks. The bar break here is a left-hand river mouth wave that peels along the sandbar at the harbour entrance, and on its day, it is world-class. When a solid east or northeast swell hits at low to mid tide, the wave jacks up on the shallow bar and produces long, hollow lefts that can run for over 200 metres. This is not a wave for beginners, the currents around the harbour mouth are powerful and the takeoff zone is critical. But if you have the skill, it is a ride you will never forget. The main beach at Whangamata also offers more forgiving waves closer to shore. Surf reports and local advice are available from the surf shops in the village.
review_summary: The Whangamata Bar Surf Break adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride the legendary left-hand bar break at Whangamata, one of NZ's best waves. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Whangamatā Ocean Beach
short: A four-kilometre curve of white sand and one of the most reliable surf breaks on the east coast.
commentary: Whangamatā's Ocean Beach runs four kilometres from the harbour bar to Taumata Rock, and the surf break at the northern end (The Bar) produces consistent hollow right-handers on east and north swells. The permanent population is 4,000; in summer it quadruples. In 2005 the town famously fought — and won — an Environment Court battle to stop a proposed marina in the harbour, a decision later reversed and the marina eventually built. The harbour is still the town's heart. The beach is still the reason to come.
review_summary: Whangamatā Ocean Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A four-kilometre curve of white sand and one of the most reliable surf breaks on the east coast. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Whangapoua Kauri Timber Port
audio_story: You are at Whangapoua Harbour, a quiet tidal inlet on the Coromandel's east coast. In the late nineteenth century this peaceful bay was one of the busiest kauri timber ports in the country. Logs felled in the Whangapoua State Forest were dragged by bullock team down steep bush tracks, or driven down creeks and rivers using timber dams that released water in a single surge. At the harbour, a sawmill cut the logs and barges carried timber out to Auckland and beyond. The forest was clear-felled in a few decades. Today the area around Whangapoua is known for the walk to New Chums Beach, still one of the most unspoiled beaches in the country, a reminder of what the entire coast once looked like. The old jetty pilings and the remains of bush tramways can still be found in the forest. Listen to the call of kereru in the regrown bush and imagine the crash of giant trees and the shouts of bullock drivers that once filled these ridges.
review_summary: Whangapoua Kauri Timber Port draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Whitianga
short: The main east-coast Coromandel town — on Mercury Bay, where James Cook observed the transit of Mercury in 1769.
commentary: Whitianga sits on Mercury Bay, named by Cook in November 1769 after he observed the transit of Mercury across the sun from a nearby headland — one of the key astronomical observations of his first Pacific voyage. Local iwi Ngāti Hei hosted Cook's party for ten days and the contact was peaceful, uniquely so for early encounters. The town itself is a family-holiday centre: Buffalo Beach for the swim, the harbour passenger ferry (a 2-minute crossing) to Ferry Landing and Shakespeare Cliff for the views. Oysters, mussels and fresh fish at the harbour.
review_summary: Whitianga draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The main east-coast Coromandel town — on Mercury Bay, where James Cook observed the transit of Mercury in 1769. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Abbey Estate Winery
short: Small Havelock North boutique winery — personal tastings with the winemaker and limited-release wines.
commentary: Abbey Estate is one of Havelock North's smaller wineries, where the tasting experience is genuinely personal — you'll often be served by the winemaker. The Merlot and Syrah from their Havelock North vineyard are concentrated and complex, and the limited production means the wines often sell out at the cellar door. It's the antidote to the bigger, more commercial operations nearby.
review_summary: The personal touch is the highlight. The wines are limited and worth seeking out. Small operation means restricted opening hours.

name: Art Deco Walking Tour, Napier
short: A daily guided walk through the most complete Art Deco city centre in the world.
commentary: Run by the Art Deco Trust from their visitor centre on Tennyson Street; daily 1-hour guided walks plus a longer afternoon option.
audio_story: You have signed up for an Art Deco walking tour in Napier and you are about to spend an hour learning to read a city. Almost everything in the central business district was built in the four years between nineteen thirty one and nineteen thirty five, after the Hawke's Bay earthquake on the third of February nineteen thirty one flattened the old Victorian centre in two and a half minutes. Two hundred and fifty six people died that morning. The rebuild started within weeks, and the architects in charge chose Art Deco, the newest and most optimistic style in the world at the time. Your guide will take you slowly along Emerson Street and Tennyson Street, pointing out the geometric sunbursts, the zigzag chevrons, the stylised Māori motifs, and the curved corner windows that mark the style. You will hear about the architect Louis Hay and the painter Charles Bourne, about the local concrete mixers who poured the foundations. You will be shown the National Tobacco Building in Ahuriri, with its great oak doors and roses, often picked as the most beautiful Art Deco building in the country. By the end of the walk you will see a city you missed when you arrived. Stay for a coffee at the Daily Telegraph Building afterward.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Art Deco Walking Tour, Napier — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A daily guided walk through the most complete Art Deco city centre in the world. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Askerne Estate Winery
short: Family-owned Havelock North winery known for aromatic whites and one of NZ's best dessert wines.
commentary: Kathryn and John Loughlin have been welcoming visitors to Askerne since 1997. The cellar door in Havelock North is warm and personal, and their Noble Selection dessert wine — made from botrytis-affected grapes — is world-class. The Gewürztraminer and Viognier are also excellent. It's the kind of place where you'll end up staying longer than planned because the hosts are so engaging.
review_summary: The personal touch from the owners wins hearts. The dessert wine is frequently called a revelation. Some wish the cellar door had a food offering, but the wines and hospitality make up for it.

name: Bistronomy
short: French-NZ fusion in Napier's art deco precinct — seasonal tasting menus with Bay wines.
commentary: Bistronomy brings French technique to Hawke's Bay produce in a charming Napier setting. The seasonal tasting menus are expertly constructed, with local lamb, seafood and seasonal vegetables given a French twist. The wine list highlights Hawke's Bay producers, and the art deco neighbourhood adds atmosphere. Lunch is excellent value; dinner is a more extended affair. Booking recommended.
review_summary: The French-NZ fusion works beautifully. The lunch menu is excellent value. Some find the space small, which can feel crowded on busy evenings.

name: Black Barn Vineyards
short: Havelock North vineyard with a top restaurant, Saturday growers' market and summer concert series.
commentary: Black Barn has built a lifestyle destination around its vineyard — the Bistro restaurant, a Saturday morning growers' market, luxury accommodation and a summer concert amphitheatre that hosts international acts. The wines (especially the Te Mere Chardonnay and the flagship red blend) are excellent, but it's the whole experience that makes Black Barn special. The growers' market is one of the best in the country.
review_summary: The Saturday market and Bistro draw near-universal praise. Summer concerts are a highlight. Some feel the experience is polished to the point of losing rustic charm, but most love the lifestyle-destination approach.

name: Bluff Hill Lookout / Mataruahou
short: The hill that rose 2 metres in the 1931 quake — now the best view of the port.
commentary: In 1931 this hill literally grew. The earthquake pushed Mataruahou up by about two metres, and dried out 3,500 hectares of sea floor to create the land Napier airport now sits on. From up here you can see exactly how the coastline changed.
sources_to_check: GNS Science — 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake uplift data
Te Ara: Hawke's Bay region geography
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Bluff Hill Lookout / Mataruahou — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The hill that rose 2 metres in the 1931 quake — now the best view of the port. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Brave Brewing Co
short: Hastings craft brewery with a buzzing taproom — rotating taps, wood-fired pizzas and live music.
commentary: In a region dominated by wine, Brave Brewing has carved out a loyal following with hop-forward IPAs, sessionable lagers and experimental brews. The taproom in Hastings is a lively gathering spot with wood-fired pizzas, regular live music and a rotating selection of guest taps from other NZ craft breweries. A great alternative for the non-wine-drinker in your group.
review_summary: Beer lovers appreciate having a quality craft option in wine country. The taproom atmosphere is fun and lively. Some note the pizza quality varies, but the beer is consistently good.

name: Brookfields Vineyards
short: Hawke's Bay's oldest boutique winery (1937) — Gold Label Cabernet Merlot is a perennial award-winner.
commentary: Founded in 1937, Brookfields is a Hawke's Bay stalwart. The cellar door on Brookfields Road is modest and charming, and the Gold Label reds are consistently excellent. Winemaker Peter Robertson has been here since the 1990s and his experience shows in wines that are structured but approachable. Great value compared to some of the flashier neighbours.
review_summary: Long-time fans praise the consistency and value. The Gold Label reds punch above their price point. The cellar door is old-school rather than glamorous, which some love and others find dated.

name: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
short: The jaw of Māui's fish — a 140m cliff face and the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony.
commentary: Two stories, same piece of rock. In Māori tradition this is Te Kauwae-a-Māui — the jawbone Māui used to fish up the North Island. In European history it's 'Cape Kidnappers', named by Cook on 15 October 1769 after a Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti party under Te Rangikoianake tried to free a young Tahitian boy, Taiata, whom they believed was being held captive on the Endeavour. Around 6,500 pairs of Australasian gannets nest on the cliff edge from September to April — the world's most accessible mainland colony. Three ways in: the low-tide walk from Clifton Beach (~3 hrs each way, check tides), tractor-trailer tours up the beach, or the overland route through Cape Kidnappers Station.
sources_to_check: DOC — Cape Kidnappers/Te Kauwae-a-Māui Gannet Reserve
Te Ara: Naming Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti / Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Inc.
review_summary: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The jaw of Māui's fish — a 140m cliff face and the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
short: The dramatic promontory where Cook was chased off in 1769 — and where Māui pulled up the North Island.
commentary: Cook named the cape after Te Aitanga-a-Hauiti attempted to seize a Tahitian crewmember. Māori name Te Kauwae-a-Māui refers to Māui''s fish hook.
