The cove where Cook careened the *Endeavour* in 1770 and returned four times — the start of the Queen Charlotte Track. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Spy Valley Wines
short: Architecturally striking cellar door near the former spy base — award-winning Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir.
commentary: Named after the nearby Waihopai spy base (visible from the vineyard), Spy Valley's modern cellar door is one of Marlborough's most visually striking. The wines are excellent across the range — the Envoy Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir are consistently gold-medal winners. The tasting experience is well-structured and the staff are knowledgeable. A must-visit for architecture and wine lovers alike.
review_summary: The architecture and spy-base story add intrigue. The wines are consistently high quality. Some visitors are disappointed they can't actually see the spy base.

name: Taylor Dam Reserve
short: A small flood-control lake just south of Blenheim — picnic area, duck pond, and gateway to the Wither Hills walking network.
commentary: Taylor Dam was built in 1983 to control Taylor River flooding in Blenheim. The reserve is now a popular family picnic spot.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Taylor Dam Reserve, on the southern outskirts of Blenheim. The dam itself was built in nineteen eighty three, not for hydroelectric power or for water supply, but simply to control the flooding of the Taylor River through the centre of Blenheim. The town had been repeatedly and disastrously flooded during heavy rain in the hills above. The dam stores the flood flow, releasing it gradually downstream. Forty years later, the small lake behind the dam has become one of the most used recreation spaces in Blenheim. There is a flat walking loop around the lake, about a kilometre, that is stroller and wheelchair friendly. There is a duck pond, a picnic area, a playground, and a swimming beach on the western side for hot days. The water is clean and sheltered. More ambitiously, Taylor Dam is one of the main access points to the Wither Hills walking network, a trail system that covers around a thousand hectares of rolling grassland country directly south of Blenheim, with dozens of kilometres of walking and mountain biking routes. You can start short and easy here, or head up onto the Wither tops for a view across the whole Wairau Valley. Something for everyone.
review_summary: Taylor Dam Reserve draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A small flood-control lake just south of Blenheim — picnic area, duck pond, and gateway to the Wither Hills walking network. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Taylor Pass Heritage Road
short: A winding pass road through premium vineyards connecting the Wairau and upper Omaka valleys.
commentary: Taylor Pass Road climbs from Blenheim over a low saddle into the upper Wairau Valley, passing some of Marlborough's most prestigious vineyards including Dog Point and Greywacke. The pass was an early settler route and offers elevated views across the entire Wairau Plain to the Richmond Range.
audio_story: You are climbing Taylor Pass Road out of Blenheim, and the vineyards here are some of the most prized in Marlborough. Dog Point, Greywacke, Clos Henri — names that make wine lovers sit up straight. The road winds uphill past mature vines planted on rocky alluvial terraces, each winery taking advantage of slightly different soils and aspects. At the top of the pass, you can look back across the entire Wairau Plain, a patchwork of green vineyard blocks stretching to the sea. This was an early settler route, used long before the valley floor roads were built, and it gives you a perspective on the wine region that you simply cannot get from down below.
review_summary: The Taylor Pass Heritage Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A winding pass road through premium vineyards connecting the Wairau and upper Omaka valleys. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Te Pokohiwi / Wairau Bar
short: NZ's most important archaeological site — earliest substantial Māori settlement, with burials dating to the 1280s.
commentary: Excavations revealed 44 burials with rich grave goods. In 2009, kōiwi were repatriated to Rangitāne and reburied. The site is wāhi tapu.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Pokohiwi / Wairau Bar as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. NZ's most important archaeological site — earliest substantial Māori settlement, with burials dating to the 1280s. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Rauparaha's Southern Raids
audio_story: You are on the Wairau plain, across which in the eighteen twenties and eighteen thirties the Ngāti Toa war chief Te Rauparaha swept with devastating effect. After leading his iwi south from Kawhia to Kapiti, Te Rauparaha obtained muskets and used them to strike deep into the South Island, capturing Kāpiti and then crossing the strait to raid Rangitāne, Ngāti Māmoe, and Ngāi Tahu settlements. His campaigns reached as far south as Kaikōura and the Canterbury plains. The demographic effects were severe. Whole communities were displaced or killed, while survivors fled into the hills. By the time Europeans began buying land in Marlborough, Ngāti Toa held rights of conquest over much of it, which is why Te Rauparaha could speak for the Wairau in eighteen forty three. These raids are remembered in many iwi, a shaping reality that preceded the colonial era. Look across the Wairau plain today with new eyes. The quiet farmland you see hides the tracks of musket war parties who reshaped the whole southern North Island and upper South Island within a generation.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Rauparaha's Southern Raids as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Whare Ra Wines
short: One of Marlborough's oldest vineyards — planted 1979 — now producing benchmark aromatic whites.
commentary: Te Whare Ra (TWR) was one of the first vineyards planted in Marlborough and today Jason and Anna Flowerday craft some of the region's most compelling aromatic wines. Their Gewürztraminer and Riesling regularly win gold medals, and the old-vine complexity is unmistakable. The cellar door is by appointment, ensuring an unhurried, personal experience with some of Marlborough's most thoughtful wines.
review_summary: Serious wine enthusiasts call TWR a hidden gem. The aromatic whites are praised as among the best in the country. By-appointment visits mean no crowds, though spontaneous visitors may miss out.

name: Tennyson Inlet & Elaine Bay
short: The far northwestern end of the Marlborough Sounds — remote, hard to reach, and some of the quietest kayaking water in the country.
commentary: Tennyson Inlet is at the outer end of the Pelorus Sound system, reached by a slow winding gravel road from Rai Valley. Elaine Bay is the road end.
audio_story: You have arrived at Tennyson Inlet, the far northwestern branch of the Pelorus Sound, which is to say, one of the more remote drivable waters in the country. Getting here is a commitment. From Rai Valley on the Havelock to Nelson highway, the road to Tennyson Inlet is thirty five kilometres of narrow winding gravel through beech forest and over a high saddle. Elaine Bay at the road end is a tiny settlement of around twenty houses and a small boat ramp. For most visitors, the journey itself is the point. The drive in is spectacular. The sealed road peels off the main highway and climbs steeply through old beech forest. At the top, Opouri Saddle gives the first glimpse down onto the inlet's turquoise water. At the bottom, Elaine Bay feels like the edge of the known world. From the boat ramp you can launch a kayak into some of the quietest paddling water in New Zealand, with native bush running right down to the shoreline in every direction, and with deep clean water for diving. The nearby Nydia Track ends at Duncan Bay, a short drive further. Pack your own food. Bring fuel. Phone reception is uncertain. This is deep Sounds country.
review_summary: Tennyson Inlet & Elaine Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The far northwestern end of the Marlborough Sounds — remote, hard to reach, and some of the quietest kayaking water in the country. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Torea Saddle & Portage
short: The saddle between Queen Charlotte Sound and Kenepuru Sound — with a classic Marlborough Sounds lodge at the waterfront below.
commentary: Torea Saddle is a low pass linking Queen Charlotte Sound and Kenepuru Sound. The Queen Charlotte Track crosses here. Portage Resort Hotel sits on Kenepuru below.
audio_story: You have arrived at Torea Saddle, a low pass that separates the waters of Queen Charlotte Sound from the waters of Kenepuru Sound, the two great arms of the outer Marlborough Sounds. The saddle sits at a spectacular point on Queen Charlotte Drive, and the Queen Charlotte Walking Track crosses here at the midpoint of its seventy kilometre length. On the Queen Charlotte Sound side, looking west, you see the long straight sweep of the main sound stretching away to Ship Cove. On the Kenepuru side, looking east, the water is a flatter shimmering inlet with the Portage Resort Hotel and a small marina nestled on the water's edge. The Portage is one of the classic Sounds lodges, dating from the eighteen nineties in its earliest form, and remains a very popular stop for walkers, cyclists, and yachties. The saddle itself is worth pausing at. There are two short walks from the car park, one to each sound, that deliver very different views in about fifteen minutes each way. And there is an honesty box at the top where local honey and preserves are often for sale. The light in the sounds changes every five minutes. Stop here, get out of the car, and let it change around you.
review_summary: Torea Saddle & Portage draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The saddle between Queen Charlotte Sound and Kenepuru Sound — with a classic Marlborough Sounds lodge at the waterfront below. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Tua Marina Monument and Wairau Affray
audio_story: You are at Tua Marina, a quiet stream crossing north of Blenheim. Here on the seventeenth of June eighteen forty three the first serious armed conflict between Māori and the British Crown after the Treaty of Waitangi took place. Surveyors from the New Zealand Company had begun laying out sections on the Wairau plains, ignoring Ngāti Toa chief Te Rauparaha's clear statement that the land had not been sold. A party of armed settler constables and magistrates, led by Police Magistrate Henry Thompson, attempted to arrest Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata at their camp. A shot was fired, Te Rangihaeata's wife Rongo was killed, and the ensuing fight left twenty two of the settlers and four Māori dead. A stone memorial erected in eighteen sixty nine on the small hill at Tua Marina records the names of those who died. Governor FitzRoy later concluded the settlers had acted unlawfully, a decision that outraged colonists but prevented wider war. Stand at the monument and remember a confrontation that foreshadowed the long land wars to come.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Tua Marina Monument and Wairau Affray — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Villa Maria Marlborough
short: New Zealand's most awarded winery — Marlborough cellar door showcasing the full premium range.
commentary: Villa Maria is New Zealand's most awarded winery (and its largest family-owned one), and the Marlborough cellar door is the best place to taste their top-tier wines. The Reserve and Single Vineyard Sauvignon Blancs show impressive depth and complexity, and the Pinot Noir programme has improved markedly. The cellar door experience is polished and professional — good for both beginners and enthusiasts.
review_summary: The premium wines are excellent. The cellar door is professional and welcoming. Some find it corporate compared to smaller operations.

name: Wairau Affray Site
short: Site of the 1843 Wairau Affray — the first armed clash between Māori and settlers.
commentary: 22 settlers and 4–6 Māori were killed. Governor FitzRoy ruled that the settlers were in the wrong. Considered the first clash of the New Zealand Wars.
review_summary: Visitors describe Wairau Affray Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Site of the 1843 Wairau Affray — the first armed clash between Māori and settlers. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Wairau Affray Site & Tuamarina Cemetery
short: The site of the 1843 Wairau Affray — the first armed clash between Māori and Pākehā settlers after the Treaty of Waitangi. A simple memorial, a quiet cemetery.
commentary: The Wairau Affray (17 June 1843) was the first armed conflict in post-Treaty New Zealand. Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata resisted an illegal survey of Wairau by settlers. Four Māori and 22 settlers died.
audio_story: You have arrived at Tuamarina, a small settlement a few kilometres north of Blenheim, and you have arrived at one of the most consequential locations in early colonial New Zealand history. On the seventeenth of June eighteen forty three, an armed party of Nelson settlers, led by Captain Arthur Wakefield and the Police Magistrate, arrived here to arrest the paramount chiefs Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata for resisting an illegal survey of Wairau. In a confused and sudden exchange of fire, a settler accidentally shot and killed Te Rongo, the wife of Te Rangihaeata. The affray that followed killed four Māori and twenty two settlers, including Wakefield. The incident became known as the Wairau Affray, the first armed conflict in post-Treaty New Zealand, and marked the start of a decade of growing hostility that would lead to the New Zealand Wars. The settlers who were killed are buried in the small cemetery on the hill above you. A simple obelisk marks the site. There is also, further up the hill, a more recent memorial recognising the Māori perspective on events. Read both carefully. The story is genuinely contested. This place, now so quiet, was once a pivot of the entire country's history.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Wairau Affray Site & Tuamarina Cemetery — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The site of the 1843 Wairau Affray — the first armed clash between Māori and Pākehā settlers after the Treaty of Waitangi. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Wairau River Wines
short: Family-owned vineyard with a popular restaurant set among the vines near Renwick.
commentary: Phil and Chris Rose planted their first vines in 1978, making Wairau River one of Marlborough's pioneers. The on-site restaurant serves locally sourced dishes under old trees beside the vineyard — it's one of the most pleasant lunch spots in the region. The reserve Sauvignon Blanc shows what the variety can do with a bit of age, and the Pinot Noir is consistently strong.
review_summary: The restaurant draws praise for its setting among the vines and the quality of the food-wine pairing. Families appreciate the relaxed atmosphere. Some say the wine list could extend beyond the estate, but most are happy with the range.

name: Wairau Valley Wine Trail
short: Cycle or drive through the vineyards that made New Zealand sauvignon blanc world-famous.
commentary: The Wairau Valley around Blenheim contains over 150 wineries and produces 77% of New Zealand's wine. Marlborough sauvignon blanc, first planted by Montana (now Brancott Estate) in 1973, revolutionised the global wine industry with its intense, zesty style. The flat terrain and network of vineyard roads make it ideal for cycling between cellar doors.
audio_story: You are cycling through the Wairau Valley, and in every direction the view is the same: row upon row of vines stretching to the distant mountains. This is where New Zealand's wine revolution began. In 1973, Montana planted the first sauvignon blanc vines here, and within two decades Marlborough had changed the world's expectations of what that grape could do. The secret is the climate — long sunny days, cool nights, and free-draining river gravels that stress the vines just enough to concentrate their flavours. There are over 150 wineries here now, from tiny family operations to global brands, and the flat roads between them were practically designed for leisurely cycling. Pull into a cellar door, taste something extraordinary, then pedal on to the next.
review_summary: The Wairau Valley Wine Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle or drive through the vineyards that made New Zealand sauvignon blanc world-famous. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Wither Hills Farm Park
short: 1,100 hectares of tussock-covered hills above Blenheim — short walks and long ones, with huge views over the vineyards.
commentary: Wither Hills Farm Park is a council reserve south of Blenheim. Working sheep farm crossed by a network of walking and mountain biking tracks.
audio_story: You are looking up at the Wither Hills from the edge of Blenheim, and you are looking at eleven hundred hectares of working sheep country that also happens to be one of the best walking parks in the South Island. A network of tracks climbs gently from the carparks along Taylor River into golden tussock ridges, then further up to the main ridgelines from which, on a clear day, the view opens to the north over the entire Wairau Plain, across the patchwork of vineyards that makes this New Zealand's most famous wine region. The tracks range from short half hour loops to several hour traverses. The tussock in late summer turns silver gold, and the sheep peel away politely as you walk past. Mountain bikers share some of the tracks, and there is an excellent network of purpose built bike trails on the eastern side of the park. The farm park is open year round except during the driest summer months, when fire risk closes certain tracks. Check the council website before you go, take water, and time your walk for the cool ends of the day. From the top, Marlborough looks exactly as beautiful as it tastes.
review_summary: Wither Hills Farm Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 1,100 hectares of tussock-covered hills above Blenheim — short walks and long ones, with huge views over the vineyards. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Yealands Estate
short: Sustainably farmed coastal estate in Seddon — cellar door with sea views and babydoll sheep among the vines.
commentary: Yealands' Seaview vineyard on the east coast of Marlborough is one of the country's most sustainably farmed wine estates. Babydoll sheep graze between the vine rows (natural lawn mowers), and the cellar door offers views across the Cook Strait. The Peter Yealands range is excellent value, and the Reserve Sauvignon Blanc is impressive. The drive down from Blenheim is scenic along the coast.
review_summary: The sustainability story and sea-view setting impress. The babydoll sheep are a hit with visitors. Some note it's further from Blenheim than most wineries.

name: Abel Tasman Canyoning
short: Abseil waterfalls and leap into pools in Abel Tasman's hidden granite canyons.
commentary: Canyoning in the Abel Tasman region takes you into hidden granite gorges with waterfall abseils, natural rock slides, and deep plunge pools. The canyon environments are lush with native bush and feature the distinctive golden granite of the region. Guided trips cater to beginners and experienced adventurers.
audio_story: You have arrived at the start of a canyoning adventure that will take you into the hidden heart of the Abel Tasman hinterland. While most visitors stick to the famous coastline, the hills behind the national park conceal dramatic granite canyons carved by millennia of rainfall. You'll abseil down waterfalls that cascade over golden granite, slide through natural rock chutes polished to glass by centuries of water, and leap into deep plunge pools surrounded by lush native bush. The Abel Tasman's distinctive golden granite gives these canyons a warmth and beauty that's unique in New Zealand. Your guides will teach you the rope skills and safety techniques as you go, so no prior experience is necessary. By the end of the day, you'll have discovered a side of Abel Tasman that most people never even know exists.
review_summary: The Abel Tasman Canyoning adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Abseil waterfalls and leap into pools in Abel Tasman's hidden granite canyons. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Abel Tasman Coast Track
short: New Zealand's most popular Great Walk — 60 km of golden sand beaches and turquoise bays.
commentary: The Abel Tasman Coast Track covers 60 km from Marahau to Wainui, typically walked in 3-5 days. It passes through native bush and along a succession of golden sand beaches with crystal-clear water. It is the most accessible Great Walk and can also be combined with sea kayaking. The park was established in 1942 on the 300th anniversary of Abel Tasman's visit.
audio_story: You are stepping onto the Abel Tasman Coast Track at Marahau, and for the next 60 kilometres, golden beaches will keep appearing around every headland like gifts. This is New Zealand's smallest national park but arguably its most beautiful, a scalloped coastline of granite and golden sand backed by native bush. The track is gentle by New Zealand standards — no alpine crossings, no exposed ridges — which is why it is the most walked Great Walk in the country. You will wade across tidal estuaries, watch seals basking on Tonga Island, and swim in water so clear you can count the fish. The park was created in 1942 to mark the 300th anniversary of Dutch navigator Abel Tasman's arrival, though Tasman himself never actually landed — Maori in waka drove him off after a violent encounter in Golden Bay.
review_summary: The Abel Tasman Coast Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. New Zealand's most popular Great Walk — 60 km of golden sand beaches and turquoise bays. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Abel Tasman National Park
short: NZ's smallest national park and arguably its prettiest — 60 km of gold-sand beaches, turquoise water, and one of the 10 Great Walks.
commentary: Abel Tasman National Park is 237 km² — NZ's smallest — but produces some of the country's most photographed coastline: golden sand, clear water, native bush right to the tideline. The 60 km Abel Tasman Coast Track (one of the 10 Great Walks) runs the full length, with water taxis letting you walk any section you want. Sea kayaking is the other way in — day tours or multi-day paddles through the sheltered bays. Tonga Island Marine Reserve has a resident NZ fur-seal colony. The park takes its name from Dutch navigator Abel Tasman, the first European to sight New Zealand in 1642.
review_summary: Abel Tasman National Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. NZ's smallest national park and arguably its prettiest — 60 km of gold-sand beaches, turquoise water, and one of the 10 Great Walks. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking
short: Sea kayak along golden beaches and turquoise coves in Abel Tasman National Park.
commentary: Abel Tasman National Park offers some of New Zealand's finest sea kayaking, with golden sand beaches, clear turquoise water, and native bush reaching to the water's edge. Multi-day guided and freedom kayak trips explore the coast, with stops at pristine beaches and chances to see seals, dolphins, and blue penguins.
audio_story: You have arrived at Abel Tasman National Park, New Zealand's smallest national park but arguably its most beautiful coastal playground. Your sea kayak will carry you along a coastline of golden sand beaches separated by granite headlands, with water so clear you can see the starfish on the seabed three metres below your paddle. The park stretches 51 kilometres along the coast, and by kayak you'll access beaches that are impossible to reach any other way. Paddle into the famous Split Apple Rock — a giant boulder split perfectly in two by geological forces — and stop for lunch on a deserted beach where the sand squeaks beneath your feet. Fur seals laze on offshore rocks, little blue penguins pop up beside your kayak, and if you're here in summer, the water temperature is warm enough for swimming. This is sea kayaking at its absolute finest, and there's nowhere else quite like it in New Zealand.
review_summary: The Abel Tasman Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sea kayak along golden beaches and turquoise coves in Abel Tasman National Park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Abel Tasman''s First Encounter Site — Wainui Bay
short: Where the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman first met Māori in 1642 — a meeting that ended in four Dutch deaths.
commentary: In December 1642 Abel Tasman anchored off Wainui Bay. Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri attacked a launch; four Dutch sailors were killed. Tasman left without landing.
audio_story: You are at Wainui Bay, near the inner edge of Golden Bay, and on the thirteenth and fourteenth of December, sixteen forty two, Dutch ships anchored in the open water you are looking at. Abel Tasman, sailing for the Dutch East India Company on a voyage of exploration, had already sighted the West Coast of the South Island. He was looking for an anchorage, fresh water, and possible trade. A number of waka came out from the shore here. The parties on either side exchanged calls. Trumpets were blown and drums beaten from both vessels, which Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri, the iwi of Mohua at the time, appear to have interpreted as a direct challenge. The following morning, as the Dutch launched a small boat between the two ships, the tangata whenua attacked. Four Dutch sailors were killed. Tasman weighed anchor without landing and left the country forever, naming the bay Murderers Bay, a name that clung on colonial maps for two hundred years. He never set foot on New Zealand soil. The first and second encounters between Europeans and Māori were separated by more than a century. Drive to the shore, stand in the quiet bay, and imagine what that misunderstanding must have looked like.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Abel Tasman''s First Encounter Site — Wainui Bay — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman first met Māori in 1642 — a meeting that ended in four Dutch deaths. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Boulder Bank & Nelson Haven
short: The natural 13km boulder spit that created Nelson''s safe harbour — and made settlement possible in 1841.
commentary: Boulder Bank is a unique 13km natural seawall that creates Nelson Haven. The NZ Company founded Nelson 1841 inside this shelter.
audio_story: You are looking at the Boulder Bank, a single thirteen kilometre natural shingle and boulder ridge that encloses Nelson Haven. It is an unusual landform, found nowhere else quite like it in New Zealand. The boulders, worked down over centuries from the schist cliffs of Mackay Bluff to the north, have been pushed south by the prevailing currents and deposited in this long narrow spit parallel to the mainland. The result is a sheltered harbour that would not otherwise exist on this exposed stretch of coast. When the New Zealand Company began looking for a second settlement site after Wellington in eighteen forty one, they chose Nelson precisely because the Boulder Bank provided a ready made protected anchorage. The surveyor Arthur Wakefield sailed in through the Haven entrance in late eighteen forty one with the first settler ships. The town was laid out the following year. The Māori name for the feature is Te Matau a Māui, recalling the fish hook of Māui, and the area was already well known to Te Ātiawa, Ngāti Kōata, and Ngāti Rārua who had settled the sheltered side of the bank. A lighthouse built on the outer end in eighteen sixty two still operates. Drive the Boulder Bank road at the northern end if you want to walk on it.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Boulder Bank & Nelson Haven — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The natural 13km boulder spit that created Nelson''s safe harbour — and made settlement possible in 1841. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Cable Bay Stand-Up Paddleboarding
short: SUP the calm turquoise waters of Cable Bay near Nelson.
commentary: Cable Bay, just north of Nelson, offers sheltered waters ideal for stand-up paddleboarding. The small bay has a sandy beach, clear water, and views across Tasman Bay to the Richmond Range. Rental and lessons are available locally, and the bay is sheltered from the prevailing winds.
audio_story: You have arrived at Cable Bay, a sheltered crescent of golden sand just north of Nelson that offers perfect conditions for stand-up paddleboarding. The bay is naturally protected from the prevailing winds, creating calm, clear water that's ideal for beginners and experienced paddlers alike. From your board, you'll glide over a seabed of sand and rock, watching fish dart beneath you in the crystal-clear water. The views across Tasman Bay to the snow-dusted Richmond Range are stunning, and the gentle shoreline makes it easy to hop off for a swim whenever the mood strikes. Nelson is one of the sunniest cities in New Zealand, so chances are you'll have blue sky and warm sunshine to complement your paddle. It's a relaxing, beautiful way to spend a morning or afternoon on the water.
review_summary: The Cable Bay Stand-Up Paddleboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. SUP the calm turquoise waters of Cable Bay near Nelson. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Cable Bay Walkway
short: Walk the coastal ridge where the first telegraph cable linked New Zealand's two islands in 1866.
commentary: The Cable Bay Walkway follows the coast from Glenduan to Cable Bay, covering 8 km one way. The route traces the ridgeline above the boulder-strewn coast with panoramic views of Tasman Bay and the Marlborough Sounds. Cable Bay takes its name from the undersea telegraph cable laid here in 1866, connecting the North and South Islands for the first time.
audio_story: You are walking the Cable Bay Walkway, following a ridgeline above the northern coast of Nelson. The views are extraordinary — Tasman Bay stretches south, the Marlborough Sounds crumple the horizon to the east, and on clear days you can see the North Island across Cook Strait. The bay at the far end takes its name from a moment in New Zealand's history: in 1866, an undersea telegraph cable was laid here, connecting the South Island to Wellington for the first time. Before that cable, news took days to cross the strait by ship. After it, messages flew in seconds. The walkway itself undulates along the coast through regenerating bush and open grassland, with the constant blue of the bay below.
review_summary: The Cable Bay Walkway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk the coastal ridge where the first telegraph cable linked New Zealand's two islands in 1866. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Centre of New Zealand Monument
short: A short steep walk to a survey trig that has become Nelson's symbolic geographic centre of the country.
commentary: The monument marks the point from which John Spence Browning triangulated the South Island in the 1870s, not the true geographic centre (which lies further south).
audio_story: You are walking up to the Centre of New Zealand monument, a short but steep climb from the Botanical Reserve in central Nelson. The story of the name is more interesting than the geography. In the eighteen seventies, the colonial surveyor John Spence Browning established a network of triangulation points across the South Island, and one of his key stations was on top of this hill. Over the years the story morphed, as stories do, and the hill came to be described as the exact geographic centre of New Zealand. In truth, it is not. The true centre lies further south, in the Spooners Range above Tapawera. But the name stuck, and the view from the top is so generous that no one has really minded. The walk up is fifteen minutes of steady climbing on a well formed path. At the top you stand on a small flat summit with a trig, a flagpole, and a ring of interpretation boards. On a clear day the whole of Nelson is spread below you, with Tasman Bay glinting north, the Richmond Range to the east, and the Western Ranges of Abel Tasman National Park on the horizon. It is one of the great quick walks of any New Zealand town.
review_summary: Centre of New Zealand Monument draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A short steep walk to a survey trig that has become Nelson's symbolic geographic centre of the country. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Collingwood Old Cemetery
short: A quiet hillside cemetery above Collingwood — the burial ground of the 1857 Aorere gold rush miners.
commentary: The Aorere gold rush began 1857 near Collingwood. The town grew rapidly to 2,000 people before the field played out. Old cemetery overlooks the town.
audio_story: You are at the old cemetery above Collingwood, at the northern end of Golden Bay. Collingwood as a town is a direct legacy of the Aorere gold rush of eighteen fifty seven, the first significant gold rush in New Zealand. Before eighteen fifty seven there was nothing here but a small surveyor''s camp. Within six months of the first strike in the upper Aorere River there were over two thousand men camped in and around the settlement, working sluice boxes in the creeks and living in rough tent and wooden shanty camps. The town was properly named Collingwood in eighteen fifty nine, supposedly at one point being considered as a possible new capital of New Zealand. By eighteen sixty two the easy surface gold had been worked out and the rush moved on to the Otago fields. Collingwood, its population collapsed back to a few hundred, reverted to a quiet port serving farming and logging communities. The old cemetery above the town holds the graves of many of those who stayed and died here. Weather beaten headstones, Chinese inscriptions on a few plots, young children''s graves. A quiet thirty minutes. Look down into the estuary as you walk out, and imagine the tent city it once was.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Collingwood Old Cemetery — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A quiet hillside cemetery above Collingwood — the burial ground of the 1857 Aorere gold rush miners. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Farewell Spit / Onetahua
short: The 26km curving sand spit at the top of the South Island — Cape Farewell, where Cook finally turned home in 1770.
commentary: Farewell Spit is 26km long. Named by Cook in March 1770 as his last point of contact with NZ before sailing home. Onetahua is the Māori name.
audio_story: You are at Farewell Spit, the long curving sand spit that arcs out twenty six kilometres from the northern tip of the South Island, enclosing Golden Bay. In Māori tradition the spit is called Onetahua, the piled up sand, and is regarded as a tupuna, ancestral form, by Ngāti Tama and the iwi of Mohua. The English name comes from Captain James Cook, who on the thirty first of March, seventeen seventy, sailed past the point as his final sight of land before striking out west across the Tasman Sea for Australia. He named the cape Farewell. The spit functions as the principal feeding ground for tens of thousands of migratory shorebirds each summer, including bar tailed godwits, knots, and oystercatchers, and is recognised as an internationally important wetland. It is also notorious as one of the great whale stranding sites in the world. Several times each decade, large pods of pilot whales strand on the flat sand of the inner spit, probably confused by the shallow bathymetry. Local and DOC response operations to refloat them are a major feature of West Coast and Tasman public life. Public foot access is permitted for about four kilometres from the base; the tip itself, including the lighthouse, can only be reached by commercial guided tour.
review_summary: Farewell Spit / Onetahua draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 26km curving sand spit at the top of the South Island — Cape Farewell, where Cook finally turned home in 1770. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Farewell Spit / Onetahua
short: The spirits' departure point — where the dead travel before leaping to the underworld.
commentary: In some traditions, spirits travel northward along the spit before leaping to Te Rerenga Wairua. The 26 km spit is wāhi tapu and home to vast numbers of migratory birds.
review_summary: Farewell Spit / Onetahua draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The spirits' departure point — where the dead travel before leaping to the underworld. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Farewell Spit / Tuhuroa
short: A 26 km sand spit — New Zealand's longest — and a Ramsar-listed bird sanctuary at the top of the South Island.
commentary: Farewell Spit is 26 km of sand and shell — the longest natural sand spit in New Zealand, growing roughly 10 m a year from the sediment the Westland rivers pour into Cook Strait. It is an internationally significant Ramsar wetland: up to 20,000 bar-tailed godwits arrive here every September from Alaska, completing the world's longest non-stop bird migration (~11,000 km). Public access is restricted; DOC permits the first 4 km, and only licensed tour operators (two, from Collingwood) go further. The 1870 lighthouse at the tip is their turnaround point.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Farewell Spit / Tuhuroa — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A 26 km sand spit — New Zealand's longest — and a Ramsar-listed bird sanctuary at the top of the South Island. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Farewell Spit Eco Tour
short: 4WD safari along the extraordinary 35 km Farewell Spit — a globally significant bird habitat.
commentary: Farewell Spit is a 35-kilometre sand spit at the northern tip of the South Island, designated a Wetland of International Importance. Guided 4WD tours are the only way to access the spit beyond the public walking area. Over 90 bird species use the spit, including thousands of migratory godwits from Alaska.
audio_story: You have arrived at Farewell Spit, a remarkable 35-kilometre finger of sand that curls into Golden Bay at the very top of the South Island. This is one of the most important bird habitats in the Southern Hemisphere, home to over 90 species and a critical stopover for bar-tailed godwits on their epic 11,000-kilometre non-stop flight from Alaska — the longest known non-stop migration of any bird. A guided 4WD tour is the only way to explore the spit beyond the first four kilometres, and your vehicle will travel along the ocean beach past the lighthouse to viewing points where thousands of wading birds feed on the vast tidal flats. The scale of the spit is astonishing — the sand dunes rise to 30 metres in places, and on a windy day, the sand streams from the crests like smoke. The Maori name, Onetahua, means 'heaped-up sand,' and it's been accumulating here for over 6,000 years.
review_summary: The Farewell Spit Eco Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. 4WD safari along the extraordinary 35 km Farewell Spit — a globally significant bird habitat. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Farewell Spit Heritage Walk
short: Walk the base of New Zealand's longest sand spit — a 26 km bird sanctuary curving into the Tasman Sea.
commentary: Farewell Spit is a 26 km sand spit extending from the northern tip of the South Island, formed by longshore drift carrying sand from the West Coast. It is a wetland of international importance under the Ramsar Convention and home to over 90 bird species including migratory godwits from Alaska. Public access beyond the base requires a licensed tour operator.
audio_story: You are walking the base of Farewell Spit, and ahead of you 26 kilometres of sand curves out into the Tasman Sea like a great golden hook. This is New Zealand's longest natural sand spit, built grain by grain from West Coast sand carried north by currents over thousands of years. The spit is a bird sanctuary of global significance — each year, bar-tailed godwits arrive here after an 11,000-kilometre non-stop flight from Alaska, the longest known migration of any bird. You can only explore the base on foot without a guide; the outer reaches are strictly protected. But even from here, the sight is remarkable: vast tidal flats stretching to the horizon, alive with wading birds, and the lighthouse blinking at the very tip where the spit finally surrenders to the sea.
review_summary: The 26km sand spit curving into the top of the South Island is a remarkable natural feature — home to internationally significant shorebird habitat and gannet colonies. Visitors can only access the full spit on guided tours, which reviewers consistently rate as excellent value for the unique experience.

name: Founders Heritage Park, Nelson
short: A living heritage village — 30+ period buildings with working blacksmith, printer, and operating 610mm heritage railway.
commentary: Founders Heritage Park brings together relocated and replica buildings from the 1840s–1940s. 610mm tourist railway operates on fine-weather weekends.
audio_story: You are at Founders Heritage Park, an open air museum on the inlet behind the port of Nelson. The park is arranged as a small heritage village, and over thirty buildings representing Nelson life from the eighteen forties to the nineteen forties have been either relocated here or reconstructed in period form. A colonial hospital, a schoolroom, a general store with period groceries on the shelves, a wool store, a printer''s shop with working letterpress machines, a tannery, a blacksmith, and a jeweller, among others. On fine weekends a working six hundred and ten millimetre narrow gauge tourist railway runs through the village, pulled by a small steam or diesel locomotive depending on the day. A craft brewery, a café, and a gallery of period curiosities round out the visit. Entry is modest. Allow two to three hours for a relaxed walk through. The park is run by a local charitable trust and volunteers, and is understandably local in flavour, which is part of its charm. It is not a professionally polished tourist experience. It is the slow, honest, craft heavy work of a community that has taken responsibility for preserving its own memory. A good one to pair with the Nelson Provincial Museum in town.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Founders Heritage Park, Nelson — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A living heritage village — 30+ period buildings with working blacksmith, printer, and operating 610mm heritage railway. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Golden Bay / Mohua / Tākaka
short: Over Tākaka Hill — a self-sufficient, idiosyncratic community the rest of the country politely calls 'alternative'.
commentary: Golden Bay — Mohua — is the curve of coastline north of the Tākaka Hill and culturally somewhat separate from the rest of the country. From the 1970s it attracted back-to-the-land settlers, permaculture pioneers, and dropouts; the resulting community is artisan-heavy, organic, friendly, and genuinely different. Tākaka is the main town: a single main street of cafés, galleries, a legendary bakery, and the Dangerous Kitchen. Pōhara and Tata Beach are the swimming beaches. Pupu Springs, Wharariki, the Abel Tasman, and Farewell Spit are all within 45 minutes. Summer only — the hill road gets icy in winter.
review_summary: Golden Bay / Mohua / Tākaka draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Over Tākaka Hill — a self-sufficient, idiosyncratic community the rest of the country politely calls 'alternative'. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Golden Bay Horse Trekking
short: Ride horses along the wild beaches and through bush in Golden Bay.
commentary: Horse trekking in Golden Bay offers rides along wide sandy beaches, through native bush, and across farmland with views of Farewell Spit. The area's remote, uncrowded character makes for a peaceful riding experience. Rides cater to all experience levels.
audio_story: You have arrived in Golden Bay, one of the most peaceful and unspoiled corners of the South Island, and a wonderful place to explore on horseback. Your ride will take you along wide golden sand beaches where the only hoofprints ahead of you are from previous treks, through pockets of native bush alive with birdsong, and across farmland with views stretching to Farewell Spit and the distant peaks of Kahurangi National Park. Golden Bay has a relaxed, creative community and a landscape that seems to slow time down. The horses are well-suited to the terrain, picking their way confidently along beach and bush tracks. Whether you're an experienced rider or sitting in a saddle for the first time, the combination of gentle horses, stunning scenery, and Golden Bay's special atmosphere makes this a ride you'll remember long after you leave.
review_summary: The Golden Bay Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses along the wild beaches and through bush in Golden Bay. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Golden Bay Paragliding
short: Tandem paraglide over Golden Bay's sweeping beaches and mountains.
commentary: Tandem paragliding from the hills above Takaka offers bird's-eye views of Golden Bay, Farewell Spit, and the Kahurangi ranges. Flights launch from hill sites with reliable thermals and land on the beach or open farmland. The coastal setting provides stunning contrasts of sea, sand, and mountain.
audio_story: You have arrived at one of the top of the South Island's most spectacular paragliding launch sites. From the hills above Takaka, you and your tandem pilot will run a few steps into the air and soar out over Golden Bay with a view that will make your heart sing. Below you, the sweep of the bay curves away to the sand ribbon of Farewell Spit. Behind you, the peaks of Kahurangi National Park march into the distance. The thermals along this coast are reliable and gentle, often providing long, smooth flights that let you truly absorb the panoramic scenery. Your landing will be on the beach or a green field, and the experience of stepping out of the sky onto solid ground again is always a gentle surprise. Golden Bay's remote beauty is best appreciated from the air, where the full scale of the landscape reveals itself.
review_summary: The Golden Bay Paragliding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem paraglide over Golden Bay's sweeping beaches and mountains. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Golden Bear Brewing Co
short: Mapua Wharf craft brewery — small-batch beers with estuary views on the Tasman Bay waterfront.
commentary: Golden Bear is one of the gems of the Mapua Wharf precinct, brewing small-batch beers using Nelson hops and local water. The tap selection changes regularly — expect hop-forward pales and IPAs, sessionable lagers, and occasional wild ferments. The wharf-side seating with Tasman Bay views is idyllic, and it pairs perfectly with smoked fish from the neighbouring Smokehouse.
review_summary: Great location with good small-batch beers. The Mapua Wharf setting adds charm. Limited seating in peak summer.

name: Gravity Cellars
short: Small-batch Upper Moutere winery — gravity-flow winemaking and minimal intervention wines.
commentary: Gravity Cellars takes its name from its winemaking approach — no pumps, just gravity moving the wine gently through the process. The result is textured, expressive wines that reflect the Upper Moutere's unique clay soils. The cellar door is intimate and the winemaker is often pouring. Their Pinot Noir and skin-contact white are standouts for anyone interested in natural winemaking.
review_summary: Natural wine enthusiasts love the minimal-intervention approach. The personal cellar door experience is praised. Some conventional palates may find the wines unusual.

name: Great Taste Trail
short: Cycle trail linking Nelson's breweries, wineries and food producers — ride, taste, repeat.
commentary: The Great Taste Trail is one of New Zealand's best cycle trails, linking cafés, breweries, wineries and food producers across the Nelson-Tasman region. The full trail is about 175km, but most people do sections — the Mapua to Nelson leg passes through orchards and past several cellar doors. Bike hire is available in Nelson and Mapua, and e-bikes make the gentle hills manageable for everyone.
review_summary: The combination of cycling and food/wine tasting is praised as a perfect day out. E-bike hire makes it accessible. Some sections are on-road which can feel less safe.

name: Great Taste Trail Cycle Ride
short: Cycle 174 km through the Nelson-Tasman region's wineries, breweries, and coastal villages.
commentary: The Great Taste Trail is a 174 km network of cycling trails linking Nelson, Richmond, Mapua, Motueka, and Kaiteriteri. The trail passes through hop gardens, vineyards, craft breweries, and along the coast. The region produces 90% of New Zealand's hops and has a thriving craft beer and artisan food scene.
audio_story: You are pedalling the Great Taste Trail, and the name is not an accident — this ride is designed to take you past the best food and drink the Nelson-Tasman region produces. The trail follows old railway lines and coastal paths through 174 kilometres of some of New Zealand's sunniest countryside. You will cycle past hop gardens that supply 90 percent of the country's hops, through vineyards producing aromatic whites, and past craft breweries that seem to appear every time you need a break. At Mapua, the old wharf has been converted into a cluster of cafes and galleries, and the estuary crossing by barge is a highlight. Between tastings, the trail threads along the Tasman Bay coast, through apple orchards, and into the foothills of Kahurangi National Park.
review_summary: The Great Taste Trail Cycle Ride route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle 174 km through the Nelson-Tasman region's wineries, breweries, and coastal villages. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Heaphy Track
short: The longest of the Great Walks — 82km through Kahurangi National Park, from the Aorere Valley to the West Coast.
commentary: Four-to-five day tramp, or 2-3 day mountain bike ride in winter. One of only two Great Walks open to cyclists.
audio_story: You are about to walk the Heaphy Track, the longest of the ten Great Walks of New Zealand. Eighty two kilometres on foot, usually over four or five days, from the Aorere Valley in the Tasman region across the spine of Kahurangi National Park down to the wild west coast at Kohaihai, north of Karamea. The route crosses an astonishing range of landscapes. It begins in farmland, climbs into deep beech forest, breaks out onto the high Gouland Downs of tussock and limestone karst, then descends through nikau palm forest to emerge, finally, on a long wild beach on the Tasman Sea. In winter the track is also one of only two Great Walks open to mountain bikers, and the two or three day ride across it is one of the most sought after mountain biking experiences in the country. Kahurangi holds many of the oldest rocks and strangest endemic species in New Zealand, including the large flightless great spotted kiwi, which you may well hear at night from one of the well appointed huts along the route. Book huts well in advance through Department of Conservation. Allow a good weather window. The Heaphy, in fine weather, is one of the great walks of your life.
review_summary: Heaphy Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The longest of the Great Walks — 82km through Kahurangi National Park, from the Aorere Valley to the West Coast. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Heaphy Track Great Walk
short: The longest Great Walk crosses Kahurangi National Park from alpine downs to wild West Coast beaches.
commentary: The Heaphy Track is 78.4 km long, running from Brown Hut near Collingwood to Kohaihai on the West Coast, typically walked in 4-6 days. It crosses open tussock downs, dense podocarp forest, and a dramatic stretch of nikau palm-lined coast. Since 2011, mountain biking has been permitted seasonally. It is named after Major Charles Heaphy, who explored the route in 1846.
audio_story: You are beginning the Heaphy Track, and over the next 78 kilometres you will cross an entire national park from east to west. The track starts in beech forest, climbs to the Gouland Downs — an eerie plateau of tussock and limestone — and then descends through layer after layer of forest into the West Coast. The final coastal section is extraordinary: you walk along wild beaches backed by nikau palms, the bush tumbling down steep hillsides to the Tasman Sea. Major Charles Heaphy explored this route in 1846, though Maori had used it for centuries before to access West Coast pounamu. Since 2011, mountain bikers have been allowed on the track between May and September, making it the only Great Walk that permits cycling.
review_summary: The Heaphy Track Great Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. The longest Great Walk crosses Kahurangi National Park from alpine downs to wild West Coast beaches. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Hooked at Marahau
short: Seafood restaurant at the gateway to Abel Tasman — fresh local catch with stunning coastal views.
commentary: Hooked sits right at the southern entrance to Abel Tasman National Park in Marahau, serving some of the freshest seafood in the region. The menu changes daily based on the local catch — expect blue cod, snapper, green-lipped mussels and crayfish when available. The views down the Astrolabe Roadstead are spectacular, and it's the perfect place for a meal before or after an Abel Tasman adventure.
review_summary: The fresh seafood and location are consistently praised. A great pre- or post-Abel Tasman meal. Some note prices reflect the captive-audience location.

name: Hop Federation Brewery
short: Riwaka brewery using locally grown hops — taproom tastings in NZ's hop-growing capital.
commentary: Nelson grows all of New Zealand's commercial hops, and Hop Federation makes the most of that proximity. Based in Riwaka, the brewery sources fresh hops from neighbouring farms and crafts some of the freshest, most hop-forward beers in the country. The taproom lets you taste the full range — the fresh hop beer, released each harvest season, is a must-try for hop lovers. The tasting paddles are excellent value.
review_summary: Hop lovers praise the freshness and quality. The harvest-season fresh hop beer is a highlight. The Riwaka location requires a car to reach.

name: Jester House Café
short: Quirky garden café near Tasman — eel feeding, native gardens and excellent brunch in a rural setting.
commentary: Jester House is one of Nelson Tasman's most characterful cafés — set in lush native gardens where you can feed tame eels in the stream, play lawn games, and wander through themed garden areas. The food is well above standard café fare, with seasonal produce, excellent cabinet food and good coffee. It's especially popular with families but charming for everyone. The rural Tasman location is a pleasant drive from Nelson.
review_summary: The eel feeding and gardens delight children and adults. The food quality surprises. A fun, quirky stop on the way to Abel Tasman.

name: Kaiteriteri Beach
short: The gateway beach to Abel Tasman — gold sand, calm water, and the family beach of choice for the top of the South Island.
commentary: Kaiteriteri is the small beach village on the southern edge of Abel Tasman National Park. The beach is a perfect crescent of gold sand (fine granite weathered out of the inland ranges), sheltered water, and year-round warm swimming. It's the embarkation point for many Abel Tasman water taxis and kayak tours. The hinterland has a small, well-made mountain-bike park — Kaiteriteri Mountain Bike Park, free entry, grades 2–5. The holiday park fills in summer; book far ahead.
review_summary: Kaiteriteri Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The gateway beach to Abel Tasman — gold sand, calm water, and the family beach of choice for the top of the South Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Kaiteriteri Snorkelling & Marine Reserve
short: Snorkel the Tonga Island Marine Reserve in Abel Tasman's crystal-clear waters.
commentary: The Tonga Island Marine Reserve within Abel Tasman National Park protects a rich marine environment around Tonga Island and its surrounds. Snorkelling reveals colourful reef fish, anemones, and kelp forests in clear water. Accessible by water taxi from Kaiteriteri, with free access once in the park.
audio_story: You have arrived at Kaiteriteri, the golden gateway to Abel Tasman National Park and some of the best snorkelling on the South Island. A short water taxi ride takes you to the Tonga Island Marine Reserve, where the water clarity is extraordinary — you can see the seabed clearly at depths of ten metres or more. Slip beneath the surface and you'll discover a world of colourful reef fish darting through swaying kelp forests, anemones waving in the gentle current, and if you keep very still, you might spot a blue cod or octopus hiding in the rocky crevices. The marine reserve has been protected since 1993, and the abundance of life here shows what healthy seas can look like. Tonga Island itself is home to a colony of New Zealand fur seals, and they sometimes swim curiously close to snorkellers. The water is warmest from December to March, but a wetsuit extends the season comfortably.
review_summary: The Kaiteriteri Snorkelling & Marine Reserve adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Snorkel the Tonga Island Marine Reserve in Abel Tasman's crystal-clear waters. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Kaiteriteri to Marahau Coastal Road
short: A short coastal drive between two beloved beach towns at the gateway to Abel Tasman.
commentary: The short drive between Kaiteriteri and Marahau passes through native bush along the coast at the southern entrance to Abel Tasman National Park. Kaiteriteri is famous for its orange-gold granite sand beach, while Marahau is the main trailhead for the Abel Tasman Coast Track. Both offer water taxi and kayak services into the park.
audio_story: You are driving the coastal road from Kaiteriteri to Marahau, and these few kilometres are the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park. Kaiteriteri's beach behind you is famous for its orange-gold sand — the colour comes from the granite rock of this coast, rich in iron and feldspar. The road winds through native bush with glimpses of the turquoise Tasman Bay through the trees. At Marahau, you reach the start of the Abel Tasman Coast Track, where water taxis idle at the wharf and kayakers prepare to paddle into some of the most beautiful coastal scenery in New Zealand. Even if you are not walking the full track, this is where the adventure begins.
review_summary: The Kaiteriteri to Marahau Coastal Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A short coastal drive between two beloved beach towns at the gateway to Abel Tasman. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mapua Wharf
short: A pretty little wharf village between Nelson and Motueka — good food, a small ferry to Rabbit Island, and relaxed afternoon energy.
commentary: Mapua sits on the Waimea Estuary, 35 minutes west of Nelson. The wharf — once an apple-export loading dock — has been revived as a cluster of restaurants, craft breweries, fish-and-chip shop, and artisan studios. The Mapua Ferry runs a 5-minute crossing to Rabbit Island (Moturoa) for swimming, picnicking and walking. It's a good afternoon stop on the drive to Motueka or Abel Tasman — busy in summer, quiet shoulder seasons, and one of the nicest-looking public spaces in the Nelson region.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Mapua Wharf is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A pretty little wharf village between Nelson and Motueka — good food, a small ferry to Rabbit Island, and relaxed afternoon energy. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Mapua Wharf
short: Former apple sheds converted to waterfront cafés and eateries — fresh seafood, craft beer and estuary views.
commentary: Mapua Wharf is where old apple-packing sheds have been reborn as a charming waterfront food precinct. The Smokehouse does excellent smoked fish and seafood, the Golden Bear brewery pours craft beer, and several cafés serve fresh local produce. The setting — overlooking the Waimea estuary on Tasman Bay — is idyllic. It's the endpoint of the Great Taste Trail if you're cycling.
review_summary: The waterfront setting and fresh seafood win visitors over. The Smokehouse is a particular favourite. Can feel touristy in peak summer, but the quality holds up.

name: Motueka
short: The laid-back gateway to Abel Tasman — a Saturday market, hop farms, and more wholefood cafes than traffic lights.
commentary: Motueka is the service town for Abel Tasman National Park. Known for hops, apples, and a thriving arts/alternative community.
audio_story: You have arrived in Motueka, the gentle service town at the southern entrance to Abel Tasman National Park, and you have arrived somewhere slightly unlike the rest of New Zealand. Motueka sits in a small sheltered plain between the mountains and the sea, with a climate mild enough to grow world class hops, apples, and boysenberries, and a human community to match. The town has long attracted alternative makers, artists, organic farmers and slow living migrants, and the main street today reflects that mix, with a disproportionate number of wholefood cafes, art galleries, and bookshops for a town of seven and a half thousand people. On Sunday mornings the Motueka Market spreads through the town centre with craft stalls, local produce, and some of the best cheap food in the region. From Motueka you can drive fifteen minutes to Kaiteriteri and the start of Abel Tasman water taxis, or ten minutes to the Riwaka Resurgence, a turquoise pool where the Riwaka River emerges fully grown from a limestone cave. Many visitors treat Motueka only as a passing stop. Stay a night. Catch the market. Linger a little.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Motueka is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The laid-back gateway to Abel Tasman — a Saturday market, hop farms, and more wholefood cafes than traffic lights. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Motueka Valley Highway
short: Drive through the hop gardens and orchards of the Motueka River valley to the mountains beyond.
commentary: The Motueka Valley Highway follows the Motueka River inland from the coast through some of New Zealand's most productive horticultural land. The valley produces hops, apples, kiwifruit, and berries. The road continues to the Upper Motueka and the eastern boundary of Kahurangi National Park, offering mountain views throughout.
audio_story: You are driving up the Motueka Valley, and the roadside is a patchwork of orchards and hop gardens. Those tall wire frames draped in green vines are hop plants, and this valley produces most of New Zealand's hops — the flowers that give beer its bitterness and aroma. The craft beer revolution has been very good to this valley. Apple orchards line the river flats too, their neat rows blooming white in spring and heavy with fruit in autumn. The Motueka River itself is a renowned trout stream, its clear water winding through willows and gravel banks. As you drive deeper into the valley, the mountains close in — the western flanks of Kahurangi National Park rising steeply from the valley floor.
review_summary: The Motueka Valley Highway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through the hop gardens and orchards of the Motueka River valley to the mountains beyond. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Moutere Hills Vineyard
short: Organic Upper Moutere vineyard — acclaimed Sarau Pinot Noir and a welcoming cellar door.
commentary: Moutere Hills has been farming organically in the Upper Moutere since the early 2000s. The Sarau Pinot Noir is their star wine — perfumed, silky and complex — and the Pinot Gris and Riesling are both excellent. The cellar door is welcoming and unpretentious, surrounded by the rolling hills that give the wines their distinctive character. A quieter alternative to the more visited Nelson wineries.
review_summary: The Sarau Pinot Noir wins consistent praise. The organic approach is genuine. Some find the Upper Moutere hard to find without GPS, but that adds to the discovery.

name: Nelson Cathedral — Christ Church Cathedral
short: The Anglican cathedral that took nearly 50 years to complete — a curious mix of Gothic planning and art deco detailing.
commentary: Began 1925 on a hill overlooking Nelson. Construction paused during the Depression, WWII, and seismic assessments. Finally consecrated 1972.
audio_story: You are at Christ Church Cathedral, Nelson, which stands on Church Hill overlooking the city centre. The cathedral has an unusually long and broken construction history. Work began in nineteen twenty five on the site of an earlier wooden church that had stood since eighteen fifty one. The original plan called for a soaring neo Gothic building in the tradition of the great English cathedrals. Construction was then paused by the Depression of the nineteen thirties, by the Second World War, and by successive seismic reviews that progressively reduced the ambition of the design. By the time work finally resumed after the war, the original lofty masonry tower plans had been abandoned as too heavy, and a simplified reinforced concrete and Takaka marble tower, drawing on art deco proportions rather than pure gothic, was substituted. The cathedral was finally consecrated in nineteen seventy two, forty seven years after the foundation stone was laid. The result is surprisingly harmonious, a genuine hybrid of twentieth century Modernism with Gothic bones. The steps up Church Hill to the front porch are a Nelson landmark in their own right, forming the visual centrepiece of the long Trafalgar Street axis. Walk up, go inside, and take ten minutes.
sources_to_check: Heritage NZ — Christ Church Cathedral Nelson
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Nelson Cathedral — Christ Church Cathedral — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The Anglican cathedral that took nearly 50 years to complete — a curious mix of Gothic planning and art deco detailing. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Nelson City / Whakatū
short: The country's sunniest city — 2,400+ hours of sunshine a year, an arts scene to match, and the geographic centre of NZ.
commentary: Nelson — Whakatū — has been settled for ~800 years (one of the earliest Māori settlements in Te Waipounamu) and founded as a European town in 1842. It's consistently New Zealand's sunniest city, with 2,400+ hours of sunshine annually. Christ Church Cathedral on its hill (completed 1972 after 47 years of construction) anchors the skyline. The World of WearableArt Museum, Founders Heritage Park, and the Suter Gallery are cultural draws. And the exact geographic centre of New Zealand sits on a hill 10 minutes' walk from the cathedral — a short track with a signpost at the top.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Nelson City / Whakatū is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The country's sunniest city — 2,400+ hours of sunshine a year, an arts scene to match, and the geographic centre of NZ. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Nelson Lakes / Lake Rotoiti / St Arnaud
short: The alpine front door to the Nelson Lakes National Park — glacial lakes, kea, and a DOC-led predator-control sanctuary.
commentary: Lake Rotoiti sits at St Arnaud, the gateway village to Nelson Lakes National Park. The lake was carved by a glacier during the last Ice Age; the bush-clad ranges behind rise to 2,500 m. Nelson Lakes is one of DOC's 'Mainland Island' intensive predator-control zones, and native birdlife has returned strongly — kea at the ski field, bellbirds in the bush, even rarer species recolonising. Short walks include the iconic jetty-at-dawn photo; longer options include the Mount Robert circuit and the 7-day Travers-Sabine circuit.
review_summary: Nelson Lakes / Lake Rotoiti / St Arnaud draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The alpine front door to the Nelson Lakes National Park — glacial lakes, kea, and a DOC-led predator-control sanctuary. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Nelson Mountain Bike Trails
short: Ride Nelson's legendary mountain bike trails, some of NZ's best singletrack.
commentary: Nelson is renowned as one of New Zealand's top mountain biking destinations, with trail networks at Codgers, Fringed Hill, and the Coppermine Trail. The trails benefit from Nelson's dry, sunny climate and range from flowing beginner tracks to technical expert singletrack. The Great Taste Trail offers scenic road and rail-trail cycling.
audio_story: You have arrived in Nelson, a city that punches well above its weight when it comes to mountain biking. The trail networks here are legendary in New Zealand cycling circles — Codgers offers tight, technical singletrack through native bush; Fringed Hill delivers flowing descents with harbour views; and the Coppermine Trail is a purpose-built masterpiece of berms, jumps, and drops. What gives Nelson its edge is the climate — it's the sunniest city in New Zealand, which means the trails dry quickly and ride well for most of the year. The local riding community is passionate and has built an incredible network of tracks that would rival any mountain biking destination in the world. If you prefer something gentler, the Great Taste Trail offers 174 kilometres of scenic cycling through vineyards, orchards, and along the coast. Nelson is a cycling city through and through.
review_summary: The Nelson Mountain Bike Trails adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride Nelson's legendary mountain bike trails, some of NZ's best singletrack. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Nelson Saturday Market
short: NZ's oldest continuous outdoor market — Saturday mornings in the CBD with local produce, arts and crafts.
commentary: The Nelson Saturday Market has been running every week since 1980, making it one of New Zealand's oldest. The Montgomery Square location fills with stalls selling local produce, artisan foods, baked goods, crafts and street food. The coffee is excellent, the produce is fresh, and the atmosphere — with live music and buskers — captures Nelson's laid-back creative spirit perfectly.
review_summary: A Nelson institution that locals and visitors love equally. The artisan food and produce are highlights. Can get crowded by mid-morning in summer.

name: Neudorf Vineyards
short: Upper Moutere estate producing New Zealand's most acclaimed Chardonnay — Moutere vineyard is world-class.
commentary: Tim and Judy Finn's Neudorf has been making wine in the Upper Moutere since 1978, and their Moutere Chardonnay is regularly cited as New Zealand's finest. The cellar door sits among apple orchards and rolling hills, and the tasting experience is personal and unhurried. The Pinot Noir from the gravelly Moutere soils is equally compelling. A pilgrimage for anyone serious about New Zealand Chardonnay.
review_summary: Wine critics consistently rank the Moutere Chardonnay among NZ's best. The setting and personal service are praised. Some find the Upper Moutere location remote, but that's part of the charm.

name: Pic's Peanut Butter World
short: New Zealand's most famous peanut butter — factory tours, tastings and a café in Nelson.
commentary: Pic Picot started making peanut butter in his Nelson garage and built it into one of New Zealand's most beloved food brands. The Pic's Peanut Butter World visitor centre offers factory tours, tastings and a café. Kids love watching the production line and tasting the different varieties. The slug-eating competition (squeeze peanut butter directly into your mouth) is oddly compelling. Great NZ food story.
review_summary: Families love the tours and tastings. The NZ success story resonates. Some adults find it more child-oriented than expected, but the peanut butter quality is undeniable.

name: Rabbit Island / Moturoa
short: A huge pine-shaded beach on a flat recreational island across the causeway from Nelson.
commentary: Moturoa/Rabbit Island is a flat 12km-long island connected by causeway. Dedicated entirely to recreation — pine-shaded camping, 8km-long safe swimming beach.
audio_story: You have driven out across the causeway to Moturoa, known universally in Nelson as Rabbit Island, and you have found yourself on an island entirely dedicated to recreation. Twelve kilometres of flat land, the whole of it planted in a mature pine forest on one side and a wide safe swimming beach on the other. In summer the island fills up with Nelson families spending the day here. There are picnic tables scattered under the pines, kilometres of flat cycling and walking tracks through the forest, and a long gentle beach where the water is sheltered by the Arthur Range on the far side of the bay and warms to unusually pleasant temperatures by mid-summer. Mountain bikers have a network of purpose built single track lines through the pines, family riders can follow the flat sealed and gravel roads, and walkers can loop the shoreline. A small cable ferry runs across the narrow Waimea Inlet to Mapua village, where cafes, breweries and fish and chips are waiting. The island is a working forestry plantation, so some roads are closed during active logging, but the great majority of it is open year round. Free entry. Free parking. Bring a picnic and a bike.
review_summary: Rabbit Island / Moturoa draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A huge pine-shaded beach on a flat recreational island across the causeway from Nelson.
