Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Rangi & Papa Sculpture, Marahau
short: A carved sculpture of sky father and earth mother at the entrance to Abel Tasman National Park.
commentary: The sculpture is a reminder that these landscapes carry deep Māori meaning beyond their natural beauty. The park was home to Māori for centuries before Abel Tasman's arrival in 1642.
review_summary: Visitors describe Rangi & Papa Sculpture, Marahau as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A carved sculpture of sky father and earth mother at the entrance to Abel Tasman National Park. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Seifried Estate
short: Nelson's largest and most established winery — cellar door, restaurant and 40+ years of winemaking.
commentary: Hermann and Agnes Seifried were Nelson wine pioneers, planting their first vines in the Waimea Plains in 1973. Today Seifried Estate is the region's largest winery, with a welcoming cellar door and popular restaurant. The Sweet Agnes Riesling (named after Agnes Seifried) is a benchmark dessert wine, and the whole range — Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir, Gewürztraminer — offers excellent value.
review_summary: The pioneer story adds depth. The restaurant is reliable and the wines offer great value. Some find it less intimate than smaller Nelson wineries.

name: Skydive Abel Tasman
short: Tandem skydive with views of Abel Tasman's golden coast and Tasman Bay.
commentary: Skydive Abel Tasman offers tandem jumps from up to 16,500 feet over one of New Zealand's most scenic coastlines. Freefall views encompass Abel Tasman National Park, Tasman Bay, the Richmond Range, and on clear days, both the North and South Islands.
audio_story: You have arrived at the drop zone for Skydive Abel Tasman, where you're about to freefall over one of the most beautiful coastlines in New Zealand. The plane will climb to 16,500 feet, and as it does, the golden beaches and turquoise bays of Abel Tasman National Park will unfurl below you like a postcard come to life. When you jump, the freefall lasts up to 75 seconds — long enough to take in views that span Tasman Bay, the Richmond Range, and on a clear day, the North Island across Cook Strait. The parachute ride down is sublime, floating above that famous golden coastline with the bush-clad hills and blue water spread out beneath you. Landing on Kaiteriteri Beach — one of New Zealand's most popular holiday beaches — is the perfect finale to an extraordinary jump.
review_summary: The Skydive Abel Tasman adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem skydive with views of Abel Tasman's golden coast and Tasman Bay. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Sprig & Fern Brewery
short: Nelson's beloved craft brewery — multiple pubs around the region pouring a wide range of styles.
commentary: Sprig & Fern is Nelson's craft beer institution, with several pubs across the region pouring a rotating selection of beers. The Hardy Street pub in Nelson city centre is the flagship — a relaxed, welcoming space with over a dozen taps covering everything from pilsners to porters. The food is pub-standard but honest, and the beer garden is popular in summer. Pick up a rigger (growler) to take away.
review_summary: A Nelson institution that locals love. The beer variety is impressive. Some find the pubs basic compared to modern taprooms, but the beer quality and atmosphere compensate.

name: Takaka Hill Drive (SH60)
short: Climb the marble mountain road that is Golden Bay's only road connection to the outside world.
commentary: Takaka Hill on SH60 rises to 791 m and is the sole road link between Motueka and Golden Bay. The road winds through marble and limestone karst country with caves, sinkholes, and natural arches. Ngarua Caves near the summit offer guided tours through spectacular formations. The hill was long considered one of New Zealand's most challenging drives.
audio_story: You are climbing Takaka Hill, the marble mountain that separates Golden Bay from the rest of New Zealand. This is the only road in, and it earns the effort. SH60 switchbacks up through a landscape of limestone and marble karst — you will see sinkholes in paddocks, cave entrances in road cuttings, and if you stop at Ngarua Caves near the summit, you can walk through caverns of pure marble draped in stalactites. The geology here is extraordinary: this marble formed from ancient sea-floor sediments hundreds of millions of years ago, then was lifted, folded, and dissolved into the honeycomb landscape you see today. At the top, pull over at the lookout. On a clear day, you can see the entire sweep of Golden Bay, with Farewell Spit curving out into the Tasman Sea like a beckoning finger.
review_summary: The Takaka Hill Drive (SH60) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Climb the marble mountain road that is Golden Bay's only road connection to the outside world. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Te Āwhina Marae, Motueka
short: The principal marae of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in Motueka — viewed respectfully from the road.
commentary: Te Āwhina Marae at Motueka serves Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua descendants who migrated south from Taranaki in the 1820s. Wharenui Tūrangapeke.
audio_story: You are passing Te Āwhina Marae, the principal marae at Motueka, and the home of the descendants of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in the Tasman district. Both iwi migrated south from Taranaki in the eighteen twenties under pressure from the northern Musket Wars, pushed down the west coast of the North Island, across Cook Strait, and into the top of the South Island, where they settled alongside and in some cases displaced the earlier tangata whenua. The wharenui at Te Āwhina is called Tūrangapeke, named for a senior ancestor of the iwi. It stands on the flat behind Motueka, within a stone''s throw of the beach. The marae has been an important centre for the southern Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua communities for over a century, hosting tangihanga, weddings, and major cultural and political hui. It is a living working marae. Casual visitors are not admitted, and the site should be viewed respectfully from the road. Pause for a moment. Remember that the iwi of Motueka are not tangata whenua from the time of first arrival but are themselves migrants from Taranaki whose migration and settlement here is barely two hundred years old, still fresh in their own traditions.
sources_to_check: Te Āti Awa o te Waka a Māui
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Āwhina Marae, Motueka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The principal marae of Te Āti Awa and Ngāti Rārua in Motueka — viewed respectfully from the road. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Waikoropupū Springs
short: The largest and clearest freshwater springs in New Zealand — wāhi tapu to local iwi, protected from all physical contact.
commentary: Te Waikoropupū releases 14,000 l/s of crystalline water. Water clarity measured over 60m. Wāhi tapu under a Water Conservation Order; no contact permitted.
audio_story: You are at Te Waikoropupū, known in English as Pupu Springs, which emerge from the limestone bedrock of Golden Bay and discharge around fourteen thousand litres of water per second into a complex of clear pools and channels. The water is filtered through the karst system for decades before it surfaces here, and the visual clarity, measured by scientific divers, regularly exceeds sixty metres, among the clearest fresh water ever recorded anywhere in the world. Te Waikoropupū is wāhi tapu to the iwi of Mohua, particularly Ngāti Tama and Te Āti Awa, and to local Pākehā residents who have campaigned for its protection for generations. Swimming, diving, drinking, or even touching the water is not permitted, both as a practical measure to protect its exceptional clarity from skin oils and sediment, and as respect for its spiritual status. Views from the boardwalk are more than adequate. A Water Conservation Order, one of only a handful in the country, formally recognises the springs'' cultural and scientific value. Walk the boardwalk slowly. Do not cross the rope. Stand and watch the sand dance in the upwelling for as long as you can. In a country full of freshwater wonders, this is arguably the most precious.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and clearest freshwater springs in New Zealand — wāhi tapu to local iwi, protected from all physical contact. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Waikoropupū Springs — Sacred Waters
short: NZ's largest freshwater springs — wāhi tapu with some of the clearest water on Earth.
commentary: Visibility exceeds 60 metres. The springs were on the pounamu trade route. Visitors are asked not to enter the water. The springs are the tears of Papatūānuku.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs — Sacred Waters as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. NZ's largest freshwater springs — wāhi tapu with some of the clearest water on Earth. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Waikoropupū Springs / Pupu Springs
short: The clearest freshwater ever measured — and a wāhi tapu. No touching, no swimming, just looking.
commentary: Te Waikoropupū Springs pump out 14 cubic metres of water per second from an underground aquifer, making them one of the largest freshwater springs in the Southern Hemisphere. In 1993, NIWA measured the water's visibility at 63 metres — a global record for freshwater clarity (only pure distilled water exceeds it). The springs are a wāhi tapu to Ngāti Tama and Te Ātiawa; after decades of concern over degradation and unauthorised swimming, a 2020 agreement strictly prohibits contact with the water. A boardwalk loops through the springs — walk, look, leave.
sources_to_check: Ngāti Tama ki Te Tau Ihu
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Waikoropupū Springs / Pupu Springs as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The clearest freshwater ever measured — and a wāhi tapu. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: The Smokehouse
short: Mapua Wharf's famous smokehouse — hot-smoked fish, mussels and seafood platters on the waterfront.
commentary: The Smokehouse at Mapua Wharf has been smoking fish for decades, and the technique shows. The hot-smoked salmon, smoked blue cod and mussel platters are all excellent, served at waterfront tables overlooking the Waimea estuary. It's simple food done brilliantly — the smoke flavour is delicate, not overpowering, and the fish is always moist. A Nelson Tasman must-visit, especially after cycling the Great Taste Trail.
review_summary: The smoked fish quality is outstanding. The waterfront setting is lovely. Can be very busy in peak summer — arrive early for the best selection.

name: The Suter Art Gallery, Nelson
short: The oldest art gallery in the country still on its original site — and a wonderful collection of early NZ painting.
commentary: The Suter opened 1899. Named after Bishop Andrew Suter. Rebuilt 2016. Strong collection of early NZ painters including John Gully and Olivia Spencer Bower.
audio_story: You are at the Suter Art Gallery, on the edge of Queens Gardens in central Nelson. It opened in eighteen ninety nine under a bequest from Bishop Andrew Suter, the second Anglican bishop of Nelson, who had amassed a significant personal collection of paintings and wanted them gifted to his adopted city. The original Edwardian building was extended several times over the twentieth century and then, in twenty sixteen, was completely rebuilt behind the original facade in a bold modern extension that triples the exhibition space. The Suter''s permanent collection is strong on early New Zealand watercolours and oils, particularly the nineteenth century Nelson painter John Gully, whose landscapes of the Nelson Lakes and the Buller Gorge remain among the best of their era. The gallery also holds important works by Olivia Spencer Bower, Rita Angus, Sir Mountford Tosswill Woollaston, and Colin McCahon, many with direct connections to the Nelson and Tasman art communities of the twentieth century. Temporary exhibitions rotate monthly and typically include contemporary local artists. Entry is free for most of the permanent galleries. An hour well spent, and the café is one of the best in the central city.
review_summary: The cultural experience at The Suter Art Gallery, Nelson is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The oldest art gallery in the country still on its original site — and a wonderful collection of early NZ painting. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Wakefield Quay — Founding Settlers Memorial
short: The quay named for Captain Arthur Wakefield — founder of the Nelson settlement, killed at Wairau the following year.
commentary: Captain Arthur Wakefield was NZ Company resident agent for Nelson from late 1841. Killed with 21 others at Tuamarina in the Wairau Affray, June 1843.
audio_story: You are at Wakefield Quay, the Nelson waterfront named for Captain Arthur Wakefield, the founder of the Nelson settlement. Arthur was the younger brother of Edward Gibbon Wakefield, the controversial theorist behind the systematic colonisation movement, and of William Wakefield, who had led the earlier Wellington settlement. Arthur arrived here in October eighteen forty one aboard the Whitby, along with the survey party sent ahead to prepare for the main body of colonists. Over the next eighteen months he supervised the landing of the first one thousand eight hundred settlers, the laying out of the town grid you see today, the early farming allocations, and the surveying of outlying districts including the lush Wairau Plains to the east. In June eighteen forty three he led a large armed expedition to Wairau to enforce a disputed survey against the Ngāti Toa chiefs Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata. The expedition ended in disaster. Arthur Wakefield and twenty one others were killed in what is now called the Wairau Affray, the first major armed conflict between colonists and Māori after the Treaty. A memorial stone on the quay names him and several of his colleagues. Nelson in its modern form is in many ways Arthur Wakefield''s legacy, both the town that exists and the limits his death placed on early colonial confidence.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Wakefield Quay — Founding Settlers Memorial — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The quay named for Captain Arthur Wakefield — founder of the Nelson settlement, killed at Wairau the following year. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Wangapeka Track
short: A rugged 52 km backcountry track through Kahurangi's remote interior — the tramper's alternative to the Heaphy.
commentary: The Wangapeka Track runs 52 km between Rolling River near Tapawera and Little Wanganui on the West Coast, crossing the main divide of the Kahurangi Range. It is considered more challenging than the Heaphy Track, with steeper terrain and river crossings. The track passes through diverse forest types and crosses two alpine saddles above 1,000 m.
audio_story: You are setting out on the Wangapeka Track, and this is the road less travelled through Kahurangi National Park. While most people walk the Heaphy, the Wangapeka offers something rawer: 52 kilometres of river valleys, alpine saddles, and forest so thick it blocks out the sky. The track crosses two passes above 1,000 metres and involves genuine river crossings where you will get your boots wet. It takes 4-5 days and you will likely have the huts to yourself — a rare luxury in a country where the popular tracks can feel crowded. The forest changes constantly as you cross the divide: beech gives way to podocarp, rata and rimu tower overhead, and if you are lucky, you might hear the booming call of a great spotted kiwi echoing through the night.
review_summary: The Wangapeka Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A rugged 52 km backcountry track through Kahurangi's remote interior — the tramper's alternative to the Heaphy. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Whakatū — Nelson Māori Heritage
short: Whakatū (Nelson) was settled by Māori centuries before Europeans — rich in pā sites and cultural associations.
commentary: Whakatū has a complex Māori history involving multiple iwi — Ngāti Tūmatakōkiri, Ngāti Kuia, Rangitāne, Ngāti Toa, and Te Ātiawa all have associations with the area. The Founders Heritage Park and Isel House contain Māori artefacts.
review_summary: Visitors describe Whakatū — Nelson Māori Heritage as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Whakatū (Nelson) was settled by Māori centuries before Europeans — rich in pā sites and cultural associations. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Wharariki Beach
short: The Archway Islands — four sea arches in the sand at low tide. Windows 10 set its opening wallpaper here.
commentary: Wharariki Beach is 20 minutes of walking from the nearest road, over a farm, past a dune — and when you arrive you face one of the great wild beaches of New Zealand. The Archway Islands, four volcanic sea stacks with huge arches, stand in the surf 100 m offshore. At low tide they appear to sit on the sand; Microsoft used them as the default Windows 10 opening screensaver. A small fur-seal colony often visits. The beach faces straight into the Tasman and the surf is dangerous. Take a jumper even in summer; the wind is constant.
review_summary: Wharariki Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The Archway Islands — four sea arches in the sand at low tide. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Wharariki Beach Walk
short: Walk to the wild, wind-sculpted Wharariki Beach with its massive archway islands and fur seal colony.
commentary: Wharariki Beach is an exposed, wind-swept beach near Cape Farewell, the northernmost point of the South Island. The 20-minute walk from the car park crosses farmland and sand dunes to reveal dramatic archway islands, rock pools, and a fur seal colony. The beach is famous for its windswept sand formations and is one of the most photographed beaches in New Zealand.
audio_story: You are walking across farmland toward Wharariki Beach, and nothing prepares you for what appears over the sand dunes. The beach is enormous, wild, and dominated by massive archway islands — rock stacks the size of buildings with holes punched clean through by the sea. The wind here comes straight off the Tasman Sea with nothing to slow it, sculpting the sand into rippled patterns that change by the hour. At the western end, a colony of New Zealand fur seals hauls out on the rocks, their pups playing in the rock pools with an energy that is hard not to smile at. This is Cape Farewell country — the northernmost point of the South Island — and the name is apt. This feels like the edge of things, where the land surrenders to the ocean and the ocean wins.
review_summary: The Wharariki Beach Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to the wild, wind-sculpted Wharariki Beach with its massive archway islands and fur seal colony. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: World of WearableArt (WOW)
short: Wearable art competition pieces and a classic car collection, displayed together — only in Nelson.
commentary: WearableArt began in Nelson in 1987 as a local gallery promotion — 'art off the wall, onto the body' — and grew into a major annual international competition (now held in Wellington). The museum in Nelson displays rotating selections of the most striking past entries: sculptural fashion made from paua, broken glass, car parts, fish skins, computer components. In the same building and with the same ticket, an enthusiast-sourced collection of classic cars, from a 1908 Cadillac to a De Lorean. An unexpected pairing; both parts excellent.
review_summary: The cultural experience at World of WearableArt (WOW) is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Wearable art competition pieces and a classic car collection, displayed together — only in Nelson. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours
short: Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV.
commentary: ATV tours from Ahipara take riders along the southern end of Ninety Mile Beach and into the spectacular Tauroa Point dune system. Tours combine beach riding with dune exploration, offering a high-energy way to experience this remote coastline. No experience necessary.
audio_story: You have arrived in Ahipara, the southern gateway to Ninety Mile Beach, and an ATV is about to take you on an unforgettable ride along one of the longest beaches in New Zealand. Despite its name, the beach is actually 88 kilometres long, but that is still an extraordinary stretch of uninterrupted sand. Your quad bike tour begins on the hard-packed sand near the waterline, where you can open the throttle and feel the salt spray on your face. Then you will head into the Tauroa Point dune system, a landscape of towering sand hills, hidden valleys, and views that stretch to the Tasman Sea. The dunes are constantly changing shape, carved by the prevailing westerly winds. No experience is needed, the guides will show you everything, and the bikes are powerful but manageable. Just be prepared for sand in places you did not know sand could reach.
review_summary: The Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Ake Ake Vineyard
short: Kerikeri boutique vineyard — cellar door and restaurant set among subtropical gardens and vines.
commentary: Ake Ake (named after the native timber) is a charming Kerikeri vineyard producing wines from tropical-climate grapes. The cellar door and restaurant sit among lush subtropical gardens, and the menu uses local Northland produce — avocados, citrus, fresh seafood. The Syrah and Pinotage show what's possible in Northland's warm climate. A peaceful stop on a Bay of Islands food trail.
review_summary: The subtropical garden setting is beautiful. The wines are interesting for their warm-climate character. Some find the restaurant hours limited.

name: Arai-te-Uru Recreation Reserve
short: Where the great navigator Kupe first set foot on Aotearoa — the traditional landing place at the Hokianga Heads.
commentary: Tradition holds that the explorer Kupe first landed in Aotearoa at the Hokianga Heads. The reserve sits on the wild, windswept northern head of the Hokianga Harbour entrance. A carved pou marks the spot. For Ngāpuhi and Te Roroa, this is among the most significant wāhi tapu in the country — the beginning point of Māori settlement of New Zealand.
review_summary: Visitors describe Arai-te-Uru Recreation Reserve as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where the great navigator Kupe first set foot on Aotearoa — the traditional landing place at the Hokianga Heads. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Bay of Islands / Pēwhairangi
short: 144 islands, early contact between Māori and Europeans, and some of the North Island's best sailing water.
commentary: The Bay of Islands has 144 islands within a 16 km radius of Paihia. In 1769 Cook's *Endeavour* anchored here and he gave the bay its English name. From around 1800 it became the main point of contact between Māori and European whalers, traders and missionaries — which is why Waitangi, Kerikeri, Russell and Paihia are all clustered on this one bay. Today it's a sailing, fishing, and tour-boat centre: Cape Brett / Hole in the Rock, Urupukapuka Island, swimming with dolphins (heavily regulated), and the passenger ferry between Paihia and Russell.
review_summary: Bay of Islands / Pēwhairangi draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 144 islands, early contact between Māori and Europeans, and some of the North Island's best sailing water. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Bay of Islands Parasailing
short: Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands.
commentary: Parasailing from Paihia lifts riders up to 400 feet above the Bay of Islands, revealing the full panorama of 144 subtropical islands. The calm, sheltered waters of the bay provide reliable conditions. Dolphin sightings from the air are common.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Paihia wharf, where a parasailing boat is ready to take you to a vantage point few people ever experience. As the boat accelerates across the Bay of Islands and the canopy fills with air, you will rise smoothly to 400 feet above the water. From this height, the full majesty of the Bay of Islands reveals itself, all 144 islands scattered across turquoise water like green jewels on blue silk. You might spot dolphins playing in the wake of passing boats, or see stingrays cruising the sandy shallows near the shore. The bay is one of the most historically significant places in New Zealand, it was here at Waitangi, visible to your right, that the Treaty was signed in 1840. The flight is surprisingly peaceful at altitude, just you and one of the most beautiful coastal landscapes on Earth.
review_summary: The Bay of Islands Parasailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Bay of Islands Scenic Route
short: Drive the coastal route linking Russell, Paihia, and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds.
commentary: The Bay of Islands scenic route connects Paihia, Russell (via car ferry), and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds. The bay contains 144 islands and is rich in both Maori and early colonial history. Russell was New Zealand's first capital and was once known as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific'.
audio_story: You are driving into the Bay of Islands, a shimmering expanse of 144 islands scattered across turquoise water. Start at the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, where on 6 February 1840 the founding document of New Zealand was signed between the British Crown and Maori chiefs. The beautifully carved meeting house here is one of the finest in the country. Take the car ferry across to Russell — New Zealand's first European capital, once so lawless it was nicknamed the 'hell-hole of the Pacific.' Today it is a genteel village of heritage buildings and waterfront restaurants. Christ Church, built in 1836, still has musket ball holes in its walls from the fighting of 1845. Back on the water, dolphins regularly swim alongside boats in these warm subtropical waters.
review_summary: The Bay of Islands Scenic Route route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the coastal route linking Russell, Paihia, and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking
short: Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands.
commentary: Sea kayaking in the Bay of Islands offers island hopping across sheltered subtropical waters. Multi-island tours visit Roberton Island (Motuarohia), Waewaetorea, and secluded beaches only accessible from the water. Dolphin encounters are common, with a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins.
audio_story: You have arrived at the launching point for a sea kayaking adventure through one of the most beautiful coastal environments in New Zealand. The Bay of Islands contains 144 islands spread across a vast harbour system, and a kayak is the perfect way to explore them at your own pace. Your route will take you across sheltered channels to islands with deserted white sand beaches, through narrow passages between rocky outcrops, and into secluded coves that no tour boat can reach. Keep your eyes on the water, this bay is home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins, and they are famously curious about kayakers. You might also spot penguins, stingrays, and eagle rays in the clear shallows. Roberton Island, with its twin lagoons visible from the hilltop, is a highlight that Captain Cook himself admired when he first visited in 1769.
review_summary: The Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Butler Point Whaling Museum
short: The 1843 home of Captain William Butler — a Pacific whaling captain who retired at Mangōnui — preserved with its original furniture and whaling artefacts.
commentary: William Butler was an English whaling captain who settled at Mangōnui in 1836 and built the Butler Point house in 1843. The house and its whaling museum preserve one of the best collections of Pacific whaling artefacts in the country.
audio_story: You have arrived at Butler Point, on the southern side of Mangōnui Harbour. Butler Point is where Captain William Butler, an English whaling captain, built a home and trading station in the eighteen thirties and forties after deciding Doubtless Bay was where he wanted to spend the second half of his life. Butler arrived in the Pacific whaling grounds in the eighteen twenties. By the mid eighteen thirties he had developed good trading relationships with Ngāti Kahu and was serving as a pilot, interpreter, and provisioner to the scores of whaling ships that called at Mangōnui. In eighteen forty three he built the house that now anchors the museum. The property has been kept in the Butler family for more than a century and a half, and is now open to visitors by appointment. The collection of whaling artefacts, the harpoons, the tryworks for boiling oil, the scrimshaw carved on sperm whale teeth, the personal letters of the captain, is one of the best collections of Pacific whaling material in the country. The setting is idyllic. A guided tour by a descendant of the captain is the standard experience. Call ahead to book.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Butler Point Whaling Museum is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The 1843 home of Captain William Butler — a Pacific whaling captain who retired at Mangōnui — preserved with its original furniture and whaling artefacts. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua
short: The leaping place of spirits — where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific and Māori tradition says souls begin their journey home.
commentary: Te Rerenga Wairua is one of the most sacred sites in Aotearoa. In Māori tradition this is where the spirits of the dead leave the land, climbing down the roots of an 800-year-old pōhutukawa tree to begin their journey back to Hawaiki. The 1941 lighthouse sits 165 m above the sea. Below, you can watch the Tasman and the Pacific visibly collide — two currents, two colours, one churn. Please: no food, no picnics on the headland. It is a tapu place. The walk from the carpark is short and easy.
review_summary: Visitors describe Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The leaping place of spirits — where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific and Māori tradition says souls begin their journey home. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Cape Reinga Lighthouse Walk
short: Walk to New Zealand's northernmost lighthouse where two oceans meet and spirits depart.
commentary: Cape Reinga (Te Rerenga Wairua) is at the northern tip of the Aupouri Peninsula. The walk to the lighthouse is a short 1 km return path from the car park. Below the cape, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean meet in visible turbulence. In Maori belief, the spirits of the dead travel here to depart for Hawaiki.
audio_story: You are walking towards the Cape Reinga lighthouse at the very tip of the North Island, and the atmosphere here is charged with something beyond the ordinary. In Maori belief, this is Te Rerenga Wairua — the leaping-off place of spirits. When a person dies, their spirit travels the length of New Zealand to this point, descends the roots of an ancient pohutukawa tree clinging to the headland, and departs for the ancestral homeland of Hawaiki. Below you, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean collide in a dramatic churning of currents visible from the clifftop. The lighthouse, built in 1941, stands on a narrow promontory 165 metres above the water. This is a sacred place — please treat it with respect and stay on the path. The sense of standing at the very edge of a land is powerful and unforgettable.
review_summary: The Cape Reinga Lighthouse Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to New Zealand's northernmost lighthouse where two oceans meet and spirits depart. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Charlotte's Kitchen
short: Bay of Islands waterfront dining — fresh local seafood and NZ wine overlooking Paihia wharf.
commentary: Charlotte's Kitchen occupies a prime waterfront spot in Paihia with views across the Bay of Islands. The menu majors on fresh local seafood — snapper, blue cod, green-lipped mussels and whatever's just off the boats — alongside NZ wines and craft beers. The sunset dinner on the terrace is a Bay of Islands highlight. Also does an excellent brunch. Book ahead in summer.
review_summary: The fresh seafood and views earn praise. A go-to for Bay of Islands dining. Can feel overrun with tourists in peak summer, but the food quality holds.

name: Christ Church, Russell
short: New Zealand's oldest surviving church — built 1836, with musket-ball holes in the timber walls from the 1845 sacking of Russell.
commentary: Built 1836 as an Anglican church and community building at Kororāreka. Charles Darwin donated to its construction during the Beagle's visit in December 1835. Holes in the walls are from the 1845 sacking.
audio_story: You have arrived at Christ Church in Russell, New Zealand's oldest surviving church. The timber walls you are looking at were raised in eighteen thirty six, when Kororāreka, as the town was then known, was the rough and notorious trading port of the South Pacific, full of whalers, deserters, and grog shops. The church was built as both a religious building and a community hall for the small settler population. Among the early donors to its construction was the young naturalist Charles Darwin, who visited Kororāreka in December eighteen thirty five aboard HMS Beagle and disapproved heartily of the settlement's debauchery. He gave, in his own words, a small sum toward the chapel. Look closely at the weatherboards on the outside of the building. Several of them show holes made by musket balls during the sacking of Kororāreka on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five. Hone Heke and Kawiti's men had orders not to damage the church, and for the most part they did not, but some stray fire hit the walls. The church is still a working parish. Walk quietly around the small cemetery. A surprising number of sailors and whalers rest here, including some killed in eighteen forty five.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Christ Church, Russell — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. New Zealand's oldest surviving church — built 1836, with musket-ball holes in the timber walls from the 1845 sacking of Russell. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Clendon House, Rawene
short: An 1860s house above the Hokianga, kept by Heritage NZ exactly as the Clendon family left it.
commentary: Built in the 1860s for James Reddy Clendon, U.S. Consul and one of the witnesses to the Treaty of Waitangi. His wife Jane Takotowi Cochrane, of Ngāpuhi descent, raised eight children here after his death in 1872 and lived in the house until 1919.
audio_story: You are standing in front of Clendon House, a wooden colonial home with a wide veranda and a view straight down the Hokianga Harbour. The house was built in the 1860s for James Reddy Clendon, a remarkable figure who was at one time the United States Consul in the Bay of Islands and who signed the Treaty of Waitangi as a witness on the morning of the sixth of February eighteen forty. He died at this house in 1872, leaving his much younger wife, Jane Takotowi Cochrane of Ngāpuhi descent, with eight children and the family debts. Jane stayed. She raised the children, ran the property, and lived in this house until 1919. Almost everything you will see inside, the books, the china, the dresses, the letters, was hers. Heritage New Zealand has cared for the house since the 1970s and has changed almost nothing. You are walking through a Victorian Māori-Pākehā family home that survived because one widow refused to let it go. Sit on the veranda before you leave. The harbour view has not changed in a century and a half.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Clendon House, Rawene — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. An 1860s house above the Hokianga, kept by Heritage NZ exactly as the Clendon family left it. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Clendon House, Rāwene
short: The home of James Clendon, US Consul and Treaty of Waitangi signatory — a 1860s Hokianga house preserved almost entirely unchanged.
commentary: Built 1866-68 for James Reddy Clendon, British ship owner who served as first US Consul to NZ and signed the Treaty of Waitangi. The house passed to his Māori wife Jane Takotowi Cochrane. Run by Heritage NZ.
audio_story: You have arrived at Clendon House in Rāwene on the southern shore of the Hokianga Harbour. The house was built between eighteen sixty six and eighteen sixty eight for James Reddy Clendon, one of the more remarkable figures of the early colonial period. Clendon was an English ship owner who had moved to Kororāreka in the eighteen thirties, befriended local Māori and chiefs, served as the first United States Consul in New Zealand, witnessed the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi in eighteen forty, and after losing his fortune moved his family to the quieter Hokianga. The house was built for his second wife, Jane Takotowi Cochrane, a rangatira of the local Te Māhurehure hapū, a generation younger than him. When James died in eighteen seventy two Jane inherited the house and raised their eight children here. She lived in the house for another fifty years. Because the property remained in the family until gifted to Heritage New Zealand in the nineteen seventies, an extraordinary quantity of the original furniture, papers, and personal items survive. Walking through Clendon House is the closest thing to stepping into an intact colonial home you will find in the country.
review_summary: Visitors describe Clendon House, Rāwene as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The home of James Clendon, US Consul and Treaty of Waitangi signatory — a 1860s Hokianga house preserved almost entirely unchanged. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Fat Camel Cafe
short: Whangarei waterfront café — authentic Israeli food, great coffee and a harbour setting.
commentary: Fat Camel brings Middle Eastern flavours to Whangarei's Town Basin waterfront. The pita pockets stuffed with shawarma meat, falafel and fresh salads are the stars, alongside authentic Israeli dishes and excellent coffee. The waterfront setting overlooking the marina is pleasant, and it's a great lunch stop between Auckland and the Bay of Islands. Family-friendly with generous portions.
review_summary: The Middle Eastern food is authentic and well-priced. The waterfront location adds to the experience. Some find parking at the Town Basin tricky on busy days.

name: First Anglican Church Site, Paihia
short: The site of Henry Williams' 1823 mission — where Samuel Marsden first preached in Aotearoa was nearby, and te reo Māori was first written down.
commentary: The third CMS mission station was established at Paihia by Henry Williams in 1823. The bilingual Williams family translated the Bible and the Treaty of Waitangi into te reo Māori here.
audio_story: You have arrived at the site of the first Paihia mission, established in eighteen twenty three by the Church Missionary Society under the redoubtable Henry Williams. The mission is no longer here in its original form, but interpretive boards on the waterfront set out the story. What happened at Paihia shaped the course of early colonial New Zealand in ways that are still being felt. Henry Williams and his brother William were fluent speakers of te reo Māori, and along with their wives they produced the first written Māori language grammars, the first te reo translation of the Bible, and, crucially, the Māori language text of the Treaty of Waitangi, which was largely drafted in a small timber house that once stood roughly where the current Anglican Church of St Paul now stands. The translation of the Treaty at Paihia, the choices Williams made in how to render English concepts such as sovereignty into a te reo framework that Māori could understand, have been debated and litigated for almost two centuries. Stand on the waterfront, look across at Waitangi just a few kilometres away, and let that weight sit on the landscape for a moment.
review_summary: Visitors describe First Anglican Church Site, Paihia as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The site of Henry Williams' 1823 mission — where Samuel Marsden first preached in Aotearoa was nearby, and te reo Māori was first written down. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Flagstaff Hill, Russell / Maiki
short: The hill above Russell where Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke famously cut down the British flagpole four times — sparking the Northern War.
commentary: Maiki Hill overlooks Russell. Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke cut down the British flagpole here in July 1844 and three more times over subsequent months, the fourth cutting on 11 March 1845 triggering open war.
audio_story: You have arrived at the top of Flagstaff Hill, known to Māori as Maiki, looking directly down over the little town of Russell and out across the Bay of Islands. This unassuming low hill was the site of one of the most symbolic acts of resistance in early colonial New Zealand. In July eighteen forty four, the Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke rode up here with an axe and cut down the British flagpole. He did it again. And again. And again. The fourth and final cutting on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five came as part of a coordinated attack on Russell itself that touched off what became known as the Northern War. For Heke, the flagpole was a symbol of the economic marginalisation of Ngāpuhi after the capital had moved south to Auckland, draining customs revenue and trade away from the Bay of Islands. Cutting it down was a calculated political act, not a vandalism. Today a flagpole stands here again, erected by Ngāpuhi themselves as part of the healing of the long relationship. Stand here, look down on the harbour, and imagine the sound of axe on wood in eighteen forty four.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Flagstaff Hill, Russell / Maiki — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The hill above Russell where Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke famously cut down the British flagpole four times — sparking the Northern War. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Hokianga Harbour / Ōpononi
short: The 'place of returning' — where the explorer Kupe is said to have left Aotearoa to sail back to Hawaiki.
commentary: The Hokianga is one of the origin places of Aotearoa. Tradition says the Polynesian navigator Kupe landed here, explored the country, and departed from this harbour — hence Te Hokianga-nui-a-Kupe, 'the great returning place of Kupe.' The huge bright sand dunes on the north head face the tiny town of Ōpononi across the water. In 1955–56 a wild dolphin called Opo played with swimmers in the shallows and briefly made Ōpononi the most visited place in New Zealand. There's a statue on the foreshore. The Rawene car ferry crosses the narrow middle of the harbour on SH12.
review_summary: Visitors describe Hokianga Harbour / Ōpononi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The 'place of returning' — where the explorer Kupe is said to have left Aotearoa to sail back to Hawaiki. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Hongi Hika Heritage Trail, Kerikeri
short: A heritage trail through the landscape of Hongi Hika — the Ngāpuhi chief who transformed NZ through the Musket Wars.
commentary: The trail around the Kerikeri Basin connects sites associated with Hongi Hika, one of the most significant figures in Māori history. Hongi visited England in 1820, met King George IV, and used gifts to acquire muskets that gave Ngāpuhi military dominance.
review_summary: Visitors describe Hongi Hika Heritage Trail, Kerikeri as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A heritage trail through the landscape of Hongi Hika — the Ngāpuhi chief who transformed NZ through the Musket Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Kai Iwi Lakes / Taharoa Domain
short: Three ancient dune lakes with white-sand bottoms and water so clear it looks tropical.
commentary: The Kai Iwi Lakes — Taharoa, Waikare and Kai Iwi — are windblown dune lakes, fed only by rainwater sitting on top of an impermeable iron-pan layer deep in the sand. The result is freshwater so clear it looks like it belongs in the Mediterranean, over a pure white quartz-sand lake bottom. They're estimated at 1.8 million years old. The Taharoa Domain has a campground, picnic areas, and sheltered swimming; Lake Taharoa is the biggest and warmest. The lakes are taonga to Te Roroa. Keep motorised craft out of the smaller lakes.
review_summary: Kai Iwi Lakes / Taharoa Domain draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Three ancient dune lakes with white-sand bottoms and water so clear it looks tropical. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Karikari Estate
short: New Zealand's northernmost winery — estate wines, vineyard restaurant and Karikari Peninsula views.
commentary: Karikari Estate is one of New Zealand's most remote wineries, perched on the Karikari Peninsula in the Far North. The warm subtropical climate produces distinctive wines — the Pinotage (a rarity in NZ) and Syrah are standouts. The vineyard restaurant serves fresh local produce with panoramic views. It's the kind of place you discover on a road trip and never forget. Call ahead to confirm opening hours.
review_summary: The remote location and unusual grape varieties create a memorable experience. The restaurant is good. Opening hours can be limited outside summer — check ahead.

name: Kauri Coast – Waipoua Forest Drive
short: Drive through ancient kauri forest on the Tasman coast, home to Tane Mahuta, lord of the forest.
commentary: State Highway 12 passes through Waipoua Forest, the largest remaining tract of mature kauri forest in New Zealand. The forest is home to Tane Mahuta, the largest known living kauri tree, estimated at 1,250–2,500 years old. Strict biosecurity measures protect the trees from kauri dieback disease.
audio_story: You are entering Waipoua Forest on State Highway 12, and the world transforms. Massive kauri trees tower above the canopy, their silver-grey trunks columnar and immense. These trees were ancient when the Treaty of Waitangi was signed. Pull over at the Tane Mahuta car park and walk five minutes to meet the lord of the forest — 51 metres tall, nearly 14 metres in girth, and somewhere between 1,250 and 2,500 years old. Stand beneath it and feel very, very small. Before you walk, you must use the boot-cleaning stations — kauri dieback disease is devastating these irreplaceable trees, and every visitor must help prevent its spread. A short drive further, Te Matua Ngahere, the father of the forest, has an even larger trunk. The Kauri Coast was once heavily logged, which makes this surviving forest all the more precious.
review_summary: The Kauri Coast – Waipoua Forest Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through ancient kauri forest on the Tasman coast, home to Tane Mahuta, lord of the forest. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Kawakawa Hundertwasser Toilets
short: The most photographed public toilets in New Zealand — designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser, 1999.
commentary: Friedensreich Hundertwasser moved to Kawakawa in 1975 and lived in the bush nearby for the rest of his life. In 1999 he designed the town's new public toilets as his gift back — mosaic tiles, bottle walls, curved organic lines, a grass roof, and his signature refusal of straight lines. They are still in use, still free, and genuinely remarkable. The Hundertwasser Memorial Park is a short walk away, and the town now has a Hundertwasser-themed cultural centre. If you stop, stop for thirty minutes, not two.
review_summary: The cultural experience at Kawakawa Hundertwasser Toilets is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The most photographed public toilets in New Zealand — designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser, 1999. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Kerikeri Mission Station
short: Kemp House (1821–22) is NZ's oldest surviving European building. The Stone Store next door is the oldest stone building.
commentary: Samuel Marsden established a Church Missionary Society station here in 1819 under the protection of Ngāpuhi chief Hongi Hika. Kemp House, a two-storey Georgian weatherboard built 1821–22, is the oldest European building still standing in New Zealand. The Stone Store beside it (1832–36) is the oldest stone building, and still operates as a shop. Across the river is Kororipo Pā — Hongi Hika's base, and the launching point of the early-1820s Musket Wars that reshaped the North Island. A 20-minute walk links the three sites.
sources_to_check: Heritage NZ — Kemp House, Stone Store
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Kerikeri Mission Station — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Kemp House (1821–22) is NZ's oldest surviving European building. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Kiwi Kai
short: Traditional Māori kai in Whangarei — hāngī, kina, fried bread, fish and chips at the Cameron Street shop.
commentary: Kiwi Kai on Cameron Street is a rare chance to try authentic Māori cuisine without booking a cultural experience. The hāngī (earth-oven cooked meat and vegetables), rewena bread (Māori sourdough), kina (sea urchin) and boil-up are all on the menu alongside excellent fish and chips. It's unpretentious — more takeaway shop than restaurant — but the food is the real deal and the prices are very reasonable.
review_summary: A unique opportunity to try authentic Māori kai. The hāngī and fried bread are highlights. Basic setting — this is about the food, not the ambiance.

name: Kororāreka / Russell
short: The first European capital of NZ — sacked by Hōne Heke in 1845 in the opening act of the Northern War.
commentary: Kororāreka was a raucous whaling port before Hōne Heke Pōkai chopped down the British flagstaff on Maiki Hill four times between 1844 and 1845, triggering the Northern War. The attack on 11 March 1845 destroyed much of the town. Today Russell is a quiet harbourside village, but the hillside pā terraces, Christ Church (NZ's oldest surviving church, still pocked with musket-ball holes), and the flagstaff replica on the hill all tell the story of the first armed resistance to Crown sovereignty.
review_summary: Visitors describe Kororāreka / Russell as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The first European capital of NZ — sacked by Hōne Heke in 1845 in the opening act of the Northern War. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Kororāreka Memorial to the Fallen
short: A simple stone memorial in Russell to the defenders and attackers of the 1845 sacking of Kororāreka — remembering both sides.
commentary: On 11 March 1845 Ngāpuhi forces under Kawiti, Hone Heke, and Pumuka attacked Kororāreka. The town was largely destroyed. Casualties on both sides. The memorial in the modern town acknowledges both Pākehā and Māori dead.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Kororāreka Memorial in the centre of modern Russell. The stone acknowledges the dead of both sides on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five, the day known to history as the sack of Kororāreka. On that morning, Ngāpuhi forces under three chiefs, Hone Heke, Kawiti, and Pumuka, attacked the settlement in a carefully coordinated three pronged assault. Most of the town was burned. The small British garrison was driven from its blockhouse. HMS Hazard in the harbour was disabled. The civilian population fled aboard American and British ships and were rescued to Auckland. Pumuka, the third chief in the attack, was killed in the fighting, as were several British marines and settlers. For Ngāpuhi, the sack was a calculated demonstration of military power in response to colonial economic policies that had devastated the Bay of Islands economy. For the settlers, it was a catastrophe. The Northern War that followed cost several hundred lives over the next ten months. The memorial was erected in the twentieth century to name the dead on both sides, not only the British casualties. A small but important piece of modern memorial work.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Kororāreka Memorial to the Fallen — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A simple stone memorial in Russell to the defenders and attackers of the 1845 sacking of Kororāreka — remembering both sides. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Kororipo Pā & Stone Store, Kerikeri
short: NZ's oldest standing European buildings — built under the protection of the chief Hongi Hika at his pā.
commentary: Kororipo Pā, overlooking the Kerikeri Basin, was the stronghold of Hongi Hika, the powerful Ngāpuhi chief who facilitated the missionaries' presence in exchange for access to European goods and firearms. The Stone Store (1836) and Mission House (1822) stand nearby as NZ's oldest surviving European buildings.
review_summary: Visitors describe Kororipo Pā & Stone Store, Kerikeri as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. NZ's oldest standing European buildings — built under the protection of the chief Hongi Hika at his pā. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Koutu Boulders, Hokianga
short: Spherical boulders bigger than the Moeraki ones, but almost no one knows they exist.
commentary: Hokianga's answer to Moeraki — large concretions exposed at low tide on the inner harbour shore at Koutu, several over 3m across. Access is across private land with permission expected.
audio_story: You are looking for the Koutu Boulders, and unlike their famous cousins on the Otago coast at Moeraki, almost no one has heard of these. The Koutu Boulders sit on the Hokianga Harbour shoreline, rounded grey concretions that grew slowly inside soft mudstone over millions of years. As the cliffs eroded, the boulders were released onto the beach. Some are over three metres across, larger than any of the Moeraki examples. They are best seen at low tide, and you will need to time your visit with the tide chart. Access is along a quiet road north of Opononi, then across a stretch of foreshore that crosses private land, so be respectful, take nothing, and follow any signs. While you are out here, take in the wider Hokianga, one of the most spiritually significant harbours in the country. According to Ngāpuhi tradition, this is where Kupe the navigator first arrived, and from where he eventually left these islands to return to Hawaiki. Stand on the sand, look at the boulders, look at the harbour, and consider the weight of all that history settling around you.
review_summary: Koutu Boulders, Hokianga draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Spherical boulders bigger than the Moeraki ones, but almost no one knows they exist. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: MĀHA Restaurant
short: Kerikeri's acclaimed Asian-influenced restaurant — subtropical gardens, local produce and creative cuisine.
commentary: Set in the lush subtropical gardens of Wharepuke, MĀHA delivers one of Northland's most refined dining experiences. The menu draws on Asian flavours and techniques, using locally sourced ingredients — think Kerikeri citrus, locally caught fish and subtropical produce. The garden setting is beautiful, and the BYO wine policy makes it great value for the quality. Booking is essential, especially in summer.
review_summary: Foodies call this Northland's best restaurant. The Asian-influenced dishes are creative and well-executed. The garden setting enhances the experience. BYO wine is a bonus.

name: Mangawhai Cliff Walk
short: Walk spectacular coastal cliffs with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands.
commentary: The Mangawhai Cliff Walk is a 5 km loop track along dramatic coastal cliffs north of Auckland. The track passes through pohutukawa forest and scrubland with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands. A circuit includes the beach return at low tide.
audio_story: You have arrived at the start of the Mangawhai Cliff Walk, one of the finest coastal walks in Northland and a trail that delivers dramatic scenery for relatively little effort. The 5-kilometre track follows the cliff edge above a wild, wind-sculpted coastline, with views stretching out to the Hen and Chicken Islands on the horizon. The path winds through groves of pohutukawa trees, New Zealand's iconic coastal native, which explode into crimson bloom every December. Along the way, you will pass above rocky coves where the ocean surges into sea caves, and across grassy headlands where the wind is fresh and the views are long. At the far end, the track descends to the beach, and at low tide you can walk back along the sand to complete a satisfying loop. Keep an eye on the ocean, this coast catches big swells from the northeast, and the waves breaking against the cliffs are mesmerising. The walk is free and well maintained, suitable for anyone with reasonable fitness.
review_summary: The Mangawhai Cliff Walk adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Walk spectacular coastal cliffs with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Mangōnui Fish Shop
short: Fish and chips on a wharf jutting into Doubtless Bay — voted New Zealand's best more times than anyone has bothered to count.
commentary: The Mangōnui Fish Shop is a green weatherboard building extending out over the harbour. Open since the 1940s; consistently rated among NZ's top fish and chip shops.
audio_story: You are at the Mangōnui Fish Shop, a small green weatherboard building that has been pushing out into the harbour on wooden piles for the better part of a century. The fish on your plate was, in many cases, swimming in this same bay yesterday. Mangōnui sits on Doubtless Bay, named by Captain Cook in seventeen sixty nine. Cook never actually entered the bay. From offshore he wrote in his journal that it was doubtless a bay, and the name stuck, slightly absurd and entirely permanent. The fish shop has been here since the 1940s, and the formula has not really changed. Order the snapper and chips. Take it outside to one of the wooden tables suspended over the water. Watch the gulls calculate their odds. If you are lucky a stingray will glide past below the deck. The chips are golden, the fish is fresh, the harbour is quiet, and the only argument any traveller has after eating here is whether this or some rival down south is the country's best. Sit a little longer than you planned. There is no hurry in Mangōnui.
review_summary: Mangōnui Fish Shop delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. Fish and chips on a wharf jutting into Doubtless Bay — voted New Zealand's best more times than anyone has bothered to count. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

name: Mangōnui Historic Precinct
short: A whaling and trading port of the 1830s preserved almost entirely — Doubtless Bay's timber-fronted main street tells a hundred and ninety years of story.
commentary: Mangōnui on Doubtless Bay was a major Pacific whaling port from the 1830s. The timber storefronts of the main street are the most intact surviving whaling-era commercial precinct in the country.
audio_story: You have arrived in Mangōnui on the southern shore of Doubtless Bay. In the eighteen thirties and forties this was one of the major whaling ports of the southern Pacific. Hundreds of American, British, French, and Australian whaling ships called here each year to reprovision, to trade with local Ngāti Kahu, and to repair before heading back out onto the Pacific whaling grounds. The main street of Mangōnui preserves more of that period, in continuous occupation, than almost anywhere else in the country. The timber storefronts with their deep verandas and false parapets, the old waterfront trading store, the stone wharf, all survive largely intact. The town's famous fish and chip shop, which claims to be the world's best, operates out of a building that was originally a customs house. Walk the length of the main street slowly. Read the Heritage New Zealand plaques on the buildings. Eat fish and chips on the wharf, watching the small fleet at rest. Mangōnui rewards an unhurried afternoon more than most New Zealand small towns. Then drive five minutes west to Butler Point for the whaling museum, which is the essential companion to the main street.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Mangōnui Historic Precinct — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A whaling and trading port of the 1830s preserved almost entirely — Doubtless Bay's timber-fronted main street tells a hundred and ninety years of story. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Mangungu Mission
short: The Wesleyan mission house where more chiefs signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi than anywhere else — 70 signatures on 12 February 1840.
commentary: Wesleyan mission at Hōreke on the southern Hokianga, established 1828. On 12 February 1840 Mangungu hosted the largest single Treaty of Waitangi signing event, with approximately 70 Hokianga chiefs signing Te Tiriti.
audio_story: You have arrived at Mangungu Mission House on the southern shore of the Hokianga Harbour. Mangungu is remarkable for one particular reason. On the twelfth of February eighteen forty, just six days after the Treaty of Waitangi was first signed at Waitangi Bay on the other coast, approximately seventy chiefs from the Hokianga iwi gathered here and added their signatures to Te Tiriti. It was the largest single signing event in the Treaty process. For decades the role of Mangungu in the Treaty's signing was under recognised, overshadowed by the more famous signing at Waitangi. That has changed. The mission house itself, built around eighteen thirty nine by Wesleyan Methodists, has been restored by Heritage New Zealand and is a beautiful example of colonial timber construction. The garden slopes down to the water. A small cemetery holds missionaries and early settlers. Every year on the twelfth of February, a commemoration is held here. It is quieter than the events at Waitangi Bay, but the people who come to this one understand exactly what happened at this place, and why it matters. Allow an hour. The drive down to Hōreke is itself lovely.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mangungu Mission as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The Wesleyan mission house where more chiefs signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi than anywhere else — 70 signatures on 12 February 1840. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mangungu Mission House
short: Where the largest single signing of Te Tiriti o Waitangi took place — 70 Hokianga chiefs signed here on 12 February 1840.
commentary: On 12 February 1840, just six days after the first signing at Waitangi, about 70 chiefs gathered on the grounds of the Wesleyan mission house at Mangungu to debate and sign Te Tiriti. It was the largest single signing event. The 1838 kauri mission house still stands on its hill above the Hokianga Harbour, managed by Heritage New Zealand.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mangungu Mission House as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where the largest single signing of Te Tiriti o Waitangi took place — 70 Hokianga chiefs signed here on 12 February 1840. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Marsden Estate Winery
short: Bay of Islands winery with a cellar door restaurant — Chardonnay and Pinot Gris in subtropical Northland.
commentary: Marsden Estate is the Bay of Islands' main winery, producing aromatic whites and Bordeaux-style reds in Northland's subtropical climate. The cellar door restaurant serves lunch among the vines with a menu built around local produce — Kerikeri avocados, fresh seafood, subtropical salads. The Black Rocks Chardonnay and Pinot Gris are the picks. A pleasant stop on the way between Kerikeri and Paihia.
review_summary: A pleasant surprise for visitors who don't expect wine in the Far North. The restaurant and setting are nice. Some note the wines don't match southern regions for complexity, but they're enjoyable.

name: Matauri Bay — Rainbow Warrior Memorial
short: The final resting place of the Rainbow Warrior — scuttled off Matauri Bay with Māori blessing after the 1985 French bombing.
commentary: After French agents bombed the Greenpeace ship Rainbow Warrior in Auckland Harbour in 1985, killing photographer Fernando Pereira, the refloated ship was scuttled off Matauri Bay in 1987 with the blessing of the local hapū Ngāti Kura. The wreck is now an artificial reef popular with divers.
review_summary: Visitors describe Matauri Bay — Rainbow Warrior Memorial as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The final resting place of the Rainbow Warrior — scuttled off Matauri Bay with Māori blessing after the 1985 French bombing. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Matauri Bay / Rainbow Warrior Memorial
short: The headland memorial to the Greenpeace ship *Rainbow Warrior*, bombed by French agents in 1985 and resting in the sea below.
commentary: On 10 July 1985 the Greenpeace flagship *Rainbow Warrior* was bombed in Auckland Harbour by agents of the French DGSE. Photographer Fernando Pereira was killed. After she was raised, iwi Ngāti Kura offered a final resting place: the ship was scuttled off the Cavalli Islands in December 1987 and is now one of New Zealand's best dive sites. A hilltop memorial above Matauri Bay — steel propellers and a carved stone — looks out over the exact water she lies in. The bay itself is one of the most beautiful on the east coast: white sand, clear water, almost no development.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Matauri Bay / Rainbow Warrior Memorial — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The headland memorial to the Greenpeace ship *Rainbow Warrior*, bombed by French agents in 1985 and resting in the sea below. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Moremonui Battlefield
short: Site of the 1807 battle where muskets were first used in Māori warfare — the opening shot of the Musket Wars.
commentary: Near present-day Dargaville, the 1807 Battle of Moremonui was the first known use of muskets in intertribal fighting. Ngāpuhi, armed with muskets acquired from European traders, attacked Ngāti Whātua — but were defeated despite their firepower. The battle set off an arms race that would engulf the entire country in the Musket Wars.
review_summary: Visitors describe Moremonui Battlefield as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Site of the 1807 battle where muskets were first used in Māori warfare — the opening shot of the Musket Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Motuarohia / Roberton Island — Cook's First Landing
short: The small island where James Cook first came ashore in the Bay of Islands in 1769 — the site now better known for its twin lagoons.
commentary: On 29 November 1769 Cook anchored off Motuarohia and came ashore with Banks and Solander. The island was the scene of a complex multi-canoe encounter with local Ngāpuhi. Now part of the Bay of Islands Maritime Park.
audio_story: You are looking at Motuarohia, sometimes called Roberton Island, sitting in the middle of the Bay of Islands. On the twenty ninth of November seventeen sixty nine, James Cook anchored the Endeavour in the lee of this island and came ashore with his naturalists Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander. It was the first European landing anywhere in what is now the Northland region, and one of the earliest meetings between Cook and Māori. The encounter was tense and nearly ended badly. A fleet of around three hundred warriors in several large waka surrounded the Endeavour's longboat in the shallows. Banks later wrote that Cook fired warning shots over their heads and the situation was defused by the bravery and skill of the Tahitian translator Tupaia, who was able to speak with the local Ngāpuhi in a mutually understandable dialect. Today Motuarohia is best known to visitors for its twin aquamarine lagoons on the northern side, reachable by boat tours from Paihia. A short walking track climbs to a lookout between the two bays with a spectacular view. Swim, snorkel, walk up to the lookout, and remember that this is where Cook first put a boot down in the north of the country.
sources_to_check: DOC — Bay of Islands Maritime Park
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Motuarohia / Roberton Island — Cook's First Landing — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The small island where James Cook first came ashore in the Bay of Islands in 1769 — the site now better known for its twin lagoons. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Ngawha Springs
short: Sixteen iwi-owned mineral pools in a swamp behind Kaikohe — the most untouristed hot pools in New Zealand.
commentary: Owned by the local hapū of Ngāpuhi (Te Uri o Hua), the Ngawha Springs complex is one of the few NZ geothermal fields outside the Taupō Volcanic Zone. Rustic, mineral-heavy waters in wooden pools.
audio_story: You have arrived at Ngawha, and you should set your expectations for something honest rather than something polished. There are sixteen pools here, each a different temperature, a different mineral profile, a different colour. Some are clear, some are inky black, some are sulphur green. The pools are owned by the local hapū of Ngāpuhi, who have used these waters for healing for centuries, and they have chosen to keep the place rustic. The boardwalks are wooden, the changing sheds are basic, and the experience is the experience. The geology here is unusual. Almost every other natural hot spring in the North Island sits in the Taupō Volcanic Zone, fed by the line of volcanoes from White Island to Ruapehu. Ngawha is hundreds of kilometres outside that zone, fed by an entirely different system of deep faults. The locals will tell you that each pool is good for a different ailment. The water smells of sulphur. Your skin will be soft for two days. Soak slowly, move from pool to pool, and try to think about nothing at all.
review_summary: Ngawha Springs draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Sixteen iwi-owned mineral pools in a swamp behind Kaikohe — the most untouristed hot pools in New Zealand. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ninety Mile Beach / Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē
short: It's 88 km long, not 90 miles, and it is a legal public highway — though rental cars are almost always banned.
commentary: Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē — 'the long beach of Tōhē' — runs 88 km up the west side of the Aupōuri Peninsula. The 'ninety mile' name comes from early missionaries who misjudged the distance their horses could travel in a day. It is gazetted as a public road, and tour buses drive it at low tide, but tides, quicksand and unmarked creek outlets have destroyed a lot of cars; nearly every rental agreement prohibits driving on it. See it from Te Paki Stream (boogie-board the giant sand dunes) or from the Scott Point lookout at the northern end.
sources_to_check: DOC — Te Paki / Ninety Mile Beach
Te Hiku o Te Ika — Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē board
review_summary: Ninety Mile Beach / Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. It's 88 km long, not 90 miles, and it is a legal public highway — though rental cars are almost always banned. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ninety Mile Beach Coastal Drive
short: Drive the length of Ninety Mile Beach on Northland's far western coast — actually 55 miles, but who is counting.
commentary: Ninety Mile Beach (Te Oneroa-a-Tohe) stretches 88 km along the Aupouri Peninsula. Despite its name, it is about 55 miles long. The beach is an official highway, though rental car insurance is typically void here. The Te Paki sand dunes at the northern end reach heights of 150 metres.
audio_story: You are on Ninety Mile Beach, and despite its name, it is actually about 55 miles — or 88 kilometres — of unbroken sand along Northland's Tasman coast. This beach is legally a highway, though check your rental car agreement because most insurers draw the line at driving on sand. The hard-packed sand near the waterline makes for surprisingly smooth driving at low tide. To your left, the Tasman Sea rolls in with a constant procession of waves. To your right, the dunes stretch inland. At the northern end, the Te Paki sand dunes rise to 150 metres — locals and visitors alike use boogie boards to slide down them. The Maori name for this beach is Te Oneroa-a-Tohe, and it was a traditional walking route to Cape Reinga. Allow three hours and check the tide tables before setting out.
review_summary: Actually 88km of unbroken west-coast beach — visitors are awed by the sheer scale and the sense of wildness. Driving on the beach is a unique experience most can only do on a guided tour due to insurance restrictions. The sand dunes at Te Paki are praised as a must-do add-on.

name: Okuratope Pā
short: One of the best-preserved fortified Māori settlements in Northland — complete terraces, ditches and palisade lines.
