In eighteen thirty five and thirty six the Ngāti Tama chief Te Puoho o te Rangi, operating from a base on the north shore of Cook Strait, led an ambitious raiding party all the way down the West Coast, through the Haast Pass, and into the eastern valleys of Otago and Southland, attacking Ngāi Tahu settlements as he went. His intention appears to have been to establish permanent Ngāti Tama settlement in southern Ngāi Tahu rohe. The raid reached Tuturau in the winter of eighteen thirty six. While Te Puoho rested his exhausted party here, the southern Ngāi Tahu chief Tūhawaiki gathered a counter attack. In the battle that followed at Tuturau, Te Puoho was killed and his war party was destroyed. The engagement ended the Musket Wars in the South Island, preserved Ngāi Tahu''s hold on Murihiku, and cemented Tūhawaiki as the most respected rangatira in the south. A memorial cairn on the roadside marks the battlefield. Stop and read the inscription. A quiet paddock that changed the political map of the South Island.
review_summary: Visitors describe Tuhawaiki Memorial, Tuturau as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Ngāi Tahu under Tūhawaiki ended Te Puoho''s 1836 raid — the southernmost engagement of the Musket Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara
short: A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds.
commentary: 266-hectare predator-free sanctuary off Stewart Island. Home to South Island saddleback, yellowhead, weka, kiwi, and many other species.
audio_story: You have caught the water taxi across Paterson Inlet from Stewart Island to Ulva Island, known to Māori as Te Wharawhara. Ulva is two hundred and sixty six hectares of ancient rimu, rātā and kāmahi forest, and it is, crucially, entirely free of rats, stoats, possums and cats. That small miracle has been achieved over decades of patient Department of Conservation work, and the result is one of the most extraordinary experiences in the country. Within minutes of stepping off the jetty, you will be surrounded by native birds that are rare or absent almost everywhere else. The South Island saddleback and the yellowhead, both nearly extinct a generation ago, are common here. Weka patter along the tracks, unconcerned by humans. Kākā screech in the canopy. Bellbirds and tūī fill the understory with song. There is a good chance of seeing a Stewart Island tokoeka kiwi in daylight, because the species here is partly diurnal. A network of well formed walking tracks loops across the island, usually taking three to four hours at a slow pace. Bring lunch. Take nothing but photographs. Brush your boots for any hidden seeds before you step ashore.
review_summary: The wildlife encounters at Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Waiau River Jet Boat - Fiordland
short: Jet boat the Waiau River through pristine Fiordland wilderness.
commentary: Jet boat tours on the upper Waiau River between Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri explore one of the most scenic river stretches in Fiordland. The short river connects the two major lakes and is flanked by dense native bush and mountain scenery. The calm sections contrast with thrilling high-speed manoeuvres.
audio_story: You have arrived at the banks of the upper Waiau River, a short but stunning stretch of water connecting Lake Te Anau to Lake Manapouri in the heart of Fiordland. Your jet boat will race along this pristine river, flanked by towering native bush and mountain scenery that has changed little in thousands of years. The Waiau is one of the most photogenic rivers in the South Island, its clear waters reflecting the beech forest and peaks above. Between the adrenaline of high-speed runs and 360-degree spins, your driver will slow down to point out native birdlife and share stories of the region's history. This river was once a vital travel route for Maori seeking pounamu on the West Coast. Today it's your express lane into the wilderness of Fiordland.
review_summary: The Waiau River Jet Boat - Fiordland adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Jet boat the Waiau River through pristine Fiordland wilderness. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Waiau River Scenic Road
short: Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast.
commentary: The road from Manapouri follows the Waiau River south through remote Southland farmland to Tuatapere. The Waiau drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri and is one of the most powerful rivers in Southland. The drive passes through a landscape of rolling green hills backed by the Takitimu Mountains and offers a glimpse of deep rural Southland.
audio_story: You are driving south along the Waiau River from Manapouri, and the road here takes you through Southland as few tourists see it. The Waiau is a big river — it drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri — and its green water moves with a steady, muscular current past farmland so quiet you can hear the birds from the car. The road follows the river valley between low hills, with the Takitimu Mountains occasionally appearing to the east. There are no towns for long stretches, just farms, sheep, and the kind of silence that takes getting used to. At Clifden, the historic suspension bridge — built in 1899 and now retired from vehicle use — is worth a stop. You can walk across it and feel it sway, imagining the horse-drawn carts that once crossed here when this was the main road south.
review_summary: The Waiau River Scenic Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Waipapa Point — Shipwreck & Settlement
short: The southernmost lighthouse in NZ — built after the Tararua shipwreck on a coast long known to Māori.
commentary: The coast around Waipapa Point was used by Māori for food gathering long before the 1881 Tararua shipwreck prompted the lighthouse. The area is rich in coastal archaeological sites.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waipapa Point — Shipwreck & Settlement as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The southernmost lighthouse in NZ — built after the Tararua shipwreck on a coast long known to Māori. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
short: A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster.
commentary: Built 1884 after the 1881 wreck of the SS Tararua, in which 131 people died. The lighthouse area is also a popular sea lion haul-out.
audio_story: You have arrived at Waipapa Point Lighthouse, a small white wooden tower on a low windswept headland at the south eastern corner of Southland. The lighthouse looks modest, almost apologetic. The reason it exists is anything but. On the twenty ninth of April eighteen eighty one, the steamship SS Tararua struck a reef just offshore here in the early hours of the morning. The ship broke up within a day. One hundred and thirty one people died, including women, children, and most of the crew. Only twenty survived. It remains the worst civilian maritime disaster in New Zealand's history. In the inquiry that followed, Waipapa Point was finally lit, and in eighteen eighty four the lighthouse you are looking at was built, one of the last remaining wooden lighthouses in the country. Today the keeper's cottage is gone, the light is automated, and the headland is a quiet sheep paddock. You may well find a sea lion sleeping on the short access path or on the rocks below. Give them a very wide berth, at least twenty metres, and never get between a sea lion and the sea. Stand at the lighthouse, look out at the reef, and consider the names on the memorial stone.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipapa Point Lighthouse — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
short: Where the 1881 wreck of the Tararua killed 131 people — the worst civilian shipwreck in NZ waters.
commentary: The SS Tararua wrecked on Waipapa reef in April 1881 with 131 lives lost. The 1884 lighthouse was the direct result.
audio_story: You are at Waipapa Point, a dune headland on the Catlins coast east of Bluff, and at the small eighteen eighty four wooden lighthouse that stands on the headland. The lighthouse exists because on the night of the twenty ninth of April, eighteen eighty one, the passenger steamer SS Tararua ran directly onto Waipapa Reef in a storm. Over one hundred and thirty of the one hundred and fifty one passengers and crew on board drowned. It remains, in terms of civilian life, the worst shipwreck to have occurred in New Zealand waters. The public outcry at the loss led directly to the construction of the lighthouse here three years later, and to improvements in lighthouses and coastal signalling throughout the country. The lighthouse tower, built entirely from kauri timber prefabricated in Auckland and shipped down, is one of the few surviving wooden lighthouse towers in New Zealand. Sea lions, Phocarctos hookeri, frequently haul out on the beach below the headland and can be a hazard for walkers approaching through the dunes. Keep a minimum distance of twenty metres. The small memorial at the lighthouse carries the names of those lost on the Tararua. The wreck itself lies in around eight metres of water just offshore and is occasionally visited by divers.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipapa Point Lighthouse — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where the 1881 wreck of the Tararua killed 131 people — the worst civilian shipwreck in NZ waters. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Whenua Hou / Codfish Island
short: A predator-free kākāpō sanctuary — co-managed by Ngāi Tahu and DOC.
commentary: Whenua Hou is one of the primary breeding sites for kākāpō. For Ngāi Tahu, the bird is a taonga species and their involvement in recovery is a deep cultural commitment.
review_summary: The wildlife encounters at Whenua Hou / Codfish Island earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free kākāpō sanctuary — co-managed by Ngāi Tahu and DOC. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Anaura Bay & Cook's Cove Walkway
short: Where Captain Cook's crew was warmly welcomed in 1769 — a beautiful bay and the short walk to the hidden cove where the Endeavour took on water.
commentary: Cook landed at Anaura Bay on 21 October 1769 and was greeted hospitably. He moved on briefly to the more sheltered Cook's Cove (Tolaga Bay area), which is now a 5.8km return walk.
audio_story: You have arrived at Anaura Bay, a wide perfect crescent of sand on the East Coast, and one of the more historically significant beaches you will probably have ever to yourself. On the twenty first of October seventeen sixty nine, Captain Cook landed here and was met not with violence but with hospitality. Local Māori brought sweet potato and water aboard the Endeavour, and Cook recorded the cleanliness and order of the cultivated gardens with what reads, between the lines of his journal, as something close to admiration. The wind shifted, his anchor dragged, and he moved south to the more sheltered Cook's Cove the next day. From the southern end of the beach, a beautiful walking track climbs across farmland and through bush to the lookout above Cook's Cove itself. The walk is around two and a half hours return. It ends at a wooden viewing platform looking down on the small green bay where the Endeavour rode at anchor for six days while Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander botanised the surrounding hills. Few historic walks in this country put you so completely in the place where so much else began.
review_summary: Anaura Bay & Cook's Cove Walkway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Where Captain Cook's crew was warmly welcomed in 1769 — a beautiful bay and the short walk to the hidden cove where the Endeavour took on water. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Bushmere Estate Winery
short: Gisborne's premier winery — estate Chardonnay, fine dining at The Vines and vineyard views.
commentary: Bushmere Estate is the jewel of Gisborne's wine scene. The family-owned 17-hectare vineyard specialises in Chardonnay — fitting for a region known as New Zealand's Chardonnay capital — and the on-site restaurant, The Vines, serves elegant dishes paired with estate wines. The Merlot, Malbec and Pinot Gris are also excellent. The peaceful vineyard setting is a world away from the city.
review_summary: The Chardonnay is consistently praised as one of Gisborne's best. The Vines restaurant is excellent. Some find the location a drive from central Gisborne.

name: Cooks Cove, Tolaga Bay
short: Where Cook and Banks came ashore for six days in October 1769 — the first peaceable encounter of the Endeavour voyage.
commentary: After the violent encounter at Gisborne, Cook anchored at Tolaga Bay from 23 to 29 October 1769 and traded peacefully with Te Aitanga-a-Hauiti. The Cooks Cove walkway (2 hours return) leads to the landing.
audio_story: You are at Cooks Cove, the small sheltered bay at the southern end of Tolaga Bay. After the violent first encounters at Tūranganui a Kiwa further south, Cook sailed the Endeavour north and anchored here on the twenty third of October, seventeen sixty nine. He and his crew stayed for six days. The encounter with the local iwi, Te Aitanga a Hauiti, was peaceful. Water was taken on from the cove stream, the botanists Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander collected plant specimens from the coastal forest, and the astronomer Charles Green made observations from shore. In return Cook gave cloth, nails and iron tools. It was the first peaceful encounter of the Endeavour voyage with Māori, and some of the most detailed journal descriptions of early Māori life in Tairāwhiti come from the week spent here. The walkway to the cove from Tolaga Bay beach takes about two hours return. The path climbs through farm paddocks, drops into the sheltered cove, and finishes at a small beach where a natural rock arch called the Hole in the Wall is visible just offshore. The old watering hole is still there. The landscape is almost unchanged. Stand at the tideline and imagine the Endeavour at anchor in the bay.
review_summary: Cooks Cove, Tolaga Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Where Cook and Banks came ashore for six days in October 1769 — the first peaceable encounter of the Endeavour voyage. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: East Cape Lighthouse
short: The easternmost point of mainland NZ — 700 steps to the first sunrise, on Ngāti Porou ancestral land.
commentary: The lighthouse stands where the first rays of each day's sun touch the land. The 700-step climb rewards with vast ocean views. The East Coast is one of NZ's most culturally rich Māori regions.
review_summary: Visitors describe East Cape Lighthouse as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The easternmost point of mainland NZ — 700 steps to the first sunrise, on Ngāti Porou ancestral land. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: East Cape Lighthouse / Whangaokeno
short: The easternmost lighthouse in the world — ~700 steps up a hill, and most of the year, the first place on Earth the sun touches.
commentary: The original light was built in 1900 on East Island (Whangaokeno), a small unstable rocky island offshore — landslides threatened the keepers' cottages and the whole operation was moved to this mainland hilltop in 1922. Three lighthouse keepers lived here until it was automated in 1985. The ~700-step climb is steep but short. On a clear winter dawn this is the first land on the planet to see the sun — although Kiribati's 1995 dateline shift technically gave Caroline Island the first new day. Geography is on our side; calendars aren't. Bring a torch if you're going for dawn, and more layers than you think.
review_summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at East Cape Lighthouse / Whangaokeno — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The easternmost lighthouse in the world — ~700 steps up a hill, and most of the year, the first place on Earth the sun touches. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum
short: The National Arboretum of New Zealand — one man's 80-year obsession, now 135 hectares of Northern Hemisphere trees.
commentary: William Douglas Cook bought this Ngātapa farm in 1910 and spent the rest of his life — until his death in 1967 — planting it. His logic: Europe's forests were being destroyed by war and industrial logging, and someone needed to preserve them. He imported thousands of species of trees, shrubs and climbers, laid out 25 km of paths, and built the extraordinary spring-flowering oak and magnolia collections for which Eastwoodhill is now famous. In 1975 Parliament declared it the National Arboretum of New Zealand — the country's largest collection of Northern Hemisphere woody plants, now ~135 ha with ~4,000 species. Best in spring (cherries, magnolias) and autumn (maples, oaks). 45 minutes west of Gisborne. Entry fee.
review_summary: Eastwoodhill Arboretum draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The National Arboretum of New Zealand — one man's 80-year obsession, now 135 hectares of Northern Hemisphere trees. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Gisborne Deep Sea Fishing Charter
short: Fish for marlin, tuna, and mahi mahi on the productive Gisborne coast.
commentary: Gisborne's offshore waters offer excellent big game fishing, with blue and striped marlin, yellowfin tuna, and mahi mahi available in the warmer months. The continental shelf sits relatively close to shore, making for shorter runs to productive deep water.
audio_story: You have arrived at Gisborne's inner harbour, where a charter boat is rigged and ready for a day on one of the North Island's most productive fishing grounds. The beauty of Gisborne's location is that the continental shelf sits remarkably close to shore, so you do not spend hours getting to the action. Within 30 to 40 minutes of leaving the harbour, you could be over deep water where blue and striped marlin cruise from January through April. Yellowfin tuna, mahi mahi, and albacore are also on the menu, along with bottom species like hapuku and bluenose for those who prefer to fish deep. Your skipper knows these waters intimately, reading the temperature breaks and current lines where the fish congregate. The East Cape coastline is stunning from the water, and there is nothing quite like fighting a big fish with the first sunrise in the world at your back.
review_summary: The Gisborne Deep Sea Fishing Charter adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish for marlin, tuna, and mahi mahi on the productive Gisborne coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Gisborne Historical Walking Trail
short: Walk through Gisborne's history from Cook's 1769 landing site to the meeting of three rivers.
commentary: Gisborne stands at the site where Captain Cook first set foot on New Zealand on 9 October 1769. The historical trail passes Kaiti Hill with its Cook statue, the Titirangi reserve, and the confluence of the Waimata, Taruheru, and Turanganui rivers. The city is also significant as a centre of Maori culture and identity.
audio_story: You are walking through Gisborne, the first place Captain James Cook set foot in New Zealand on 9 October 1769. Climb Kaiti Hill — Titirangi — for the panoramic view Cook himself would have seen from the Endeavour: a wide bay backed by fertile plains, with three rivers converging at their mouth. The landing did not go well — a misunderstanding led to violence, and Cook named the area Poverty Bay because he could not obtain the supplies he needed. The name has been a source of grievance ever since, and there are ongoing discussions about reverting to the Maori name Turanganui-a-Kiwa. At the base of the hill, the Tairawhiti Museum tells the complex story of both Maori and Pakeha history in the region. Gisborne is the first city in the world to see the sunrise each day, and on New Year's Eve, thousands gather here to welcome the new year before anyone else on the planet. Walk along the riverbank to where the three rivers meet — it is a beautiful, contemplative spot in the heart of town.
review_summary: The Gisborne Historical Walking Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through Gisborne's history from Cook's 1769 landing site to the meeting of three rivers. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Kaiti Hill / Titirangi — First Contact Site
short: Where Māori and Europeans first met — Cook landed at the foot of this pā hill in October 1769.
commentary: On 8 October 1769, Cook's Endeavour anchored here and the first meetings between Māori and Europeans took place — tragically, several Māori were shot. The summit pā terraces are still visible.
review_summary: Visitors describe Kaiti Hill / Titirangi — First Contact Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Māori and Europeans first met — Cook landed at the foot of this pā hill in October 1769. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Māhia Peninsula — Rongomaiwahine Rohe
short: The ancestral rohe of Rongomaiwahine — the tūpuna wahine after whom the iwi of Māhia is named.
commentary: Māhia is the home of Rongomaiwahine iwi, named for a famous ancestress who married Kahungunu and then left him. The peninsula has been occupied for over 700 years.
audio_story: You are driving onto the Māhia Peninsula, a rugged thumb of land jutting into the Pacific between Poverty Bay and Hawkes Bay. This is the rohe of Rongomaiwahine, an iwi named for one of the most famous ancestresses in Māori tradition. Rongomaiwahine was the senior wife of the great rangatira Kahungunu, but she is said to have left him because of his womanising and returned here, to her own lands, where she raised their children and maintained her mana in her own right. Her descendants have held this peninsula ever since. Māhia has been settled for over seven hundred years. There are pā sites on almost every headland, the remnants of an unbroken occupation by people who lived from the sea and from the kumara gardens of the sheltered eastern shore. In the twentieth century the peninsula became quietly famous for its surf beaches and its near total resistance to the worst kinds of tourism development. More recently, at the very southern tip of the peninsula, the Rocket Lab launch complex has put Māhia on maps of a different kind, firing small rockets to orbit from what is still essentially farmland. Old and new, in a single landscape.
review_summary: Visitors describe Māhia Peninsula — Rongomaiwahine Rohe as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The ancestral rohe of Rongomaiwahine — the tūpuna wahine after whom the iwi of Māhia is named. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mahia Peninsula Coastal Loop
short: Drive the loop around Mahia Peninsula, where surf beaches meet a modern rocket launch site.
commentary: Mahia Peninsula is a remote coastal area south of Gisborne known for excellent surf breaks and rugged scenery. Rocket Lab's Launch Complex 1, the world's first private orbital launch site, is located at the tip of the peninsula. The area has significant cultural importance to Rongomaiwahine iwi.
audio_story: You are driving the loop road around Mahia Peninsula, where the ancient and the futuristic exist side by side. This rugged headland juts into the Pacific, its eastern cliffs battered by ocean swells that wrap around to create excellent surf breaks at Opoutama and Mahia Beach. The peninsula is the rohe — traditional territory — of Rongomaiwahine iwi, and the cultural connection to this land runs deep. Then, at the peninsula's tip, you encounter something unexpected: Rocket Lab's Launch Complex 1, the world's first private orbital launch site. Since 2017, Electron rockets have been launched from this remote New Zealand headland into orbit, delivering satellites for clients worldwide. If you time your visit during a launch window, you might witness a rocket arcing into space from this pastoral coastline — a surreal juxtaposition. The beaches here are uncrowded, the fishing is excellent, and the drive itself offers sweeping views of Hawke Bay to the south.
review_summary: The Mahia Peninsula Coastal Loop route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the loop around Mahia Peninsula, where surf beaches meet a modern rocket launch site. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mahia Peninsula Sea Kayaking
short: Kayak the sheltered bays of Mahia Peninsula among seals, penguins, and reef fish.
commentary: The Mahia Peninsula offers sheltered sea kayaking in a remote and beautiful setting. Multiple launch points access rocky coastline, sandy beaches, and reef systems. The peninsula is home to fur seal colonies, little blue penguins, and diverse marine life.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Mahia Peninsula, a remote and spectacularly beautiful headland that juts into the Pacific between Hawke's Bay and Poverty Bay. Sea kayaking here is an exercise in coastal discovery, paddling between rocky headlands and into sheltered coves that feel like they belong to another era entirely. The peninsula's reefs are rich with marine life, and the clear water lets you watch fish darting below your kayak as you paddle. Fur seals haul out on the rocks at the peninsula's tip, and little blue penguins nest along the coastline. Mahia is also the home of Rocket Lab's launch facility, so you might spot the launch pad on the eastern side. The waters here are sheltered from the prevailing swell on the harbour side, making conditions manageable even for intermediate paddlers. Bring your own kayak or arrange hire through local operators. This is East Coast exploration at its most peaceful.
review_summary: The Mahia Peninsula Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak the sheltered bays of Mahia Peninsula among seals, penguins, and reef fish. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Manutūkē — Carved Churches of the East Coast
short: A cluster of elaborately carved Māori churches — blending Christian faith with traditional carving arts.
commentary: The East Coast has the finest concentration of carved Māori churches in the country. Manutūkē's churches show the creative fusion of Christianity and Māori art that Apirana Ngata championed.
review_summary: Visitors describe Manutūkē — Carved Churches of the East Coast as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A cluster of elaborately carved Māori churches — blending Christian faith with traditional carving arts. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Manutuke Churches — Toko Toru Tapu & Te Mana o Turanga
short: Two elaborately carved Māori churches — among the finest fusions of Māori and Christian architecture in New Zealand.
commentary: Toko Toru Tapu (Holy Trinity) was built 1913, carved by masters of Rongowhakaata. The older Te Mana o Turanga whare nearby dates from 1883.
audio_story: You have arrived at Manutuke, a small settlement south of Gisborne that contains one of the most remarkable concentrations of Māori carving in the country. The Anglican church of Toko Toru Tapu, the Holy Trinity, was completed in nineteen thirteen. Unlike almost any other church in New Zealand, its interior was entirely carved in the traditional manner by master carvers of Rongowhakaata, with painted tukutuku panels, woven kōwhaiwhai rafters, and poupou of ancestors standing along the walls. It is a Christian church, but one in which the architecture speaks in the visual grammar of the wharenui. Close by stands the wharenui Te Mana o Turanga, built in eighteen eighty three, which is considered one of the finest nineteenth century carved houses to survive in Aotearoa. The house is a living building of Rongowhakaata, not open for casual visits, but can sometimes be arranged through the iwi for interested visitors. Toko Toru Tapu is generally open to respectful visitors. Step quietly inside, take your hat off, and sit for a few minutes in the pews. You are sitting inside a work of art that took generations of skill to bring into being, and that continues to serve the community it was built for.
review_summary: Visitors describe Manutuke Churches — Toko Toru Tapu & Te Mana o Turanga as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Two elaborately carved Māori churches — among the finest fusions of Māori and Christian architecture in New Zealand. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Maraehako Bay & Te Araroa Pōhutukawa
short: The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the country — over 600 years old, with a 40m crown.
commentary: Te Waha-o-Rerekohu in Te Araroa schoolground is reputed to be the largest pōhutukawa in NZ.
audio_story: You have arrived in the small East Cape village of Te Araroa, and behind the local school stands a tree that, by most reasonable measures, is the largest and oldest pōhutukawa in New Zealand. It is called Te Waha-o-Rerekohu, the mouth of Rerekohu, named for an ancestor of Ngāti Porou. The tree is reputed to be more than six hundred years old. Its trunk has divided into many great limbs that lean out and back to the ground over a crown forty metres across. In summer when it flowers, the whole village glows red beneath it. There is no fence, no entry fee, no gift shop. You simply walk across the schoolground, when school is not in session, and stand under the canopy. Te Araroa itself sits in a long curved bay on the wild northeast coast. From here it is another twenty kilometres on a winding sealed road to East Cape Lighthouse, the easternmost point of mainland New Zealand and one of the first places in the world to see the new day's sun. The village is also the start of the Te Araroa Trail, the country's longest hiking route. Spend a few minutes in the shade of the tree before you go.
review_summary: Visitors describe Maraehako Bay & Te Araroa Pōhutukawa as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the country — over 600 years old, with a 40m crown. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Marina Restaurant
short: Gisborne's harbour-side dining — consistently excellent seafood and NZ wines with marina views.
commentary: The Marina Restaurant sits between the two rivers at Gisborne's inner harbour, serving consistently excellent food with marina views. The seafood focus reflects Gisborne's coastal character — fresh local fish, green-lipped mussels and crayfish when available. The wine list leans into Gisborne Chardonnay (naturally), and the service is warm and professional. It's where Gisborne celebrates special occasions.
review_summary: Gisborne's go-to for a quality meal. The seafood is fresh and well-prepared. Consistently good reviews from both locals and visitors.

name: Matawhero
short: Site of the November 1868 attack by Te Kooti and his Ringatū followers — a night that left around 60 dead and still shadows the district.
commentary: On 10 November 1868, after escaping the Chathams, Te Kooti led a raid on Matawhero. Around 60 people were killed, including settlers and Māori not aligned with Ringatū. The church at Matawhero (1862) survives.
audio_story: You are at Matawhero, a small rural settlement a few kilometres west of Gisborne. On the night of the tenth of November, eighteen sixty eight, Te Kooti and a party of his Ringatū followers, recently escaped from imprisonment on the Chatham Islands, descended on this district. Over the course of that night and the following day, around sixty people were killed. The dead included colonial settler families, Māori who were not aligned with Te Kooti, and at least one magistrate whom Te Kooti held personally responsible for his unjust imprisonment. It is remembered in local settler history as the Matawhero Massacre, though the fuller picture includes the years of wrongful detention that preceded it and the cycles of colonial violence that followed. The small wooden Matawhero Church, built in eighteen sixty two, was the only structure the raiders deliberately spared on that night and still stands on its original site today. It is one of the oldest Anglican churches in the country. Stop at the church, read the memorial to those who died, and sit for a moment in the quiet of the old wooden interior. This is painful ground, carefully tended.
review_summary: Visitors describe Matawhero as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Site of the November 1868 attack by Te Kooti and his Ringatū followers — a night that left around 60 dead and still shadows the district. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Matawhero Wines
short: Boutique Gisborne winery among vines and olive trees — dry-farmed wines and gourmet platters.
commentary: Just 10 minutes south of Gisborne, Matawhero offers wine tasting among vines and olive trees. The wines are dry-farmed (no irrigation), which concentrates the flavours — the Chardonnay and Gewürztraminer are particularly rich and intense. Gourmet platters pair beautifully with the wines, and the relaxed, rural setting is a peaceful escape. One of the pioneers of Gisborne's modern wine industry.
review_summary: The dry-farmed wines have real character. The platters and setting are lovely. A smaller operation than some visitors expect.

name: Morere Hot Springs
short: Hot saline pools in the middle of a nikau-palm rainforest — steamy water that started the journey as ancient seawater, now warmed underground.
commentary: Morere sits in a 364 ha scenic reserve on SH2 between Wairoa and Gisborne, protecting a rare coastal stand of nikau-palm rainforest. The pools are filled from a hot spring whose water originated as 10-million-year-old seawater trapped underground, now heated to 60 °C — it still tastes faintly salty. The reserve has four public pools (two cool, two hot) and private tubs for hire; the hottest pools sit right under the nikau canopy. A short forest walk through the reserve passes giant puriri and towering tawa. Small fee. 1 hour south of Gisborne, a useful stop on the SH2 run to Napier. Cafe at the entrance.
review_summary: Morere Hot Springs draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Hot saline pools in the middle of a nikau-palm rainforest — steamy water that started the journey as ancient seawater, now warmed underground. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Motu River White Water Rafting
short: Raft the remote Motu River through Grade 4 rapids and untouched wilderness gorges.
commentary: The Motu River is one of the last unmodified river systems in the North Island, offering multi-day rafting through remote gorge country with Grade 3-4 rapids. The river flows through native bush with no road access, making it a true wilderness experience. Trips typically run over 2-4 days.
audio_story: You are about to embark on one of the North Island's great wilderness rafting journeys, the Motu River. This is one of the last unmodified river systems in the region, flowing through deep gorges of native bush with no roads, no bridges, and no cellphone reception for the entire journey. Over the next few days, you will navigate Grade 3 and 4 rapids with names that hint at what is ahead, and between the white water, you will drift through pools of astonishing clarity where the bush comes right down to the water's edge. The Motu was the subject of one of New Zealand's most famous conservation battles in the 1980s, when it was saved from a proposed hydro dam. Camp each night on riverside terraces, cook over a fire, and fall asleep to the sound of morepork calling from the bush. This is not a theme park adventure, it is the real thing, genuinely remote and utterly unforgettable.
review_summary: The Motu River White Water Rafting adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Raft the remote Motu River through Grade 4 rapids and untouched wilderness gorges. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Motu Trails – Great Ride
short: Cycle the Motu Trails, linking Opotiki to the East Cape through native bush, coastal cliffs, and remote beaches.
commentary: The Motu Trails are a NZ Cycle Trail Great Ride featuring three distinct sections: the Dunes Trail along the coast, the Motu Road Trail through remote bush, and the Pakihi Track through native forest. The trails connect Opotiki with the East Cape and range from easy coastal riding to challenging backcountry.
audio_story: You are riding the Motu Trails, one of the more remote and rewarding Great Rides in the New Zealand Cycle Trail network. The route links three distinct sections, each with its own character. The Dunes Trail follows the coast from Opotiki along wild, wind-swept beaches and through coastal forest — easy riding with the sound of surf alongside. The Motu Road Trail heads inland along a remote gravel road through the Motu River valley, one of the most pristine river systems in the North Island. The Pakihi Track is the challenging section — native bush singletrack with climbs and descents through dense podocarp forest. The Motu River itself is famous for whitewater rafting, and the surrounding bush is home to whio — blue duck — one of New Zealand's rarest birds. This ride is for those who want to experience a genuinely wild part of New Zealand that few tourists ever reach. Come prepared with food and tools — services are scarce out here.
review_summary: The Motu Trails – Great Ride route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle the Motu Trails, linking Opotiki to the East Cape through native bush, coastal cliffs, and remote beaches. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mount Hikurangi
short: Sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou — the first place on mainland NZ to see the sunrise.
commentary: Mount Hikurangi (1,752m) is where Māui's waka was turned to stone. Whakairo on the summit represent the crew. Access requires permission from Ngāti Porou — the pre-dawn climb to catch first light is unforgettable.
review_summary: Visitors describe Mount Hikurangi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou — the first place on mainland NZ to see the sunrise. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mt Hikurangi
short: The first point on the North Island to see each sunrise — the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou, with nine massive carved pou near the summit.
commentary: Mt Hikurangi (1,752 m) is the highest non-volcanic mountain on the North Island and the first point on the mainland to receive each new day's light. For Ngāti Porou the mountain is an ancestor: in their tradition it is the prow of Māui's waka, which fished up the North Island. On the ridges below the summit stand nine 2-metre-tall carved wooden pou depicting Māui and his whānau — installed in 1999. Access is across Ngāti Porou farmland; a 4WD guided tour from Pakihiroa Station to the summit (or to the carvings, much lower) is the standard way. Unguided hiking requires a permit through Te Rūnanganui o Ngāti Porou. Weather is brutal — check forecasts, dress for worse than you expect.
sources_to_check: Te Rūnanganui o Ngāti Porou — ngatiporou.com
review_summary: Visitors describe Mt Hikurangi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The first point on the North Island to see each sunrise — the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou, with nine massive carved pou near the summit. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site
short: Where Cook landed on 9 October 1769, where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was killed, and where Māia had already built a pā generations earlier.
commentary: Cook's first New Zealand landfall was on 9 October 1769, at the mouth of the Tūranganui River. Nine Māori were killed across the first two days of contact, the first of them the Ngāti Oneone rangatira Te Maro. Cook, unable to secure supplies, named the place 'Poverty Bay' and sailed south. The older name — Tūranganui-a-Kiwa, 'the great standing place of Kīwa' — records the much earlier arrival of the Horouta waka captained by Kīwa, and of Māia, who built a pā here called Puhi Kai Iti. The 1906 Cook obelisk is still on site; since Tuia 250 (2019) the reserve has been reinterpreted with iwi-carved pou and signage that tells both stories. A short climb up Titirangi / Kaiti Hill brings you to the Ruatanuika lookout and a memorial to Te Maro.
sources_to_check: DOC — Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing National Historic Reserve
Wikipedia — Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site
review_summary: Visitors describe Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Cook landed on 9 October 1769, where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was killed, and where Māia had already built a pā generations earlier. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rere Falls & Rere Rockslide
short: A 10-metre wide waterfall, and a 60-metre natural rock waterslide downstream — both free, both on the same river.
commentary: The Wharekopae River west of Gisborne gives you two remarkable free swim spots within 5 km of each other. Rere Falls is a 10 m wide, 5 m high curtain waterfall — you can walk behind it. A few minutes further upriver is the Rere Rockslide: a 60 m long smooth rock slab angled gently enough that you can slide down it on a boogie board, an inflatable, or just in shorts, into a deep pool at the bottom. Locals have been using it for generations. No charge, no lifeguard, no facilities — bring a boogie board or thick shorts (nothing white, nothing loose), a towel, and check the flow is safe. 45 minutes west of Gisborne on the way to Eastwoodhill.
review_summary: Rere Falls & Rere Rockslide draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 10-metre wide waterfall, and a 60-metre natural rock waterslide downstream — both free, both on the same river. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Rere Falls and Rockslide Road Trip
short: Drive inland from Gisborne to Rere Falls and the natural 60-metre rockslide — bring a boogie board.
commentary: Rere Falls is a 5-metre-wide waterfall about 50 km northwest of Gisborne. Nearby, the Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock water slide where locals ride boogie boards down a smooth rock face into a pool. The drive through the Wharekopae Valley follows a scenic back road through farmland.
audio_story: You are driving inland from Gisborne through the Wharekopae Valley on a back road that delivers two of the Tairawhiti region's best-kept secrets. First, Rere Falls — a broad, photogenic waterfall where the Wharekopae River drops over a wide rock ledge surrounded by native bush. It is beautiful but what comes next is the main event. A short drive further, the Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock water slide where a thin sheet of water flows over smooth volcanic rock into a deep pool below. Grab a boogie board — locals leave spares at the site — climb to the top, lie down, and let the water carry you down at surprising speed. It is an absolute blast and completely free. The rock has been worn glass-smooth by millennia of flowing water. The slide works best after rain when the water flow is stronger. This is the kind of place that does not make it into most guidebooks but absolutely should.
review_summary: The Rere Falls and Rockslide Road Trip route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive inland from Gisborne to Rere Falls and the natural 60-metre rockslide — bring a boogie board. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Rere Rockslide
short: A 60m natural rockslide on the Wharekopae River where you can ride a bodyboard or inner tube down a smooth waterfall.
commentary: The Rere Rockslide on the Wharekopae River near Gisborne is a 60m sloping rock face used as a natural water slide. Bring an inflatable, lilo or bodyboard.
audio_story: You have arrived at the Rere Rockslide, and if you brought any kind of inflatable mattress, bodyboard or air filled tube with you, today is your day. The Wharekopae River runs west of Gisborne through gentle farm country, and at one particular point it slides over a sixty metre long sheet of sloping rock that is, by some unlikely accident of geology, exactly the right angle and exactly the right smoothness to be one of the longest natural waterslides in the country. Walk to the top, sit on whatever you brought to ride, and let the water carry you down. The pool at the bottom is shallow but deep enough to take the landing. People have been doing this for generations. It is free. It is open. There are no staff, no lifeguards, no entry charges, and only a discreet sign reminding you to use common sense. On a hot summer day local families turn it into a barbecue afternoon. Just downstream the same river drops over the much taller Rere Falls, a wide twenty metre waterfall you can walk across the front of. Make a day of it. Bring towels.
review_summary: Rere Rockslide draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 60m natural rockslide on the Wharekopae River where you can ride a bodyboard or inner tube down a smooth waterfall. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Rere Rockslide
short: Slide 60 metres down a natural rock waterslide in the Gisborne backcountry.
commentary: Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock waterslide formed by smooth, angled rock with a thin sheet of water flowing over it. Located 50 km from Gisborne, it is free to use. Locals ride it on boogie boards, bodyboards, or anything that slides. The nearby Rere Falls is also worth a visit.
audio_story: You have arrived at Rere Rockslide, one of New Zealand's most joyful free adventures and a local secret that deserves to be better known. Before you is a 60-metre stretch of smooth rock angled at a perfect gradient, with a thin sheet of water flowing over it to create a completely natural waterslide. Grab a boogie board, a pool noodle, an old surfboard, or honestly, just lie on your back and let the water carry you down. The ride takes about 10 seconds and ends in a deep pool at the bottom. There is no entrance fee, no queue, and no rules beyond common sense. Locals have been sliding down this rock for generations. The surface is remarkably smooth, worn by centuries of flowing water, and the water depth varies with recent rainfall, more rain means a faster ride. Rere Falls, a beautiful 5-metre cascade, is just a few minutes up the road.
review_summary: The Rere Rockslide adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Slide 60 metres down a natural rock waterslide in the Gisborne backcountry. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Rongopai Painted Meeting House
short: A uniquely painted meeting house — floral murals instead of carvings, created by followers of Te Kooti in 1887.
commentary: Rongopai's interior is covered in painted figurative murals — portraits, floral designs, and religious imagery. A radical departure from carving tradition and a unique fusion of Māori and European styles. Category I historic place.
review_summary: Visitors describe Rongopai Painted Meeting House as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A uniquely painted meeting house — floral murals instead of carvings, created by followers of Te Kooti in 1887. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rongopai Wharenui, Patutahi
short: A remarkable painted wharenui built in 1887 to welcome Te Kooti home after his pardon — unique in NZ Māori architecture.
commentary: Rongopai was built 1887 by Te Whānau a Kai to welcome Te Kooti home to Tairāwhiti. When his return was blocked, the elaborate painted interior became a unique art treasure. Access only by arrangement with Te Whānau a Kai.
audio_story: You are near Patutahi, a small settlement west of Gisborne, and nearby stands a wharenui called Rongopai that is unlike any other in the country. It was built in eighteen eighty seven by the people of Te Whānau a Kai, specifically to welcome Te Kooti Arikirangi home to the East Coast after his pardon four years earlier. The interior of the whare is painted rather than carved, in bold figurative imagery that includes biblical figures, historical ancestors, flowering plants, and vivid colours. It was an experimental response to the loss of traditional carving materials and carvers during the wars, and it produced one of the most original interiors in Māori art history. Te Kooti, in the end, was not able to return. The colonial government continued to pressure him to stay away from Tairāwhiti, and he died in Ōhiwa in eighteen ninety three without ever seeing Rongopai. The whare stood empty of its intended welcome, but it remains a sacred house for Te Whānau a Kai today. The wharenui is not open to casual visitors. Access is only possible by arrangement with the iwi. Pass the marae with respect. The painted walls inside are one of the great secret treasures of Aotearoa.
review_summary: Visitors describe Rongopai Wharenui, Patutahi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A remarkable painted wharenui built in 1887 to welcome Te Kooti home after his pardon — unique in NZ Māori architecture. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ruatōria — Ngāti Porou Heartland
short: Deep in Ngāti Porou territory where Māori culture is the dominant way of life.
commentary: Here, te reo Māori is the everyday language and marae are the centres of community life. For visitors willing to travel the remote East Cape road, Ruatōria offers an authentic window into Māori New Zealand.
review_summary: Visitors describe Ruatōria — Ngāti Porou Heartland as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Deep in Ngāti Porou territory where Māori culture is the dominant way of life. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Sir Apirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini
short: Memorial to Sir Apirana Ngata — the statesman who championed Māori language, culture, and land development.
commentary: Sir Apirana Ngata (1874–1950) was the greatest Māori political leader of the 20th century. He revived Māori arts, established Māori land development schemes, and championed te reo Māori. His face appears on NZ's $50 note.
review_summary: Visitors describe Sir Apirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Memorial to Sir Apirana Ngata — the statesman who championed Māori language, culture, and land development. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Sir Āpirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini
short: The home marae and memorial of Sir Āpirana Ngata — Ngāti Porou leader, first Māori university graduate, architect of twentieth-century Māori revival.
commentary: Sir Āpirana Turupa Ngata (1874–1950), Ngāti Porou, was the first Māori to graduate from a NZ university and served as an MP for 38 years. His home marae is at Waiomatatini.
audio_story: You are in the Waiapu Valley, the heartland of Ngāti Porou, and near a marae called Waiomatatini where one of the most influential Māori leaders of the twentieth century is buried. Sir Āpirana Turupa Ngata was born here in eighteen seventy four. He was the first Māori to gain a university degree in New Zealand, earning a bachelor of arts and then a law degree at Canterbury College. He went on to represent the Eastern Māori electorate in Parliament for thirty eight years, longer than almost anyone in New Zealand political history. Ngata is remembered for two great projects. The first was his effort to rebuild the Māori economy through land consolidation and farm development schemes that helped Ngāti Porou and other iwi retain control of what remained of their ancestral lands. The second was the cultural revival. Ngata personally organised the carving and building of dozens of wharenui across the country in the nineteen twenties and thirties, rescuing traditional arts that were on the edge of being lost. He died in nineteen fifty and is buried here in his home valley. Pass Waiomatatini with respect. Without Āpirana Ngata, much less of what is now called Māori culture would have survived the twentieth century intact.
review_summary: Visitors describe Sir Āpirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The home marae and memorial of Sir Āpirana Ngata — Ngāti Porou leader, first Māori university graduate, architect of twentieth-century Māori revival. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tatapouri Stingray Encounter
short: Wade out at low tide to feed wild short-tail stingrays — a guided experience run by local Ngāi Tāmanuhiri whānau.
commentary: Operated by Dive Tatapouri, a small family business north of Gisborne. The stingrays are wild and free to leave; they have learned to come for fish during the daily encounter.
audio_story: You are about to wade into the sea at Tatapouri, a few kilometres north of Gisborne, with a wetsuit on and a hand-held bait bag, and a wild stingray is going to come and eat from your hand. The stingrays here are not captive. They live in the open ocean and choose, every day, whether or not to swim into the shallows for the daily encounter. Most days they choose to come. Local guides take small groups to a shallow reef just offshore at low tide, where short-tail and eagle stingrays glide silently in to investigate. The bigger short-tails can be over two metres across the wing, and yet they move with such gentleness that they barely disturb the water around your knees. Your guide will show you how to hold a piece of bait between your thumb and finger so the ray takes it cleanly with its mouth on the underside of its body. The whole experience is run by local whānau and is grounded in tikanga, with a karakia at the beginning and end of each session. You will leave with cold feet, a wet wetsuit, and a story that will, frankly, last for life.
review_summary: Visitors describe Tatapouri Stingray Encounter as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Wade out at low tide to feed wild short-tail stingrays — a guided experience run by local Ngāi Tāmanuhiri whānau. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Araroa & Hinerupe Marae
short: The village closest to the East Cape — and home to a 600-year-old pōhutukawa and the historic Hinerupe Marae.
commentary: Te Araroa sits on the broad curve of Kawakawa Bay near the East Cape. The giant pōhutukawa called Te Waha-o-Rerekohu in the school grounds is reputed to be over 600 years old.
audio_story: You have arrived at Te Araroa, the last real settlement before the East Cape lighthouse and the easternmost inhabited point of the New Zealand mainland. The village wraps around the broad curve of Kawakawa Bay, sheltered by high cliffs on both sides. At the local school grounds stands one of the most venerable trees in the country, a pōhutukawa called Te Waha o Rerekohu, reputed to be over six hundred years old. Beneath its enormous canopy generations of local children have played, waited for buses, and grown up. Nearby stands Hinerupe Marae, the principal marae of Te Whānau a Hinerupe, the hapū of Ngāti Porou who hold this land. The carvings on the wharenui trace the descent of the hapū from Porourangi and onward to the ancestors specific to this valley. This is a tight knit community. It is one of the few parts of the country where the first language you hear at the school gate is still likely to be te reo Māori, spoken naturally between grandparents and children. Buy something at the store. Fill up with fuel before you push on to the cape itself. And if you are lucky enough to pass through when a hui is being held, keep your distance and your voice low.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Araroa & Hinerupe Marae as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The village closest to the East Cape — and home to a 600-year-old pōhutukawa and the historic Hinerupe Marae. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Kooti Arikirangi Birthplace Area — Te Karaka
short: Near where Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki was born around 1832 — founder of the Ringatū faith and one of the most controversial figures of New Zealand history.
commentary: Te Kooti was born near Te Karaka to a Rongowhakaata father. Wrongly imprisoned on the Chathams in 1866, he led a famous escape in 1868 and founded the Ringatū Church.
audio_story: You are travelling through the flat farming country of Te Karaka, a small settlement in the Waipaoa Valley north of Gisborne. Somewhere close to here, around the year eighteen thirty two, a child was born to a Rongowhakaata family who would grow up to become one of the most extraordinary and divisive figures in New Zealand history. His name was Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki. As a young man Te Kooti was arrested by the colonial government, without trial, on the charge of spying for Māori forces. He protested his innocence but was deported with hundreds of other prisoners to the Chatham Islands in eighteen sixty six. On the islands, during his two years of imprisonment, he experienced a series of visions and began preaching a new faith founded on the Old Testament, which became known as Ringatū, meaning the upraised hand. In eighteen sixty eight Te Kooti and his followers seized a schooner and escaped back to the mainland, landing on the Poverty Bay coast. What followed was four years of war across the central North Island. Te Kooti was never captured. He was pardoned in eighteen eighty three. The Ringatū Church he founded remains a living faith today. Pass this land with quiet attention.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Kooti Arikirangi Birthplace Area — Te Karaka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Near where Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki was born around 1832 — founder of the Ringatū faith and one of the most controversial figures of New Zealand history. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae
short: One of the largest and most elaborately carved meeting houses in NZ — at the foot of Kaiti Hill.
commentary: Te Poho-o-Rawiri features magnificent traditional carvings and tukutuku panels. Its scale and artistry make it one of the finest examples of Māori architectural craftsmanship.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the largest and most elaborately carved meeting houses in NZ — at the foot of Kaiti Hill. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae, Kaiti
short: One of the largest wharenui in New Zealand — home of Ngāti Oneone and Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki. Viewed respectfully from the road.
commentary: Te Poho-o-Rawiri is the principal marae of Ngāti Oneone, built in 1929 after the previous wharenui burned. At 28m long it is among the largest wharenui in Aotearoa.
audio_story: You are passing Te Poho o Rāwiri, one of the great wharenui of Aotearoa. This is the principal marae of Ngāti Oneone, the iwi of the Kaiti foreshore, and the place where Cook himself first encountered tangata whenua in seventeen sixty nine. The present wharenui was built in nineteen twenty nine to replace an earlier building that had burned. At twenty eight metres long it is among the largest wharenui in the country. The elaborately carved interior includes panels that tell the story of Kiwa, the captain of the Horouta waka who gave the traditional name of this region, Tūranganui a Kiwa. The house is not open to casual tourists. It is a working marae, a living building, used for tangihanga, weddings, hui, and the tangible business of Ngāti Oneone as an iwi. From the road you can see the grand bargeboards and the carved amo of the porch. Pause for a moment to acknowledge the mana of this place, and then continue. If you have genuine business to bring to the marae, it must be arranged formally with the iwi in advance. Behind the marae rises Titirangi, the sacred hill, whose summit you can visit by a short walk and where the wider story of this coastline is told.
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae, Kaiti as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the largest wharenui in New Zealand — home of Ngāti Oneone and Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Urewera (Lake Waikaremoana)
short: Lake Waikaremoana — a great deep-bush lake, a Great Walk, and the heart of Te Urewera, which has its own legal personhood.
commentary: Te Urewera was vested as a legal person in 2014 under the Tūhoe settlement, the first land in the world to receive such a status. Lake Waikaremoana sits in its heart.
audio_story: You have come to Te Urewera, the great mountain forest in the heart of the North Island. Te Urewera is the homeland of Tūhoe, who are sometimes called the Children of the Mist, and in twenty fourteen this entire forested landscape became the first land in the world to be granted legal personhood. Te Urewera is no longer owned by the Crown. It is no longer a national park in the conventional sense. It is, in law, itself, with rights of its own, jointly cared for by Tūhoe and the Crown. At its heart sits Lake Waikaremoana. The lake was formed when a giant landslide blocked a river around two thousand years ago, and the dark drowned forests are still preserved beneath its surface. The Lake Waikaremoana Track, one of New Zealand's Great Walks, runs forty six kilometres around the western shore over three to four days. There are also short walks from the visitor centre at Aniwaniwa, including the spectacular Waikaremoana Falls track. The road in is long and remote. The forest is deep. The mist sits low. Move quietly through this place, and remember it has its own name and its own story.
review_summary: Te Urewera (Lake Waikaremoana) draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Lake Waikaremoana — a great deep-bush lake, a Great Walk, and the heart of Te Urewera, which has its own legal personhood. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Te Waha-o-Rerekohu
short: The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the world — 600+ years old, 37-metre branch span — in the grounds of Te Araroa Area School.
commentary: In the grounds of Te Araroa Area School stands Te Waha-o-Rerekohu, 'the mouth of Rerekohu' — named for a rangatira of this rohe whose kūmara pātaka (food store) tradition places at the base of the tree. Dendrochronology dates its germination to around the year 1421, making it around 600 years old, give or take 200. The branch span was measured at over 37 metres in 1950. It's the largest and oldest known pōhutukawa on earth. Publicly accessible during reasonable hours; treat the tree with the respect of a taonga, which it is.
sources_to_check: Te Ara — Te Waha o Rerekohu photo
Te Whānau-a-Rerekohu / Ngāti Porou — cultural content
review_summary: Visitors describe Te Waha-o-Rerekohu as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the world — 600+ years old, 37-metre branch span — in the grounds of Te Araroa Area School. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: The Wharf Bar & Grill
short: Gisborne marina waterfront bar and grill — local seafood, NZ wines and the first sunrise in the world.
commentary: The Wharf Bar & Grill sits on the Gisborne marina where you can watch fishing boats coming and going. The menu features fresh local seafood — the fish and chips use today's catch — alongside steaks, burgers and seasonal dishes. Gisborne is the first city in the world to see the sunrise, and an early breakfast here on New Year's Day is a bucket-list experience. The Gisborne Chardonnay list is excellent.
review_summary: Good waterfront dining in Gisborne. The fresh fish is a highlight. Some find the menu broad rather than focused.

name: Tītīrangi / Kaiti Hill Lookout, Gisborne
short: The hill above Gisborne where Captain Cook first sighted the New Zealand mainland in 1769 — and where Ngāti Oneone built one of their great pā.
commentary: Tītīrangi is sacred to Ngāti Oneone. Beneath the hill lies the Cook landing site at Puhi Kai Iti. Today the summit is a public reserve with sweeping views across Poverty Bay.
audio_story: You have climbed up Tītīrangi, the hill the Pākehā maps call Kaiti Hill, and you are standing on the highest ground above Gisborne. From here you can see almost all of Poverty Bay, from the river mouth at your feet out to Young Nicks Head on the southern arm of the bay. It was from this very piece of land, in October seventeen sixty nine, that the Endeavour was first sighted by people on shore, and from the deck of the Endeavour, that the boy Nicholas Young first sighted the New Zealand mainland and earned the headland its English name. The hill itself is sacred to Ngāti Oneone, the local hapū, and was once the site of a great fortified pā. The terraces are still visible on the slopes. The summit lookout has been thoughtfully redeveloped in recent years with carved pou, interpretation panels, and a James Cook memorial that, importantly, is balanced now by signs that tell the harder story of the first contact, in which several local Māori were shot by the Endeavour crew. Stand here for a few minutes. Two stories meet on this hill, and both belong here.
review_summary: Tītīrangi / Kaiti Hill Lookout, Gisborne draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The hill above Gisborne where Captain Cook first sighted the New Zealand mainland in 1769 — and where Ngāti Oneone built one of their great pā. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Summit
short: Ancestral pā of Ngāti Oneone above Gisborne — the best interpretive view of Cooks landing, Young Nicks Head, and the first encounter.
commentary: Titirangi (Kaiti Hill) was a major pā of Ngāti Oneone. The summit lookout at Ruatanuika interprets the Cook arrival and the death of Te Maro. 15-minute walk from the carpark.
audio_story: You are climbing Titirangi, also known as Kaiti Hill, the steep headland that guards the northern entrance to Poverty Bay. Before colonisation this was one of the great pā of Ngāti Oneone, a terraced hill fort with a commanding view over the whole of Tūranganui a Kiwa. From the summit lookout at Ruatanuika you can see the entire landscape of first encounter. Below you, at the river mouth, is Puhi Kai Iti, where Cook came ashore in seventeen sixty nine and where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was shot dead within hours of arrival. Across the bay, the pale cliff of Young Nicks Head, Te Kurī a Pāoa, is the promontory Cooks surgeons boy first sighted from the rigging of the Endeavour. Out to sea, somewhere in the open water, the Endeavour herself lay at anchor. Closer to you, the streets of modern Gisborne lay out on the flat behind the beach, the apartment blocks and sports fields sitting on the old kumara gardens of the iwi. Since Tuia two hundred and fifty in twenty nineteen, the summit has been reinterpreted with pou carved by iwi artists, telling the story from both sides. A memorial to Te Maro stands at the very top. The walk up takes fifteen minutes. The view is one of the most historically dense in the country.
review_summary: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Summit draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Ancestral pā of Ngāti Oneone above Gisborne — the best interpretive view of Cooks landing, Young Nicks Head, and the first encounter. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Tolaga Bay / Ūawa — Takitimu Waka Landing
short: A beautiful East Coast bay with NZ's longest wharf — and a landing site of the Takitimu waka.
commentary: Ūawa is a landing site of the Takitimu waka. The 660m concrete wharf is NZ's longest. Behind the bay, the Cooks Cove Walkway passes through a natural rock arch.
review_summary: Visitors describe Tolaga Bay / Ūawa — Takitimu Waka Landing as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A beautiful East Coast bay with NZ's longest wharf — and a landing site of the Takitimu waka. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tolaga Bay Wharf / Ūawa
short: 660 metres of concrete reaching into the Pacific — the longest wharf in the southern hemisphere, saved from the sea by the town that built it.
commentary: Designed by marine engineer Cyrus Williams to solve Tolaga Bay's open-roadstead problem, construction began in 1926 and finished in 1929. Storms damaged the works during construction and the harbour board had to borrow another £20,000 to finish. At its peak in 1936, 133 ships a year called at the wharf. The last sailing was the coastal steamer Kopara in 1966. From the 1990s the Tolaga Bay Save the Wharf Charitable Trust has been restoring it section by section. It's now Heritage NZ Category 1, and you can walk the full 660 metres. Just up the road is Cooks Cove — where the Endeavour sheltered 23–29 October 1769 and Banks and Solander did much of their first NZ botanical collecting.
sources_to_check: Tolaga Bay Save the Wharf Charitable Trust
Te Ara — Tolaga Bay wharf photo
review_summary: Tolaga Bay Wharf / Ūawa draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 660 metres of concrete reaching into the Pacific — the longest wharf in the southern hemisphere, saved from the sea by the town that built it. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: USSCO Bar & Bistro
short: Gisborne's riverside bar and bistro — seasonal NZ food, local wines and a convivial atmosphere.
commentary: USSCO (named after the former Union Steam Ship Company building) sits where Gisborne's rivers meet, with a menu built around seasonal local produce. The Gisborne wine list is comprehensive, and the bistro food — think grilled fish, NZ lamb, seasonal salads — is well-executed. The bar is a gathering spot for locals, and the atmosphere is warm and welcoming. A good introduction to Gisborne's food and wine.
review_summary: A Gisborne favourite with good food and atmosphere. The local wine list is a strong point. Some find it noisy on weekend evenings.

name: Waerenga-a-Hika Battle Site
short: Where a Pai Mārire pā was besieged and taken in November 1865 — the battle that led to Te Kootis unjust arrest.
commentary: In November 1865 colonial forces and pro-government Māori besieged the Pai Mārire (Hauhau) pā at Waerenga-a-Hika. After a week it fell. Prisoners including Te Kooti were sent to the Chathams.
audio_story: You are at Waerenga a Hika, the site of a week long siege in November eighteen sixty five that few people today remember but whose consequences rippled across the next decade of New Zealand history. The followers of the Pai Mārire faith, a syncretic Māori religious movement born in Taranaki, had built a fortified pā on this ground. A force of colonial militia and pro government Māori surrounded the pā on the seventeenth of November. For seven days the defenders held out under fire. By the twenty fourth of November, short of water and with their earthworks crumbling, the defenders surrendered. Around seventy one Māori were killed in the fighting. Several hundred prisoners were taken, and in the chaotic aftermath a number of men who had taken no part in the fighting were also arrested, including a Rongowhakaata rangatira named Te Kooti Arikirangi. All were shipped to the Chatham Islands for indefinite imprisonment without trial. It was on the Chathams that Te Kooti founded the Ringatū faith. Three years later his dramatic escape and the violence that followed changed the East Coast forever. Everything that came later, began here, on this quiet paddock.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waerenga-a-Hika Battle Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where a Pai Mārire pā was besieged and taken in November 1865 — the battle that led to Te Kootis unjust arrest. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waiapu Valley — Te Reo Stronghold
short: One of the last places in NZ where te reo Māori is the everyday language — the heartland of Ngāti Porou.
commentary: The valley has more marae per capita than almost anywhere else. The area produced Sir Apirana Ngata, who championed Māori language and culture. Travelling through is like entering a different New Zealand.
review_summary: Visitors describe Waiapu Valley — Te Reo Stronghold as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the last places in NZ where te reo Māori is the everyday language — the heartland of Ngāti Porou. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waiapu Valley & Ngāti Porou Cathedral
short: The heartland of Ngāti Porou — and the site of St Marys Tikitiki, the most extraordinary Māori church in Aotearoa.
commentary: The Waiapu Valley on the northern East Coast is the ancestral heartland of Ngāti Porou. St Marys Church, Tikitiki (1924–26), with its entirely carved and painted interior, was built as a memorial to Ngāti Porou who died in WWI.
audio_story: You are driving through the Waiapu Valley, the ancestral heartland of Ngāti Porou, the iwi of the northern East Coast. Every marae you pass here, every small church and every carved gateway, belongs to hapū of Ngāti Porou who trace their descent from Porourangi, the eponymous ancestor who lived on these rivers perhaps thirty generations ago. At the village of Tikitiki you will find St Marys Anglican Church. From outside it looks modest. Inside, it is extraordinary.
