commentary: Motutapu is connected to Rangitoto by a causeway and is undergoing one of NZ's most ambitious ecological restorations. Predator-free since 2011, the island is seeing the return of species lost for generations. Volunteer planting days are popular.
review_summary: More rugged than Tiritiri but rewarding. Combine with Rangitoto climb.

name: Auckland Zoo — NZ Wildlife Precinct
short: Auckland Zoo's NZ precinct features kiwi, tuatara, kea, kākāpō displays, and a native nocturnal house.
commentary: The Te Wao Nui precinct recreates six NZ habitats — coast, islands, wetlands, night, forest, and high country. The nocturnal kiwi house is one of the best in the country, and conservation breeding programmes for kōkako, whio, and Archey's frog operate behind the scenes.
review_summary: Excellent native wildlife section. The kiwi viewing is very reliable. Good for rainy days.

name: Long Bay – Ōkura Marine Reserve
short: Protected marine area on Auckland's North Shore — snorkel with snapper, eagle rays, and crayfish.
commentary: Established in 1995, this 980-hectare reserve protects reef systems from Long Bay to Ōkura. Snapper populations have increased tenfold since protection. Snorkelling is best at the rocky reef near Piripiri Point.
review_summary: Great snorkelling close to the city. Fish are noticeably bigger and more abundant than unprotected areas.

name: Goat Island Marine Reserve / Te Hāwere-a-Maki
short: NZ's first marine reserve — crystal-clear water teeming with snapper, blue maomao, and crayfish.
commentary: Established in 1975, Goat Island was New Zealand's first marine reserve and proved the concept of marine protection. Snapper here grow to massive sizes, blue maomao school in thousands, and you can snorkel with stingrays in the shallows. Glass-bottom boat tours available for non-swimmers.
review_summary: Must-visit for snorkelling. Water clarity is excellent. The fish are incredibly tame and abundant.

name: Ambury Regional Park Shorebirds
short: Manukau Harbour mudflats where thousands of migratory waders — bar-tailed godwit and knot — feed each summer.
commentary: Ambury's tidal flats are one of Auckland's best spots for shorebirds. From September to March, bar-tailed godwits (kuaka) and red knots arrive after their epic non-stop flight from Alaska. Wrybills and NZ dotterels breed nearby.
review_summary: Outstanding birding spot close to the city. Best at low tide during summer migration season.

name: Waitākere Ranges — Kōkako Country
short: One of the last mainland strongholds for the North Island kōkako — listen for their haunting organ-like call at dawn.
commentary: The Waitākere Ranges are home to around 100 pairs of North Island kōkako, thanks to decades of intensive pest control. Dawn is the time to hear their extraordinary call. Ark in the Park, a community conservation project in the Cascades, has been a particular success story.
review_summary: Magical birding at dawn. The kōkako call is unforgettable. Tracks are well-maintained but kauri dieback protocols apply.

name: Rangitoto Island — Pest-Free Sanctuary
short: Auckland's youngest volcano is now pest-free — little blue penguins and forest birds are returning to the lava fields.
commentary: A massive 2011 aerial poison drop cleared rats and stoats from Rangitoto and neighbouring Motutapu. Little blue penguins now breed along the shore, kākāriki have been released, and the unique pōhutukawa forests are regenerating. The summit walk offers panoramic views.
review_summary: Iconic Auckland day trip. The pest-free status means much more birdlife than before. Summit track is a must.

name: Karekare – Ark in the Park Kōkako Trail
short: Community-run conservation area where reintroduced kōkako, hihi, and whitehead are thriving in West Auckland bush.
commentary: Ark in the Park is a 2,270-hectare community pest-control project in the Cascades Kauri area of the Waitākere Ranges. Kōkako were reintroduced in 2006, hihi (stitchbird) in 2007, and whiteheads in 2008. Early morning walks give the best chance of hearing kōkako.
review_summary: Real conservation in action. Volunteer days are rewarding. Dawn is best for birding.

name: Wenderholm Regional Park
short: Pūkeko-filled coastal park with mature pōhutukawa forest and NZ dotterel nesting beaches.
commentary: Wenderholm was Auckland's first regional park, established 1965. The pōhutukawa forest is home to kererū, tūī, and fantail, while the beach hosts breeding NZ dotterel. The Schischka homestead area is good for spotting pūkeko and paradise shelduck.
review_summary: Beautiful park, great for families. Nice bird variety between the bush and beach habitats.

name: Whatipu Wetland & Caves
short: Wild, remote wetland at the southern head of the Manukau Harbour — bittern, fernbird, and banded rail habitat.
commentary: Whatipu is one of Auckland's wildest spots — a vast sand dune system, wetland, and cave complex at the southern Manukau head. Australasian bittern (one of NZ's rarest birds) breed in the wetlands, and fernbird and banded rail can be heard in the rushes.
review_summary: Remote and atmospheric. Not easy to get to but worth it for serious birders. The caves are impressive too.

name: Ōhiwa Harbour Wildlife Refuge
short: One of New Zealand's most important estuaries for wading birds — dotterel, oystercatcher, and godwit.
commentary: Ōhiwa Harbour is a shallow tidal estuary between Whakatāne and Ōpōtiki, rated as one of NZ's top five estuaries for shorebird populations. NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and bar-tailed godwit are resident, with Arctic migrants arriving each summer.
review_summary: Outstanding birding. Best at low tide. The harbour itself is beautiful and uncrowded.

name: Whale Island / Moutohorā
short: Predator-free island nature reserve — tuatara, saddleback, and red-crowned kākāriki thrive here.
commentary: Moutohorā is a 143-hectare island off Whakatāne, cleared of pests and now home to translocated tuatara, North Island saddleback, red-crowned kākāriki, and little spotted kiwi. DOC runs limited guided visits during summer.
review_summary: Incredible wildlife experience. Very limited access (ballot system). The tuatara viewing alone is worth the trip.

name: Maketū Estuary — Kaituna Wetland
short: Restored wetland where the Kaituna River meets the sea — bittern, fernbird, and royal spoonbill.
commentary: The Kaituna River re-diversion in 2018 reconnected the Maketū Estuary to its natural water supply, restoring critical habitat. The wetlands now support Australasian bittern, fernbird, spotless crake, and growing numbers of royal spoonbill.
review_summary: Great restoration success story. Boardwalk makes access easy. Best for birding at dawn.

name: Kiwi360 — Native Bird Walk
short: Beyond the kiwifruit, a native bird trail through regenerating bush with kererū, tūī, and bellbird.
commentary: While primarily a kiwifruit attraction, Kiwi360 in Te Puke has developed a native bird trail through its regenerating bush block. The pest-control programme has brought back good numbers of kererū, tūī, and bellbird. A family-friendly introduction to NZ bush birds.
review_summary: Fun for families. Combines the kiwifruit experience with some nice bush birding.

name: Pāpāmoa Beach Dotterel Nesting
short: One of the most important NZ dotterel nesting beaches — volunteer wardens protect nests each spring.
commentary: Pāpāmoa Beach hosts one of the Bay of Plenty's largest NZ dotterel breeding populations. During spring and summer, volunteer wardens monitor and protect nests from predators and disturbance. The rare shore plover has also been sighted here.
review_summary: Great conservation effort. Keep dogs on leads during nesting season. Beautiful beach regardless.

name: Whakaari Seabird Coast
short: Boat trips past seabird colonies — grey-faced petrel, Australasian gannet, and shearwater breeding grounds.
commentary: The coastal waters between Whakatāne and White Island host significant seabird colonies. Boat tours pass grey-faced petrel nesting cliffs, Australasian gannet colonies, and in the right season, hundreds of thousands of sooty shearwaters heading to their breeding islands.
review_summary: Spectacular seabird viewing. Best combined with a White Island scenic cruise. Bring binoculars.

name: McLaren Falls Glowworm Walk
short: Free glowworm walk through native bush — thousands of tiny lights along the stream bank after dark.
commentary: McLaren Falls Park near Tauranga hosts a free glowworm walk along a bush-lined stream. After dark, the banks light up with thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa. No booking needed — just bring a torch for the walk in, then turn it off at the viewing area.
review_summary: Amazing free experience. Best on dark, humid nights. Gets busy in summer so arrive early.

name: Marine Education Tauranga — Reef Check
short: Citizen science reef monitoring programme — join a guided snorkel survey of Tauranga's reef systems.
commentary: Reef Check NZ runs guided snorkel surveys along the Tauranga coast where volunteers help monitor reef health. Learn to identify NZ marine species and contribute to real conservation data. Sessions run from November to March.
review_summary: Excellent marine education experience. Great for older kids and adults. Book ahead in summer.

name: Aongatete Forest — Predator Control Zone
short: Community-maintained pest control in Kaimai bush — excellent for kererū, kākā, and North Island robin.
commentary: The Aongatete Forest Restoration Trust has maintained a trapping network since 2004, dramatically reducing predator numbers. The result: kererū are everywhere, kākā have returned, and North Island robin can be seen along the walking tracks. The loop track is moderate and well-maintained.
review_summary: Superb bush birding. The trapping programme has made a visible difference. Robins are very tame and curious.

name: Little Waihi Estuary — Wetland Walk
short: Quiet estuary boardwalk with royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, and seasonal wading birds.
commentary: The Little Waihi Estuary boardwalk provides easy viewing of a productive tidal wetland. Royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, pied stilt, and kingfisher are year-round residents. In summer, godwits and knots arrive from the Arctic.
review_summary: Peaceful walk with good bird variety. Boardwalk is pushchair-friendly. Best at low tide.

name: Welcome Bay Bush — Kiwi Listening
short: Community pest-control success — North Island brown kiwi can now be heard calling in the bush above Welcome Bay.
commentary: Intensive community trapping in the hills above Welcome Bay has allowed kiwi to return to areas where they hadn't been heard for decades. Evening listening walks from the bush edge can hear kiwi calling, especially during winter and spring.
review_summary: Exciting to hear kiwi so close to the city. Best in winter evenings. Join a guided listening event.

name: Mount Maunganui — Little Blue Penguin Colony
short: Little blue penguins nest in the rocks around the base of Mauao — watch them come ashore at dusk.
commentary: A small colony of kororā (little blue penguins) nests in rock crevices around the base of Mauao / Mount Maunganui. At dusk, especially between September and February, you can watch them waddle ashore. Keep your distance and no torches.
review_summary: Delightful surprise in an urban setting. Very dependent on timing and luck. Best at dusk during breeding season.

name: Ōkere Falls — Giant Longfin Eel Viewing
short: Watch giant longfin eels — some over 100 years old — in the crystal-clear pools below Ōkere Falls.
commentary: The pools at the base of Ōkere Falls on the Kaituna River are home to giant longfin eels (tuna), some estimated to be over a century old. These ancient creatures are endemic to NZ and increasingly rare. A short walk from the car park leads to viewing spots.
review_summary: Fascinating to see these ancient creatures up close. Short, easy walk. Combine with the Kaituna rafting.

name: Kaikōura Peninsula Seal Colony
short: Hundreds of NZ fur seals basking on the rocks at Point Kean — a 5-minute walk from Kaikōura town.
commentary: The Kaikōura Peninsula walkway leads past a large colony of NZ fur seals (kekeno). The seals are remarkably tolerant of visitors but maintain a 10-metre distance. Pups play in rock pools during summer. The walkway continues around the peninsula with spectacular mountain-meets-sea views.
review_summary: Must-do in Kaikōura. Seals are incredibly photogenic. The peninsula walkway is excellent. Free.

name: Dolphin Encounter Kaikōura
short: Swim with hundreds of dusky dolphins in the wild — Kaikōura's iconic marine encounter.
commentary: Dusky dolphins gather in pods of 100-1000 in Kaikōura's productive waters. You can swim among them — they're curious and playful, often performing acrobatics around snorkellers. Non-swimmer viewing trips are also available. Best from October to April.
review_summary: Unforgettable experience. The dolphins are incredibly interactive. Water is cold — wetsuits provided. Book early.

name: Kaikōura Albatross Encounter
short: Up-close encounters with albatross, petrels, and shearwaters attracted by natural chum on the Kaikōura shelf.
commentary: Albatross Encounter takes you to the edge of the Kaikōura Canyon where fish oils and chum attract a remarkable gathering of pelagic seabirds. Giant petrels, Hutton's and Buller's shearwaters, and several albatross species come within metres of the boat.
review_summary: Incredible for birders. Up to 12 species of albatross and petrel. Bring a good camera. Seasickness possible.

name: Orana Wildlife Park — NZ Conservation
short: NZ's only open-range zoo — breeding programmes for kiwi, tuatara, and Canterbury's endangered species.
commentary: Orana Park takes a conservation-first approach with successful breeding programmes for great spotted kiwi, tuatara, blue duck, and Canterbury gecko. The kiwi encounter lets you into the nocturnal house for close viewing. Also home to NZ's only gorilla troop.
review_summary: Excellent conservation focus. The kiwi encounter is outstanding. Great for families. Spacious grounds.

name: Willowbank Wildlife Reserve — Kiwi House
short: See kiwi up close in a purpose-built nocturnal house — one of the best kiwi viewing experiences in NZ.
commentary: Willowbank's kiwi house is widely considered one of the best places to see kiwi in New Zealand. The reversed day-night cycle means birds are active when you visit. Also home to tuatara, kea, and endangered Canterbury species. The Ko Tāne cultural experience runs here at night.
review_summary: Best kiwi viewing experience many visitors have. The birds are very active and close. Combine with Ko Tāne.

name: Akaroa Harbour — Hector's Dolphin Cruise
short: The world's smallest and rarest dolphin — Hector's dolphins play in Akaroa Harbour year-round.
commentary: Akaroa Harbour is one of the most reliable places to see Hector's dolphins, the world's smallest marine dolphin, found only in NZ. Their distinctive rounded dorsal fin makes them easy to identify. Black Cat Cruises and Fox II Sailing offer nature cruises with very high sighting rates.
review_summary: Hector's dolphins almost guaranteed. The harbour itself is stunning. Swimming with dolphins available in summer.

name: Banks Peninsula — White-Flippered Penguin Colony
short: The white-flippered penguin — a rare subspecies found only on Banks Peninsula — comes ashore at dusk.
commentary: The white-flippered little blue penguin is found only on Banks Peninsula and Motunau Island. Pohatu Penguins at Flea Bay runs dusk viewing tours where you can watch these rare penguins waddle up to their nest boxes. The conservation project has boosted numbers from 600 to over 5,000 pairs.
review_summary: Incredible conservation success story. The penguins are adorable. Book ahead for the evening tour. Gravel road in.

name: Arthur's Pass — Kea Encounters
short: The world's only alpine parrot — cheeky kea roam Arthur's Pass village stealing sandwiches and dismantling cars.
commentary: Arthur's Pass village is arguably the best place in NZ to see kea. These intelligent alpine parrots hang around car parks and the village, investigating (and often destroying) anything they can get their beaks on. Entertaining but remember — don't feed them.
review_summary: Kea are hilarious and fascinating. They will investigate your car. Great photo opportunities. Don't leave anything unsecured.

name: Te Waihora / Lake Ellesmere — Wetland Birds
short: Canterbury's vast coastal lagoon — NZ's most important site for black stilt and other wetland birds.
commentary: Te Waihora is NZ's fifth-largest lake but only 2 metres deep, making it incredibly productive for birdlife. Over 160 species have been recorded here, including the critically endangered black stilt (kakī). The Greenpark Sands and Kaitorete Spit are best for birding.
review_summary: Outstanding birding but vast and exposed. Bring a scope. Best spots are Greenpark Sands and Kaitorete Spit.

name: Motunau Beach — Penguin Colony
short: White-flippered and little blue penguins nest along this quiet Canterbury coast — watch from the beach at dusk.
commentary: Motunau Beach hosts both white-flippered and standard little blue penguins. The local community runs a predator-trapping programme that has significantly increased penguin numbers. Evening viewing from the beach is free — maintain distance and keep torches off.
review_summary: Quiet, uncrowded penguin viewing. Best at dusk during breeding season (Sep-Feb). Support the local trapping project.

name: Ashburton Lakes — Black Stilt Habitat
short: The braided rivers and alpine lakes of inland Canterbury — last wild stronghold of the critically endangered kakī (black stilt).
commentary: The Ashburton Lakes and surrounding braided rivers are the only remaining wild breeding habitat for kakī, the world's rarest wading bird with fewer than 200 wild individuals. DOC's Kakī Recovery Programme operates from nearby Twizel. Sightings require patience and binoculars.
review_summary: A pilgrimage for birders. Kakī sightings not guaranteed but the braided river landscape is stunning regardless.

name: Travis Wetland Nature Heritage Park
short: Christchurch's largest freshwater wetland — bittern, fernbird, and pūkeko in the heart of the city.
commentary: Travis Wetland is a 116-hectare freshwater wetland that survived Christchurch's urban expansion. Boardwalks loop through raupo swamp, sedgeland, and open water. Australasian bittern, spotless crake, and fernbird are present, plus abundant waterfowl.
review_summary: Excellent urban wetland. Well-maintained boardwalks. Good for families. Best early morning for bittern.

name: Hinewai Reserve — Banks Peninsula Forest
short: Hugh Wilson's remarkable native forest restoration — 1,230 hectares of regenerating bush teeming with birds.
commentary: Hinewai Reserve is the life's work of botanist Hugh Wilson, who let 1,230 hectares of Banks Peninsula farmland regenerate to native forest. The result is extraordinary — bellbird, kererū, and kōkako-reintroduction candidates fill the bush. Walking tracks cross the reserve.
review_summary: Inspiring conservation story. The bush is dense and birdy. Tracks can be steep and muddy. Free to visit.

name: Godley Head — Penguin Corridor
short: Protected penguin corridor on the Christchurch coast — little blue penguins commute through here at dusk.
commentary: Godley Head on the Lyttelton Harbour entrance has a protected penguin corridor where little blue penguins cross from the harbour to their nesting burrows. The DOC-managed site includes predator traps and monitoring. Watch from the designated viewing areas at dusk.
review_summary: Nice combined walk and penguin viewing. The harbour views are spectacular. Dusk visits essential for penguins.

name: Rakaia River — Braided River Birds
short: Canterbury's braided rivers host black-fronted tern, wrybill, and banded dotterel — unique NZ species found nowhere else.
commentary: Canterbury's braided rivers are globally significant habitats. The Rakaia is home to black-fronted tern (tara), wrybill (ngutu parore), banded dotterel, and black stilt. These species evolved to breed on the shingle islands. 4WD access to good viewing spots.
review_summary: Unique birding experience. Bring binoculars and a spotting scope. Spring breeding season is best.

name: Pegasus Bay — Seabird Watching
short: Pelagic seabirds visible from shore — Hutton's shearwater, albatross, and petrel in the Canterbury Bight.
commentary: Pegasus Bay and the Canterbury Bight offer some of NZ's best shore-based pelagic birding. Strong easterly winds push Hutton's shearwaters, various albatross species, and giant petrels close to shore. The Kaikōura coast is particularly productive.
review_summary: Requires patience and the right conditions. A scope is essential. Best in strong onshore winds.

name: Twizel Kakī Recovery Centre
short: DOC's breeding programme for the world's rarest wading bird — see kakī (black stilt) up close.
commentary: The Kakī Recovery Centre near Twizel is where DOC breeds and rears kakī (black stilt) for release into the wild. With fewer than 200 in the wild, every chick matters. Guided visits offer a window into one of NZ's most critical conservation programmes.
review_summary: Fascinating and humbling conservation work. Small-group visits only. Call ahead to arrange.

name: Cathedral Cove Marine Reserve
short: Crystal-clear water, volcanic reefs, and abundant fish life in the Coromandel's premier marine reserve.
commentary: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve protects 840 hectares of coast around Cathedral Cove. Snorkelling reveals massive snapper, crayfish, kina, and colourful reef fish. Gemstone Bay has an underwater snorkel trail with interpretive signs.
review_summary: Outstanding snorkelling. Gemstone Bay underwater trail is excellent. Water clearest in summer. Busy in January.

name: Kuaotunu Kiwi Sanctuary
short: Community-led kiwi conservation — North Island brown kiwi are returning to the bush above Kuaotunu.
commentary: The Kuaotunu Bird Rescue and Conservation Trust has been running intensive predator control since 2005. Kiwi numbers are increasing and you can hear them calling at night along Blackjack Road. The trust runs occasional guided listening walks.
review_summary: Exciting to hear kiwi in the wild. Best in winter/spring evenings. Support the local trust.

name: Whangapoua Beach — NZ Dotterel Colony
short: One of the Coromandel's most important NZ dotterel nesting beaches — protected by volunteer wardens.
commentary: Whangapoua hosts one of the Hauraki's largest NZ dotterel breeding populations. DOC and volunteer wardens protect nests with predator traps and temporary fencing during the Sept-Feb breeding season. The beach is stunning and relatively uncrowded.
review_summary: Beautiful beach with real conservation happening. Keep dogs on leads. The dotterels are surprisingly well-camouflaged.

name: Ōpito Bay — Marine Life Snorkelling
short: Sheltered bay with excellent reef snorkelling — parore, snapper, and eagle rays in warm, clear water.
commentary: Ōpito Bay is one of the Coromandel's most sheltered snorkelling spots. The rocky reef on the south side of the bay hosts parore, snapper, triplefin, and occasional eagle rays. The water is warm by NZ standards and usually calm.
review_summary: Great snorkelling in calm, warm water. Less crowded than Cathedral Cove. Best at high tide.

name: Thames Coast — Pūkorokoro Miranda
short: One of NZ's most important shorebird sites — bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and wrybill.
commentary: The Firth of Thames mudflats at Miranda/Pūkorokoro are internationally significant for migratory wading birds. The Robert Findlay Wildlife Reserve and Stilt Ponds offer excellent viewing. The Miranda Shorebird Centre provides binoculars and expert advice.
review_summary: World-class birding. The shorebird centre is excellent. Low tide is essential. Spring and autumn migration peaks.

name: The Pinnacles — Long-Tailed Bat Habitat
short: The Kauaeranga Valley forests are home to the endangered long-tailed bat — listen for echolocation clicks at dusk.
commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley's mature podocarp forests provide habitat for the endangered long-tailed bat (pekapeka). As you hike to The Pinnacles, dusk brings the chance to see bats flitting along forest edges. DOC bat monitoring stations operate in the valley.
review_summary: Classic Coromandel tramp. Bat sightings are a bonus — they're hard to spot but bat detectors help.

name: Wentworth Valley — Bush Bird Walk
short: Easy bush walk through pest-controlled forest — North Island robin, tomtit, and whiteheads are common.
commentary: The Wentworth Valley near Whangamata has benefited from years of community pest control. The loop walk passes through beautiful bush where North Island robin, tomtit, and whiteheads are regularly seen. The waterfall at the end is a bonus.
review_summary: Lovely walk with good birding. Robins are very tame. The waterfall is worth the walk alone.

name: Mahakirau Forest Estate — Kiwi Sanctuary
short: Private forest estate with intensive kiwi protection — one of the Coromandel's kiwi strongholds.
commentary: Mahakirau Forest Estate is a private conservation trust running intensive predator control across 1,000 hectares of Coromandel bush. Kiwi numbers have increased significantly, and the estate is now a source population for kiwi translocations to other sites.
review_summary: Important conservation work. Limited public access but the trust welcomes supporters and volunteers.

name: Moehau Range — Northern Kiwi Stronghold
short: DOC-managed kiwi sanctuary in the rugged northern Coromandel — one of the peninsula's most important kiwi populations.
commentary: The Moehau Range at the northern tip of the Coromandel is home to a significant population of North Island brown kiwi. DOC runs an intensive stoat trapping programme here to protect kiwi chicks. The tramping tracks are challenging but the bush is spectacular.
review_summary: Serious tramping territory. Bush is dense and rugged. The kiwi population here is nationally important.

name: New Chums Beach — Coastal Wildlife
short: Pristine undeveloped beach with NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and reef fish in the rock pools.
commentary: New Chums Beach is regularly voted one of NZ's most beautiful beaches. Its remoteness (30-minute walk through bush) means it's also good for coastal wildlife — NZ dotterel nest here, variable oystercatchers patrol the tide line, and the rock pools are rich with marine life.
review_summary: Stunning beach. The walk in keeps crowds down. Look for dotterel and oystercatchers on the sand.

name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony
short: The world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony — 20,000 birds nesting on dramatic coastal cliffs.
commentary: Cape Kidnappers hosts one of the world's largest and most accessible mainland gannet colonies, with around 20,000 birds in residence from July to April. You can reach the colony via a spectacular coastal walk at low tide or by tractor-trailer tour. The birds are remarkably unperturbed by visitors.
review_summary: Bucket-list wildlife experience. The tractor tour is fun but the walk is more rewarding. Check tides carefully.

name: Boundary Stream Mainland Island
short: DOC mainland island with intensive pest control — kiwi, kōkako, and robin thrive in regenerating bush.
commentary: Boundary Stream Scenic Reserve is one of DOC's six mainland island sites. Intensive predator control has allowed kiwi, kōkako, North Island robin, and whiteheads to flourish. Multiple walking tracks ranging from 30 minutes to full-day offer excellent birding.
review_summary: Exceptional birding in beautiful bush. The robins are incredibly tame. Multiple loop track options.

name: Waitangi Regional Park Wetland
short: Restored estuary wetland near Napier — royal spoonbill, bittern, and seasonal wading birds.
commentary: The Waitangi Estuary at the mouth of the Ngaruroro River has been partially restored as a regional park. Royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, pied stilt, and Australasian bittern frequent the wetlands. A boardwalk provides easy viewing access.
review_summary: Good birding close to Napier. The boardwalk is well maintained. Best at low tide.

name: Te Angiangi Marine Reserve
short: Remote rocky reef marine reserve on the southern Hawke's Bay coast — crayfish, paua, and reef fish.
commentary: Te Angiangi Marine Reserve protects 446 hectares of rocky reef and sandy seabed near Aramoana. The reserve is remote and uncrowded, with excellent rock pool exploring and snorkelling when conditions allow. Crayfish and paua numbers are noticeably higher than surrounding areas.
review_summary: Off the beaten track but rewarding. Best in calm conditions. Rock pools are excellent at low tide.

name: Pekapeka Swamp — Wetland Restoration
short: Major wetland restoration project on the Heretaunga Plains — spotless crake and Australasian bittern habitat.
commentary: Pekapeka Swamp is one of the last remnants of the vast wetlands that once covered the Heretaunga Plains. Restoration efforts are underway to enhance habitat for nationally threatened species including Australasian bittern, spotless crake, and fernbird.
review_summary: Important restoration project. Bird viewing requires patience and binoculars. Best at dawn.

name: Gannet Beach Adventures — Clifton
short: Tractor-trailer rides along the beach to Cape Kidnappers gannet colony — a unique NZ wildlife tour.
commentary: Gannet Beach Adventures runs tractor-trailer tours from Clifton along the beach to the Cape Kidnappers gannet colony. The ride itself is an experience, passing beneath eroding clay cliffs with visible geological layers millions of years old. Booking essential in peak season.
review_summary: Great fun for all ages. The tractor ride is half the experience. Book ahead in summer.

name: Opouahi Scenic Reserve — Bush Birds
short: Remnant lowland podocarp forest with kaka, kererū, and rifleman in the Hawke's Bay hinterland.
commentary: Opouahi Scenic Reserve protects a rare remnant of lowland podocarp forest in the Maungaharuru Range foothills. The forest is home to kaka, kererū, rifleman, and long-tailed cuckoo. A short loop track passes through impressive rimu and matai trees.
review_summary: Lovely bush walk with good bird variety. The old-growth trees are impressive. Track is well maintained.

name: Lake Tūtira — Wetland Birds
short: Scenic lake with excellent waterbird viewing — black swan, dabchick, and NZ scaup on calm waters.
commentary: Lake Tūtira, made famous by Herbert Guthrie-Smith's book Tutira, is a picturesque lake surrounded by regenerating bush. Black swan, NZ dabchick, grey teal, NZ scaup, and shag species are easily observed from the shore and walking tracks around the lake.
review_summary: Beautiful lake with good bird variety. The loop walk is pleasant. Historical significance adds interest.

name: Blowhard Bush Scenic Reserve — Kiwi Habitat
short: Remote bush reserve with intensive kiwi management — North Island brown kiwi calling at night.
commentary: Blowhard Bush in the Kaweka Range foothills is part of DOC's kiwi recovery programme for Hawke's Bay. Intensive stoat trapping protects breeding kiwi. The reserve's remoteness means excellent native bush with minimal human disturbance. Night listening possible from the track edges.
review_summary: For serious birders and kiwi enthusiasts. Remote access adds to the experience. Best in breeding season.

name: Ahuriri Estuary — Wading Birds
short: Inner harbour estuary in Napier with excellent shorebird viewing — godwit, pied stilt, and oystercatcher.
commentary: The Ahuriri Estuary in Napier's inner harbour is one of Hawke's Bay's most accessible birding sites. Bar-tailed godwit, pied stilt, variable oystercatcher, and royal spoonbill are regular visitors. The Westshore Wildlife Reserve on the estuary margin provides good viewing.
review_summary: Easy access birding in town. Best at low tide. The Westshore end has the best viewing spots.

name: Mahia Peninsula — Blue Penguin Nesting
short: Little blue penguins nest along the rocky Mahia Peninsula coast — watch them come ashore at dusk.
commentary: The Mahia Peninsula's rocky coastline provides nesting habitat for a population of little blue penguins. The Mahia Beach area and rocky outcrops around the peninsula headlands are the best spots. Dusk viewing from a respectful distance during breeding season (Sep-Feb).
review_summary: Beautiful remote setting. Penguin viewing requires patience and timing. The peninsula itself is worth exploring.

name: Shine Falls — Boundary Stream Ecology
short: 58-metre waterfall walk through regenerating bush — longfin eels and native fish in the stream below.
commentary: The track to Shine Falls passes through Boundary Stream Mainland Island, offering both a spectacular 58-metre waterfall and excellent ecological interpretation. The stream below the falls holds longfin eels and native fish species. Bush birds are abundant along the track.
review_summary: Stunning waterfall with great bush birding on the way. Track is moderate. Combine with Boundary Stream walks.

name: Queen Charlotte Sound — Dolphin Watch
short: Bottlenose and dusky dolphins in the sheltered waters of Queen Charlotte Sound — cruise encounters.
commentary: Queen Charlotte Sound's calm, deep waters are home to resident bottlenose dolphins and visiting dusky dolphins. Several operators run nature cruises from Picton that combine dolphin watching with scenic exploration of the sound's bays and islands. King shag and little blue penguin also seen.
review_summary: Beautiful cruise with good dolphin sighting rates. The sound itself is spectacular. Combine with a mail boat run.

name: Long Island Marine Reserve
short: Marlborough Sounds marine reserve — blue cod, crayfish, and kelp forests in crystal-clear water.
commentary: Long Island Marine Reserve in Queen Charlotte Sound protects 619 hectares of marine habitat. Blue cod grow to impressive sizes, crayfish are abundant, and kelp forests shelter diverse reef communities. Accessible by water taxi from Picton. Snorkelling and diving.
review_summary: Impressive marine life. Water taxi access adds to the adventure. Best in calm conditions.

name: Duffers Reef — King Shag Colony
short: One of only four breeding colonies of the critically rare king shag — found only in the Marlborough Sounds.
commentary: The king shag (kawau) is one of the world's rarest cormorants, with only about 600 individuals, all in the Marlborough Sounds. Duffers Reef hosts one of the four breeding colonies. Boat tours from Picton pass at a respectful distance for viewing.
review_summary: Rare bird sighting opportunity. Boat tours provide good viewing distance. Binoculars essential.

name: Motuara Island Bird Sanctuary
short: Predator-free island sanctuary — saddleback, South Island robin, and king shag in Queen Charlotte Sound.
commentary: Motuara Island was cleared of predators and now hosts translocated South Island saddleback, South Island robin, and yellow-crowned kakariki. The island is also a king shag roosting site. Water taxi access from Picton with walking tracks to the summit viewpoint.
review_summary: Outstanding birding. The saddleback and robins are very confiding. Water taxi trip adds to the experience.

name: Cape Campbell — Fur Seal Colony
short: NZ fur seals haul out along the wild Marlborough coast near Cape Campbell — stunning coastal scenery.
commentary: The rocky coastline around Cape Campbell hosts a growing NZ fur seal colony. Accessible via a coastal walk from the Awatere Valley road end. The seals are best viewed from a safe distance on the rock platforms. Spectacular views of the Kaikoura Ranges across the strait.
review_summary: Wild and scenic. The seals are impressive but keep your distance. Remote access adds to the experience.

name: Wairau Lagoons Wetland
short: Extensive wetland system at the mouth of the Wairau River — banded rail, bittern, and royal spoonbill.
commentary: The Wairau Lagoons are a significant wetland at the mouth of the Wairau River near Blenheim. The diverse habitats support banded rail, Australasian bittern, royal spoonbill, and marsh crake. Walking and cycling tracks provide good viewing access.
review_summary: Excellent birding close to Blenheim. The cycle trail is a good way to explore. Best early morning.

name: White's Bay — Little Blue Penguin Nesting
short: Little blue penguins nest in the rock crevices around White's Bay — dusk viewing in a beautiful setting.
commentary: White's Bay, a short drive from Blenheim, hosts a small colony of little blue penguins that nest in rock crevices and under boulders along the shore. Dusk viewing during breeding season can be rewarding. The bay itself is a popular picnic and swimming spot.
review_summary: Lovely bay with bonus penguin viewing. Timing is everything — arrive at dusk. Quiet and uncrowded.

name: Pelorus Bridge — Giant Longfin Eels
short: Crystal-clear river pools with giant longfin eels and native fish — walk through beautiful beech forest.
commentary: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve, famous as a Hobbit filming location, has crystal-clear swimming holes where you can see giant longfin eels in the pools. The surrounding beech forest holds bellbird, tomtit, and kereru. Short and long loop walks available.
review_summary: Beautiful spot. The eels are fascinating — some are enormous. The forest walk is excellent. Great for families.

name: Para Wetland — Swamp Restoration
short: Community-led wetland restoration supporting fernbird, spotless crake, and marsh crake.
commentary: Para Wetland near Blenheim is a community-driven restoration project re-establishing native wetland habitat. Fernbird, spotless crake, and marsh crake have been recorded. The site demonstrates how even small wetland remnants can support significant biodiversity.
review_summary: Nice community conservation project. Small but productive for birding. Best visited with local knowledge.

name: Maud Island Scientific Reserve
short: Predator-free island reserve housing some of NZ's rarest species — Hamilton's frog, kakapo recovery base.
commentary: Maud Island in Pelorus Sound is one of DOC's most important scientific reserves. Predator-free, it has been a key site for kakapo recovery and houses populations of Hamilton's frog and Maud Island frog — two of the world's most primitive frog species. Limited guided visits available.
review_summary: Incredible conservation site. Very restricted access but DOC occasionally runs guided visits. Unique wildlife.

name: Vernon Lagoons — Wading Bird Haven
short: Coastal lagoon system supporting diverse wading birds — pied stilt, godwit, and wrybill.
commentary: The Vernon Lagoons near Blenheim are shallow coastal lagoons that attract diverse wading birds including pied stilt, bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, and banded dotterel. Access is easy along the lagoon margins. An underrated birding site.
review_summary: Underrated birding spot. Easy access from Blenheim. Low tide is best for wader viewing.

name: Abel Tasman Tonga Island — Fur Seal Colony
short: NZ fur seal breeding colony on Tonga Island — kayak past basking seals in Abel Tasman National Park.
commentary: Tonga Island, just off the Abel Tasman coast, hosts a significant NZ fur seal breeding colony. Kayakers paddle past the haul-out and can observe seals from the water at a safe distance. During breeding season (Nov-Jan), pups play in the shallows.
review_summary: Highlights of any Abel Tasman kayak trip. The seals are incredibly photogenic. Maintain 20m distance.

name: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary
short: NZ's largest predator-fenced urban sanctuary — kaka, bellbird, and South Island robin minutes from Nelson CBD.
commentary: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary is enclosed by a 14 km predator-proof fence, making it NZ's largest fenced urban sanctuary. Kaka, South Island robin, bellbird, and other species flourish in the regenerating bush. Walking tracks range from gentle strolls to hill climbs. Just 10 minutes from central Nelson.
review_summary: Outstanding urban sanctuary. The kaka are delightful. Easy access from Nelson. Tracks for all fitness levels.

name: Pepin Island — Little Blue Penguin Colony
short: Little blue penguins nest around the rocky shores of Pepin Island near Cable Bay.
commentary: Pepin Island at the end of Cable Bay Road hosts a colony of little blue penguins that nest in rock crevices around the headland. Dusk visits during breeding season offer the best chance of seeing penguins coming ashore. The walk around the headland is scenic.
review_summary: Pleasant coastal walk with penguin potential. Timing at dusk is key. Cable Bay area is beautiful.

name: Kahurangi National Park — Long-Tailed Bat Habitat
short: Ancient forests harboring NZ's endangered long-tailed bats — listen for echolocation clicks at dusk.
commentary: Kahurangi National Park's old-growth beech and podocarp forests provide important habitat for the long-tailed bat (pekapeka). The Oparara and Wangapeka areas are known bat habitats. Dusk at forest edges offers the best chance of seeing bats foraging. DOC bat monitors can help detection.
review_summary: Wild and remote. Bat sightings require patience and the right conditions. The forest is spectacular regardless.

name: Wainui Falls — Abel Tasman Freshwater Ecology
short: Bush walk to a beautiful waterfall with native fish, eels, and kōura (freshwater crayfish) in the pools.
commentary: The Wainui Falls track near Abel Tasman passes through lush podocarp forest to a photogenic waterfall. The stream and pools below contain longfin eels, kōkopu, and kōura (freshwater crayfish). Bush birds including bellbird and tui are abundant along the track.
review_summary: Beautiful walk to a pretty waterfall. The freshwater ecology adds interest. Easy track suitable for families.

name: Lake Rotoiti Nature Recovery Project
short: DOC's flagship South Island mainland island — intensive pest control brings bush birds back to honeydew beech forest.
commentary: The Lake Rotoiti Nature Recovery Project in Nelson Lakes National Park covers 825 hectares of honeydew beech forest. Intensive pest control has allowed kaka, South Island robin, rifleman, and great spotted kiwi to thrive. The Bellbird Walk and Lakeside Track offer excellent birding.
review_summary: Exceptional birding in stunning setting. The honeydew beech forest is unique. Kaka are often seen around the lake.

name: Waimea Estuary — Shorebird Haven
short: Nelson's major estuary supports godwit, spoonbill, and variable oystercatcher year-round.
commentary: The Waimea Estuary near Nelson is the region's most important shorebird site. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The estuary margins are accessible by foot and cycle trail. Best birding at low tide.
review_summary: Good birding close to town. Low tide is essential. The cycle trail makes for easy exploration.

name: Wharariki Beach — Seal Colony
short: Dramatic wind-sculpted beach with a NZ fur seal colony in sea caves and rock arches.
commentary: Wharariki Beach at the top of the South Island is one of NZ's most photogenic beaches — dramatic rock arches, sea caves, and wind-sculpted dunes. A NZ fur seal colony inhabits the offshore rock platforms and caves. The 20-minute walk crosses farmland with views to Farewell Spit.
review_summary: Breathtaking scenery with seals as a bonus. The rock arches are incredible. Can be very windy.

name: Heaphy Track — Nikau Palm Coast Birds
short: The Heaphy Track's coastal section passes through nikau palm forests alive with tui, bellbird, and weka.
commentary: The Heaphy Track's coastal section between Scotts Beach and the Kohaihai River is one of NZ's most unique birding environments. Nikau palm forests extend to the shore, filled with tui, bellbird, weka, and fernbird. The blue duck (whio) inhabits the upper river sections.
review_summary: Iconic Great Walk with incredible birdlife on the coastal section. Multi-day commitment required.

name: Oparara Basin — Cave & Forest Ecology
short: Ancient limestone arches, glowworm caves, and rare cave spiders in pristine old-growth forest.
commentary: The Oparara Basin near Karamea contains massive limestone arches, cave systems with glowworms, and the rare Nelson cave spider. The surrounding old-growth podocarp forest is one of the least disturbed in the country. Great spotted kiwi inhabit the forest floor.
review_summary: Otherworldly place. The arches are enormous. Allow time for the short walks to multiple cave entrances.

name: Mapua Estuary Bird Walk
short: Easy estuary walk near Mapua village — shags, herons, and oystercatchers on the tidal flats.
commentary: The Mapua Estuary walkway provides easy access to productive tidal flats where pied shag, white-faced heron, variable oystercatcher, and kingfisher are regularly seen. The walk connects to the Mapua waterfront village for refreshments afterward.
review_summary: Pleasant walk with reliable bird sightings. Family-friendly. Combines well with Mapua village visit.

name: Tōtaranui — Coastal Wildlife
short: Abel Tasman golden beach with little blue penguins, weka, and rich rock pool marine life.
commentary: Tōtaranui in Abel Tasman National Park offers a combination of golden sand beach, weka encounters, and little blue penguin nesting habitat along the headlands. The rock pools at each end of the beach are rich with marine invertebrates. Shearwaters and gannets visible offshore.
review_summary: Beautiful beach with plenty of wildlife. The weka are bold and entertaining. Great rock pools for kids.

name: Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve
short: World top-10 dive site — subtropical fish, giant kelp forests, and massive cave systems.
commentary: The Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve is consistently rated among the world's top ten dive sites. Subtropical currents bring warm water and exotic species rarely seen elsewhere in NZ. Massive archways, cave systems, and kelp forests support incredible biodiversity. No landing permitted — diving and snorkelling only.
review_summary: Bucket-list diving. Even snorkellers are blown away by the fish life. Several operators from Tutukaka.

name: Tiritiri Matangi Island — Open Sanctuary
short: NZ's most successful open sanctuary — tākahē, kōkako, hihi, and little spotted kiwi on a ferry-accessible island.
commentary: Tiritiri Matangi is the gold standard of NZ island restoration. Community volunteers planted 300,000 trees, and now endangered species including tākahē, kōkako, hihi (stitchbird), and little spotted kiwi thrive. A ferry from Auckland's Gulf Harbour makes it easily accessible for day trips.
review_summary: Best wildlife day trip from Auckland. The birds are incredibly tame. Guided walks highly recommended. Ferry books out.

name: Waipoua Forest — Kiwi Night Walk
short: Night walks among the ancient kauri to see North Island brown kiwi — guided by local Māori.
commentary: Footprints Waipoua runs evening guided walks through Waipoua Forest, home of Tāne Mahuta, to see North Island brown kiwi in the wild. The Māori guides share cultural stories of the kauri and the forest. Kiwi sighting rates are very high due to the healthy population here.
review_summary: Unforgettable experience. The Māori-guided cultural connection makes it special. Kiwi sightings near-guaranteed.

name: Bay of Islands — Dolphin Swimming
short: Swim with bottlenose dolphins in the warm waters of the Bay of Islands — NZ's most popular dolphin encounter.
commentary: The Bay of Islands is home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins. Several operators run swim-with-dolphin trips from Paihia and Russell, with high sighting rates year-round. The warm water and beautiful island scenery add to the experience.
review_summary: Popular for good reason. Dolphin encounters are magical. Operators are professional and conservation-minded.

name: Marsden Cross — Little Blue Penguin Colony
short: Little blue penguins nest among the rocks at Rangihoua Bay near the historic Marsden Cross.
commentary: The rocky coastline at Rangihoua Bay near the historic Marsden Cross site hosts a small colony of little blue penguins. The combination of historical significance and wildlife makes this a unique stop. Dusk visits during breeding season offer the best viewing.
review_summary: Combines history and wildlife. Penguin viewing requires patience and good timing. Beautiful bay.

name: Bream Head Scenic Reserve — Seabird Colony
short: Headland reserve with grey-faced petrel colonies and spectacular Hen and Chickens Islands views.
commentary: Bream Head is a rugged headland east of Whangarei with significant grey-faced petrel breeding colonies. The track to the summit is steep but rewarding, with panoramic views over the coast and to the Hen and Chickens Islands. Petrel activity is best at dusk during breeding season.
review_summary: Challenging but rewarding walk. Great seabird viewing. The views from the top are outstanding.

name: Urupukapuka Island — Pest-Free Haven
short: Predator-free island in the Bay of Islands — walking tracks, beaches, and native birds.
commentary: Urupukapuka Island, the largest island in the Bay of Islands, has been cleared of predators. Walking tracks cross the island through regenerating bush where North Island robin, bellbird, and dotterel thrive. Sandy beaches and clear snorkelling waters add to the appeal.
review_summary: Great day trip from Paihia. Easy walking with good bird variety. The beaches are beautiful.

name: Trounson Kauri Park — Giant Snail & Kiwi
short: Night walks reveal kiwi and Kauri giant snail — Northland's nocturnal treasures in ancient forest.
commentary: Trounson Kauri Park is a mainland island where intensive pest control has boosted kiwi numbers. DOC-guided night walks reveal North Island brown kiwi and the endemic Kauri snail (Paryphanta busbyi), one of the world's largest land snails. The ancient kauri trees are spectacular.
review_summary: Excellent night walk. Kiwi sightings are likely. The kauri snails are fascinatingly large. Book ahead.

name: Mangawhai Heads — NZ Fairy Tern Colony
short: One of the last breeding sites of the critically endangered NZ fairy tern — fewer than 40 remain.
commentary: Mangawhai Heads is one of only four breeding sites for the NZ fairy tern, the country's rarest bird with fewer than 40 individuals. DOC wardens protect nests during the breeding season (Oct-Feb). NZ dotterel also breed here. Observe from marked viewing areas only.
review_summary: Privilege to see NZ's rarest bird. Follow all DOC signage. The beach and sandspit are beautiful.

name: Karikari Peninsula — Dotterel & Shore Plover
short: Remote Northland peninsula with significant NZ dotterel nesting beaches and rare shore plover sightings.
commentary: The Karikari Peninsula's remote sandy beaches provide important nesting habitat for NZ dotterel. The dune-backed beaches are also occasional habitat for the rare shore plover. Community volunteer groups monitor and protect nests during the breeding season.
review_summary: Remote and uncrowded. Great for dotterel observation. The beaches are stunning. Keep dogs on leads.

name: Whangarei Heads — Backyard Kiwi
short: Community conservation success — kiwi now call in suburban backyards at Whangarei Heads.
commentary: The Backyard Kiwi project at Whangarei Heads is one of NZ's great community conservation success stories. Intensive trapping by local volunteers has boosted kiwi numbers so dramatically that residents regularly hear them calling at night from their homes.
review_summary: Inspiring community conservation. Hearing kiwi from a suburban garden is surreal. Best at night in winter.

name: Russell Forest — Bush Bird Haven
short: Native forest walks behind Russell with kaka, tui, kereru, and North Island robin.
commentary: The Russell Forest behind the historic Bay of Islands township offers excellent bush birding on well-maintained tracks. Intensive pest control has brought back kaka, tui in large numbers, kereru, and North Island robin. The Cape Brett track starts from this forest.
review_summary: Surprisingly good birding. The forest is lush and atmospheric. Multiple track options for different fitness levels.

name: Matakohe Limestone Island — Wildlife Refuge
short: Small predator-free island in Whangarei Harbour — banded rail, NZ robin, and red-crowned kakariki.
commentary: Matakohe Limestone Island in Whangarei Harbour has been predator-free since 2002. Banded rail, North Island robin, red-crowned kakariki, and pateke (brown teal) have been released. The community-run Friends of Matakohe-Limestone Island run regular guided visits by kayak or boat.
review_summary: Great community conservation. The guided visits are well run. Kayak access adds to the adventure.

name: Spirits Bay / Piwhane — Coastal Wildlife
short: Remote beach at the tip of NZ — NZ dotterel, fur seals, and dolphins in pristine waters.
commentary: Spirits Bay near Cape Reinga is one of NZ's most remote and pristine beaches. NZ dotterel nest on the dunes, NZ fur seals haul out on the headlands, and bottlenose dolphins frequent the bay. The remoteness and cultural significance (it's near the spirits' leaping place) make it special.
review_summary: Truly remote and beautiful. Self-contained camping available. The wildlife encounters feel authentic.

name: Maitai Bay — Reef Snorkelling
short: Warm, clear water snorkelling over reef systems — subtropical fish species at their southern limit.
commentary: Maitai Bay in the Karikari Peninsula area offers some of Northland's best snorkelling. The rocky reef shelters subtropical species rarely seen further south, including Lord Howe coral fish and mosaic leatherjacket. The water is among NZ's warmest.
review_summary: Excellent snorkelling in warm water. The fish variety surprises many visitors. Best in summer.

name: Tutukaka Coast Marine Reserve
short: Gateway to the Poor Knights Islands — mainland reefs with diverse marine life and dive operators.
commentary: The Tutukaka Coast serves as the gateway to the Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve, with several dive operators based here. The mainland coast itself offers excellent shore diving and snorkelling at spots like Matapōuri and Whale Bay. Marine life is abundant.
review_summary: Great base for Poor Knights diving. Mainland snorkelling is also excellent. Multiple operators to choose from.

name: Aroha Island Ecological Centre
short: Kiwi listening and night walks on a predator-controlled peninsula in Kerikeri Inlet.
commentary: Aroha Island near Kerikeri is a community-run ecological centre on a predator-controlled peninsula. Night walks offer excellent chances of hearing — and sometimes seeing — North Island brown kiwi. The centre also has a small wetland with fernbird and banded rail.
review_summary: Reliable kiwi encounters. The guides are knowledgeable and passionate. Camping and cabins available.

name: Royal Albatross Centre — Taiaroa Head
short: The world's only mainland royal albatross breeding colony — 3-metre wingspan birds nesting at Taiaroa Head.
commentary: Taiaroa Head on the Otago Peninsula hosts the world's only mainland breeding colony of northern royal albatross. With a wingspan of over 3 metres, these birds are extraordinary to watch. The Royal Albatross Centre offers guided tours with viewing hide access during breeding season.
review_summary: World-class wildlife experience. The albatross are magnificent. Guided tour essential. Book ahead.

name: Penguin Place — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Reserve
short: Private conservation reserve protecting NZ's most endangered penguin — approach via unique trench-hide system.
commentary: Penguin Place on the Otago Peninsula is a private conservation reserve for hoiho (yellow-eyed penguin), the world's rarest penguin. An innovative trench-and-hide system allows visitors to approach nesting birds without disturbance. Conservation levy funds habitat restoration and predator control.
review_summary: Unique penguin viewing experience. The trench system is clever and non-intrusive. Conservation funding model is excellent.

name: Pilots Beach — Blue Penguin Colony
short: Watch little blue penguins waddle ashore at dusk at the base of Taiaroa Head.
commentary: Pilots Beach at the base of Taiaroa Head on the Otago Peninsula hosts a monitored little blue penguin colony. Guided evening viewing from a grandstand allows visitors to watch penguins come ashore after dark. Numbers peak during breeding season.
review_summary: Reliable penguin viewing. The grandstand setup is well managed. Arrive early for best seats.

name: Sandfly Bay — NZ Sea Lion Colony
short: NZ sea lions haul out on this wild Otago Peninsula beach — one of the rarest pinnipeds on earth.
commentary: Sandfly Bay on the Otago Peninsula is a regular haul-out for NZ sea lions, one of the world's rarest pinnipeds with fewer than 10,000 remaining. Yellow-eyed penguins also nest in the dunes. Access via a steep sandy track. Maintain a 10-metre distance from all wildlife.
review_summary: Wild and beautiful. Sea lion encounters are thrilling. Keep your distance — they can be aggressive.

name: Moeraki — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Viewing
short: Yellow-eyed penguins nest near the famous Moeraki Boulders — watch them come ashore at Katiki Point.
commentary: Katiki Point near the Moeraki Boulders hosts a DOC-managed yellow-eyed penguin viewing hide. Late afternoon is the best time to see these endangered birds returning from the sea. NZ fur seals also haul out on the rocks below the lighthouse. Combine with a Moeraki Boulders visit.
review_summary: Great combination with the boulders. Penguin viewing requires patience. The DOC hide is well positioned.

name: Sinclair Wetlands — Birding Paradise
short: 1,200 hectares of Otago wetland — bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and abundant waterfowl.
commentary: Sinclair Wetlands between Dunedin and Balclutha is one of the South Island's most significant remaining wetlands. Over 70 bird species have been recorded, including Australasian bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and abundant waterfowl. Guided canoe tours available.
review_summary: Outstanding wetland birding. The canoe tour is the best way to experience it. Book ahead.

name: Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony
short: NZ's largest accessible little blue penguin colony — watch hundreds waddle ashore each evening.
commentary: The Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony is the largest easily accessible kororā colony in NZ, with over 200 birds returning to nest each evening during peak season. Grandstand and premium viewing options available. Daytime nest-box viewing and backstage tours also offered.
review_summary: Fantastic penguin experience. The numbers are impressive. Premium seating is worth the upgrade.

name: Hoopers Inlet — Wading Bird Estuary
short: Sheltered Otago Peninsula inlet with excellent shorebird viewing — godwit, spoonbill, and oystercatcher.
commentary: Hoopers Inlet on the Otago Peninsula is a productive tidal estuary supporting diverse wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The inlet is easily viewed from the road and several pull-off points.
review_summary: Easy roadside birding. Good variety of wading birds. Low tide is best. Combine with peninsula wildlife loop.

name: Aramoana Wildlife Refuge
short: Harbour mouth refuge with diverse birdlife — NZ dotterel, royal spoonbill, and Stewart Island shag.
commentary: Aramoana at the entrance to Otago Harbour is a designated wildlife management area. The salt marsh, mudflats, and rocky shore support diverse birdlife including NZ dotterel, royal spoonbill, and the southernmost Stewart Island shag colony. Walk the mole for pelagic seabird viewing.
review_summary: Good variety of habitats and birds in a small area. The mole walk is great for seabirds. Can be windy.

name: Macraes Flat — Grand & Otago Skink Habitat
short: Last stronghold of the critically endangered grand skink and Otago skink — unique NZ lizards.
commentary: Macraes Flat in inland Otago is the last significant population centre for the grand skink and Otago skink, both critically endangered. DOC runs an intensive predator control programme. The skinks bask on rock tors and are best seen on warm, sunny days. Guided tours available.
review_summary: Unique reptile viewing. Warm, sunny weather essential. The landscape is stark and beautiful. DOC can advise on access.

name: Shag Point — Fur Seal & Yellow-Eyed Penguin
short: Rocky headland with NZ fur seals and yellow-eyed penguin nesting — free roadside wildlife.
commentary: Shag Point between Oamaru and Dunedin hosts NZ fur seals on the rocks and yellow-eyed penguins nesting in the vegetation above. A short walk from the car park leads to viewing platforms. NZ sea lions occasionally visit. Free and easily accessible.
review_summary: Easy free wildlife viewing. Great variety of species. Short detour off SH1 well worth it.

name: Waikouaiti Estuary — Wetland Walk
short: Coastal estuary north of Dunedin with white-faced heron, pied stilt, and seasonal godwit.
commentary: The Waikouaiti Estuary provides a pleasant wetland walk with diverse wading bird viewing. White-faced heron, pied stilt, NZ shoveler, and seasonal bar-tailed godwit frequent the area. The walking track follows the estuary edge through coastal scrub.
review_summary: Quiet and uncrowded. Good birding at low tide. Easy walk suitable for families.

name: Sutton Salt Lake — Unique Ecology
short: NZ's only inland salt lake — unique ecosystem supporting species found nowhere else.
commentary: Sutton Salt Lake near Middlemarch is NZ's only naturally occurring inland saline lake. The lake and surrounding tussock support a unique community of salt-tolerant plants and invertebrates found nowhere else in the country. The landscape is stark Central Otago at its most atmospheric.
review_summary: Unique geological feature. The surrounding tussock landscape is beautiful. Best combined with Taieri Gorge trip.

name: Katiki Point Lighthouse — Penguin & Seal Viewing
short: Historic lighthouse headland with yellow-eyed penguins, fur seals, and breeding NZ fur seals.
commentary: Katiki Point lighthouse near Moeraki overlooks a rocky coast where NZ fur seals haul out and yellow-eyed penguins nest. A DOC viewing hide provides close penguin viewing without disturbance. The lighthouse adds historical interest. Free access from the car park.
review_summary: Excellent free wildlife viewing. The hide is well positioned. Late afternoon best for penguins.

name: Bushy Beach — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Hide
short: DOC viewing hide overlooking one of Oamaru's yellow-eyed penguin nesting beaches.
commentary: Bushy Beach south of Oamaru has a DOC viewing hide overlooking the sandy beach where yellow-eyed penguins come ashore in late afternoon. The hide allows viewing without disturbing the birds. NZ fur seals sometimes rest on the rocks at the south end.
review_summary: Good free penguin viewing from the hide. Late afternoon is essential. Don't walk on the beach during penguin time.

name: Kiwi Birdlife Park
short: See kiwi, tuatara, kea, and native bush birds in a compact wildlife park beside Lake Wakatipu.
commentary: Kiwi Birdlife Park in central Queenstown offers close encounters with kiwi in a nocturnal house, plus tuatara, kea, morepork, and native bush birds. The conservation show is informative and entertaining. A great family activity in the heart of Queenstown.
review_summary: Reliable kiwi viewing in central Queenstown. Good for families. The kea are entertaining.

name: Routeburn Track — Alpine Bird Country
short: Great Walk through mountain beech forest and alpine meadows — kea, rock wren, and whio territory.
commentary: The Routeburn Track passes through habitats ranging from beech forest to alpine herbfields. Kea are common around Harris Saddle, rock wren inhabit the alpine zone, and whio (blue duck) live in the valley streams. The bird diversity on multi-day walks is outstanding.
review_summary: World-class tramping with great birdlife. Kea encounters at Harris Saddle are memorable. Book huts well ahead.

name: Dart River — Blue Duck (Whio) Habitat
short: Pristine river valley where the endangered whio (blue duck) lives — jet boat and kayak access.
commentary: The Dart River valley near Glenorchy is prime whio (blue duck) habitat. These rare ducks live only on clean, fast-flowing rivers, making the Dart one of their strongholds. Jet boat operators sometimes spot whio on the riverbanks. Kayaking offers quieter viewing opportunities.
review_summary: Beautiful river setting. Whio sightings are a bonus — they're well camouflaged. Jet boat trip is spectacular.

name: Milford Sound — Bottlenose Dolphins & Seals
short: Bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins in the iconic fiord.
commentary: Milford Sound cruises regularly encounter bottlenose dolphins, NZ fur seal colonies on the rocks, and occasionally rare Fiordland crested penguins. The marine environment of the fiord — with a freshwater layer over seawater — creates unique viewing conditions. Multiple cruise operators.
review_summary: The wildlife adds another dimension to the scenery. Seal sightings are almost guaranteed. Penguins are a lucky bonus.

name: Glenorchy Lagoon Walkway — Wetland Birds
short: Easy boardwalk around a scenic lagoon — paradise shelduck, scaup, and crested grebe with mountain backdrop.
commentary: The Glenorchy Lagoon boardwalk circles a beautiful wetland at the head of Lake Wakatipu. Paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, grey teal, and Australasian crested grebe are regularly seen. The mountain backdrop — Earnslaw and the Humboldt Range — makes this one of NZ's most scenic bird walks.
review_summary: Beautiful setting for an easy walk. The crested grebes are a highlight. Mountain reflections on calm mornings.

name: Bob's Cove — Wetland & Beech Forest Walk
short: Lakeside wetland and beech forest walk near Queenstown — bellbird, fantail, and rifleman in the bush.
commentary: Bob's Cove between Queenstown and Glenorchy offers a short loop walk through beech forest and lakeside wetland. The bush is alive with bellbird, fantail, rifleman, and tomtit. The wetland attracts grey teal, paradise shelduck, and pied stilt.
review_summary: Lovely short walk with good bird variety. Easy to access between Queenstown and Glenorchy. Peaceful.

name: Moke Lake — Mountain Lake Birding
short: Scenic mountain lake with paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, and bush birds in the surrounding forest.
commentary: Moke Lake is a picturesque mountain lake 10 km from Queenstown. The lake margins attract paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, and grey teal. The surrounding bush holds bellbird, grey warbler, and fantail. The loop walk is easy and scenic.
review_summary: Peaceful alternative to busy Queenstown. Good bird variety. The loop walk is pleasant. Free camping available.

name: Lake Wakatipu — Crested Grebe Viewing
short: Australasian crested grebes breed on Lake Wakatipu — watch their elaborate courtship displays.
commentary: Lake Wakatipu supports a population of Australasian crested grebes (kāmana), estimated at fewer than 500 nationally. The Queenstown Gardens lakefront and Frankton Arm are good viewing spots. Their elaborate courtship dance, with synchronized head-shaking and weed-carrying, is spectacular.
review_summary: Unusual bird viewing in an urban setting. The grebes are beautiful. Patience needed to spot them on the large lake.

name: Rees Valley — Kea Country
short: Mountain valley walk where kea, rock wren, and NZ falcon patrol the alpine zone.
commentary: The Rees Valley track from Glenorchy enters prime kea habitat as it climbs towards the Rees Saddle. Kea are commonly seen around the mountain huts, NZ falcon (kārearea) hunt the tussock, and rock wren inhabit the high ground. The valley scenery is spectacular.
review_summary: Excellent alpine birding in stunning scenery. Kea are reliable around the huts. Multi-day tramp for full experience.

name: Twelve Mile Delta — Lakeside Birding
short: Lakeside delta area with diverse waterbirds — shoveler, crested grebe, and paradise shelduck.
commentary: The Twelve Mile Delta on Lake Wakatipu is a popular camping and picnic area that also offers excellent waterbird viewing. NZ shoveler, Australasian crested grebe, paradise shelduck, and black shag are regular. The adjacent bush has bellbird and grey warbler.
review_summary: Easy lakeside birding. Good variety. Combine with a picnic. Free camping area nearby.

name: Rainbow Springs — National Kiwi Hatchery
short: See kiwi chicks being raised for release — NZ's most important kiwi hatchery hatches 150+ chicks per year.
commentary: Rainbow Springs hosts the National Kiwi Hatchery Aotearoa, which hatches and rears over 150 kiwi chicks annually under the Operation Nest Egg programme. Behind-the-scenes tours show the incubation and rearing process. The nocturnal kiwi house offers reliable viewing of adult birds.
review_summary: Must-visit for understanding kiwi conservation. The hatchery tour is fascinating. Kiwi viewing is excellent.

name: Whakarewarewa Living Village — Kiwi Encounters
short: See kiwi in a geothermal setting — the living village combines Māori culture with native wildlife viewing.
commentary: Te Whakarewarewa The Living Māori Village offers a unique combination of Māori cultural experience and wildlife viewing in an active geothermal setting. Kiwi reside in the village grounds, and guided tours include opportunities to see them along with native bush birds.
review_summary: Unique cultural and wildlife combination. The kiwi viewing in a geothermal setting is memorable.

name: Lake Okareka Wetland — Bush & Water Birds
short: Scenic lake with NZ dabchick, crested grebe, and excellent bush birding on the walkway.
commentary: Lake Okareka near Rotorua has a walking track through native bush along the lake edge. The lake supports NZ dabchick, Australasian crested grebe, NZ scaup, and grey teal. The bush holds tui, bellbird, kereru, and North Island robin. The predator-control programme has boosted bird numbers.
review_summary: Pleasant walk with good bird variety. The lake setting is peaceful. Best in early morning.

name: Sulphur Point — Geothermal Wetland Birding
short: Geothermal wetland on Rotorua lakefront — black swan, shag, and pied stilt in a steaming landscape.
commentary: Sulphur Point wildlife refuge on the Rotorua lakefront combines geothermal features with productive bird habitat. The warm water attracts black swan, NZ scaup, pied shag, pied stilt, and grey teal. The viewing platform and boardwalk provide excellent access.
review_summary: Unusual birding in a geothermal setting. Easy access from town. Good variety of waterbirds.

name: Blue Lake / Tikitapu — Freshwater Ecology
short: Crystal-clear volcanic lake with native fish and koura — walk the scenic bush track around the shore.
commentary: Blue Lake / Tikitapu is one of the Rotorua Lakes' clearest, with visibility of up to 10 metres. The lake supports populations of koaro, common bully, and freshwater crayfish (koura). The loop track through native bush is excellent for tui, bellbird, and kereru.
review_summary: Beautiful lake walk. The water clarity is impressive. Good bush birding on the track.

name: Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre
short: NZ's only raptor conservation centre — see kārearea (NZ falcon) and Australasian harrier up close.
commentary: Wingspan in Rotorua is dedicated to NZ's birds of prey, particularly the kārearea (NZ falcon). The centre breeds and releases falcons and offers close-up viewing and flying displays. Learn about the ecology and threats facing NZ's only native raptor.
review_summary: Excellent raptor centre. The flying displays are thrilling. Important conservation work. Good for families.

name: Waimangu Volcanic Valley — Geothermal Ecology
short: Walk through the world's youngest geothermal ecosystem — unique extremophile biology and thermal bird habitat.
commentary: Waimangu Volcanic Valley was created by the 1886 Tarawera eruption and is the world's youngest geothermal ecosystem. The thermal pools support unique extremophile bacteria and algae. The warm waters attract bird species not found elsewhere in the region. Excellent self-guided or guided walks.
review_summary: Fascinating combination of geology and biology. The walk downhill to the lake is excellent. Highly recommended.

name: Ōkere Falls — Glowworm Caves
short: Free glowworm viewing in caves near Ōkere Falls — thousands of bioluminescent larvae in the dark.
commentary: The caves near Ōkere Falls on the Kaituna River hold impressive colonies of Arachnocampa luminosa glowworms. A short walk from the car park leads to the cave entrance. No booking required — bring a torch for the walk and turn it off inside. Free and family-friendly.
review_summary: Great free alternative to Waitomo. Short easy walk to the caves. Best on dark, humid evenings.

name: Whirinaki Forest — Kiwi & Kokako Territory
short: Ancient podocarp forest with North Island brown kiwi, kokako, and kaka — one of NZ's finest bush walks.
commentary: Whirinaki Te Pua-a-Tāne Conservation Park contains some of NZ's most magnificent podocarp forest. Intensive pest control supports North Island brown kiwi, kokako, kaka, and kereru. The Whirinaki Track passes through towering rimu, matai, and kahikatea. Dawn walks are best for kokako.
review_summary: Magnificent forest. The bird life is outstanding. Dawn walks for kokako are unforgettable. Tracks well maintained.

name: Hell's Gate — Geothermal Biology
short: Active geothermal park with unique thermophilic life — bacteria, algae, and insects adapted to extreme heat.
commentary: Hell's Gate (Tikitere) is Rotorua's most active geothermal park, with boiling pools, mud volcanoes, and steaming fumaroles. Beyond the spectacular geology, the park hosts unique thermophilic (heat-loving) bacteria and algae communities, plus insects found only in geothermal environments.
review_summary: Raw and powerful geothermal experience. The biology interpretation adds depth. The mud bath is fun.

name: Lake Rotoehu Forest — Bush Bird Walk
short: Regenerating native forest with excellent tui, bellbird, and North Island robin populations.
commentary: The bush around Lake Rotoehu has benefited from community pest control, resulting in excellent bush bird populations. Tui, bellbird, kereru, North Island robin, and whitehead are all common. Several short walking tracks provide access through the regenerating forest.
review_summary: Good bush birding near Rotorua. The tracks are easy and well maintained. Morning visits are best.

name: Paradise Valley Springs Wildlife Park
short: Natural spring-fed park with giant trout, native eels, and native bush birds in a garden setting.
commentary: Paradise Valley Springs features crystal-clear spring water pools where giant rainbow and brown trout are easily observed. The park also has longfin eels, native bush walks with tui and kereru, and a small native wildlife area. Family-friendly with a gentle, park-like atmosphere.
review_summary: Nice family-friendly attraction. The trout viewing is impressive. Pleasant bush walks.

name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara — Bird Sanctuary
short: Predator-free island sanctuary — saddleback, yellowhead, Stewart Island robin, and weka in ancient forest.
commentary: Ulva Island in Paterson Inlet, Stewart Island, is a predator-free open sanctuary where endangered birds thrive in ancient podocarp forest. Saddleback, yellowhead (mohua), Stewart Island robin, rifleman, and weka are easily seen on the walking tracks. Water taxi from Golden Bay wharf.
review_summary: NZ's finest bird island. The birds are incredibly tame. Allow at least 3 hours. Water taxi runs regularly.

name: Stewart Island — Wild Kiwi Encounter
short: See wild kiwi on the beach — Stewart Island tokoeka forage on the sand at night.
commentary: Stewart Island / Rakiura is the only place in NZ where you can reliably see kiwi in the wild without a special setup. Stewart Island tokoeka come out to forage on Ocean Beach at night. Guided tours from Oban take you by water taxi and then walking to viewing spots.
review_summary: Life-changing kiwi experience. Seeing them on the beach is magical. Book the guided tour — it's worth every cent.

name: Curio Bay — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Colony
short: Yellow-eyed and little blue penguins alongside a 180-million-year-old petrified forest.
commentary: Curio Bay in the Catlins hosts both yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) and little blue penguins, plus the remarkable Jurassic-era petrified forest on the rock platform. A DOC viewing hide allows close penguin observation without disturbance. Late afternoon is best for hoiho.
review_summary: Incredible combination of penguins and petrified forest. The DOC hide is well positioned. Free and uncrowded.

name: Nugget Point — Seal & Seabird Colony
short: Iconic lighthouse headland with fur seals, sea lions, and diverse seabird colonies on the 'nugget' rocks.
commentary: Nugget Point in the Catlins is one of the South Island's most dramatic wildlife viewing locations. The rocky stacks ('nuggets') host NZ fur seals, elephant seals, royal spoonbill, and breeding spotted shag. The lighthouse walk offers panoramic views over the colony.
review_summary: Iconic NZ wildlife site. The lighthouse walk is a must. Binoculars essential for the seabird colonies.

name: Catlins Coast — Hector's Dolphins
short: Hector's dolphins play in the surf along the Catlins coast — watch from the beach at Porpoise Bay.
commentary: Porpoise Bay in the Catlins is one of the most reliable places to see Hector's dolphins from shore. The small dolphins often surf the waves close to the beach, sometimes within metres of swimmers. The bay is also popular for surfing and has a beautiful sandy beach.
review_summary: Amazing to see dolphins from the beach. They come incredibly close. Best in summer. Free.

name: Jackson Bay — Fiordland Crested Penguin
short: NZ's rarest penguin nests in the rainforest-clad Fiordland coast — breeding season viewing July-November.
commentary: The Fiordland crested penguin (tawaki) is NZ's rarest penguin, nesting in the dense rainforest along the Fiordland and West Coast coastlines. DOC monitoring sites offer limited viewing opportunities during breeding season. The penguins' forest nesting habitat makes them unique among penguin species.
review_summary: Rare and special penguin. Limited viewing opportunities. Breeding season only. Check with DOC for access.

name: Lake Hauroko — Deep Lake Forest Birds
short: NZ's deepest lake surrounded by pristine beech and podocarp forest — yellowhead, kaka, and robin.
commentary: Lake Hauroko, at 462 metres NZ's deepest lake, is surrounded by untouched beech and podocarp forest. The birdlife is outstanding — yellowhead (mohua), kaka, South Island robin, rifleman, and brown creeper. Access is via a winding forestry road. The atmosphere of remoteness is powerful.
review_summary: Remote and beautiful. The birdlife is exceptional. The road in is rough but worth it. Few other visitors.

name: Riverton Estuary — Wetland Birds
short: Productive Southland estuary with bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and South Island pied oystercatcher.
commentary: The Riverton (Aparima) Estuary is an important Southland wetland supporting diverse wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit arrive each spring from Alaska, joining resident royal spoonbill, pied oystercatcher, and pied stilt. Easy viewing from the estuary margins and the Riverton walkway.
review_summary: Good birding in a pleasant setting. The town of Riverton is worth exploring too. Low tide best for waders.
