name: Bluff Hill / Motupōhue — Seabird Viewing
short: NZ's southernmost mainland seabird viewing — titi (sooty shearwater) streaming past at dusk in season.
commentary: Bluff Hill lookout offers excellent seabird viewing from NZ's southernmost mainland point. During autumn, millions of titi (sooty shearwater) stream past heading to their breeding islands. NZ fur seals sometimes haul out below. The views to Stewart Island are panoramic.
review_summary: Great for seabird watching in season. The views to Stewart Island are worth the drive alone.

name: Mason Bay — Wild Kiwi on the Beach
short: Remote Stewart Island beach where kiwi forage in daylight — one of the world's great wildlife experiences.
commentary: Mason Bay on Stewart Island's west coast is one of the few places where kiwi forage on the beach during daylight hours. The remote location requires a multi-hour tramp or charter flight. The beach is vast and wild, and the experience of watching kiwi foraging in the open is unparalleled.
review_summary: One of NZ's ultimate wildlife experiences. Daylight kiwi on the beach. Remote but absolutely worth the effort.

name: Waiau River — Blue Duck Habitat
short: Clean Fiordland river supporting endangered whio (blue duck) — NZ's rarest waterfowl.
commentary: The Waiau River in Fiordland is one of the key South Island habitats for whio (blue duck). These rare ducks require clean, fast-flowing rivers with stable riverbanks for nesting. DOC's whio recovery programme operates trapping lines along the river to protect breeding pairs.
review_summary: Wild river setting. Whio sightings require patience and keen eyes. The river is beautiful regardless.

name: Lake Wilkie — Bog Ecology Walk
short: Boardwalk loop through a unique sphagnum bog ecosystem — podocarp succession in miniature.
commentary: Lake Wilkie in the Catlins is a small glacial lake slowly being overtaken by sphagnum bog and podocarp forest. The short boardwalk loop demonstrates ecological succession beautifully — open water to sphagnum to shrubs to forest. Fernbird and tomtit in the surrounding bush.
review_summary: Fascinating ecological walk. Short and easy. Great for kids to learn about succession. Well-interpreted.

name: Roaring Bay — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Hide
short: DOC viewing hide overlooking a yellow-eyed penguin beach in the Catlins — late afternoon viewing.
commentary: Roaring Bay near Nugget Point has a DOC viewing hide positioned to watch yellow-eyed penguins come ashore in late afternoon. The hide allows extended observation without disturbing the birds. NZ fur seals sometimes rest on the nearby rocks.
review_summary: Excellent free penguin viewing. The hide is well positioned. Patience rewarded in late afternoon.

name: Doubtful Sound — Fiordland Wildlife
short: Bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins in NZ's most remote accessible fiord.
commentary: Doubtful Sound is quieter and more remote than Milford, with excellent wildlife. A resident pod of about 60 bottlenose dolphins inhabits the fiord, NZ fur seals bask on the rocks, and Fiordland crested penguins nest along the shore. Overnight cruises offer the best wildlife encounters.
review_summary: More remote and intimate than Milford. The dolphins are almost always seen. Overnight cruise recommended.

name: Omaui Penguin Haven
short: Community-run penguin conservation project protecting little blue and yellow-eyed penguins near Invercargill.
commentary: Omaui near Invercargill is a community-run penguin conservation project protecting both little blue and yellow-eyed penguins. Volunteer-maintained predator traps and nest boxes have boosted numbers. The community welcomes respectful visitors during penguin time (dusk for blues, late afternoon for hoiho).
review_summary: Wonderful community conservation. Small-scale and authentic. Contact the group for visiting advice.

name: Te Anau Glowworm Caves
short: Boat trip across Lake Te Anau to limestone caves filled with thousands of glowworms.
commentary: The Te Anau Glowworm Caves are accessed by boat across Lake Te Anau. Inside, the limestone caves host thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa glowworms creating a stunning bioluminescent display. The cave system includes waterfalls, whirlpools, and a silent glowworm grotto.
review_summary: Magical experience. The boat trip adds to the atmosphere. Smaller and more intimate than Waitomo.

name: New River Estuary / Oreti — Wading Birds
short: Major Southland estuary with internationally significant wader populations — bar-tailed godwit and red knot.
commentary: The New River Estuary near Invercargill is internationally significant for migratory wading birds. Thousands of bar-tailed godwit and red knot arrive from the Arctic each spring. Banded dotterel, wrybill, and South Island pied oystercatcher are also present. The Oreti Beach road provides access.
review_summary: Outstanding wader birding. Massive godwit flocks in spring/autumn. Scope essential. Best at low tide.

name: Mahia Peninsula — Marine Life Snorkelling
short: Sheltered reef snorkelling with diverse fish life at the meeting of warm and cold ocean currents.
commentary: Mahia Peninsula sits where warm East Cape currents meet cooler southern waters, creating unusually diverse marine life. Rocky reef snorkelling around the headlands reveals species from both warm and cool water communities. The sheltered eastern bays offer the calmest conditions.
review_summary: Surprisingly good marine life. The water can be warmer than expected. Best in calm conditions.

name: Young Nicks Head — Coastal Birdlife
short: Historic headland with NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and white-fronted tern colony.
commentary: Young Nicks Head, the first land sighted by Cook's crew in 1769, is now primarily significant for its coastal birdlife. NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and white-fronted tern breed here. The walk to the headland passes through farmland with views along Poverty Bay.
review_summary: Historical and ecological interest combined. Easy coastal walk. Good for dotterel and tern viewing.

name: Raukumara Conservation Park — Kiwi Sanctuary
short: Remote East Coast bush with North Island brown kiwi in pristine podocarp forest.
commentary: The Raukumara Range is one of the most remote forested areas in the North Island, with significant populations of North Island brown kiwi. DOC's pest control in targeted areas is boosting kiwi survival. The forest is dense and challenging to access but the wilderness experience is unmatched.
review_summary: Remote wilderness. For experienced trampers only. The kiwi population is nationally significant.

name: East Cape — Dolphin Encounters
short: Common dolphins frequently seen from shore along the East Cape — sometimes in pods of hundreds.
commentary: The waters around East Cape attract large pods of common dolphins, often visible from the coastal road. Pods can number in the hundreds and their acrobatic displays are spectacular. The lighthouse at East Cape is the easternmost point on the NZ mainland.
review_summary: Exciting when the dolphins are in. Great coastal drive regardless. The lighthouse is worth visiting.

name: Wherowhero Lagoon — Wetland Walk
short: Coastal lagoon walkway in Gisborne with pied stilt, royal spoonbill, and white-faced heron.
commentary: Wherowhero Lagoon on the Gisborne coast provides an easy wetland walk with reliable bird sightings. Pied stilt, royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, and paradise shelduck are regulars. The boardwalk is accessible for pushchairs and wheelchairs.
review_summary: Easy, accessible wetland walk in town. Good bird variety. Pleasant at any time of day.

name: Cook's Cove Walkway — Coastal Wildlife
short: Historic coastal walkway to a dramatic cove — fur seals, seabirds, and the famous rock arch.
commentary: The Cook's Cove Walkway near Tolaga Bay leads through farmland to a dramatic coastal cove where Captain Cook landed in 1769. NZ fur seals occasionally haul out on the rocks, seabirds nest on the cliffs, and the famous rock arch provides a stunning backdrop.
review_summary: Beautiful walk with historical significance. The rock arch is impressive. Allow time for the full walk.

name: Waikanae Creek Estuary — Urban Birding
short: Gisborne's inner city estuary with kingfisher, shag, and seasonal wading birds.
commentary: Waikanae Creek runs through central Gisborne and its estuary mouth provides surprisingly good birding. Kingfisher, pied shag, white-faced heron, and seasonal wading birds are easily observed from the footbridge and walkways. A pleasant urban nature experience.
review_summary: Easy urban birding. Nothing rare but good variety. Pleasant walk along the creek.

name: Anaura Bay — Reef Ecology
short: Sheltered East Coast bay with rich reef ecology and rock pools — subtropical influence on marine life.
commentary: Anaura Bay is a beautiful sheltered bay on the East Coast with rocky reefs extending from each headland. The reef communities reflect the warm East Cape current, with species diversity higher than expected at this latitude. Excellent rock pooling and snorkelling in calm conditions.
review_summary: Beautiful bay with good reef life. Very peaceful and uncrowded. Best in calm summer conditions.

name: Te Tapuwae o Rongokako Marine Reserve
short: East Coast marine reserve protecting reef systems — crayfish, paua, and diverse reef fish recovering.
commentary: Te Tapuwae o Rongokako Marine Reserve near Gisborne protects 2,450 hectares of coastal reef and sandy seabed. Since protection in 1999, crayfish and paua numbers have increased significantly. The reef snorkelling reveals abundant fish life. The reserve has strong iwi involvement in management.
review_summary: Good snorkelling when conditions allow. The iwi co-management story adds depth. Fish life visibly recovering.

name: Pouakai Crossing — Kiwi Country
short: Alpine crossing through kiwi habitat on Mt Taranaki — the Tarns reflect the volcano at dawn.
commentary: The Pouakai Crossing on Mt Taranaki passes through goblin forest and alpine tussock where North Island brown kiwi thrive thanks to DOC's predator control. The Pouakai Tarns, reflecting Taranaki at dawn, are iconic. Kea, NZ falcon, and fernbird also inhabit the zone.
review_summary: One of NZ's great day walks. The tarn reflection is iconic. Kiwi are heard at night from nearby huts.

name: Rotokare Scenic Reserve — Predator-Free Sanctuary
short: Taranaki's predator-fenced sanctuary — tieke (saddleback), hihi, and whio released into pest-free bush.
commentary: Rotokare Scenic Reserve near Eltham is enclosed by a predator-proof fence. Tieke (saddleback), hihi (stitchbird), pateke (brown teal), and whio (blue duck) have been released. The reserve centres on Lake Rotokare with walking tracks through mature bush. A real community conservation success.
review_summary: Impressive community-driven sanctuary. The birds are thriving. Easy tracks around the lake.

name: Sugarloaf Islands Marine Protected Area
short: Volcanic island chain off New Plymouth — NZ fur seals, blue penguins, and diverse seabirds.
commentary: The Sugarloaf Islands off New Plymouth host NZ fur seal colonies, little blue penguin nesting sites, and diverse seabird breeding colonies including shag, petrel, and tern species. Kayak tours provide the best viewing. The islands are visible from the Coastal Walkway.
review_summary: Great wildlife close to town. Kayak tours are the best way to see them. Seals are reliable.

name: Tapuae Marine Reserve
short: Marine reserve around the Sugarloaf Islands — protected reef systems with recovering fish populations.
commentary: Tapuae Marine Reserve protects the waters around the Sugarloaf Islands and the adjacent coast. Reef fish populations are recovering, with blue cod, butterfish, and crayfish increasing. Snorkelling and diving reveal kelp forests and diverse invertebrate communities.
review_summary: Good snorkelling in protected waters. Fish life is increasing since reserve status. Best in calm conditions.

name: Egmont National Park — Whio Habitat
short: Mt Taranaki's rivers support endangered whio (blue duck) — one of the North Island's key populations.
commentary: The rivers draining Mt Taranaki provide important habitat for whio (blue duck), NZ's rarest waterfowl. DOC's whio recovery programme includes stoat trapping and river monitoring. The Stony River and Waiwhakaiho are known whio sites. Patient scanning of river margins may be rewarded.
review_summary: Beautiful river walks. Whio sightings require patience and luck. The mountain streams are pristine.

name: Barrett Lagoon — Wetland Walk
short: Coastal lagoon near New Plymouth with black swan, pied stilt, and seasonal wading birds.
commentary: Barrett Lagoon on the New Plymouth coast is an accessible wetland for bird viewing. Black swan, pied stilt, grey teal, and paradise shelduck are resident. Seasonal waders including godwit visit during migration. The lagoon connects to the New Plymouth Coastal Walkway.
review_summary: Easy birding on the Coastal Walkway. Good variety of waterbirds. Pleasant in any weather.

name: Pukekura Park — Bush Birds & Eels
short: Heritage park in central New Plymouth with excellent bush birds, eels in the lakes, and native plantings.
commentary: Pukekura Park in the heart of New Plymouth features mature native bush with excellent populations of tui, kereru, fantail, and grey warbler. The park lakes hold longfin eels and native fish. The waterfall and fern house add botanical interest.
review_summary: Lovely urban park with good bush birds. The eels in the lake are fascinating. Free and central.

name: Ōākura Beach — Rock Pool Ecology
short: Rich rock pools and reef flats — sea anemones, starfish, and crabs at low tide.
commentary: The rock platforms at Ōākura Beach reveal rich intertidal ecology at low tide. Sea anemones, cushion stars, hermit crabs, and chitons inhabit the pools, while the reef flats support diverse algae communities. An excellent family-friendly marine education site.
review_summary: Great for kids. The rock pools are teeming with life at low tide. Easy access from the car park.

name: Lake Mangamahoe — Forest Birds & Waterfowl
short: Scenic lake with Mt Taranaki reflection — NZ scaup, dabchick, and bush birds in the surrounding forest.
commentary: Lake Mangamahoe near Inglewood offers the classic Mt Taranaki reflection photo. The lake supports NZ scaup, NZ dabchick, and grey teal. The surrounding native bush holds tui, bellbird, kereru, and fantail. A loop walk circles the lake through the forest.
review_summary: Beautiful scenery. Good bird variety. The Taranaki reflection is iconic on a calm morning.

name: Cape Egmont — Seabird Coast
short: Taranaki's western cape with seabird viewing — gannets, shearwaters, and petrels offshore.
commentary: Cape Egmont on Taranaki's western coast offers good seabird viewing, particularly during strong westerly winds that push pelagic birds close to shore. Australasian gannet, flesh-footed shearwater, and various petrel species are regular. The lighthouse adds scenic interest.
review_summary: Good seabird viewing in the right conditions. The lighthouse is photogenic. Can be very windy.

name: Tongariro National Park — Whio & Alpine Birds
short: NZ's oldest national park — whio on the rivers, NZ falcon above the tussock, and fernbird in the wetlands.
commentary: Tongariro National Park's rivers support whio (blue duck), the park's most important bird species. NZ falcon patrol the tussock, pipits work the alpine zone, and fernbird inhabit lower wetlands. The park's DOC whio recovery programme has stabilized the local population.
review_summary: Exceptional mountain birding. Whio in the rivers are the star. The alpine bird community is unique.

name: Lake Taupō — Trout Stream Ecology
short: World-famous trout fishery with fascinating freshwater ecology — spawning runs visible in feeder streams.
commentary: Lake Taupō's feeder streams host NZ's most famous trout spawning runs, visible from stream banks during autumn and winter. Beyond trout, the streams support native fish including kōaro, bullies, and longfin eels. The Tongariro River National Trout Centre explains the ecology.
review_summary: Fascinating to watch the spawning runs. The Trout Centre is excellent. Best during spawning season.

name: Tongariro National Trout Centre
short: Free visitor centre with native fish displays, trout hatchery, and underwater viewing of the Tongariro River.
commentary: The Tongariro National Trout Centre near Turangi offers free entry to its native fish aquarium, trout hatchery, and riverside walks. The underwater viewing chamber allows you to watch trout and native fish in the Tongariro River. Educational displays cover freshwater ecology and conservation.
review_summary: Excellent free attraction. The underwater viewing is fascinating. Great for families. Well-presented displays.

name: Pureora Forest Park — Kōkako & Kiwi
short: Magnificent podocarp forest saved from logging — now home to kōkako, kiwi, and whio.
commentary: Pureora Forest Park was saved from logging in 1978 when protesters literally sat in the trees. Now the ancient podocarp forest supports North Island kōkako, kiwi, kaka, and whio on the forest streams. The Totara Walk passes through some of the most impressive trees in NZ.
review_summary: Magnificent forest with incredible birds. The kōkako dawn chorus is unforgettable. Totara Walk is a must.

name: Orakei Korako — Geothermal Ecology
short: The 'hidden valley' of silica terraces and geothermal features with unique thermophilic biology.
commentary: Orakei Korako is considered NZ's best remaining example of silica terraces. The geothermal features support unique thermophilic organisms — heat-loving bacteria and algae create the vivid colours in the pools. Native bush surrounds the thermal area, with good bird populations.
review_summary: Stunning geothermal features with biological interest. Less crowded than Wai-O-Tapu. The boat ride adds fun.

name: Kaimanawa Ranges — Wild Horse & Bird Country
short: Tussock ranges with wild Kaimanawa horses and diverse native bird populations.
commentary: The Kaimanawa Ranges east of Taupō are home to NZ's iconic wild horse herds, managed by DOC. The tussock and beech forest habitats also support NZ falcon, fernbird, and diverse bush birds. The landscape is stark and beautiful, with access from the Desert Road.
review_summary: Unique landscape. The wild horses are iconic if difficult to spot. Good birding in the forest margins.

name: Huka Falls — Longfin Eel Viewing
short: Giant longfin eels visible in the crystal-clear Waikato River above the famous Huka Falls.
commentary: Above the dramatic Huka Falls, the Waikato River runs crystal clear through a narrow canyon. Giant longfin eels can be spotted in the calm pools upstream. The riverside walk provides excellent viewing of both the eels and the spectacular falls.
review_summary: Combine the iconic falls with eel viewing. The river clarity is remarkable. Easy walk from the car park.

name: Wairakei Natural Thermal Valley Wetlands
short: Thermal wetland boardwalk with geothermal features and waterbirds — pūkeko, dabchick, and shag.
commentary: The Wairakei Natural Thermal Valley combines geothermal features with productive wetland bird habitat. The boardwalk passes through thermal areas where warm water creates year-round feeding conditions for pūkeko, NZ dabchick, shag, and grey teal.
review_summary: Pleasant walk combining geothermal and bird viewing. Easy access. Good for families.

name: Whirinaki — Long-Tailed Bat Surveys
short: Long-tailed bats detected in Whirinaki's ancient podocarp forest — DOC monitoring programme.
commentary: Whirinaki Te Pua-a-Tāne Conservation Park's ancient podocarp forests provide critical habitat for the endangered long-tailed bat (pekapeka). DOC's bat monitoring programme uses acoustic detectors along the forest margins. Dusk is the best time for bat observation near the forest edge.
review_summary: Challenging to see but rewarding when detected. The forest at dusk is atmospheric. Combine with kiwi listening.

name: Lake Rotopounamu — Forest Bird Walk
short: Sacred lake surrounded by bush — excellent tui, kereru, and robin on an easy loop track.
commentary: Lake Rotopounamu, a taonga to local iwi, is a peaceful forest-fringed lake near Turangi. The loop track passes through mature bush with excellent populations of tui, kereru, North Island robin, and whitehead. Pest control has boosted bird numbers. The lake is culturally significant — no swimming.
review_summary: Beautiful peaceful walk. Great bush birding. Respect the swimming restriction. Easy loop track.

name: Maungatautari Ecological Island
short: NZ's largest predator-fenced mainland island — 3,400 hectares of bush with takahē, kiwi, and tuatara.
commentary: Maungatautari is enclosed by 47 km of predator-proof fence, making it the largest mainland island in NZ. Takahē, kiwi, tuatara, hihi (stitchbird), and kōkako have been released into the pest-free bush. Two visitor entry points offer guided and self-guided walks.
review_summary: Incredible conservation achievement. The Tautari Wetland walk is excellent. Guided tours recommended for best wildlife viewing.

name: Miranda Shorebird Centre / Pūkorokoro
short: Internationally significant shorebird site — bar-tailed godwits arrive after 11,000 km non-stop from Alaska.
commentary: The Miranda/Pūkorokoro Shorebird Centre on the Firth of Thames is one of the most important shorebird sites in NZ. Bar-tailed godwits (kuaka) arrive each September after an 11,000 km non-stop flight from Alaska. The centre provides binoculars, hides, and expert guidance.
review_summary: World-class birding. The shorebird centre is excellent — knowledgeable staff. Low tide is essential. Spring migration peak.

name: Waitomo Glowworm Caves
short: NZ's most famous glowworm display — boat ride through caverns lit by millions of bioluminescent larvae.
commentary: The Waitomo Glowworm Caves are one of NZ's most iconic attractions. A guided boat ride through the caves reveals a galaxy of Arachnocampa luminosa glowworms on the cave ceiling. The experience is magical and suitable for all ages. Multiple cave experiences available from gentle to adventure.
review_summary: Must-do NZ experience. The glowworm boat ride is magical. Book ahead in peak season. Various experience levels available.

name: Ruakuri Cave — Cave Ecology Experience
short: Deep cave system with glowworms, cave weta, and ancient stalactites — more extensive than the main Waitomo cave.
commentary: Ruakuri Cave offers a deeper cave experience than the main Waitomo cave, with extensive formations and impressive glowworm displays. Cave weta (large insects) are often visible. The spiral entrance is architecturally stunning. The cave ecology interpretation is excellent.
review_summary: Better than the main cave for many visitors. More extensive and less crowded. The formations are spectacular.

name: Hamilton Lake / Rotoroa — Urban Waterbirds
short: Urban lake with black swan, shag, dabchick, and seasonal wading birds — easy lakeside walk.
commentary: Hamilton Lake (Lake Rotoroa) in central Hamilton is a pleasant urban birding site. Black swan, little shag, NZ dabchick, grey teal, and paradise shelduck are resident. The lakeside loop walk is easy and popular. Native plantings around the margins attract bush birds.
review_summary: Easy urban birding. Good variety for a city lake. Pleasant walk at any time of day.

name: Karangahake Gorge — Bush Bird Walk
short: Gold mining heritage walk through regenerating bush with excellent tui, kereru, and North Island robin.
commentary: The Karangahake Gorge walkway follows the Ohinemuri River through regenerating bush and gold mining ruins. The bush holds excellent populations of tui, kereru, fantail, and North Island robin. The Windows Walk (mining tunnel) and suspension bridges add adventure.
review_summary: Great walk combining history and nature. The robins are very tame. The Windows Walk is atmospheric.

name: Hakarimata Scenic Reserve — Kaka & Kōkako
short: Regenerating bush near Huntly where kaka and kokako are returning thanks to community pest control.
commentary: The Hakarimata Scenic Reserve behind Ngaruawahia and Huntly has seen remarkable bird recovery thanks to community trapping. Kaka have returned, kokako are occasionally heard, and tui and bellbird populations have surged. The track to the summit is a popular fitness walk.
review_summary: Inspiring recovery story. The summit walk is steep but rewarding. Dawn birding is excellent.

name: Whale Bay — Marine Ecology
short: Rich rock pool ecology on the Raglan coast — sea anemones, starfish, and diverse intertidal life.
commentary: Whale Bay near Raglan has extensive rock platforms exposed at low tide, revealing diverse intertidal ecology. Sea anemones, cushion stars, chitons, and various crab species inhabit the pools. The bay is also popular for surfing and has a beautiful bush-lined beach.
review_summary: Great rock pooling at low tide. Beautiful setting. Combine with Raglan surfing and cafe culture.

name: Waingaro Hot Springs — Giant Eels
short: Giant longfin eels in the streams around the hot springs — some estimated at 80+ years old.
commentary: The streams near Waingaro Hot Springs hold giant longfin eels, some estimated to be over 80 years old. Feeding sessions allow close viewing of these ancient endemic fish. The hot springs themselves are a family-friendly attraction.
review_summary: Fascinating eel viewing. The hot springs are fun for families. The eels are impressively large.

name: Pirongia Forest Park — Bush Bird Recovery
short: Intensive predator control bringing bush birds back to the Waikato's highest peak — kokako, kiwi, and kaka.
commentary: Pirongia Forest Park on the Waikato's highest peak is a focus for intensive predator control. Kokako have been successfully reintroduced, kiwi numbers are increasing, and kaka are returning. The summit track is challenging but the forest is magnificent.
review_summary: Excellent bush birding on a rewarding tramp. The kokako reintroduction is a success story. Summit track is challenging.

name: Whangamarino Wetland — Ramsar Site
short: Internationally significant Ramsar wetland — bittern, fernbird, and marsh crake in vast peat bogs.
commentary: Whangamarino Wetland near Meremere is a Ramsar site of international importance, protecting 7,290 hectares of peat bog, swamp, and open water. Australasian bittern, fernbird, spotless crake, and NZ dabchick inhabit the wetland. Access is limited but rewarding for dedicated birders.
review_summary: Significant wetland — one of NZ's largest. Difficult access but important for serious birders. Bittern are present.

name: Kāwhia Harbour — Estuarine Birds
short: Sheltered harbour with bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and white-faced heron.
commentary: Kāwhia Harbour is a large sheltered harbour on the Waikato west coast that supports good populations of wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, and variable oystercatcher are regular. The harbour is peaceful and uncrowded, offering a quiet birding experience.
review_summary: Peaceful harbour birding. Off the tourist trail. Good wader variety at low tide.

name: Zealandia Te Māra a Tāne — Ecosanctuary
short: World-first fully fenced urban ecosanctuary — kaka, takahē, tuatara, and little spotted kiwi in central Wellington.
commentary: Zealandia is the world's first fully fenced urban ecosanctuary, protecting 225 hectares of regenerating bush within Wellington city. Rare species including takahē, little spotted kiwi, tuatara, and hihi (stitchbird) thrive within the predator-proof fence. Kaka from Zealandia now roam the whole city.
review_summary: World-class urban conservation. The birds are incredibly tame. Night tours for kiwi and tuatara highly recommended.

name: Kāpiti Island Nature Reserve
short: Predator-free island fortress — kōkako, little spotted kiwi, and saddleback in pristine bush.
commentary: Kāpiti Island is one of NZ's most important nature reserves, pest-free since 1996. The island supports little spotted kiwi, kōkako, saddleback, stitchbird, and many other rare species. Limited daily permits (max 50 visitors) ensure minimal disturbance. Boat from Paraparaumu.
review_summary: Premier NZ wildlife destination. Permits book months ahead. The birding is extraordinary. Take a guided walk.

name: Pauatahanui Inlet — Estuarine Birds
short: Wellington's most important estuary for wading birds — godwit, spoonbill, and oystercatcher.
commentary: Pauatahanui Inlet north of Wellington is the region's most significant estuarine bird habitat. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The inlet walkway provides excellent viewing access. Best at low tide.
review_summary: Good birding close to Wellington. The walkway is well maintained. Low tide is essential for waders.

name: Matiu / Somes Island — Wildlife Refuge
short: Predator-free harbour island — tuatara, little blue penguins, and weta a ferry ride from the city.
commentary: Matiu/Somes Island in Wellington Harbour is predator-free and hosts tuatara, little blue penguins, giant weta, and diverse bush and shore birds. The East by West ferry stops here, making it an easy half-day trip. Historical quarantine station buildings add interest.
review_summary: Wonderful day trip. The tuatara are impressive. Penguin nesting boxes visible. Easy ferry access.

name: Red Rocks — Fur Seal Colony
short: NZ fur seal colony on Wellington's wild south coast — up to 100 seals hauled out during winter.
commentary: The Red Rocks coastal walk on Wellington's south coast leads to a NZ fur seal colony where up to 100 seals haul out, particularly during winter. The walk passes dramatic red-coloured rocks (pillow lavas) and fossil-rich limestone. The wild coast is a dramatic contrast to the nearby city.
review_summary: Great coastal walk with reliable seal sightings in winter. The geology is fascinating. Can be very windy.

name: Miramar Peninsula — Little Penguin Corridor
short: Little blue penguins nest around Wellington's Miramar Peninsula — community protection in an urban setting.
commentary: The Miramar Peninsula and adjacent Shelly Bay area host little blue penguin nesting sites in an urban setting. The Wellington community actively protects nesting penguins with predator traps and nest boxes. Dusk viewing from public viewing points during breeding season.
review_summary: Urban penguin conservation in action. Dusk timing is essential. Community engagement is impressive.

name: Remutaka / Wainuiomata — Kiwi Territory
short: North Island brown kiwi reintroduced to the Remutaka Range — intensive pest control supports the growing population.
commentary: North Island brown kiwi were reintroduced to the Remutaka Range near Wellington in 2017 as part of the Capital Kiwi project. Intensive predator trapping across thousands of hectares supports the growing population. Night listening from forest edges can be rewarding.
review_summary: Exciting to have kiwi near Wellington. Night listening requires patience. The bush is beautiful.

name: Taputeranga Marine Reserve
short: Wellington's marine reserve — kelp forests, blue cod, and crayfish recovering on the south coast.
commentary: Taputeranga Marine Reserve on Wellington's south coast protects 8.55 sq km of rocky reef, kelp forest, and sandy seabed. Blue cod, butterfish, and crayfish are recovering. The Island Bay marine education centre provides interpretation. Snorkelling and diving access from several points.
review_summary: Good marine life close to the city. Best on calm days — the coast can be rough. Fish populations are increasing.

name: Ngā Manu Nature Reserve
short: Wetland sanctuary with kiwi, tuatara, eels, and bush birds — night tours available.
commentary: Ngā Manu Nature Reserve near Waikanae is a 14-hectare wildlife sanctuary combining wetlands, bush, and enclosures for threatened species. Kiwi, tuatara, longfin eels, and native bush birds are all here. Night tours offer kiwi and morepork viewing. Educational and family-friendly.
review_summary: Great for families. The night tour is excellent. Good variety of native species. Well-run sanctuary.

name: Makara Peak Mountain Bike Park — Bush Birds
short: Regenerating bush on Wellington's western hills — excellent tui, kaka, and kereru from Zealandia spill-over.
commentary: Makara Peak, while known as a mountain bike park, has excellent bush birding thanks to predator control and the Zealandia spill-over effect. Kaka from Zealandia visit regularly, and tui, kereru, and bellbird populations are strong. Walking tracks offer quieter birding than the bike trails.
review_summary: Good birding on Wellington's doorstep. The kaka from Zealandia are a bonus. Use walking tracks for best birding.

name: East Harbour Regional Park — Forest Birding
short: Regenerating forest across the harbour from Wellington — kaka, bellbird, and North Island robin.
commentary: East Harbour Regional Park covers the hills between Eastbourne and Wainuiomata. Community pest control has boosted bush bird populations significantly. Kaka from Zealandia visit, bellbird are abundant, and North Island robin have been translocated. Multiple track options.
review_summary: Good bush birding near Wellington. Multiple track options for different fitness levels. The Zealandia effect is noticeable.

name: Lake Wairarapa Wetlands — Ramsar Site
short: Ramsar-listed wetland — bittern, fernbird, and NZ's largest black shag colony.
commentary: Lake Wairarapa and its surrounding wetlands form a Ramsar site of international importance. The wetlands support Australasian bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and NZ's largest black shag breeding colony. The Matthews Reserve and Lake Domain provide viewing access.
review_summary: Significant wetland. The shag colony is impressive. Access points well sign-posted. Best in early morning.

name: Cape Palliser — Fur Seal Colony
short: NZ's largest North Island fur seal colony — hundreds of seals on the rocks below the lighthouse.
commentary: Cape Palliser hosts the largest NZ fur seal colony in the North Island, with hundreds of seals hauled out on the rocky coast below the lighthouse. The 250-step climb to the lighthouse offers panoramic views. The drive along the Palliser coast is scenic and remote.
review_summary: Impressive seal colony. The lighthouse climb is worth it. The drive is dramatic. Watch for seal pups on the rocks.

name: Johnston Hill — Kaka & Tui Haven
short: Wellington Town Belt bush walk with abundant kaka, tui, and kereru — the Zealandia effect in action.
commentary: Johnston Hill in Wellington's Town Belt has become a hotspot for kaka from nearby Zealandia. The walk through native bush delivers close encounters with kaka, abundant tui, kereru, and fantail. The kaka are bold and photogenic, often landing on track-side trees.
review_summary: Easy access to kaka in the wild. The Town Belt bush is lush. Great for a morning or evening walk.

name: Ōkārito — Rowi Kiwi Sanctuary
short: Home of the rarest kiwi — the Ōkārito brown kiwi (rowi) with only 600 remaining.
commentary: Ōkārito is the only home of the rowi, the rarest kiwi species with around 600 individuals. Okarito Kiwi Tours runs guided night walks with an extremely high kiwi sighting rate. The adjacent Ōkārito Lagoon is also excellent for white heron and royal spoonbill.
review_summary: Extraordinary kiwi experience. Sighting rate is very high. Book well ahead. The guides are exceptional.

name: White Heron / Kōtuku Sanctuary
short: NZ's only white heron breeding colony — guided jet boat and boardwalk to the nesting site.
commentary: The white heron (kōtuku) breeding colony near Ōkārito is the only one in NZ, with about 30-50 pairs nesting each season. White Heron Sanctuary Tours runs guided visits by jet boat and boardwalk. The heron is highly sacred in Māori culture. Breeding season Oct-Feb.
review_summary: Unique NZ wildlife experience. The jet boat trip is fun. The herons are beautiful. Book ahead for breeding season.

name: Punakaiki — Westland Petrel & Penguin Colony
short: The only mainland breeding colony of Westland petrel, plus little blue penguins at the Pancake Rocks.
commentary: The hills above Punakaiki host the world's only breeding colony of Westland petrel (tāiko). The rare seabirds return to their burrows at dusk from March to November. Little blue penguins also nest around the coast. The famous Pancake Rocks blowholes are a bonus.
review_summary: Unique seabird viewing. Petrel watching requires dusk timing and patience. The Pancake Rocks themselves are worth visiting.

name: Hokitika Gorge — Freshwater Ecology
short: Turquoise glacial river with native fish, longfin eels, and lush rainforest surroundings.
commentary: Hokitika Gorge is famous for its turquoise water colour caused by glacial flour. The river supports longfin eels and native fish. The surrounding rainforest is lush and atmospheric, with good bush bird populations. A short walk from the car park to the swing bridge.
review_summary: Stunning water colour. Short easy walk. The bush birds are a bonus. Very photogenic.

name: Franz Josef — West Coast Wildlife Centre
short: Kiwi breeding facility in Franz Josef — see rowi and Haast tokoeka kiwi chicks being raised for release.
commentary: The West Coast Wildlife Centre in Franz Josef runs a kiwi breeding programme for rowi and Haast tokoeka — two of NZ's rarest kiwi. Behind-the-scenes tours show the incubation and rearing process. The nocturnal kiwi house offers reliable viewing of adult birds.
review_summary: Excellent kiwi conservation centre. The behind-the-scenes tour is highly recommended. Good rainy-day activity.

name: Lake Matheson — Forest Bird Walk
short: Famous reflection lake surrounded by kahikatea forest — excellent bush birding on the loop track.
commentary: Lake Matheson's loop track passes through mature kahikatea swamp forest rich in birdlife. Kereru, bellbird, fantail, and grey warbler are common. Dawn visits offer the famous Aoraki/Mt Cook reflection and peak bird activity. The forest is atmospheric and ancient.
review_summary: Beautiful walk at any time. Dawn is magical for both reflections and birding. The bush is spectacular.

name: Haast Tokoeka — Jackson Bay Kiwi
short: Wild Haast tokoeka kiwi in remote South Westland forests — one of the rarest kiwi populations.
commentary: The Haast tokoeka is one of the rarest kiwi taxa, restricted to the forests of South Westland. DOC's recovery programme includes intensive stoat trapping and Operation Nest Egg. The Jackson Bay area is the most accessible part of their range, though sightings require night walks.
review_summary: Remote and challenging but meaningful. Contributing to kiwi conservation awareness. Night walks essential.

name: Monro Beach — Fiordland Crested Penguin
short: Walk through rainforest to a remote beach where Fiordland crested penguins come ashore July-November.
commentary: Monro Beach south of Lake Moeraki is one of the most accessible sites to see Fiordland crested penguins (tawaki) on the mainland. The 40-minute walk through stunning rainforest leads to a wild beach where penguins come ashore during the breeding season (Jul-Nov). Late afternoon is best.
review_summary: Incredible penguin experience. The rainforest walk is beautiful. Timing and season are critical. Free.

name: Cape Foulwind — Fur Seal Colony
short: Large NZ fur seal colony on the walkway at Cape Foulwind — viewing platforms above the breeding rocks.
commentary: The Cape Foulwind walkway near Westport passes above a large NZ fur seal colony. Viewing platforms provide excellent views of basking seals and, during breeding season, playful pups. The coastal walk itself is scenic, with views to the Paparoa Range.
review_summary: Reliable seal viewing. The viewing platforms are well positioned. Easy walk suitable for all ages. Free.

name: Ross Goldfields — Kiwi & Bush Bird Walk
short: Historic gold mining walks through regenerating bush — kiwi territory with excellent bush birding.
commentary: The Ross Goldfields walking tracks pass through regenerating bush that is now kiwi territory. DOC pest control has allowed kiwi and bush birds to thrive. Bellbird, tui, kereru, and grey warbler are abundant. Historical gold mining relics along the track add interest.
review_summary: Nice combination of history and nature. The bush birds are excellent. Kiwi are nocturnal so dawn/dusk best.

name: Ship Creek — Kahikatea Swamp & Dune Lake Walk
short: Atmospheric walks through kahikatea swamp forest and coastal dune lakes — unique West Coast ecology.
commentary: Ship Creek south of Haast offers two short walks showcasing unique West Coast ecology. The kahikatea swamp walk passes through atmospheric wetland forest, while the dune lake walk explores coastal lagoon ecology. Fernbird and grey warbler in the bush; wading birds on the lagoon.
review_summary: Atmospheric walks. The kahikatea swamp is otherworldly. Short walks but very rewarding. Free.

name: Ōpārara Arches — Bush Bird Haven
short: Ancient podocarp forest with great spotted kiwi, kaka, and rare cave spiders in limestone arches.
commentary: The Ōpārara Basin in Kahurangi National Park has magnificent old-growth podocarp forest with excellent bush bird populations. Great spotted kiwi inhabit the forest floor, kaka are often heard overhead, and the massive limestone arches harbour rare cave spiders. Several short walks.
review_summary: Otherworldly forest. The arches are enormous. Great spotted kiwi territory. Allow time for all the short walks.

name: Blue Pools — Freshwater Ecology
short: Crystal-clear blue pools with giant trout visible in the depths — surrounded by ancient beech forest.
commentary: The Blue Pools on the Makarora River are famous for their extraordinary clarity and blue-green colour. Giant rainbow and brown trout are visible in the pools, along with native fish. The short walk through beech forest to the swing bridge is magical. Bush birds are abundant.
review_summary: Stunning water clarity. The trout are enormous. Short easy walk. Very popular — arrive early.

name: Jackson Bay — Fur Seals & Marine Life
short: Remote South Westland bay with fur seals, Fiordland crested penguins, and Hector's dolphins.
commentary: Jackson Bay at the end of the road in South Westland is one of NZ's most remote accessible coastal sites. NZ fur seals haul out on the rocks, Fiordland crested penguins nest nearby (Jul-Nov), and Hector's dolphins occasionally visit the bay. The Whitebaiting Museum is a quirky bonus.
review_summary: Remote end-of-the-road atmosphere. Good wildlife variety. The drive in is scenic. Allow a full day from Fox Glacier.

name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi — Predator-Free Sanctuary
short: Predator-fenced sanctuary near Whanganui — NI robin, hihi, and kaka in magnificent old-growth bush.
commentary: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi near Whanganui is a predator-fenced sanctuary protecting 87 hectares of old-growth lowland forest. North Island robin, hihi (stitchbird), kaka, and whitehead have been translocated here. The ancient rata, rimu, and kahikatea trees are magnificent.
review_summary: Outstanding old-growth forest. The birds are thriving. Easy walking tracks. A real conservation success.

name: Manawatū Estuary — Shorebird Roost
short: Internationally significant shorebird roost — bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, and banded dotterel.
commentary: The Manawatū River Estuary at Foxton Beach is one of the most important shorebird sites in the lower North Island. Bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, banded dotterel, and red knot use the estuary during migration. The Manawatū Estuary Trust runs guided walks and monitoring.
review_summary: Excellent wader birding. The trust provides useful information. Low tide is essential. Migration season is best.

name: Rangitatau Reserve — Bush Bird Walk
short: Whanganui's urban bush reserve with tui, kereru, and fantail on easy walking tracks.
commentary: Rangitatau Reserve on the edge of Whanganui city offers easy walking tracks through native bush. Community pest control has boosted bird populations, with good numbers of tui, kereru, fantail, and grey warbler. The reserve provides a pleasant bush experience close to town.
review_summary: Nice urban bush walk. Good bird variety for a city reserve. Easy tracks suitable for families.

name: Whanganui River — Giant Longfin Eels
short: Giant longfin eels in the Whanganui River — culturally significant tuna in a river with legal personhood.
commentary: The Whanganui River, which has legal personhood under NZ law (Te Awa Tupua), is home to giant longfin eels (tuna). These ancient fish are culturally significant to local iwi. Viewing spots along the lower river allow observation of large eels in clear pools.
review_summary: Fascinating combination of ecology and law. The eels are impressive. The river's legal status adds depth.

name: Pukaha Mount Bruce — National Wildlife Centre
short: National wildlife centre with kōkako, kiwi, tuatara, and the famous white kiwi — Manukura's legacy.
commentary: Pukaha Mount Bruce is a national wildlife centre and breeding facility for NZ's rarest species. The centre is famous as the birthplace of Manukura, the white kiwi. Kōkako, Campbell Island teal, shore plover, and tuatara are all housed here. The surrounding bush has been pest-controlled for decades.
review_summary: Excellent conservation centre. The kokako are a highlight. Good bush walks around the centre. Family-friendly.

name: Rangitikei River Gorge — NZ Falcon Habitat
short: Dramatic river gorge where NZ falcon (kārearea) hunt from the cliff faces.
commentary: The Rangitikei River Gorge provides dramatic cliff nesting habitat for NZ falcon (kārearea). The falcons hunt from cliff-top perches, stooping on prey at high speed. The gorge scenery is spectacular, with the river cutting through papa rock. Several viewpoints along the road.
review_summary: Dramatic scenery. Falcon sightings require patience. The gorge itself is impressive. Good from roadside viewpoints.

name: Kāpiti Marine Reserve
short: Marine reserve around Kāpiti Island — protected reef systems with blue cod, crayfish, and kelp forests.
commentary: The Kāpiti Marine Reserve protects the waters around Kāpiti Island, one of the North Island's most important nature reserves. Blue cod, crayfish, butterfish, and diverse reef communities thrive in the protected waters. Snorkelling and diving from the mainland shore or by boat.
review_summary: Excellent marine reserve. Fish life is visibly recovering. Best accessed with a local dive operator.

name: Foxton River Loop — Wetland Birds
short: Old river loop wetland with pūkeko, paradise shelduck, and seasonal wading birds near Foxton.
commentary: The Foxton River Loop is a former course of the Manawatū River, now a productive wetland. Pūkeko, paradise shelduck, grey teal, and seasonal wading birds are easily observed from walking tracks. The de Molen windmill nearby adds a quirky Dutch-themed attraction.
review_summary: Pleasant wetland walk. Good bird variety. Combine with the de Molen windmill visit.

name: Tōtara Reserve — Ancient Forest Birds
short: Ancient tōtara forest with excellent bush birds — kererū, tūī, and fantail in towering old-growth trees.
commentary: Tōtara Reserve in the Pohangina Valley protects a magnificent stand of ancient tōtara trees, some over 500 years old. The forest is alive with kererū, tūī, fantail, and grey warbler. The loop track is easy and well-maintained, passing through one of the best remaining lowland podocarp forests in the region.
review_summary: Magnificent old-growth forest. The giant tōtara trees are impressive. Easy walking. Good bush birding.

name: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki
short: New Zealand's largest art institution with over 17,000 works spanning centuries.
commentary: Housed in a stunning heritage building adjoining Albert Park, Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki holds New Zealand's most extensive collection of national and international art. The gallery blends a French Renaissance-style heritage wing with a striking modern extension featuring a canopy of kauri-like forms. Highlights include works by Colin McCahon, Ralph Hotere, and Gottfried Lindauer's Māori portraits.
review_summary: Visitors love the free entry and world-class collection. The blend of heritage and modern architecture is frequently praised. Some wish for a larger café space.

name: Q Theatre
short: Auckland's premier independent theatre hosting cutting-edge drama and dance.
commentary: Q Theatre sits at the heart of Auckland's theatre district on Queen Street, offering two flexible performance spaces. It champions new New Zealand works alongside international productions, making it a vital hub for the country's performing arts scene. The Rangatira auditorium seats 350 while the intimate Loft space hosts experimental work.
review_summary: Theatre-goers appreciate the intimate venues and bold programming. The central Queen Street location is convenient. Shows often sell out quickly.

name: New Zealand Maritime Museum Hui Te Ananui a Tangaroa
short: Explore New Zealand's seafaring heritage from Polynesian voyaging to America's Cup glory.
commentary: Located on Auckland's Viaduct Harbour, this museum traces the maritime story of Aotearoa from the earliest Polynesian migrations through European exploration to modern yachting triumphs. Interactive exhibits include a full-size steerage cabin and a Blue Water Black Magic gallery celebrating New Zealand's America's Cup victories. Harbour sailing trips on heritage vessels are available.
review_summary: Families enjoy the interactive displays and harbour sail options. Maritime enthusiasts appreciate the depth of the collection. Some find it compact but well-curated.

name: Aotea Centre
short: Auckland's largest performing arts venue hosting world-class concerts, opera, and ballet.
commentary: The Aotea Centre anchors Aotea Square in central Auckland, serving as the city's premier venue for large-scale performing arts. The ASB Theatre seats over 2,000 and hosts the New Zealand Opera, Royal New Zealand Ballet, and Auckland Philharmonia Orchestra. The complex includes multiple event spaces and the restored Civic Theatre next door, a lavish 1929 atmospheric cinema.
review_summary: Audiences praise the acoustics and diverse programming. The Civic Theatre's atmospheric ceiling is a highlight. Parking can be challenging on show nights.

name: The Covert Theatre
short: New Zealand's only dedicated improv comedy theatre for spontaneous laughs.
commentary: Tucked away in K' Road, The Covert Theatre is New Zealand's only full-time dedicated improv comedy venue. Every show is completely unscripted, with performers creating scenes, characters, and stories from audience suggestions. The intimate space seats around 60, creating an electric atmosphere where anything can happen. Multiple shows run weekly with different troupes and formats.
review_summary: Audiences love the spontaneity and intimate setting. A unique night out in Auckland. Shows are family-friendly early and edgier late.

name: City Gallery Wellington Te Whare Toi
short: Wellington's leading contemporary art gallery pushing boundaries since 1980.
commentary: City Gallery Wellington occupies a grand Art Deco building in Civic Square, showcasing thought-provoking contemporary art from New Zealand and around the world. The gallery is known for ambitious, often provocative exhibitions that spark conversation. Free entry to most exhibitions makes it one of the best cultural experiences in the capital. The Nikau Gallery Café is a favourite brunch spot.
review_summary: Art lovers appreciate the bold curatorial vision. Free entry is a big plus. Some exhibitions can be challenging but always stimulating.

name: Weta Workshop Unleashed
short: Step behind the scenes of the Oscar-winning workshop that brought Middle-earth to life.
commentary: Weta Workshop Unleashed in Miramar offers an immersive experience through the creative world of the legendary special effects company behind The Lord of the Rings, Avatar, and dozens of blockbusters. The guided tour reveals props, costumes, and miniatures up close, while interactive exhibits let visitors try their hand at special effects techniques. The gift shop is a treasure trove for film fans.
review_summary: Film buffs call it a must-visit. Guides are passionate and knowledgeable. The hands-on elements make it engaging for all ages.

name: New Zealand Portrait Gallery Te Pūkenga Whakaata
short: The nation's faces captured in art — a gallery celebrating New Zealand identity.
commentary: Located on the Wellington waterfront in Shed 11, the New Zealand Portrait Gallery presents rotating exhibitions that explore the people who have shaped Aotearoa. From historic oil paintings to contemporary photography and digital art, the gallery tells New Zealand's story through its faces. Entry is free, and the waterfront location makes it an easy addition to a harbour walk.
review_summary: Visitors enjoy the intimate space and rotating exhibitions. Free entry on the waterfront is appreciated. A quieter alternative to Te Papa next door.

name: BATS Theatre
short: Wellington's scrappy, beloved independent theatre nurturing NZ's next generation of performers.
commentary: BATS Theatre on Kent Terrace has been the beating heart of Wellington's independent theatre scene since 1989. This small but mighty venue programs over 100 productions a year, from raw debut works to established indie companies. Many of New Zealand's most celebrated actors, writers, and directors got their start on BATS' stages. The bar is a great spot to mingle with the creative community after shows.
review_summary: Theatre lovers treasure BATS for its raw energy and affordable tickets. Programming is hit-or-miss but always adventurous. A Wellington institution.

name: Circa Theatre
short: Professional theatre on Wellington's waterfront producing New Zealand's finest drama.
commentary: Circa Theatre enjoys a prime waterfront position and has been producing professional theatre since 1976. With two performance spaces, Circa presents a mix of New Zealand premieres, international works, and new writing. The theatre's café-bar overlooks the harbour, making pre-show dining easy. It has launched countless careers and remains central to Wellington's reputation as NZ's cultural capital.
review_summary: Consistent quality across productions. The waterfront setting adds to the experience. Pre-show meals at the in-house café are a ritual for regulars.

name: Cable Car Museum
short: Free museum at the top of Wellington's iconic cable car revealing the funicular's 120-year history.
commentary: At the top terminal of Wellington's famous cable car, this free museum tells the story of the city's beloved funicular railway since 1902. Original grip cars and winding machinery are on display, along with historic photographs and interactive exhibits. The location offers panoramic views over the city and harbour, and connects to the Botanic Garden and Space Place observatory.
review_summary: A charming free addition to the cable car ride. Small but well-presented. Great views from the hilltop location.

name: Nga Taonga Sound & Vision
short: New Zealand's audiovisual archive preserving the nation's film, TV, and sound heritage.
commentary: Ngā Taonga Sound & Vision is the guardian of New Zealand's moving image and sound heritage, holding over 750,000 items spanning from the earliest colonial-era films to contemporary productions. Their public screenings and exhibitions offer a fascinating window into Aotearoa's cultural history. Wellington's film heritage runs deep — this is the city that gave the world Peter Jackson and Taika Waititi.
review_summary: Film historians and cinephiles treasure this institution. Public events are engaging and well-curated. A hidden gem for those interested in NZ's screen heritage.

name: Christchurch Art Gallery Te Puna o Waiwhetū
short: Canterbury's premier art gallery with a striking curved-glass façade and free entry.
commentary: Christchurch Art Gallery's distinctive undulating glass and metal façade has become an icon of the rebuilt city. Inside, the collection spans historic European works to bold contemporary New Zealand art, including significant pieces by Bill Hammond, Shane Cotton, and Rita Angus. Free entry and daily guided tours make it accessible to all. The gallery played a vital civil defence role after the 2011 earthquake.
review_summary: Praised for its stunning building and excellent free collection. Guided tours add great context. The rooftop sculpture is a popular photo spot.

name: Court Theatre
short: New Zealand's longest-running professional theatre company producing world-class drama.
commentary: The Court Theatre has been Christchurch's theatrical home since 1971, producing a year-round programme of drama, comedy, and musicals. After the earthquakes destroyed its original home, the company thrived in a temporary venue before moving to a purpose-built theatre in the Addington creative precinct. Known for nurturing local talent and staging bold New Zealand premieres alongside international classics.
review_summary: Consistently excellent productions. The new purpose-built venue is intimate and well-designed. A testament to Christchurch's resilience.

name: CoCA Centre of Contemporary Art
short: Christchurch's bold contemporary art space showcasing experimental and emerging artists.
commentary: CoCA is Christchurch's dedicated contemporary art gallery, presenting exhibitions that challenge and inspire. Focused on new and experimental work by emerging and established artists, CoCA occupies a striking modern building in the arts precinct. Free entry and a rotating programme ensure there's always something new to discover. The gallery shop stocks unique art publications and prints.
review_summary: Art enthusiasts appreciate the adventurous programming. Free entry makes it easy to pop in. Exhibitions rotate frequently so repeat visits are rewarded.

name: Teece Museum of Classical Antiquities
short: Three thousand years of human history through Greek, Roman, and Egyptian artefacts.
commentary: Housed within the Arts Centre, the Teece Museum displays the University of Canterbury's remarkable collection of classical antiquities spanning Greek, Roman, Egyptian, and Near Eastern civilizations. Highlights include Attic black-figure pottery, Roman glass, and Egyptian funerary objects. It's a surprising and delightful cultural jewel — you don't expect to find genuine ancient artefacts in Christchurch.
review_summary: A hidden gem that surprises visitors. The collection is small but impressive for its authenticity. Free entry and knowledgeable staff add to the experience.

name: Dunedin Public Art Gallery
short: New Zealand's oldest public art gallery with a world-class collection since 1884.
commentary: Established in 1884, the Dunedin Public Art Gallery is the oldest in New Zealand and holds one of the country's most significant collections. Major works by Frances Hodgkins, Ralph Hotere, and international artists including Monet and Gainsborough grace its galleries. The building itself, in the Octagon, is a commanding presence. Free entry makes this essential Dunedin viewing.
review_summary: Art lovers are impressed by the calibre of the collection for a city of Dunedin's size. Free entry is a bonus. The Frances Hodgkins gallery is a highlight.

name: Dunedin Street Art Trail
short: A self-guided walking tour through Dunedin's vibrant outdoor gallery of large-scale murals.
commentary: Dunedin has become one of Australasia's premier street art destinations, with large-scale murals by international and local artists transforming the city's walls. The trail takes you through laneways and streets discovering works by artists like Phlegm, ROA, and Pixel Pancho. New works appear regularly, making each visit different. Pick up a map from the i-SITE or follow the trail via app.
review_summary: A wonderful free activity that reveals Dunedin's creative side. The quality of the murals is world-class. Best enjoyed on foot over 1-2 hours.

name: Govett-Brewster Art Gallery & Len Lye Centre
short: A bold contemporary art gallery paired with the shimmering Len Lye Centre — New Plymouth's cultural jewel.
commentary: The Govett-Brewster has championed contemporary art since 1970, and the adjacent Len Lye Centre — a gleaming stainless-steel building — houses the kinetic sculptures and experimental films of New Zealand's most celebrated modernist artist. Len Lye's giant moving sculptures are mesmerising. Together, the two galleries make New Plymouth an unexpected cultural powerhouse worth a dedicated visit.
review_summary: The Len Lye Centre's architecture alone is worth the visit. Kinetic sculptures are captivating for all ages. Strong contemporary programme in the Govett-Brewster.

name: Sarjeant Gallery Te Whare o Rehua
short: A beautifully restored heritage gallery on Pukenamu Queens Park with an impressive national collection.
commentary: The Sarjeant Gallery has been Whanganui's art treasure since 1919, recently reopened after a major seismic strengthening and restoration project. The gallery holds over 10,000 works including significant New Zealand and international pieces. Its hilltop setting in Queens Park provides lovely views. The restoration has added modern exhibition spaces while preserving the original Oamaru stone building's grandeur.
review_summary: The restoration is stunning. Collection quality surprises visitors who don't expect such riches in Whanganui. Free entry and beautiful grounds.

name: Waikato Museum Te Whare Taonga o Waikato
short: Hamilton's cultural anchor combining art, science, and Tainui history on the river's edge.
commentary: Sitting on the banks of the Waikato River, this museum blends art galleries, science exhibitions, and Tainui Māori cultural displays. The permanent Tainui gallery tells the story of the Waikato region's tangata whenua, while rotating exhibitions range from contemporary art to natural history. The building's riverside setting makes it a pleasant stop on a Hamilton city walk.
review_summary: A good rainy-day activity in Hamilton. Tainui gallery is particularly impressive. Science exhibitions are great for families.

name: The Suter Art Gallery Te Aratoi o Whakatū
short: Nelson's premier art gallery in Queens Gardens showcasing the region's rich artistic tradition.
commentary: The Suter has been Nelson's art gallery since 1899, recently transformed by a major rebuild that added contemporary exhibition spaces. Nelson has one of New Zealand's highest concentrations of working artists, and The Suter reflects this creative energy with exhibitions spanning historical to contemporary work. The gallery's position in Queens Gardens, with its café overlooking the park, is idyllic.
review_summary: A lovely gallery in a beautiful setting. The rebuild is impressive. The café with garden views is a highlight.

name: MTG Hawke's Bay Museum Theatre Gallery
short: Napier's combined museum, theatre, and gallery complex on the stunning Marine Parade.
commentary: MTG (Museum Theatre Gallery) is Hawke's Bay's premier cultural institution, combining a museum of regional history, art gallery, and Century Theatre in one waterfront complex. The museum tells the story of the devastating 1931 earthquake and the Art Deco rebuild, while the gallery showcases significant New Zealand art. The building's Marine Parade location offers sea views from the upper galleries.
review_summary: The earthquake exhibit is moving and informative. Good art gallery. The building and location are impressive.

name: Southland Museum & Art Gallery
short: Home to the famous tuatarium and Southland's stories from prehistoric seas to pioneer days.
commentary: The distinctive pyramid-shaped Southland Museum in Queens Park holds everything from tuatara (the world's most successful captive breeding programme) to sub-Antarctic gallery displays and Southland's settler history. The museum's collection spans natural history, Māori taonga, and fine art. Queens Park itself is a beautiful 80-hectare garden worth exploring before or after your visit.
review_summary: The tuatara are a highlight for many visitors. Good rainy day activity. Queens Park adds to the overall experience.

name: Rotorua Arts Village / Toi Ohomai
short: A creative hub of artist studios and galleries in Rotorua's historic Bath House precinct.
commentary: Rotorua's Arts Village brings together working artists, galleries, and community spaces in the Government Gardens precinct. Local painters, sculptors, and craftspeople showcase and sell their work directly from studios. Regular exhibitions, markets, and workshops make it a vibrant spot to discover Rotorua's creative community beyond the geothermal attractions. The surrounding Government Gardens are free to explore.
review_summary: A pleasant surprise for visitors expecting only geothermal Rotorua. Meet local artists in their studios. The Government Gardens setting is beautiful.

name: Taupō Museum & Art Gallery
short: Compact museum telling the volcanic and human story of the great lake region.
commentary: Taupō Museum covers the dramatic geological and human history of the Lake Taupō region, from the cataclysmic eruption of 186 AD to Māori settlement and European timber milling. The Ora Garden of Wellbeing outside features interactive sculptures. Rotating art exhibitions showcase local and national artists. A good complement to the outdoor adventures that dominate most Taupō itineraries.
review_summary: Informative displays about the region's volcanic history. The garden is a peaceful spot. Small but worthwhile, especially on a rainy day.

name: Tairāwhiti Museum & Arts
short: Gisborne's museum telling the rich stories of Tairāwhiti from waka landings to surf culture.
commentary: Tairāwhiti Museum sits on the Gisborne riverbank, telling the region's unique stories through Māori taonga, settler history, and contemporary art. The maritime gallery features the wheelhouse of the Star of Canada, wrecked on Kaiti Beach in 1912. Exhibits explore the first encounters between Māori and Europeans at Tūranganui-a-Kiwa (Gisborne) and the region's deep Māori cultural heritage.
review_summary: Surprisingly rich for a regional museum. The Star of Canada wheelhouse is fascinating. Good introduction to East Coast history.

name: Mercury Bay Museum
short: Whitianga's charming museum exploring the Coromandel's maritime, kauri, and gold rush heritage.
commentary: Mercury Bay Museum in Whitianga tells the story of the Coromandel Peninsula from its earliest Polynesian visitors through the kauri logging and gold mining eras. A replica of the Mercury Bay stone, marking Captain Cook's observation of the Transit of Mercury in 1769, is a highlight. The museum also covers the area's boat-building heritage and natural history. Small but packed with local character.
review_summary: A lovingly maintained local museum. Volunteers are passionate and knowledgeable. Good for understanding the Coromandel's heritage.

name: Whakatāne Museum & Gallery Te Kōputu a te whanga a Toi
short: Eastern Bay of Plenty's cultural hub celebrating the region's Māori and natural heritage.
commentary: Whakatāne Museum sits on the banks of the Whakatāne River, offering exhibitions on the region's rich Māori heritage, volcanic landscape, and settler history. The gallery space hosts touring and local exhibitions. It's a good starting point for understanding the significance of the Eastern Bay of Plenty, including the story of Whakaari / White Island and the area's deep connection to early Polynesian navigation.
review_summary: Informative regional museum. Good context for understanding the Eastern Bay. Staff are welcoming and knowledgeable.

name: Hundertwasser Art Centre — Wairau Māori Art Gallery
short: A whimsical architectural masterpiece celebrating Friedensreich Hundertwasser's art and legacy.
commentary: Opened in 2022, the Hundertwasser Art Centre in Whangārei is the final building designed by the legendary Austrian-New Zealand artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser. With its trademark irregular windows, living roof garden, golden onion domes, and vibrant mosaics, the building is itself a work of art. Inside, galleries display Hundertwasser's prints and paintings alongside Māori art exhibitions in the Wairau Gallery.
review_summary: The building alone is worth the visit. A joyful, colourful experience. Whangārei's biggest drawcard. The rooftop garden and views are a bonus.

name: New Zealand Glassworks
short: Watch master glassblowers create stunning works at New Zealand's only hot glass studio.
commentary: New Zealand Glassworks is a nationally significant hot glass studio on Whanganui's Rutland Street arts precinct. Visitors can watch skilled artists shape molten glass into beautiful objects through the viewing gallery. Workshops let visitors try their hand at glass-blowing, bead-making, and fusing. The gallery shop sells unique art glass pieces. Whanganui has become known as NZ's glass art capital.
review_summary: Mesmerising to watch the glassblowers at work. Workshop experiences are a unique activity. The gallery pieces make special souvenirs.

name: Omarama Clay Cliffs
short: Otherworldly pinnacles of eroded clay formed by glaciers over a million years ago.
commentary: The Clay Cliffs near Omarama are a spectacular natural formation of sharp ridges and pinnacles, sculpted over millennia by erosion. Located on private farmland (small entry fee), you'll likely have this dramatic landscape to yourself. The cliffs glow golden in late afternoon light, making sunset the ideal visiting time. Omarama is also famous as a world-class gliding destination.
review_summary: Stunning and uncrowded. Best visited in golden hour light. The short access road is rough but manageable.

name: Okarito Lagoon
short: New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland — kayak among white herons with the Southern Alps as backdrop.
commentary: Okarito Lagoon is a pristine tidal lagoon on the West Coast, home to the only breeding colony of kōtuku (white heron) in New Zealand. Kayaking at dawn on the mirror-still water, with the Southern Alps reflected around you, is one of the South Island's most magical experiences. The tiny settlement of Okarito (population ~30) was once a gold rush town of 4,000.
review_summary: Kayaking here is utterly peaceful and beautiful. White heron sightings depend on season. The remoteness is part of the appeal.

name: Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve
short: One of the world's largest dark sky reserves — the Milky Way like you've never seen it.
commentary: The Aoraki Mackenzie region holds Gold-tier International Dark Sky Reserve status, making it one of the best places on Earth for stargazing. On a clear night, the Milky Way blazes overhead with an intensity that takes your breath away. Mt John Observatory offers guided stargazing tours, while Lake Tekapo's Church of the Good Shepherd provides an iconic foreground. Winter months offer the longest viewing windows.
review_summary: Life-changing stargazing experiences. Clear nights are essential — check weather forecasts. The observatory tours are excellent but book ahead.

name: Emerald Lakes — Tongariro Alpine Crossing
short: Vivid turquoise crater lakes set against Mars-like volcanic terrain on New Zealand's most famous day walk.
commentary: The Emerald Lakes are the showpiece of the Tongariro Alpine Crossing, sitting in explosion craters coloured by volcanic minerals. Their vivid turquoise and green hues contrast dramatically with the surrounding red and ochre volcanic scoria. The crossing itself is a 19.4km one-way hike through a UNESCO World Heritage landscape of active volcanoes, steam vents, and alpine desert.
review_summary: One of NZ's most spectacular landscapes. Weather can change rapidly — come prepared. Shuttle services handle the one-way logistics.

name: The Catlins Coastal Heritage Trail
short: Rugged, remote coastline where ancient forests meet wild seas — NZ's best-kept secret.
commentary: The Catlins stretch along Southland's southeastern coast, offering dramatic scenery without the crowds. Highlights include Purakaunui Falls, the Cathedral Caves (accessible at low tide), Curio Bay's petrified forest, and Slope Point — the South Island's southernmost tip. Wildlife is abundant: sea lions, penguins, and dolphins are regularly spotted. Allow a full day to explore this magical, untouched coastline.
review_summary: One of NZ's most underrated regions. Roads are slower but scenery is worth it. Wildlife encounters feel authentic and unmanaged.

name: Kawhia Hot Water Beach
short: Dig your own hot pool on a wild black-sand beach without the crowds of Coromandel's version.
commentary: Kawhia's Ocean Beach is the Waikato's answer to Hot Water Beach — dig into the black sand at low tide and hot mineral water bubbles up to fill your self-made pool. Unlike the Coromandel's famous beach, you'll likely share it with only a handful of locals. The drive to Kawhia passes through rolling farmland, and the sleepy harbour town offers authentic, unhurried New Zealand.
review_summary: The uncrowded alternative to Hot Water Beach. Timing with low tide is essential. Kawhia town has a lovely, forgotten feel.

name: Waipoua Kauri Forest — Tāne Mahuta
short: Stand beneath Tāne Mahuta, the lord of the forest — the largest living kauri tree on Earth.
commentary: Waipoua Forest is the last great kauri forest, and Tāne Mahuta is its crown jewel — a 2,000-year-old giant standing 51 metres tall with a trunk girth of nearly 14 metres. The short walk from the road to the tree is a humbling experience. Nearby, Te Matua Ngahere (Father of the Forest) is even older, though smaller. Footwear cleaning stations help prevent kauri dieback disease — please use them.
review_summary: A spiritual experience for many visitors. The scale of the trees is hard to comprehend until you're standing beneath them. Clean footwear is essential.

name: Wairakei Terraces & Thermal Health Spa
short: Soak in naturally heated pools beneath ancient silica terraces reformed over decades.
commentary: The Wairakei Terraces offer a more intimate geothermal experience than Taupō's larger attractions. Natural silica terraces have formed over 60 years from geothermal runoff, creating a landscape reminiscent of the famous Pink and White Terraces destroyed by the 1886 Tarawera eruption. Hot pools range from 36-40°C, and the Māori cultural experience includes carved palisades and a traditional village.
review_summary: A peaceful alternative to the busier thermal parks. The reforming terraces are fascinating. Evening soaks under the stars are magical.

name: Split Apple Rock / Tokangawha
short: A perfectly cleaved granite boulder sitting in the sea — one of NZ's most photographed natural features.
commentary: Split Apple Rock sits just offshore in Tasman Bay between Kaiteriteri and Marahau, a massive granite boulder split cleanly in two as if sliced by a giant knife. Geologists believe it was split by ice expanding in a crack during the last ice age. You can kayak right up to it, swim to it at low tide, or admire it from the coastal track. Best photographed in morning light.
review_summary: A remarkable natural curiosity. Kayaking to it is the best way to experience it. The surrounding coastline is gorgeous.

name: New Chum Beach
short: One of the world's most beautiful beaches — pristine, undeveloped, and reached only on foot.
commentary: Consistently rated among the world's best beaches, New Chum Beach on the Coromandel Peninsula has no road access, no shops, and no development — just 1km of golden sand backed by native bush. A 30-minute walk from Whangapoua Beach brings you to this paradise. The lack of facilities is the point — bring everything you need and leave nothing behind. Swimming is generally safe but there are no lifeguards.
review_summary: Utterly pristine and uncrowded. The walk in adds to the sense of discovery. Bring water and sun protection — no facilities.

name: Lake Matheson Mirror Lake
short: The perfect mirror — Aoraki/Mount Cook and Mount Tasman reflected in a dark tannin lake at dawn.
commentary: Lake Matheson near Fox Glacier is famous for its mirror-like reflections of Aoraki/Mount Cook and Mount Tasman. The dark tannin-stained water creates a natural mirror, and dawn visits offer the stillest conditions and best reflections. A well-formed 2.6km loop track circles the lake through beautiful kahikatea forest. The Reflection Island viewpoint at the far end is the money shot.
review_summary: Dawn visits are essential for the best reflections. The loop walk is easy and beautiful. The café at the start serves excellent coffee.

name: Lake Marian Track
short: A hidden alpine lake surrounded by towering peaks in the Hollyford Valley.
commentary: The Lake Marian Track is a rewarding 3-hour return hike to a stunning alpine lake nestled in a glacial cirque. The trail climbs through beech forest before emerging at the lake, surrounded by sheer granite walls and cascading waterfalls. Less crowded than nearby Milford attractions, it offers solitude and grandeur. The track can be muddy — sturdy footwear is essential.
review_summary: A stunning reward for moderate effort. Less visited than Key Summit or Routeburn. Can be very muddy — boots essential.

name: Red Rocks / Pariwhero Seal Colony
short: Walk along Wellington's wild south coast to a fur seal colony resting on ancient red volcanic rocks.
commentary: The Red Rocks coastal walk follows an old quarry road along Wellington's rugged south coast to a NZ fur seal colony. Between May and October, up to 100 seals haul out on the rocks. The red-coloured pillow lava rocks are 200 million years old — some of the oldest in the region. The walk is exposed and can be wild in southerly weather, which only adds to the dramatic atmosphere.
review_summary: A surprisingly wild walk so close to the city. Seal numbers peak in winter. Can be very windy — dress warmly.

name: Rotorua Canopy Tours
short: Zipline through an ancient native forest canopy — NZ's original and best treetop adventure.
commentary: Rotorua Canopy Tours offers an award-winning zipline experience through a 500-year-old native forest. The Original Canopy Tour features 6 ziplines and 2 swing bridges, while the Ultimate tour adds more lines and a cliff walk. Knowledgeable guides share the ecology of the forest as you fly between platforms in the canopy. Profits fund pest trapping that has brought native birds flooding back to the forest.
review_summary: Consistently rated NZ's top adventure experience. Guides are passionate about conservation. The forest is magical and you may spot rare birds.

name: Waitomo Black Labyrinth — Black Water Rafting
short: Float through a glowworm-lit underground river on an inner tube in the darkness of Ruakuri Cave.
commentary: The Black Labyrinth is Waitomo's iconic black water rafting experience. You'll don a wetsuit, grab an inner tube, and float through Ruakuri Cave's underground river beneath a ceiling of thousands of glowworms. The trip includes a backwards leap off a waterfall into the dark, eeling through narrow passages, and long stretches of silent floating gazing up at the bioluminescent display. Unforgettable.
review_summary: A unique NZ adventure that everyone should try. The glowworm ceiling is mesmerising. Wetsuits keep you warm enough. Not for the claustrophobic.

name: Hanmer Springs Jet Boat & Bungy
