The garments are displayed in theatrical lighting with dramatic staging that brings them to life. Downstairs, the classic car collection showcases over 100 immaculate vehicles in themed displays. The juxtaposition of wearable art and automotive design actually works — both celebrate human creativity, craftsmanship, and the desire to make beautiful objects. The museum is a Nelson original and unlike anything else in the country. The annual WOW show itself has moved to Wellington, but the museum keeps the magic alive year-round.
review_summary: Even non-fashion visitors are blown away by the WearableArt displays. The theatrical presentation brings the garments to life. The classic car collection adds unexpected depth and appeals to a different audience.

name: Tūpapa Heritage Trail
short: 4km trail with 10 markers from waterfront to Kaiti Hill sharing tangata whenua stories.
commentary: Tūpapa is an app-guided heritage trail weaving 4 kilometres through central Gisborne, from the inner harbour to the summit of Kaiti Hill. Ten interpretation points share the stories of tangata whenua — the people who have lived here for centuries before Cook's arrival.
review_summary: Walkers appreciate the depth of storytelling and the app-guided format. The trail gives genuine insight into Māori history that most visitors would otherwise miss.

name: Tatapouri Reef Ecology Experience
short: Walk on reef at low tide to encounter wild stingrays, eagle rays, and marine life.
commentary: At low tide, Tatapouri's reef platform becomes a natural aquarium. Guided by local operators, visitors wade onto the exposed reef to encounter wild short-tailed stingrays, eagle rays, and a rich array of marine invertebrates.
review_summary: An unforgettable wildlife encounter — stingrays glide right up to you. Guides are knowledgeable about marine ecology.

name: Cooks Cove Walkway & Hole in the Wall
short: Walk through farmland to secluded coves and dramatic cliff opening.
commentary: From the end of Wharf Road near Tolaga Bay, this track crosses farmland and climbs to a saddle before descending to Cooks Cove — where the Endeavour anchored in October 1769. The star attraction is the Hole in the Wall, a massive natural arch punched through a rocky headland by the sea.
review_summary: The Hole in the Wall is spectacular and worth the walk. Farmland section is exposed on hot days. Swimming through the arch on calm days is a highlight.

name: Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure
short: Pedal tandem bikes along abandoned coastal railway line — unique NZ experience.
commentary: The Gisborne Rail Bike Adventure repurposes a section of the disused Gisborne-Napier railway line, letting visitors pedal specially designed tandem rail bikes along the tracks through coastal scenery.
review_summary: Unique and fun — nothing else like it in NZ. Coastal scenery is beautiful. Easy enough for families with children.

name: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Reserve
short: Hilltop with 360° views over Gisborne, Cook Landing Site, and sacred significance.
commentary: Kaiti Hill — known by its Māori name Titirangi — rises above the confluence of the Waimata, Taruheru, and Tūranganui rivers, offering commanding 360-degree views over Gisborne and Poverty Bay.
review_summary: Outstanding panoramic views and a place of real historical significance. The drive or walk up is easy. The dual Cook/Te Maro memorials prompt genuine reflection.

name: Wyllie Cottage
short: Oldest European house in Gisborne — colonial domestic life preserved.
commentary: Wyllie Cottage is the oldest surviving European building in Gisborne, dating from the early 1870s. Now preserved in the grounds of Tairāwhiti Museum.
review_summary: A small but interesting heritage building, best visited as part of a Tairāwhiti Museum visit.

name: Sunshine Brewery
short: Gisborne's own craft brewery producing distinctive East Coast ales.
commentary: Sunshine Brewery has been crafting beer in Gisborne since 1989, making it one of New Zealand's pioneering craft breweries.
review_summary: A genuine craft brewery with character and history. The Gisborne Gold is a standout.

name: Young Nick's Head / Te Kuri-a-Pāoa
short: The first land sighted by Cook's crew in 1769, named after cabin boy Nicholas Young.
commentary: Young Nick's Head — Te Kuri-a-Pāoa in te reo Māori — is the dramatic headland at the southern end of Poverty Bay. It was the first land sighted from the Endeavour on 6 October 1769.
review_summary: A historically significant landmark best viewed from the Gisborne waterfront or Kaiti Hill.

name: Motu River Trails
short: Remote wilderness river offering jet boat and white water rafting adventures.
commentary: The Motu River is one of New Zealand's last untouched wilderness rivers, flowing through remote gorges and native bush in the Raukūmara Range.
review_summary: True wilderness — feels like you're the first person to see this landscape. Rafting trips are exceptional.

name: Morere Hot Springs
short: Natural hot springs in lush native bush — public and private pools surrounded by nikau forest.
commentary: Morere Hot Springs is a small, DOC-managed hot springs complex set in a beautiful stand of nikau palm forest.
review_summary: A hidden gem — natural hot pools in beautiful bush setting. Very relaxing and uncrowded.

name: Tokomaru Bay
short: Atmospheric small East Cape settlement with ruined wharf, surf beach, and strong community spirit.
commentary: Tokomaru Bay is one of the most atmospheric settlements on the East Cape — a small community centred around a beautiful bay with a ruined concrete wharf.
review_summary: Authentic and atmospheric — the ruined wharf is hauntingly beautiful. Great surf beach.

name: Anaura Bay Recreation Reserve
short: Pristine bay where Cook first found fresh water — bush walk, beach, and total peace.
commentary: Anaura Bay is where Cook's crew first found fresh water in New Zealand in October 1769. Today it's a DOC recreation reserve with a pristine beach.
review_summary: Pristine and peaceful — one of the most beautiful bays on the East Cape.

name: Te Kuri a Paoa / Young Nick's Head Walk
short: Dramatic coastal walk across farmland to the peninsula that changed NZ history.
commentary: This coastal walk crosses private farmland to reach the Te Kuri-a-Pāoa / Young Nick's Head peninsula — the first land sighted by Cook's crew in 1769.
review_summary: A walk through history with stunning coastal scenery. The sense of remoteness at the headland is powerful.

name: Mine Bay Māori Rock Carvings
short: 10m-tall carvings of ancestral navigator on cliffs accessible only by water.
commentary: Carved in the late 1970s by master carver Matahi Whakataka-Brightwell, the Mine Bay carvings depict Ngātoro-i-rangi, the navigator who guided the Tūwharetoa and Te Arawa peoples to the Taupō region.
review_summary: Spectacular carvings in an incredible setting. Kayaking there is the best way to experience them.

name: Wairakei International Golf Course
short: World-ranked championship course through ancient volcanic terrain.
commentary: Wairakei International Golf Course is consistently ranked among the top golf courses in New Zealand and the world.
review_summary: A world-class course in a unique volcanic setting. Very well maintained and surprisingly affordable.

name: Taupō Public Sculpture Trail
short: Collection of commissioned sculptures by NZ's top artists scattered along the lakefront.
commentary: Taupō's public sculpture trail features commissioned works by some of New Zealand's leading artists, scattered along the lakefront.
review_summary: A pleasant addition to a lakefront walk. Some striking pieces. Free and easily combined with the Great Lake Walkway.

name: Taupō DeBretts Hot Springs
short: Family-friendly thermal resort with hot pools, hydroslides, and warm-water playground.
commentary: DeBretts is Taupō's premier thermal hot pool complex, offering a range of mineral pools at different temperatures plus hydroslides.
review_summary: Great family destination with something for everyone. Hot pools are lovely in the evening.

name: Five Mile Bay & Whakaipo Bay
short: Sheltered lake beaches backed by native bush — peaceful swimming and picnicking.
commentary: Five Mile Bay and nearby Whakaipo Bay offer sheltered lake beaches backed by native bush.
review_summary: Beautiful sheltered beaches away from the crowds. Clear lake water is lovely for swimming.

name: ZeaYou Gallery, Brantry House
short: Boutique gallery showcasing NZ contemporary art in a heritage lakefront building.
commentary: ZeaYou Gallery occupies Brantry House, a heritage building on the Taupō lakefront, showcasing contemporary New Zealand art.
review_summary: A quality small gallery with well-curated NZ art. The heritage building is charming.

name: Lava Glass Sculpture Gallery
short: Extraordinary glass sculpture garden and gallery beside Lake Taupō.
commentary: Lava Glass is a glass art gallery and sculpture garden on the outskirts of Taupō, featuring extraordinary handblown glass sculptures inspired by New Zealand's volcanic landscape.
review_summary: Stunning glass art in a beautiful garden setting. Glass-blowing demonstrations are fascinating.

name: Kinloch & Western Bays
short: Western shore of Lake Taupō — mountain biking trails, kayaking, and lake access away from the crowds.
commentary: Kinloch sits on the western shore of Lake Taupō and offers a quieter alternative to the main town.
review_summary: Great mountain biking trails and beautiful lake access. Much quieter than Taupō town.

name: Mountain Jade Rotorua
short: Watch artisans hand-carve pounamu and learn the cultural stories behind each design.
commentary: Mountain Jade's Rotorua studio offers visitors the chance to watch skilled artisans hand-carve pounamu and learn about the deep cultural significance of different designs.
review_summary: Fascinating to watch the carving process. Staff explain the cultural significance well.

name: Blue Lake / Tikitapu
short: Crystal-clear lake surrounded by bush — excellent swimming, walking, and mountain biking.
commentary: Blue Lake — Tikitapu — is a crystal-clear volcanic lake surrounded by native bush and exotic forest.
review_summary: Beautiful clear lake with a great walking track. Swimming is lovely in summer.

name: Ōkere Falls & Kaituna River
short: White water rafting on the Kaituna — includes the world's highest commercially rafted waterfall (7m Tutea Falls).
commentary: The Kaituna River at Ōkere Falls offers one of the world's great white water rafting experiences. The highlight is the 7-metre drop over Tutea Falls.
review_summary: The Tutea Falls drop is absolutely unforgettable — pure adrenaline. One of the best rafting experiences in the world.

name: Skyline Rotorua Gondola & Luge
short: Gondola to summit with gravity-powered luge rides, zip-lining, and panoramic views over lake and city.
commentary: Skyline Rotorua takes visitors by gondola to the top of Mount Ngongotahā, where panoramic views stretch across Lake Rotorua.
review_summary: The luge is addictive — kids and adults always want 'just one more run.' Views from the top are fantastic.

name: Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre
short: NZ's only dedicated raptor conservation centre — flying displays with native falcon (kārearea) and morepork.
commentary: Wingspan is New Zealand's only dedicated centre for birds of prey conservation.
review_summary: The flying displays are mesmerising — seeing a kārearea in action is thrilling. Important conservation work.

name: Lake Ōkataina
short: One of the most beautiful and least-visited lakes — surrounded by untouched native bush.
commentary: Lake Ōkataina is often called the 'jewel' of the Rotorua lakes — a deep, clear volcanic lake almost entirely surrounded by native bush.
review_summary: Absolutely beautiful — feels like a hidden paradise. Kayaking on the still water is unforgettable.

name: Govett-Brewster Art Gallery / Len Lye Centre
short: NZ's first and only museum of contemporary art — stunning stainless-steel Len Lye Centre with kinetic sculptures.
commentary: The Govett-Brewster Art Gallery, founded in 1970, was NZ's first dedicated contemporary art museum. The Len Lye Centre was added in 2015.
review_summary: The Len Lye kinetic sculptures are mesmerising. The architecture is stunning. One of NZ's best art institutions.

name: Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Protected Area
short: Volcanic rock stacks offshore from New Plymouth — seals, penguins, and diving.
commentary: The Sugar Loaf Islands (Ngā Motu) are ancient volcanic rock stacks forming a marine protected area with fur seals and penguins.
review_summary: Kayaking among the islands is wonderful. Seal encounters are common. Dramatic volcanic geology.

name: Surf Highway 45 / Coastal Arts Trail
short: Scenic drive along Surf Highway with art galleries, studios, and surf breaks.
commentary: Surf Highway 45 follows the Taranaki coast from New Plymouth to Hāwera, passing surf breaks and artist studios.
review_summary: A great way to explore the Taranaki coast. The art studios are genuinely interesting.

name: Aotea Utanganui — Museum of South Taranaki
short: Modern museum in Pātea with rich geological, art, and social history of South Taranaki.
commentary: Aotea Utanganui in Pātea tells the story of South Taranaki through geology, social history, and art.
review_summary: A well-curated small museum with surprising depth. The Pātea Māori Club exhibition is a highlight.

name: Tūpare Heritage Garden
short: Arts and Crafts style house and garden designed by James Chapman-Taylor — free heritage gem.
commentary: Tūpare is a heritage garden and house designed by James Chapman-Taylor in his Arts and Crafts style.
review_summary: A beautiful free garden with a stunning house. Peaceful riverside setting.

name: Pukeiti Rhododendron Garden
short: World-class rhododendron collection set in 360ha of rainforest on the slopes of Mt Taranaki.
commentary: Pukeiti is a 360-hectare garden on the slopes of Mount Taranaki, home to one of the world's largest collections of rhododendrons.
review_summary: Spectacular in spring when the rhododendrons bloom. The rainforest setting is magical. Free entry.

name: Len Lye Wind Wand
short: A 48m kinetic sculpture swaying on the waterfront — Len Lye's dramatic public artwork.
commentary: The Wind Wand is a 48-metre kinetic sculpture on the New Plymouth waterfront, designed by Len Lye.
review_summary: Mesmerising to watch — especially in strong wind. Beautiful when lit up at night.

name: Lake Mangamahoe
short: A tranquil lake offering postcard-perfect reflections of Mt Taranaki.
commentary: Lake Mangamahoe is a small man-made lake that on still mornings perfectly reflects Mt Taranaki.
review_summary: The reflection of Mt Taranaki on a still morning is picture-perfect. Easy lakeside walk.

name: Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway
short: Walk through mining tunnels, over bridges, and past ruins in a dramatic river gorge.
commentary: Karangahake Gorge is where the Ōhinemuri River cuts through the Kaimai Range, and where gold miners tunnelled from the 1870s.
review_summary: One of NZ's best heritage walks. The tunnels are thrilling and the gorge is beautiful.

name: Victoria Battery, Waikino
short: Largest gold-stamping battery in NZ when built 1897 — underground tours and heritage tramway.
commentary: The Victoria Battery at Waikino was the largest gold-stamping battery in NZ when built in 1897.
review_summary: Impressive industrial heritage. Underground tours are fascinating. Heritage tramway is a nice touch.

name: Thames School of Mines & Treasury
short: Historic mining school (1886-1954) now housing mining artifacts and mineralogical museum.
commentary: The Thames School of Mines operated from 1886 to 1954 and now houses a museum with mining artifacts and a remarkable mineralogical collection.
review_summary: Excellent mining heritage museum with knowledgeable volunteers. The mineral collection is impressive.

name: Coromandel Town Heritage Walk
short: Charming gold-rush era town with heritage buildings, art galleries, and artisan studios.
commentary: Coromandel Town retains the character of a gold-rush era settlement with colonial heritage buildings.
review_summary: A charming small town with genuine heritage character. The art galleries are worth browsing.

name: Ōtama Beach
short: A stunning crescent of white sand far less crowded than Cathedral Cove or Hot Water Beach.
commentary: Ōtama Beach is a stunning crescent of white sand on the eastern Coromandel coast.
review_summary: A beautiful quiet beach — the Coromandel's best-kept secret. Safe swimming and lovely white sand.

name: Wentworth Valley Walk & Waterfall
short: Beautiful bush walk to a cascading waterfall with a deep swimming hole surrounded by ferns.
commentary: The Wentworth Valley Walk near Whangamatā follows a stream through native bush to a cascading waterfall.
review_summary: Lovely bush walk with a rewarding waterfall at the end. The swimming hole is refreshing.

name: Thames Goldmine Experience
short: Go underground in a real mine, learn about stamper batteries, and try gold panning.
commentary: The Thames Goldmine Experience takes visitors underground into a real gold mine from the 1860s gold rush era.
review_summary: Going underground in a real mine is exciting. Gold panning is fun for all ages.

name: Coroglen Farmers Market & Pub
short: Popular Saturday market and legendary rural pub with live music — a Coromandel institution.
commentary: The Coroglen Farmers Market runs every Saturday morning during summer. The neighbouring Coroglen Tavern hosts legendary live music events.
review_summary: A great Saturday morning market with genuine local produce. The Coroglen Tavern gigs are legendary.

name: The 309 Road — Waiau Kauri Grove & Waterworks
short: Winding unsealed road with ancient kauri trees and the bizarre Waterworks.
commentary: The 309 Road crosses the Coromandel Range with two remarkable stops: the Waiau Kauri Grove and the quirky Waterworks.
review_summary: The ancient kauri trees are awe-inspiring. The Waterworks is brilliantly quirky. Allow more time than you think.

name: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve
short: Marine reserve surrounding Cathedral Cove — exceptional snorkelling, kayaking, and marine life.
commentary: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve protects 840 hectares of coastal marine environment around Cathedral Cove.
review_summary: The snorkelling is exceptional — huge snapper everywhere. Kayaking through the sea caves is magical.

name: Napier Art Deco Quarter
short: The Art Deco capital of the world — rebuilt in the 1930s style after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
commentary: On 3 February 1931, a 7.8 magnitude earthquake devastated Napier. The rebuild created one of the most concentrated collections of Art Deco architecture in the world.
review_summary: A stunning collection of Art Deco architecture — genuinely world-class. The earthquake story is sobering and compelling.

name: MTG Hawke's Bay Museum
short: Museum, Theatre, Gallery complex — Hawke's Bay's premier cultural institution.
commentary: MTG Hawke's Bay houses a significant collection of Māori taonga, a powerful earthquake exhibition, and rotating art shows.
review_summary: The earthquake exhibition is powerful and moving. Māori taonga collection is excellent.

name: Te Mata Peak
short: Iconic summit with 360° views — Māori legend says it's the body of a sleeping giant.
commentary: Te Mata Peak rises 399 metres above the Heretaunga Plains, offering 360-degree views.
review_summary: Spectacular views — one of the best easily-accessible viewpoints in NZ. Sunset from the summit is unforgettable.

name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony
short: The world's largest mainland gannet colony — thousands of birds nesting on dramatic coastal cliffs.
commentary: Cape Kidnappers is home to the world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony, with around 20,000 gannets.
review_summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience — the sheer number of birds is overwhelming.

name: National Aquarium of New Zealand
short: NZ's only public aquarium — kiwi encounter, tuatara, sharks, and marine wildlife.
commentary: The National Aquarium of New Zealand in Napier is the country's only public aquarium.
review_summary: The kiwi encounter alone is worth the visit. Shark tank tunnel is impressive.

name: Birdwoods Gallery & Sweet Shop
short: Charming art gallery and old-fashioned sweet shop in heritage grounds near Havelock North.
commentary: Birdwoods combines an art gallery, old-fashioned sweet shop, and garden café in heritage grounds.
review_summary: The sweet shop is a delight for kids and adults. Gallery has quality NZ art.

name: Ōtātara Pā Historic Reserve
short: One of NZ's largest pā sites — terraces and ditches of ancient Māori fortification with commanding views.
commentary: Ōtātara Pā is one of the largest and best-preserved pā sites in New Zealand.
review_summary: Impressive earthworks that bring pre-European Māori life to life. Commanding views over the Heretaunga Plains.

name: Napier Urban Winery Precinct
short: Walkable cluster of urban wineries, craft breweries, and restaurants in central Napier.
commentary: Central Napier has developed a walkable cluster of urban wineries and craft breweries.
review_summary: Great concept — wine tasting without driving. Good combination of wine, beer, and food options.

name: Shine Falls / Te Paripari
short: Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall at 58 metres — a beautiful bush walk through farmland and native forest.
commentary: Shine Falls drops 58 metres — it's Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall and one of the most impressive in the North Island.
review_summary: Impressive waterfall — the 58m drop is dramatic. Best after rain for maximum water flow.

name: Forgotten World Highway (SH43)
short: NZ's loneliest highway — 150km through hill country, heritage tunnels, and Whangamomona.
commentary: State Highway 43 runs 150 kilometres from Stratford to Taumarunui through some of the most remote hill country in the North Island.
review_summary: One of NZ's great drives — remote, atmospheric, and genuinely different. Fill up with fuel before you start.

name: Te Āpiti / Manawatū Gorge Track
short: Walk through the dramatic gorge where the Manawatū River cuts through the Ruahine Range.
commentary: Te Āpiti is a dramatic geological feature where the Manawatū River has cut through the Ruahine Range.
review_summary: Dramatic gorge scenery with good bush walking. Native birdlife is abundant.

name: Durie Hill Elevator & Tower
short: Underground tunnel to NZ's only public elevator inside a hill — then climb the war memorial tower for views.
commentary: The Durie Hill Elevator: enter through a tunnel, walk 213 metres into the hill, ride NZ's only underground elevator 66 metres up.
review_summary: A unique NZ experience — the tunnel and elevator are quirky and fun. Views from the tower are excellent.

name: Owlcatraz
short: NZ's only owl sanctuary — meet morepork, ruru, and other native birds on guided tours.
commentary: Owlcatraz is New Zealand's only dedicated owl sanctuary, featuring morepork (ruru) and other native birds.
review_summary: The night tours are magical — hearing morepork call in the dark is unforgettable.

name: The Esplanade & Wildbase Recovery
short: Beautiful park with world-class Wildbase Recovery wildlife hospital — see injured native birds being rehabilitated.
commentary: The Esplanade features rose gardens, bush walks, and the Wildbase Recovery centre — a public-facing wildlife hospital.
review_summary: Wildbase Recovery is fascinating — seeing native birds being rehabilitated is special.

name: Rangitīkei River — White Water Grade 5
short: One of NZ's best white water rafting rivers — Grade 5 rapids through a stunning gorge.
commentary: The Rangitīkei River offers some of the North Island's best white water rafting with Grade 5 rapids.
review_summary: Serious white water in a stunning gorge. Not for beginners but experienced rafters will love it.

name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi Ecosanctuary
short: Predator-free forest sanctuary with a heritage homestead — rich birdlife including North Island robin and kākā.
commentary: Bushy Park is a 100-hectare predator-free ecosanctuary enclosed by a pest-proof fence allowing native birds to thrive.
review_summary: The birdlife is incredible — robins hop right up to you. The bush is magnificent ancient forest.

name: Tairāwhiti Museum Gisborne
short: Gisborne's regional museum with outstanding Māori art, maritime heritage, and the story of NZ's first sunrise coast.
commentary: Tairāwhiti Museum in Gisborne is the East Coast's principal cultural institution, presenting the rich and diverse stories of this distinctive region. The museum features outstanding collections of East Coast Māori art and taonga, maritime heritage including the historic Star of Canada wheelhouse displayed on the riverside, and exhibits covering the region's agricultural and surfing heritage. Gisborne's unique position as the first city in the world to see the sunrise each day is celebrated, along with the region's deep Māori cultural heritage — the East Coast has the highest proportion of Māori population of any New Zealand region. The museum's Te Moana Maritime Gallery tells the story of the region's relationship with the sea, from voyaging waka to modern shipping. The riverside setting is attractive, and the museum is within walking distance of Gisborne's compact city centre. Admission is affordable and well worth it. Allow 1-1.5 hours for the main exhibitions.
review_summary: A really impressive regional museum with strong Māori art collections and engaging maritime displays. The Star of Canada wheelhouse is fascinating. Gives great context for exploring the wider East Coast.

name: St Mary's Church Tikitiki
short: Extraordinary Māori-decorated church with stunning carvings and tukutuku panels, a taonga of Ngāti Porou.
commentary: St Mary's Church in the small East Coast settlement of Tikitiki is one of the most extraordinary buildings in New Zealand — an Anglican church whose interior is entirely decorated with exquisite Māori carvings, tukutuku (woven panels), and kōwhaiwhai (painted scroll patterns) of the highest quality. The church was built in 1924 as a memorial to Ngāti Porou soldiers who served in World War I, and the interior decoration represents some of the finest examples of traditional Māori carving and weaving in the country. Each panel tells stories of Ngāti Porou genealogy and history. The fusion of Christian church architecture with traditional Māori art creates a profoundly moving cultural experience. The church is a Category I historic place and an active place of worship. Visitors are welcome but should treat the building with respect — it is a sacred space and a living taonga of the Ngāti Porou people. Remove shoes before entering and follow any signage. Located on State Highway 35 (Pacific Coast Highway), north of Gisborne.
review_summary: One of the most beautiful and moving buildings in New Zealand. The carving and weaving are breathtaking in their skill and artistry. A deeply sacred place — visit with reverence and respect.

name: Wainui Beach
short: Gisborne's premier surf beach with consistent breaks, golden sand, and spectacular East Coast sunrises.
commentary: Wainui Beach is Gisborne's most popular surf beach, a long crescent of golden sand just south of the city, renowned for its consistent and varied surf breaks. The beach is a mecca for East Coast surfers, with breaks suitable for beginners through to experts depending on conditions and the section of beach. The northern end offers more sheltered conditions, while the middle and southern sections catch more swell. Being on the East Coast, Wainui faces directly into the sunrise, making dawn surf sessions here a spectacular experience. The beach is backed by a mix of residential properties and bush-clad hills. Surf lifeguards patrol during summer months. The surfing community here is strong and welcoming — local surf schools offer lessons for beginners. Wainui is also excellent for bodyboarding, swimming (between the flags), and beach walking. The Gisborne region's warm climate and eastern exposure mean the beach catches the most sun hours of almost any spot in New Zealand. A quintessential East Coast beach experience.
review_summary: Excellent surf beach with great waves for all levels. The sunrise sessions are unforgettable. Warm water by NZ standards and a strong, welcoming surf culture. One of the best beach towns in the country.

name: Tokomaru Bay Wharf
short: Historic wharf and freezing works ruins at a picturesque East Coast bay, a poignant reminder of past prosperity.
commentary: Tokomaru Bay's historic wharf and adjacent freezing works ruins stand as evocative reminders of the East Coast's once-thriving agricultural export industry. The concrete wharf, built in 1910, stretches into the bay where ships once loaded frozen meat and wool for overseas markets. The freezing works, which operated from 1910 until closure in 1952, employed hundreds of workers and was the economic lifeblood of the community. Today the ruins are a photogenic landmark, their concrete walls and loading bays slowly being reclaimed by vegetation and salt air. The bay itself is beautiful, with a curved beach and clear water popular for swimming in summer. The small settlement retains a community spirit with a pub, general store, and backpacker accommodation. Tokomaru Bay is on the Pacific Coast Highway (SH35) around the East Cape, about two hours north of Gisborne.
review_summary: A hauntingly beautiful heritage site with a powerful story of boom and decline. The ruins are photogenic and the bay is lovely. A highlight of the East Cape road trip.

name: East Cape Road Trip (SH35)
short: Scenic coastal highway around the East Cape, one of NZ's most remote and culturally rich road journeys.
commentary: State Highway 35 around the East Cape is one of New Zealand's most rewarding road trips, tracing 334 kilometres of rugged Pacific coastline between Opotiki and Gisborne. This is one of the least-travelled main highways in the country, passing through predominantly Maori communities with beautifully carved meeting houses, small coastal settlements, and pristine beaches. Highlights include Te Araroa, home to New Zealand's oldest pohutukawa tree; the East Cape Lighthouse, the first place in mainland New Zealand to see the sunrise; the ornate Tikitiki Church with its stunning Maori carvings; and the surf breaks of Hicks Bay and Tokomaru Bay. Allow at least two days to do the journey justice, with overnight stops available at small motels, campgrounds, and backpackers along the route. Fuel up whenever you can, as petrol stations are infrequent. The road is sealed throughout but narrow and winding in places.
review_summary: An authentic, off-the-beaten-track New Zealand experience far from tourist crowds. The coastal scenery is stunning and the Maori cultural presence is deeply felt. Allow more time than planned as the road is slow.

name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum
short: New Zealand's national arboretum with the Southern Hemisphere's largest collection of Northern Hemisphere trees.
commentary: Eastwoodhill Arboretum near Ngatapa, 35 kilometres from Gisborne, is New Zealand's national arboretum and home to the largest collection of Northern Hemisphere trees in the Southern Hemisphere. Founded by Douglas Cook who began planting in 1910, the 131-hectare property contains over 25,000 trees from 4,000 species, including oaks, maples, beeches, and conifers from around the world. The arboretum is spectacular in autumn (April-May) when the deciduous trees create a blaze of colour rarely seen in New Zealand. Walking tracks of varying length wind through different collection areas, from formal avenues to wild garden areas. The Homestead provides accommodation for overnight stays. Spring flowering and summer shade offer year-round appeal, but the autumn display is the primary draw. The arboretum operates on a modest entry fee and is open daily. A remarkable living legacy of one man's vision for conservation and beauty.
review_summary: A stunning arboretum with extraordinary autumn colour that rivals any Northern Hemisphere display. The variety of trees is remarkable. A hidden gem that deserves to be better known.

name: Tolaga Bay Wharf
short: The longest concrete wharf in the Southern Hemisphere, stretching 660 metres into Tolaga Bay.
commentary: Tolaga Bay Wharf is the longest concrete wharf in the Southern Hemisphere, stretching an impressive 660 metres into the Pacific Ocean. Built in 1929 to serve the coastal shipping trade, the wharf was used for loading wool, frozen meat, and dairy products until closure in 1969. Today it stands as a heritage landmark and popular walking destination, with the journey to the end rewarded by ocean views and occasional dolphin sightings. The wharf has been strengthened and is safe to walk, though some sections show the weathering of decades of salt and storm exposure. Tolaga Bay itself is a beautiful curved beach with clear water, popular for swimming and surfing. The nearby Cook's Cove walkway leads to the cove where Captain Cook anchored in 1769, passing through a spectacular natural rock arch. Located on SH35 about an hour north of Gisborne, Tolaga Bay is a key stop on the East Cape road trip.
review_summary: An impressive heritage structure with a rewarding walk to the end. The ocean views and sense of history are compelling. Combine with the Cook's Cove walk for a full Tolaga Bay visit.

name: Spa Thermal Park Hot Stream
short: Free natural hot stream flowing into the Waikato River, a local favourite for a relaxing thermal soak.
commentary: Spa Thermal Park is a beloved local gem in Taupō — a natural geothermal stream that flows through a park before joining the Waikato River, creating free, accessible hot water bathing. The stream maintains a comfortable temperature perfect for soaking, and where it meets the cooler river water, bathers can find their ideal temperature by moving between the two. The park is well maintained with changing facilities and easy access from a short walk from the car park. This is the real deal — no admission fee, no artificial pools, just naturally heated water in a bush setting. The walk from town along the Waikato River trail takes about 20 minutes and is scenic in itself. The area can get busy on weekends and holidays but never feels overcrowded thanks to the length of the stream. Visit at dusk for a magical atmosphere with steam rising in the evening light. Bring a towel and water shoes for the rocky river bed. One of Taupō's best free experiences.
review_summary: One of the best free things to do in Taupō — natural hot water in a lovely bush setting. Finding the perfect temperature where hot meets cold is great fun. Walk along the river trail to get there.

name: Taupō Great Lake Walkway
short: Scenic lakefront walking and cycling path with volcanic views, public art, and Taupō town connections.
commentary: The Taupō Great Lake Walkway is a flat, scenic path following the shore of Lake Taupō through the heart of town, offering beautiful views across New Zealand's largest lake to the volcanic peaks of Tongariro National Park. The walkway connects multiple reserves, beaches, and attractions along the lakefront, making it the perfect way to explore Taupō on foot or by bicycle. Starting from the boat harbour near the town centre, the path extends several kilometres along the lake edge through parks and gardens, passing public art installations, historic sites, and swimming beaches. The views of the lake and the volcanic mountains beyond are consistently stunning, particularly in the late afternoon light. The path is flat and accessible, suitable for all ages and abilities including wheelchairs and pushchairs. Bike hire is available from several operators in town. The walkway connects to longer trails including the route to Spa Thermal Park and the Huka Falls. Free to use at all times and well maintained year-round.
review_summary: A lovely flat walk or cycle along the lakeshore with incredible volcanic views. Connects all the best parts of Taupō's waterfront. Perfect for a morning stroll or sunset walk. Easy and accessible for everyone.

name: Mount Tauhara Track
short: Sacred volcanic peak above Taupō with panoramic views of the lake and central North Island mountains.
commentary: Mount Tauhara (1,088m) is the prominent volcanic dome rising above the eastern shore of Lake Taupō, offering one of the region's most rewarding day walks with spectacular summit views. The track climbs steadily through native bush before emerging onto the open summit ridge, where panoramic views encompass Lake Taupō, the Tongariro volcanic peaks, the Kaimanawa Ranges, and vast tracts of central North Island farmland and forest. The walk takes approximately 2-3 hours return and is moderately challenging with a steady uphill gradient. Mount Tauhara holds deep cultural and spiritual significance for the local Ngāti Tūwharetoa iwi, who consider it a sacred ancestor. Visitors are asked to respect the mana of the mountain — stay on the track, do not take rocks or plant material, and carry out all rubbish. The trailhead is located off Mountain Road, east of Taupō town. Clear days are best for views — check the weather before committing. The summit can be cold and windy even when Taupō town is warm.
review_summary: Excellent half-day walk with incredible summit views. The lake and volcanic panorama is breathtaking on a clear day. Respect the mountain's cultural significance and stay on the track. Bring warm layers for the top.

name: Craters of the Moon Walk
short: Otherworldly geothermal landscape of steaming craters, mud pools, and fumaroles on Taupō's outskirts.
commentary: Craters of the Moon is a dramatic geothermal walkway on the outskirts of Taupō, traversing an otherworldly landscape of steaming vents, hissing fumaroles, bubbling mud, and barren craters that truly evoke a lunar surface. The geothermal activity here increased dramatically in the 1950s when the nearby Wairakei Power Station lowered underground water levels, causing surface features to intensify. The well-maintained loop walk takes about 45 minutes and follows boardwalks through the steaming, sulphur-scented landscape with excellent interpretive signage explaining the geology. The geothermal features are constantly changing — new vents open and old ones close. The contrast between the steaming barren ground and the surrounding green bush is striking. A small admission fee supports the site's maintenance. Located on Karapiti Road, just north of Taupō near the Wairakei Tourist Park. Best visited on cool or overcast days when the steam is most dramatic. Safe for families with supervised children — stay on the boardwalks at all times.
review_summary: An amazing geothermal experience at a fraction of the cost of the big attractions. The steaming landscape is genuinely otherworldly. Easy walking on boardwalks. Best on cool, misty days for maximum atmospheric effect.

name: Taupo Rock n Ropes
short: Outdoor climbing and high ropes adventure park in Taupo with challenges for all ages and abilities.
commentary: Rock n Ropes is Taupo's outdoor climbing and high-ropes adventure park, offering a range of aerial challenges in a bush setting on Spa Road. The park features a high ropes course with swinging logs, cargo nets, and balance beams at height, plus climbing walls of varying difficulty, a giant swing, and the Leap of Faith, where you jump from a platform to catch a trapeze bar. Activities are graded for different ages and abilities, making it suitable for families with children as young as five through to adults seeking a physical challenge. All equipment and safety briefings are provided, with experienced instructors guiding participants through each element. The bush setting provides shade on hot Taupo summer days. Allow two hours for a full session. The park is open year-round, though booking is recommended during school holidays. Located within walking distance of central Taupo.
review_summary: A fun, physically engaging activity for families and groups. The range of challenges suits all confidence levels. Great instructors who encourage without pressuring. Good value for a couple of hours.

name: Kinloch Craft Beer & Wine Precinct
short: Lakeside dining and tasting precinct in Kinloch village near Taupo with craft beverages and local cuisine.
commentary: The lakeside village of Kinloch, on the western shore of Lake Taupo, has developed a reputation as a food and beverage destination with several quality establishments within walking distance. The Kinloch area offers a quieter alternative to bustling Taupo town, with lake views and a relaxed village atmosphere. Local venues serve craft beers sourced from Central North Island breweries alongside wines from the broader region. The dining scene champions local produce with casual and refined options available. The village is set against native bush with walking tracks connecting to lake beaches. During summer, the waterfront is a popular swimming and kayaking spot. Kinloch is about 20 minutes from Taupo via a scenic drive through farmland and native bush. The combination of good food and drink, lake access, and a peaceful setting makes it a rewarding half-day outing from Taupo.
review_summary: A pleasant escape from central Taupo with good food and beverage options. The lakeside setting is lovely and the village has a relaxed feel. Worth the short drive for lunch.

name: Mine Bay Maori Rock Carvings
short: Monumental Maori rock carvings on Lake Taupo's western cliffs, accessible only by boat or kayak.
commentary: The Mine Bay Maori Rock Carvings are a monumental piece of modern Maori art carved into the cliff face on the western shore of Lake Taupo. The main carving depicts Ngatoroirangi, the Maori navigator who guided the Tuwharetoa and Te Arawa tribes to the Taupo region over a thousand years ago. Standing over 14 metres high, the carving was created in the late 1970s by master carver Matahi Whakataka-Brightwell and a team of artists, taking four summers to complete. The carvings are accessible only by water, adding to their sense of remote grandeur. Multiple operators offer boat cruises, kayak tours, and paddleboard trips to the site from the Taupo waterfront. The surrounding cliff face features additional smaller carvings depicting Celtic and South African themes, reflecting the artists' diverse heritage. The morning light illuminates the carvings best. Kayak tours offer the most intimate experience, allowing you to paddle close and appreciate the scale. A distinctly Taupo cultural experience.
review_summary: A powerful and impressive piece of cultural art in a dramatic lakeside setting. The boat trip to reach the carvings adds to the experience. The scale of the main carving is genuinely striking up close.

name: Huka Falls
short: New Zealand's most visited natural attraction, where the Waikato River thunders through a narrow chasm.
commentary: Huka Falls is New Zealand's most visited natural attraction, where the full volume of the Waikato River, New Zealand's longest river, is forced through a narrow rock chasm just 15 metres wide before plunging over an 11-metre fall. The result is an extraordinary display of power, with up to 220,000 litres of water per second surging through the chasm in a brilliant turquoise-blue torrent. A footbridge over the falls provides heart-stopping views directly down into the churning water. Short walking tracks along both banks of the river above the falls offer multiple viewing angles and access to the crystal-clear blue pools upstream. The falls are a 5-minute drive north of Taupo on Huka Falls Road, with a large free car park. The site is free to visit and accessible year-round. Jet boat tours from downstream bring visitors to the base of the falls for a dramatic spray-soaked perspective. The river walk extends upstream to the Spa Park thermal area.
review_summary: An awe-inspiring display of natural power that photographs brilliantly. The turquoise water colour is extraordinary. Free, easily accessible, and genuinely impressive regardless of how many waterfalls you have seen.

name: Lake Rotopounamu Walk
short: Peaceful bush walk around a pristine volcanic lake near Tongariro.
commentary: Lake Rotopounamu is a small, perfectly formed volcanic lake nestled in native bush on the southern slopes of Pīhanga, the mountain that overlooks the Tongariro National Park. The lake loop track takes about ninety minutes and passes through beautiful podocarp-broadleaf forest alive with birdsong. The lake's still, dark waters reflect the surrounding bush perfectly on calm days, creating stunning mirror-image reflections. Unlike nearby Lake Taupō, Rotopounamu is free of introduced trout, preserving its pristine native ecology. The lake is significant to Ngāti Tūwharetoa as a sacred place associated with the love story of Pīhanga. The track is mostly flat and well-formed, suitable for families. Located on State Highway 47 between Turangi and National Park, making it a convenient stop.
review_summary: A peaceful and beautiful bush walk around a pristine lake. The birdsong is wonderful. Easy walking suitable for families.

name: Rotorua Museum Te Whare Taonga o Te Arawa
short: Iconic Tudor-style former bathhouse in Government Gardens, housing Rotorua's regional museum (check reopening status).
commentary: The Rotorua Museum Te Whare Taonga o Te Arawa is housed in one of New Zealand's most iconic buildings — the magnificent Tudor-style Government Bathhouse, built in 1908 as a thermal spa facility in the beautifully landscaped Government Gardens. The building itself is a Category I historic place and an Instagram-worthy landmark. The museum's collections tell the story of Te Arawa people, the volcanic landscape, the Pink and White Terraces (destroyed in the 1886 Tarawera eruption), and the development of tourism in Rotorua. The basement reveals the original bathhouse plumbing and acid-eaten pipes. Note: the museum has been closed for earthquake strengthening since 2016 — check the current status before visiting, as reopening has been subject to delays. Even when closed, the Government Gardens and building exterior are well worth visiting. The formal gardens, croquet lawns, and bowling greens surrounded by steaming thermal features create a unique and photogenic setting unlike anywhere else in the world.
review_summary: The building and Government Gardens are absolutely stunning even if the museum is still closed for strengthening. The setting with steam rising among formal gardens is uniquely Rotorua. Check reopening status before planning a museum visit.

name: Blue Baths Rotorua
short: Beautifully restored 1933 Art Deco thermal swimming pool in Rotorua's Government Gardens.
commentary: The Blue Baths is a charming heritage thermal bathing facility in Rotorua's Government Gardens, originally opened in 1933 as one of the first public swimming pools in New Zealand to allow mixed bathing. The building is a beautiful example of the Spanish Mission architectural style with Art Deco influences, featuring white stucco walls, arched windows, and a terracotta tile roof. Closed in 1982, the building was lovingly restored and reopened in 1999 as a thermal pool and function venue. The heated pool uses naturally warm geothermal water, providing a relaxing swimming experience in an elegant heritage setting. The venue also hosts private events, and the laughing gas comedy nights are popular. The setting within the Government Gardens, alongside the Rotorua Museum bathhouse building, creates a unique atmosphere of Edwardian-era bathing culture. The small museum area inside tells the story of the baths' colourful history. Pool temperatures are maintained at a comfortable level for swimming, not as hot as some thermal pools.
review_summary: A lovely, relaxed thermal bathing experience in a gorgeous heritage building. Not as hot as some pools but the setting and history make it special. The Government Gardens location is beautiful.

name: Tarawera Falls Walk
short: Dramatic waterfall bursting from a cliff face in native forest, reached by a beautiful bush walk.
commentary: Tarawera Falls is one of the most spectacular waterfalls in the North Island — a powerful torrent of water that bursts directly from a cliff face in the Tarawera River, having flowed through underground lava tubes from Lake Tarawera. The water emerges from holes and crevices in the basalt rock face, creating a dramatic and unusual waterfall unlike any other. The walk to the falls passes through beautiful native forest, predominantly podocarp species including rimu, mataī, and kahikatea. Birdlife is abundant — listen for tūī, bellbirds, and kererū. The track takes about 45 minutes each way and is well formed, making it suitable for families with older children. The falls are located in the Tarawera Forest, and access requires a forestry permit (available from the Kawerau i-SITE or online). The gravel forestry roads require care but are suitable for standard vehicles. The remote setting and native bush surroundings make this walk feel like a genuine wilderness experience, despite being relatively close to Rotorua.
review_summary: An extraordinary waterfall — water literally bursts from the rock face. The bush walk there is beautiful too. You need a forestry permit to access, which adds a small hassle, but it's absolutely worth it.

name: Capers Epicurean
short: Award-winning gourmet cafe and delicatessen in central Rotorua championing local Bay of Plenty produce.
commentary: Capers Epicurean is Rotorua's premier gourmet cafe and delicatessen, located on Eat Streat, the city's dedicated dining precinct on Tutanekai Street. The cafe has won multiple hospitality awards for its commitment to showcasing local Bay of Plenty and Waikato produce in creative dishes. The cabinet overflows with artisan breads, pastries, salads, and savoury items, while the lunch menu features seasonal dishes made with locally sourced ingredients. The retail section stocks a curated selection of New Zealand artisan products, from local honeys and preserves to specialty chocolates and condiments, making it an excellent spot for edible souvenirs. Coffee is expertly prepared and the cabinet baking is outstanding. The vibrant Eat Streat location means you are surrounded by other dining options, but Capers consistently stands out for quality. Arrive before noon to beat the lunch rush.
review_summary: Consistently excellent food and coffee with a focus on local produce. The cabinet selection is outstanding and the deli items make great gifts. A Rotorua dining highlight.

name: Velocity Valley Adventure Park
short: Multi-activity adventure park in Rotorua with jet sprinting, bungy, freefalling, and the Shweeb monorail.
commentary: Velocity Valley (formerly Agroventures) is Rotorua's one-stop adventure park, packing multiple adrenaline activities into a single location on Western Road. The headline attraction is the Agrojet, a jet sprint boat that rockets through a narrow water channel at 100km/h, pulling extreme G-forces on the turns. Other activities include the Rotorua Bungy, a 43-metre jump over the Ngongotaha Stream; the Swoop, a giant sky swing reaching 130km/h; the Shweeb, the world's first human-powered monorail raceway; and freefall Xtreme, a vertical wind tunnel simulating skydiving. Multi-ride combo passes offer the best value and allow you to experience several activities in a half-day visit. Each ride includes full safety briefings and professional staff. Located five minutes from central Rotorua, it is easily combined with other Rotorua attractions. Suitable for ages 10 and up for most activities.
review_summary: A fantastic one-stop adventure venue with something for every thrill-seeker. The Agrojet is heart-pounding and the Shweeb is uniquely fun. Great value with combo passes.

name: Skyline MTB Gravity Park
short: Gondola-accessed mountain bike park on Mt Ngongotaha with downhill trails for all skill levels.
commentary: Skyline Rotorua's MTB Gravity Park is one of New Zealand's premier mountain biking destinations, offering gondola-accessed downhill trails on the slopes of Mt Ngongotaha. The Skyline Gondola lifts riders and bikes to the top, where a network of trails descends through native bush and open terrain. Trails range from gentle Grade 2 flow trails suitable for beginners to demanding Grade 5 black diamond runs with technical rock gardens and big drops for expert riders. The park has hosted UCI Mountain Bike World Cup events, cementing its international reputation. Full bike hire and protective gear are available, and the on-site bike shop handles any mechanical issues. Uplift passes allow unlimited gondola rides for a session. The Redwoods mountain bike trails in the Whakarewarewa Forest are nearby, offering cross-country riding to complement the Skyline gravity trails. Rotorua is widely regarded as New Zealand's mountain biking capital.
review_summary: World-class mountain biking with the convenience of gondola uplift. Trail variety caters to all abilities. The combination of Skyline gravity trails and Redwoods cross-country makes Rotorua a mountain biking paradise.

name: Whakarewarewa - The Living Maori Village
short: Living Maori village where Tuhourangi Ngati Wahiao people have lived among geothermal features for centuries.
commentary: Whakarewarewa is a living Maori village where the Tuhourangi Ngati Wahiao people have resided for over 200 years, using the geothermal activity for cooking, bathing, and heating. Guided tours led by village residents provide authentic insights into daily life, traditions, and the unique relationship between the community and their geothermal environment. You will see geysers, hot pools, and silica terraces while learning about Maori customs, history, and the practical uses of geothermal energy. Cultural performances include haka, poi, and traditional songs in the carved meeting house. The village shop sells locally made arts and crafts. Hangi meals cooked in geothermal steam are available. This is a genuine community experience, not a theme park, and residents' hospitality makes it deeply memorable. Tours depart regularly throughout the day. Located in the southern part of Rotorua city.
review_summary: An authentic and moving cultural experience guided by village residents who share their stories with warmth and humour. The geothermal setting adds a dramatic backdrop. A highlight of any Rotorua visit.

name: Te Puia - NZ Maori Arts & Crafts Institute
short: National centre for Maori arts featuring carving and weaving schools, the Pohutu Geyser, and cultural performances.
commentary: Te Puia is the home of the New Zealand Maori Arts and Crafts Institute, the national school for traditional Maori carving (whakairo) and weaving (raranga). Located in the Te Whakarewarewa Geothermal Valley, the centre combines living Maori arts education with spectacular geothermal features including Pohutu Geyser, the Southern Hemisphere's largest active geyser, which erupts up to 30 metres high. Guided tours take visitors through the carving and weaving schools where students learn these taonga (treasured art forms), the geothermal valley with its boiling mud pools and silica terraces, and a recreated traditional village. Cultural performances featuring haka and poi are held in the carved meeting house. The centre also houses a kiwi conservation programme where visitors may see the nocturnal bird. Night-time tours offer a different perspective with geothermal features dramatically lit. Te Puia is a cornerstone Rotorua cultural experience.
review_summary: A comprehensive Maori cultural experience with the added spectacle of Pohutu Geyser. Watching carving and weaving students at work is inspiring. Cultural performances are powerful and authentic.

name: Buried Village of Te Wairoa
short: Archaeological site of the village buried by the 1886 Mt Tarawera eruption, with museum and waterfall walk.
commentary: The Buried Village of Te Wairoa is an archaeological site preserving the remains of a village destroyed by the catastrophic eruption of Mt Tarawera on 10 June 1886. The eruption killed over 100 people and buried the village under metres of mud and ash, also destroying the famous Pink and White Terraces, then considered the eighth wonder of the natural world. The museum tells the story of the eruption through artefacts excavated from the ruins, photographs of the terraces, and accounts from survivors. A walking trail leads through the excavated village site, where foundations, walls, and personal belongings have been uncovered. The trail continues through native bush to Te Wairoa Falls, a beautiful waterfall cascading into a bush-lined gorge. The site offers a powerful connection to one of New Zealand's most significant natural disasters. Located about 15km southeast of Rotorua on the road to Lake Tarawera. The combination of history, archaeology, and natural beauty makes this a compelling Rotorua visit.
review_summary: A moving and educational heritage site that brings the 1886 eruption story to life. The excavated village is fascinating and the waterfall walk is beautiful. A compelling alternative to Rotorua's geothermal attractions.

name: Back Beach / Ngā Motu New Plymouth
short: Wild surf beach with dramatic Sugar Loaf Islands offshore and stunning Taranaki mountain views.
commentary: Back Beach (also known as Ngā Motu Beach) is New Plymouth's wild western beach, stretching beneath the dramatic volcanic remnants of the Sugar Loaf Islands (Ngā Motu) — a chain of rocky islets that are the eroded remains of an ancient volcano. The beach offers powerful surf breaks popular with experienced surfers, though conditions can be challenging. The setting is spectacular — volcanic rock formations, the Sugar Loaf Islands rising from the sea, and on clear days, the perfect cone of Mount Taranaki towering behind the city. The Paritutu Rock track nearby provides a steep but rewarding climb to a summit with extraordinary 360-degree views. The Sugar Loaf Islands are a marine protected area home to fur seals, little blue penguins, and rare reef fish. Kayak tours to the islands run in summer. The Coastal Walkway connects Back Beach to the city centre along the waterfront. This is Taranaki's most dramatic coastal landscape, combining raw natural beauty with accessible adventure.
review_summary: Dramatic and beautiful — the Sugar Loaf Islands are stunning and the surf is powerful. Climb Paritutu Rock for unforgettable views of the coast and mountain. The Coastal Walkway connection is brilliant.

name: Fitzroy Beach New Plymouth
short: Popular New Plymouth surf and swimming beach with a vibrant local atmosphere and mountain backdrop.
commentary: Fitzroy Beach is one of New Plymouth's most popular beaches, a stretch of dark volcanic sand (characteristic of the Taranaki coast) with reliable surf breaks and a strong local beach culture. The beach is patrolled during summer and offers good swimming conditions between the flags, while surfers spread along the beach to catch the various breaks. The surf club is a community hub, and the beach has a vibrant, family-friendly atmosphere on summer weekends. Fitzroy is connected to the city centre via the celebrated New Plymouth Coastal Walkway, a 13-kilometre shared path that is one of the best urban coastal walks in New Zealand. The dark sand, crashing waves, and views of Mount Taranaki on clear days create a distinctively Taranaki beach experience. The East End Reserve behind the beach offers shade and picnic facilities. Fitzroy is easy to reach from central New Plymouth and has good parking. The waves are generally suitable for intermediate surfers, with surf schools operating for beginners.
review_summary: A great surf and swimming beach with a real community feel. The dark volcanic sand is unique and the mountain views on clear days are stunning. The Coastal Walkway connection is a huge bonus.

name: Mike's Organic Brewery
short: New Zealand's first certified organic brewery, producing natural beers in a rural Urenui setting.
commentary: Mike's Organic Brewery holds the distinction of being New Zealand's first certified organic brewery, established by Mike Neilson in the small coastal settlement of Urenui, north of New Plymouth. The brewery produces a range of beers using only certified organic ingredients, from malt and hops to the yeast, resulting in clean, natural flavours without artificial additives. The range includes a refreshing Organic Pale Ale, a robust Porter, and seasonal specials. The brewery and tasting room occupy a rustic rural property where visitors can sample the range and learn about organic brewing practices. Mike's passion for sustainability extends to the brewery operations, with waste grain going to local farms and minimal packaging. The location is about 30 minutes north of New Plymouth on SH3, easily combined with a visit to the Three Sisters rock formations or the White Cliffs walkway nearby. Phone ahead to confirm opening hours.
review_summary: A genuine, small-scale brewing operation with beers that taste authentically different. Mike is a passionate host who loves sharing his organic philosophy. The rural setting adds to the charm.

name: Forgotten World Highway
short: New Zealand's most remote heritage highway, 150km through ghost towns, tunnels, and dramatic hill country.
commentary: The Forgotten World Highway (SH43) between Stratford and Taumarunui is New Zealand's loneliest highway, winding 150 kilometres through some of the North Island's most rugged and sparsely populated hill country. The road passes through the Tangarakau Gorge, one of New Zealand's deepest road gorges, the Moki Tunnel blasted through solid rock, and the ghost town of Whangamomona, a self-declared republic since 1989 that holds biennial Republic Day celebrations. Whangamomona's historic hotel offers refreshments and the chance to get your passport stamped. The highway traverses land once heavily milled for timber and farmed, now largely returned to regenerating bush. No fuel is available between Stratford and Taumarunui, so fill up before departing. The road includes some unsealed sections and is narrow and winding throughout. Allow at least four hours for the drive with stops. The sense of remoteness and the dramatic landscape make this one of New Zealand's most memorable drives.
review_summary: A truly unique New Zealand road trip through dramatically remote country. Whangamomona is a delightful eccentric stop. The Tangarakau Gorge is spectacular. Ensure a full tank of fuel before starting.

name: Cape Egmont Boat Club & Coastline
short: Dramatic volcanic coastline with rock pools, surf breaks, and spectacular sunsets on Taranaki's western shore.
commentary: The coastline around Cape Egmont on Taranaki's western shore offers some of the region's most dramatic coastal scenery. Dark volcanic rock platforms extend into the Tasman Sea, creating tide pools teeming with marine life and forming the base for powerful surf breaks. The area around the boat ramp is a favourite spot for sunset watching, with the sun dropping into the sea framed by the rugged coastline. On clear days, Mt Taranaki looms behind, creating an iconic combination of mountain and sea. The coastline is part of the Surf Highway 45 route between New Plymouth and Hawera. Rock-pooling at low tide reveals starfish, anemones, and small fish in the volcanic pools. The area is exposed and can be dangerous in rough seas, so stay well back from the waterline during storms. Several Taranaki surf breaks are accessible from nearby pulloffs along the highway.
review_summary: Stunning volcanic coastline with incredible sunset views. The combination of dramatic rocks and Mt Taranaki backdrop is uniquely Taranaki. Great for photography and rock-pooling at low tide.

name: Mike's Organic Brewery
short: New Zealand's only certified organic brewery, in Urenui near New Plymouth.
commentary: Mike's Organic Brewery in Urenui holds the distinction of being New Zealand's only fully certified organic brewery. Founded by Mike Johnson, the brewery produces a range of beers using certified organic malt, hops, and yeast, with the water drawn from a local aquifer. The brewery's Organic Pilsner and Pale Ale have won numerous awards. The cellar door is open for tastings and sales, and the relaxed atmosphere reflects the brewery's ethos of quality over quantity. Urenui is a small coastal settlement about thirty minutes north of New Plymouth on State Highway 3, and the brewery makes an interesting stop on a coastal drive. The surrounding area offers surf beaches and bush walks. Group tours can be arranged by appointment.
review_summary: Unique organic beers that taste great. The small-scale operation has genuine character. Worth the detour from New Plymouth.

name: Kuaotunu Beach
short: Unspoilt golden sand beach in a relaxed Coromandel settlement, perfect for swimming and kayaking.
commentary: Kuaotunu Beach is one of the Coromandel Peninsula's most appealing and unspoilt beaches, a lovely crescent of golden sand in a quiet holiday settlement north of Whitianga. The beach is sheltered and safe for swimming, with warm, clear water in summer that makes it popular with families. The small community maintains a relaxed, uncommercialised atmosphere — there is a charming general store and the acclaimed Luke's Kitchen café, but otherwise it is beach, bush, and birdsong. The beach faces north, catching sun all day, and the backdrop of native bush-clad hills adds to the beauty. Kayaking and stand-up paddleboarding are popular in the calm conditions. The Black Jack Road scenic drive from Kuaotunu to Whitianga is a winding coastal route with spectacular views. Kuaotunu also provides access to some beautiful coastal walks. Summer holiday period gets busy but never crowded. Limited facilities, so bring supplies.
review_summary: One of the Coromandel's best-kept secrets — gorgeous, uncrowded beach with crystal-clear water. Luke's Kitchen does amazing food. The perfect place to slow down and unwind.

name: Matarangi Beach
short: Long golden sand beach in an upmarket Coromandel resort community with safe swimming and ocean views.
commentary: Matarangi Beach stretches for several kilometres along the northern Coromandel coast, a broad expanse of golden sand backed by an upmarket resort community and golf course. The beach offers safe swimming conditions with relatively gentle surf, making it popular with families. The Dunes Golf Course provides a links-style golfing experience with ocean views. Matarangi has grown from a small holiday spot into one of the Coromandel's premier resort communities, with a café, restaurant, and well-maintained facilities. The beach is less well-known than neighbouring Whitianga or Hot Water Beach, meaning it remains relatively uncrowded even in peak summer. Walking the full length of the beach at low tide is a wonderful experience. The area also offers access to the Rings Beach walk and other coastal tracks. A relaxed, family-oriented destination that captures the best of the Coromandel's beach lifestyle without the crowds.
review_summary: A beautiful, spacious beach that never feels too busy. Great for families with safe swimming and plenty of room. The golf course is a bonus and the café does good food.

name: Driving Creek Railway
short: Quirky narrow-gauge railway climbing through native bush and art installations to a hilltop viewpoint.
commentary: Driving Creek Railway is the creation of potter and conservationist Barry Brickell, who spent decades building this narrow-gauge mountain railway through regenerating native kauri forest on his property near Coromandel Town. The one-hour return journey climbs 165 metres through tunnels, across bridges, and around spirals, passing Brickell's ceramic sculptures and art installations integrated into the bush. The terminus at the Eyefull Tower provides panoramic views over Coromandel Harbour and the Hauraki Gulf. The railway was built primarily to carry clay and firewood for Brickell's pottery kiln, but became a beloved tourist attraction. The surrounding land is a conservation project with extensive native tree planting. Multiple departures run daily and booking is recommended, especially during summer. The pottery and gallery at the base are worth browsing.
review_summary: A truly unique New Zealand experience that delights visitors of all ages. The combination of engineering ingenuity, art, and conservation is inspiring. The hilltop views are stunning on a clear day.

name: The Waterworks
short: Eccentric water-powered theme park in the bush with kinetic sculptures and interactive water contraptions.
commentary: The Waterworks is a delightfully eccentric interactive park hidden in native bush north of Coromandel Town. Created from recycled materials, the park features dozens of water-powered contraptions, kinetic sculptures, musical instruments, and hands-on exhibits spread along bush tracks beside a stream. Highlights include water cannons, a flying fox, pedal-powered watercraft on a small lake, and a water-powered clock. The park embodies Kiwi ingenuity and DIY spirit, appealing equally to children who love getting wet and adults who appreciate the inventive engineering. Allow at least two hours and bring a change of clothes for children. The bush setting is beautiful, with swimming holes in the stream during summer. Located 7km north of Coromandel Town along the 309 Road, a scenic gravel route across the peninsula.
review_summary: A wonderfully quirky and uniquely Kiwi attraction. Kids absolutely love the interactive water play while parents appreciate the creative engineering. The bush setting adds to the charm.

name: Coromandel Stamper Battery
short: Restored 19th-century gold stamper battery and water wheel near Coromandel Town, a working heritage attraction.
commentary: The Coromandel Stamper Battery is a restored gold-processing plant dating from the 1890s gold-mining era, located a short drive from Coromandel Town along the 309 Road. The battery features a massive working water wheel that once powered the stamps used to crush gold-bearing quartz rock. Guided tours demonstrate the stamping process, explaining how raw ore was crushed to extract gold, and tell the stories of the miners who worked the Coromandel goldfields. The site includes the original machinery, settling tables, and processing equipment in their bush setting. The water wheel, powered by a diverted creek, is particularly impressive and still operational. The 309 Road itself is a scenic gravel route across the peninsula, connecting the eastern and western coastlines. Combine with nearby attractions including The Waterworks and Castle Rock walk for a full day on the 309 Road. A hands-on heritage experience that brings gold-rush history to life.
review_summary: A fascinating working heritage site that brings gold-mining history to life. The water wheel is impressive and the guided explanations are engaging. A good stop on the scenic 309 Road.

name: Hot Water Beach
short: Unique beach where hot thermal water rises through the sand, allowing you to dig your own hot pool at low tide.
commentary: Hot Water Beach is one of New Zealand's most unique natural experiences, where geothermal springs rise through the sand on a stretch of Coromandel coastline. At low tide, visitors dig holes in the sand with spades (available for hire from nearby shops) to create personal hot pools that fill with naturally heated water. The thermal area is confined to a small section of the beach, marked by steam rising from the sand, and temperatures can exceed 64 degrees Celsius, so mixing with cold seawater is essential to achieve a comfortable bathing temperature. The experience works best in the two hours either side of low tide. The beach itself is beautiful, backed by headlands and popular for surfing. The thermal springs are believed to be connected to the same volcanic system that feeds the Coromandel's other geothermal features. Located on the eastern Coromandel coast between Tairua and Whitianga, it is a must-visit on any Coromandel road trip. Can get very busy in peak summer.
review_summary: A truly unique natural experience that delights visitors of all ages. Digging your own hot pool on a beach is pure joy. Check tide times carefully and arrive early in peak season to secure a good spot.

name: Cathedral Cove
short: Iconic natural sea arch on a pristine white sand beach, reached by a scenic coastal walk through native bush.
commentary: Cathedral Cove (Te Whanganui-A-Hei) is the Coromandel Peninsula's most iconic landmark, a dramatic natural sea arch framing a white sand beach between sculpted rock walls. The cove is reached by a scenic 45-minute walk from the car park at Hahei, descending through native bush with views across Mercury Bay to volcanic islands. The trail passes several smaller bays and lookout points before arriving at the cove, where the massive arch connects two beaches. The rock formations are sculpted from ancient volcanic ash (ignimbrite), and the clear turquoise water is excellent for swimming and snorkelling. The area is a marine reserve, rich in marine life. Water taxi and kayak access is available from Hahei for those who prefer not to walk. The cove featured in the Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian. A new shuttle bus service from Hahei to the trailhead eases parking pressure during peak summer. Early morning visits offer the best light and fewer people.
review_summary: One of New Zealand's most beautiful natural features. The walk to the cove is scenic and the arch itself is breathtaking. Visit early morning or late afternoon for the best experience.

name: Thames Goldmine Experience
short: Underground gold mine tour and museum in Thames, the gateway town to the Coromandel Peninsula.
commentary: The Thames Goldmine Experience offers a guided underground tour through a historic gold mine in Thames, the former gold-mining capital of New Zealand. In its 1870s heyday, Thames had a population of 18,000 and was one of the largest towns in the country, fuelled by the wealth extracted from the quartz reefs beneath the surrounding hills. The tour takes visitors underground into actual mine tunnels where the working conditions, mining techniques, and geological formations are explained by knowledgeable guides. Above ground, the museum displays mining equipment, gold specimens, and photographs from the boom years. The experience provides valuable context for understanding the Coromandel's gold-mining heritage, which shaped the peninsula's settlement patterns and landscape. Thames itself retains heritage character in its main street buildings. Located at the southern gateway to the Coromandel Peninsula, the goldmine makes an interesting stop before heading up the coast.
review_summary: An engaging underground experience that brings gold-mining history to life. The guides are informative and the tunnel walk is atmospheric. A good introduction to the Coromandel's mining heritage.

name: MTG Hawke's Bay Museum Theatre Gallery
short: Napier's museum, theatre, and gallery complex telling the dramatic story of the 1931 earthquake and Art Deco rebuild.
commentary: MTG Hawke's Bay (Museum Theatre Gallery) is Napier's principal cultural institution, located on the Marine Parade waterfront. The museum's permanent exhibition powerfully tells the story of the devastating 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake that destroyed Napier and the remarkable Art Deco rebuild that followed, creating the unique streetscape the city is famous for today. Displays include earthquake relics, survivor testimonies, a recreated 1930s streetscape, and extensive Art Deco architectural photography. The gallery spaces host rotating exhibitions of regional and national art. The building itself is a modern, award-winning design that sits well alongside Napier's historic waterfront architecture. The century theatre hosts films, performances, and talks. Admission to permanent exhibitions is free. MTG provides essential context for understanding Napier's Art Deco heritage — visit here before taking a walking tour of the town. The café overlooks the waterfront, and the shop stocks excellent local art and design.
review_summary: The earthquake exhibition is deeply moving and really brings the 1931 disaster to life. Essential background before exploring Napier's Art Deco streets. Great gallery exhibitions too, and free entry.

name: National Tobacco Company Building
short: Napier's finest Art Deco building with stunning Māori-inspired reliefs and original 1930s interior details.
commentary: The National Tobacco Company Building on Ahuriri's waterfront is widely regarded as the finest Art Deco building in New Zealand and one of the best in the world. Designed by Louis Hay and built in 1933 after the earthquake, the building is a masterpiece of the Art Deco Stripped Classical style, featuring extraordinary exterior reliefs of roses, raupo (bulrushes), and nikau palms executed in a stylised Māori-influenced design. The interior retains original terrazzo floors, pressed zinc ceilings, and beautiful leadlight windows. The building is classified as a Category I historic place and was a key site in Napier's successful Art Deco heritage campaign. Now operated as a commercial venue, the building can be viewed from outside at any time, and interior access may be available during business hours or Art Deco Festival weekends. Located in the Ahuriri waterfront area, it combines well with a walk around this atmospheric harbour precinct.
review_summary: Absolutely stunning building — the decorative reliefs are incredible works of art. A must-see for anyone interested in Art Deco architecture. The Ahuriri location adds to the atmosphere.

name: Hastings Municipal Theatre (Toitoi)
short: Beautifully preserved Art Deco theatre in Hastings, now the Toitoi performing arts hub for Hawke's Bay.
commentary: The Hastings Municipal Theatre, now branded as Toitoi — Hawke's Bay Arts & Events Centre, is one of the finest remaining Art Deco theatres in New Zealand. Built in 1915 and extensively remodelled in the Art Deco style after the 1931 earthquake, the theatre features a magnificent interior with decorative plasterwork, period lighting, and elegant proportions. The Opera House auditorium seats approximately 950 and hosts a year-round programme of touring shows, local theatre, music, dance, and comedy. The complex also includes the intimate Playhouse Studio for smaller productions. Toitoi serves as Hawke's Bay's premier performing arts centre, complementing Napier's Municipal Theatre across the twin cities. The Art Deco architectural details throughout the building reward close inspection. Check the programme for current shows — the venue attracts quality touring productions. Located on Hastings Street in the centre of town, surrounded by more examples of Art Deco commercial architecture.
review_summary: A beautiful Art Deco theatre with real character and charm. The interior is gorgeous and seeing a show here feels special. Great programme of events throughout the year.

name: Ocean Beach Hawke's Bay
short: Wild and dramatic surf beach south of Cape Kidnappers with powerful waves and remote, untamed beauty.
commentary: Ocean Beach is a spectacular wild beach south of Cape Kidnappers on the Hawke's Bay coast, offering a dramatically different experience from the region's gentler harbour beaches.
