The ingenious mechanism used the gun's recoil to lower it behind a parapet for reloading, making it nearly invisible to enemy ships. Only one other example survives worldwide. Guided tours explain the engineering and the geopolitical paranoia that put a state-of-the-art weapon on a headland in the South Pacific. It's the kind of obscure historical footnote that New Zealand does brilliantly.
review_summary: A fascinating piece of military history. Guided tours bring the engineering to life. Easily combined with the albatross colony visit. Some note the tours are weather-dependent.

name: Emerson's Brewery & Taproom, Dunedin
short: The godfather of New Zealand craft beer — Emerson's taproom in Dunedin pours 20+ beers with excellent food to match.
commentary: Richard Emerson started brewing in Dunedin in 1992, well before craft beer was fashionable, and his Pilsner and Bookbinder remain benchmarks of New Zealand brewing. The purpose-built taproom and restaurant in Anzac Avenue has over 20 beers on tap including seasonal specials you won't find anywhere else, paired with a menu designed to complement them. Brewery tours run regularly and offer insight into both the brewing process and Emerson's role in pioneering the New Zealand craft beer movement. Even if you're not a beer enthusiast, the Bookbinder session ale and a plate of beer-battered fish will convert you.
review_summary: Outstanding beer and food in an impressive space. The brewery tour is informative and fun. The Pilsner and Bookbinder are must-tries. Can get busy at weekends — book ahead.

name: The Lost Gypsy Gallery, Papatowai
short: A fantastical museum of automata art in a bus in the Catlins — push every button and wind every handle.
commentary: The Lost Gypsy Gallery is the creation of Blair Somerville, who builds intricate mechanical automata, kinetic sculptures and curiosities in a converted bus and surrounding sheds at Papatowai in the Catlins. Every surface is covered with things to push, wind, pull and watch — mechanical fish swim through wooden oceans, tiny figures perform complex dances, and optical illusions play tricks on your perception. It's part art gallery, part workshop, part cabinet of curiosities. Children are enchanted and adults are equally absorbed. Blair is often on-site and happy to explain his creative process. Nothing else in New Zealand is quite like it.
review_summary: Universally adored by visitors of all ages. The creativity and craftsmanship are extraordinary. Blair is a generous and fascinating host. Allow longer than you think — there's always more to discover.

name: Otago Harbour Heritage Cruise
short: A harbour cruise from Dunedin along the Otago Peninsula — albatross, seals, penguins and colonial history from the water.
commentary: Cruising Otago Harbour by boat offers a completely different perspective on the peninsula's wildlife and heritage. Departing from central Dunedin, the cruise follows the harbour to Taiaroa Head, passing Port Chalmers, Quarantine Island, and the dramatic volcanic cliffs of the peninsula. Wildlife encounters may include albatross, blue penguins, fur seals and shags, while the guide narrates the harbour's Māori and colonial history. The return journey at dusk is particularly special as blue penguins begin arriving at their nesting sites along the harbour edge.
review_summary: A relaxing way to see the peninsula wildlife. The guides are knowledgeable about both ecology and history. Afternoon departures offer the best penguin viewing. Can be cold — dress warmly.

name: Teapot Land, Owaka
short: A whimsical garden in the Catlins adorned with over 1,200 teapots — delightfully eccentric roadside art.
commentary: Teapot Land in Owaka is exactly what it sounds like — a garden festooned with over 1,200 teapots of every size, shape, colour and vintage, dangling from trees, perched on fences, nestled among flowers and arranged in elaborate displays. It started as a small collection and grew into a full-blown installation that now draws visitors from around the world. The owner maintains it with obvious love and humour, and entry is free (donations appreciated). It's the kind of gloriously eccentric Kiwi folk art that you can only find by leaving the main highways. Allow 20 minutes and bring a camera.
review_summary: A delightful and quirky surprise. Photography opportunities are endless. The owner's dedication is impressive. A brief but memorable stop in the Catlins.

name: Monkey Island / Te Puka o Takitimu Walk
short: A tiny island accessible on foot at low tide, with stairs to the summit offering spectacular views over Te Waewae Bay and Fiordland.
commentary: Monkey Island is a small rocky islet connected to the shore at low tide near Orepuki. A stairway carved into the rock leads to the summit, where views stretch across Te Waewae Bay to the snow-capped mountains of Fiordland. The name has nothing to do with primates — it likely derives from a corruption of a Māori name. The island is best visited at low tide when you can walk across the sand causeway; check tide tables before setting out. The surrounding beach is wild and windswept, and the sense of standing at the edge of the world is palpable.
review_summary: The views from the summit are breathtaking. Check tide times — you can get stranded. The stairs are steep but manageable. A wonderful off-the-beaten-track stop in western Southland.

name: Colac Bay / Ōraka Aparima Surfing
short: An uncrowded surf beach in southern Southland with consistent breaks, wide sands, and sunsets over Foveaux Strait.
commentary: Colac Bay is tucked away south of Riverton, offering a quieter, more authentic coastal experience than the more famous surf spots further north. The beach receives consistent swells from the Southern Ocean, producing reliable waves that suit intermediate surfers. The wide, sandy beach is rarely crowded — on a good day you might share the lineup with half a dozen locals. The sunsets here are extraordinary, with the sky turning gold and crimson over Foveaux Strait and Stewart Island silhouetted on the horizon. The small settlement has a holiday-park feel and a genuinely friendly local community.
review_summary: Great surf in uncrowded conditions. The sunsets are legendary. Water is cold — a good wetsuit is essential. The beach is wide and beautiful. A peaceful escape from tourist routes.

name: Two Wee Bookshops & The Snug, Manapōuri
short: Two of Aotearoa's smallest bookshops, tucked away in Manapōuri — the tiniest for children's books, the larger for southern stories.
commentary: Two Wee Bookshops are exactly that — two diminutive bookshops in Manapōuri, each barely bigger than a garden shed. The smaller focuses entirely on children's books, while the larger stocks titles about the deep south, Fiordland natural history, and New Zealand writing. The attached Snug café serves excellent coffee. Run by a couple with deep roots in the Fiordland community, the shops are a labour of love that punches well above their square footage. It's the perfect stop before or after a lake cruise, and the kind of place where a quick browse turns into an hour of discovery.
review_summary: Utterly charming and surprisingly well-stocked. The owners are warm and knowledgeable. The coffee is excellent. Don't skip it on your way to or from Doubtful Sound.

name: Switzers Museum, Waikaia
short: A community museum in Waikaia preserving the stories and artefacts of Southland's gold-mining era and rural heritage.
commentary: Switzers Museum in the small eastern Southland town of Waikaia is one of those volunteer-run gems that rewards the curious traveller. The collection spans the region's gold-mining heritage, farming history and community life, with artefacts ranging from mining equipment to household objects that tell the story of pioneer life in inland Southland. The museum is named after the Swiss settlers who were among the first to mine gold in the area. Volunteers are usually on hand to add stories that no label could capture. It's a genuine slice of provincial New Zealand heritage.
review_summary: A charming small-town museum with passionate volunteers. The gold-mining exhibits are interesting. Limited opening hours — check ahead. Worth combining with the drive through rural Southland.

name: The Giant's House, Akaroa
short: A whimsical garden of mosaic sculptures in the heart of Akaroa — playful, colourful and utterly original.
commentary: The Giant's House is the creation of artist Josie Martin, who has transformed the garden of her 1880 villa into a fantasia of mosaic sculpture. Life-size figures, animals, and abstract forms covered in shattered china, glass and tile spill across terraced gardens with harbour views. The work is joyful, irreverent and technically accomplished — Martin studied at the Canterbury School of Fine Arts and has exhibited internationally. The house itself is a B&B, but the sculpture garden is open to day visitors. Children adore it and the photo opportunities are endless.
review_summary: A delightful and unique experience. The artistry is impressive. Children love it. The harbour views add to the experience. Worth booking ahead in summer when it gets busy.

name: Lake Pearson / Moana Rua Wildlife Refuge
short: A high-country wildlife refuge sheltering the endangered Southern crested grebe — one of the best birding stops on Arthur's Pass Road.
commentary: Lake Pearson (Moana Rua) is a tussock-fringed alpine lake on the road to Arthur's Pass, designated as a wildlife refuge to protect its population of Southern crested grebe — one of New Zealand's rarest waterbirds with fewer than 600 remaining. The grebes are most visible in summer when they perform elaborate courtship dances, rising from the water in synchronised displays. The lake also supports scaup, paradise shelduck, and occasionally bittern. A layby on SH73 offers easy access to the shore. Bring binoculars and a long lens — the birds can be distant but the setting is magnificent.
review_summary: A beautiful alpine lake and excellent birding spot. The grebes are the star attraction. Bring binoculars — birds can be distant. The mountain backdrop is stunning. A worthwhile stop en route to Arthur's Pass.

name: Orana Wildlife Park Conservation Experience
short: New Zealand's only open-range zoo, with native species conservation programmes and close-up animal encounters.
commentary: Orana Wildlife Park on the outskirts of Christchurch is New Zealand's only open-range zoo, where animals roam in large, naturalistic enclosures. The park's New Zealand section focuses on conservation breeding programmes for kiwi, tuatara, kea, and blue duck, while the international section houses African and Asian species in spacious habitats. The park offers several unique encounter experiences — hand-feed giraffes, meet the kea up close, or join a behind-the-scenes conservation tour. The combination of open-range design, conservation focus and interactive experiences makes it one of the best wildlife attractions in the South Island.
review_summary: The open-range design sets it apart from conventional zoos. Conservation programmes are impressive. The giraffe feeding is a highlight. Some note it's a large park — allow plenty of time.

name: Barker's Foodstore & Cinema, Geraldine
short: Barker's famous fruit preserves and sauces, plus a boutique cinema — Geraldine's foodie destination in the heart of South Canterbury.
commentary: Barker's of Geraldine has been making fruit-based sauces, chutneys and preserves since 1969, using South Canterbury-grown fruit. The flagship store offers tastings of their full range alongside local cheeses, crafts and specialty foods. The attached boutique cinema screens current releases in plush comfort — a surprisingly sophisticated experience in a small South Canterbury town. Geraldine itself is a pleasant base for exploring the Mackenzie Country and Canterbury foothills, and Barker's makes a perfect refreshment stop. Their fruit syrups and berry chutneys make excellent souvenirs.
review_summary: The tastings are generous and the products excellent. The cinema is an unexpected bonus. A great stop between Christchurch and Mackenzie Country. The store is well-stocked with local goods.

name: The Lost Spring, Whitianga
short: Geothermal hot pools in a lush tropical garden setting — one of the Coromandel's most luxurious relaxation experiences.
commentary: The Lost Spring draws naturally heated water from deep underground — discovered by accident during a property survey — and channels it through a series of beautifully designed pools set among tropical gardens, waterfalls and palm trees. The water emerges at 58°C and is cooled to bathing temperature in different pools, each with its own character. There's a swim-up bar serving cocktails and the day spa offers treatments using natural products. It feels like a Balinese retreat transplanted to the Coromandel. Booking is essential, especially in summer and school holidays, as numbers are limited to maintain the tranquil atmosphere.
review_summary: A beautiful and relaxing experience. The tropical setting is stunning. The swim-up bar is a highlight. Some find prices steep but consider it worth it. Book well in advance.

name: Orokawa Bay Coastal Walk
short: A 45-minute coastal walk from Waihi Beach to a secluded bay — pristine sand, clear water, and blissful solitude.
commentary: Orokawa Bay is one of those places that rewards the effort of getting there. The 45-minute walk from the northern end of Waihi Beach crosses farmland and passes through coastal bush before dropping down to a perfect crescent of golden sand. The bay is sheltered and the swimming is excellent in calm conditions. There are no facilities — bring everything you need — and the bay is usually shared with only a handful of other walkers. The return walk is the same track, so time your visit to allow for a swim and lunch on the sand before heading back.
review_summary: A beautiful hidden beach worth every step of the walk. The swimming is excellent. No facilities so come prepared. Can be busy on summer weekends but quieter midweek.

name: Stingray Bay / Te Whanganui-a-Hei
short: A tranquil, sheltered beach often overlooked by visitors heading to Cathedral Cove — perfect for quiet swimming and snorkelling.
commentary: Stingray Bay sits just north of Cathedral Cove but receives a fraction of the visitors. The beach is smaller and more intimate, with clear water sheltered by rocky headlands that make it ideal for swimming and snorkelling. It falls within the Te Whanganui-a-Hei Marine Reserve, so underwater life is abundant — expect to see snapper, blue maomao, and colourful reef fish. Access is via the same Cathedral Cove walking track, with a short spur branching off to Stingray Bay. Most people walk right past the turnoff without noticing. Their loss is your gain.
review_summary: A beautiful alternative to crowded Cathedral Cove. The snorkelling is excellent. Much quieter than its famous neighbour. Some note the access path is steep. Marine life is abundant.

name: Kauri Block Track, Coromandel Town
short: A short, steep walk through regenerating kauri forest to a panoramic lookout over Coromandel Harbour and the Hauraki Gulf.
commentary: The Kauri Block Track in Coromandel Town is a compact gem — a 30-minute climb through regenerating kauri forest to a lookout with panoramic views over the harbour, township and Hauraki Gulf islands. The track passes through bush that was heavily logged in the 19th century but is now growing back strongly, with young kauri alongside mature podocarps and tree ferns. The viewpoint is particularly rewarding at sunset when the harbour turns gold. It's a less crowded, more accessible alternative to the longer kauri walks on the peninsula.
review_summary: Great views for minimal effort. The regenerating kauri forest is interesting. Best at sunset. Some find the track steep but it's short. A good option if you're staying in Coromandel Town.

name: Lonely Bay
short: A small, intimate beach framed by towering pōhutukawa trees and rocky cliffs — one of the Coromandel's loveliest swimming spots.
commentary: Lonely Bay lives up to its name — a small crescent of golden sand sheltered by headlands and shaded by ancient pōhutukawa trees, accessed via a short walk from Cooks Beach or by car along a narrow road. The swimming is safe and the water clear, with rocky edges perfect for snorkelling. In December and January the pōhutukawa bloom crimson red, creating one of the most photogenic beach scenes on the peninsula. Despite its proximity to popular Cooks Beach and Cathedral Cove, Lonely Bay is often quiet, especially on weekday mornings. A towel, a book, and a few hours here is time well spent.
review_summary: A beautiful, intimate beach. The pōhutukawa in bloom are spectacular. Swimming is safe and enjoyable. Can get busy on summer weekends but midweek it's peaceful.

name: Ship Cove / Meretoto Heritage Walk
short: Walk in Captain Cook's footsteps at his favourite anchorage in the Marlborough Sounds — rich in maritime and natural history.
commentary: Ship Cove (Meretoto) was Captain James Cook's favourite anchorage in New Zealand — he visited five times between 1770 and 1777, using the sheltered bay to rest and resupply his ships. Today the cove is the starting point for the Queen Charlotte Track, and a heritage walk along the shore traces the history of Cook's visits, the interactions with local Māori, and the astronomical observations he made here. Interpretive panels explain the significance of specific sites. Access is by water taxi from Picton. The bush and birdlife are superb, and the sense of historical connection is powerful.
review_summary: A moving historical site beautifully presented. The water taxi ride adds to the experience. The bush and birdlife are excellent. Good interpretation panels bring the history alive.

name: Molesworth Station Heritage Drive
short: New Zealand's largest cattle station, open for summer driving — a remote high-country adventure through tussock, rivers and 180,000 hectares of history.
commentary: Molesworth Station covers 180,000 hectares of high country between Blenheim and Hanmer Springs — it's the largest farm in New Zealand. The road through the station is open to vehicles from late December to Easter (weather permitting) and offers one of the most remote driving experiences in the country. The route crosses the Awatere and Acheron Rivers, climbs through tussock basins rimmed by snow-capped ranges, and passes through country that has barely changed since the first runholders arrived in the 1850s. There are no facilities — bring fuel, food, water and a spare tyre. The solitude is magnificent.
review_summary: An extraordinary high-country driving experience. The remoteness is the main attraction. Only open in summer — check DOC website for dates. Come fully self-sufficient. Allow a full day.

name: Tui Nature Reserve, Queen Charlotte Sound
short: A predator-free forest sanctuary in the Marlborough Sounds where native birdsong has returned to extraordinary levels.
commentary: Tui Nature Reserve occupies a peninsula in Queen Charlotte Sound that has been trapped and baited to near predator-free status, allowing native bird populations to recover dramatically. The result is a forest alive with birdsong — bellbird, tūī, kererū, fantail, weka, and robin all thrive here at densities rarely heard on the mainland. Walking tracks loop through the regenerating bush to golden sand beaches, and the water taxi from Picton drops you at the doorstep. It's a powerful example of what mainland predator control can achieve, and the soundtrack of the forest stays with you long after you leave.
review_summary: The birdsong is astonishing. The walks are well-maintained and scenic. Access by water taxi is easy. A great addition to a Sounds itinerary. Some tracks are steep.

name: Bob's Cove Heritage & Wetland Walk
short: A gentle loop through ancient beech forest and wetland on Lake Wakatipu's shore — one of Queenstown's quietest walks.
commentary: Bob's Cove is a 20-minute drive from Queenstown along the Glenorchy road, but it feels a world apart from the adventure capital. The DOC walk loops through red and silver beech forest, across a small wetland boardwalk, and down to a rocky lakeshore with views across Wakatipu to the mountains. Interpretive signs explain the area's history — lime kilns from the 1860s gold-rush era still stand among the trees. The beech forest is atmospheric, with sunlight filtering through the canopy onto a carpet of moss. It's an excellent option for anyone wanting nature without the adrenaline.
review_summary: A peaceful contrast to Queenstown's adventure activities. The beech forest is beautiful. Easy walking suitable for all ages. The heritage lime kilns add interest. Can be muddy after rain.

name: Canyon Brewing, Queenstown
short: A craft brewery and restaurant perched above the Shotover River gorge — excellent beer with jaw-dropping canyon views.
commentary: Canyon Brewing sits on the edge of the Shotover River canyon between Queenstown and Arrowtown, with a deck that offers vertigo-inducing views straight down into the gorge. The beer is excellent — a rotating lineup of lagers, ales and seasonal specials brewed on-site — and the food menu is designed to pair with it. The location is the star though; watching jet boats roar through the canyon 100 metres below while sipping a cold IPA is a uniquely Queenstown experience. It's a popular spot so booking is recommended, especially for the outdoor tables.
review_summary: The canyon views are spectacular. The beer quality is consistently high. Food is good pub-style fare. Outdoor tables are in high demand — book ahead. A great alternative to the Queenstown bar scene.

name: Skippers Pack Track Heritage Walk
short: Walk the original pack track into Skippers Canyon — the route gold miners took into one of New Zealand's richest goldfields.
commentary: The Skippers Pack Track follows the original route that miners walked into the Shotover River goldfield from the 1860s. The track descends steeply into the canyon, passing the remains of stone buildings, mining equipment and water races. The Shotover was once called 'the richest river in the world' and the ruins along this track tell the story of the thousands who came to seek their fortune. The scenery is dramatic — schist canyon walls, the turquoise river far below, and tussock-covered peaks above. Allow 3 hours return and wear sturdy boots. The history and landscape combine powerfully.
review_summary: A fascinating heritage walk with dramatic scenery. The gold-rush history is compelling. Some sections are steep and exposed. Not suitable for very young children. A great alternative to the commercial canyon experiences.

name: Napier Art Deco Heritage Walking Tour
short: Walk through the world's most concentrated collection of Art Deco buildings — rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
commentary: Napier was devastated by a magnitude 7.8 earthquake on 3 February 1931, which killed 256 people and destroyed the central business district. The city was rebuilt rapidly in the prevailing Art Deco style of the era, creating what is now the world's most concentrated collection of Art Deco architecture. Guided walking tours (daily from the Art Deco Centre) explain the buildings' stories, architectural features, and the earthquake that necessitated them. The Māori motifs incorporated by architect Louis Hay are particularly notable. Self-guided walks with audio are also available. The Daily Telegraph building, the Municipal Theatre, and the dome of the former Fire Station are highlights.
review_summary: The guided tours are excellent and bring the architecture to life. The earthquake history is moving. The Art Deco buildings are beautifully maintained. Self-guided options work well too.

name: Craggy Range Winery, Havelock North
short: One of New Zealand's finest wineries at the foot of Te Mata Peak — exceptional wines and dining in a dramatic setting.
commentary: Craggy Range sits at the base of Te Mata Peak, with vineyards climbing the lower slopes of the limestone ridge. The winery produces some of New Zealand's most acclaimed wines — their Gimblett Gravels Syrah and Te Muna Road Pinot Noir are regularly world-rated. The cellar door offers tastings in a beautifully designed space with views of the peak, and the on-site restaurant serves food matched to the wines. The setting is stunning — few wineries in the world can match the drama of Te Mata's limestone cliffs rising directly behind the vines. Book the restaurant ahead; walk-in tastings are usually available.
review_summary: World-class wines in an extraordinary setting. The restaurant food is superb. The cellar door experience is informative. The Te Mata Peak backdrop is dramatic. Book dining well ahead.

name: Ocean Beach (Waimārama) Wild Coast Walk
short: A wild, wind-swept beach south of Hastings — kilometres of sand backed by dunes and farmland, with barely another soul.
commentary: Ocean Beach stretches south from Waimārama along Hawke's Bay's coast, a vast sweep of sand pounded by Pacific surf and backed by dunes and pastoral farmland. The beach is popular with locals for walking, horse riding and fishing but rarely busy — walk 15 minutes in either direction and you'll likely have the coast to yourself. The swimming can be hazardous due to rips, so the beach is better for walking and beachcombing. On clear days the views north to Cape Kidnappers are excellent. The light here is particularly beautiful in the golden hour before sunset.
review_summary: A beautiful, wild beach perfect for long walks. Rarely crowded. Not ideal for swimming due to strong currents. The views to Cape Kidnappers are excellent. Bring sun protection — no shade.

name: Pauatahanui Wildlife Reserve & Inlet
short: A wetland reserve on the shores of Pauatahanui Inlet — one of the Wellington region's richest birding sites.
commentary: Pauatahanui Wildlife Reserve protects a stretch of wetland and tidal mudflats at the head of Pauatahanui Inlet, attracting a remarkable variety of wading and water birds. At low tide, the exposed mudflats host godwits, royal spoonbills, pied stilts, herons and shags, while the freshwater wetland behind supports pūkeko, spotless crake and the occasional bittern. A boardwalk and hides allow close observation without disturbance. The reserve is at its best from October to March, when migratory waders arrive from the Arctic — bar-tailed godwits, knots and turnstones. A 45-minute drive from central Wellington but a world away.
review_summary: Excellent birding in a peaceful setting. The boardwalk and hides are well-positioned. Best at low tide for wading birds. Spring and summer bring the most species. Bring binoculars.

name: Waitangi Mangrove Boardwalk & Haruru Falls
short: A boardwalk through mangrove forest connecting Paihia to Haruru Falls — a gentle walk through a unique coastal ecosystem.
commentary: The Waitangi Mangrove Boardwalk follows the Waitangi Estuary from Paihia through dense mangrove forest to the horseshoe-shaped Haruru Falls. The boardwalk elevates you above the mangrove roots and tidal channels, offering close views of crabs, mudskippers and wading birds that inhabit this estuarine ecosystem. Mangroves are New Zealand's only tropical tree, growing at their southern limit in Northland, and the interpretive signs explain their ecological importance. The walk takes about 45 minutes each way and ends at the falls, where the river drops over a basalt ledge in a broad horseshoe. An excellent family walk.
review_summary: A pleasant walk through an unusual ecosystem. The boardwalk is well-maintained and accessible. Haruru Falls is a worthwhile destination. Best at low tide for wildlife viewing. Easy walking for all ages.

name: Tāne Mahuta Night Experience
short: Experience the Waipoua Forest at night — guided walks to Tāne Mahuta and through the bush listening for kiwi calls.
commentary: The Waipoua Forest takes on a completely different character at night. Guided nocturnal tours led by local Māori guides begin with a visit to Tāne Mahuta — the largest known kauri tree — lit only by the glow of the forest. The party then walks quietly through the bush listening for the piercing call of the North Island brown kiwi, which are common in this forest. The guides weave Māori storytelling and forest ecology together seamlessly, creating an experience that is both educational and deeply atmospheric. Seeing Tāne Mahuta in the darkness, without the daytime crowds, is a genuinely spiritual experience for many visitors.
review_summary: An unforgettable experience. The guides are outstanding storytellers. Kiwi are frequently heard and sometimes seen. Tāne Mahuta at night is awe-inspiring. Book well in advance — tours are very popular.

name: McLaren Falls Park Glow Worm Walk
short: A free evening walk through native bush to see thousands of glow-worms sparkling along a stream bank — a magical alternative to Waitomo.
commentary: McLaren Falls Park between Tauranga and Taupō offers one of New Zealand's most accessible free glow-worm experiences. After dark, a short walk along the stream in the park reveals thousands of glow-worm larvae (Arachnocampa luminosa) lighting up the bank like a constellation brought to earth. The effect is genuinely magical — blue-green pinpricks of light twinkling in the darkness. Bring a torch for the walk in but switch it off at the viewing area to see the full display. No booking required, no entry fee. The park also has a lake, waterfall and excellent daytime walking. A Tauranga after-dark must-do.
review_summary: A magical free experience. The glow-worms are abundant and beautiful. Bring a torch and wear sturdy shoes. Best on dark, overcast nights. Can be busy in summer but the stream bank is long enough for all.

name: Tūhua / Mayor Island Marine Reserve
short: A volcanic island marine reserve in the Bay of Plenty with exceptional diving, snorkelling, and rare obsidian beaches.
commentary: Tūhua (Mayor Island) is a dormant volcano rising from the Bay of Plenty, surrounded by a marine reserve that protects some of the best subtropical diving in New Zealand. The crystal-clear waters host large schools of fish including kingfish, trevally and pink maomao, along with spectacular kelp gardens and reef formations. The island itself features rare obsidian (volcanic glass) beaches and a crater lake. Access is by charter boat from Tauranga — typically a day trip with time for diving or snorkelling and a walk ashore. The island is a DOC nature reserve and landing permits may be required during certain seasons.
review_summary: Outstanding marine life and diving. The obsidian beaches are unique. The boat trip is part of the adventure. Weather can cancel trips — be flexible. A genuine Bay of Plenty highlight.

name: Tiritiri Matangi Island Bird Sanctuary
short: One of the world's most successful open conservation projects — a predator-free island alive with rare native birds, a ferry ride from Auckland.
commentary: Tiritiri Matangi Island is a conservation triumph. Once bare farmland, the 220-hectare island in the Hauraki Gulf has been replanted with native bush and declared predator-free, creating a haven for some of New Zealand's rarest birds. Walking the island's tracks, you'll encounter takahē, kōkako, saddleback, stitchbird and North Island robin at close range — species that have vanished from most of the mainland. The kōkako's haunting song alone is worth the ferry trip. Regular ferries run from Auckland's Gulf Harbour. Guided walks with volunteer guides dramatically enhance the experience and are highly recommended.
review_summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience. The birds are abundant and approachable. Volunteer guides are excellent. The ferry ride is scenic. Book ahead in summer. Bring lunch and water — no shop on the island.

name: York Street Creative Precinct, New Plymouth
short: A cluster of independent galleries, studios and shops on New Plymouth's York Street — the creative heart of Taranaki.
commentary: York Street and its surrounds form the creative quarter of New Plymouth, housing independent galleries, artist studios, vintage shops and speciality cafés. The precinct has a strong community of local artists working in glass, ceramics, painting and textile art, many of whom open their studios to visitors. First Friday evening events bring live music, late openings and a festive atmosphere. It's a great place to find unique Taranaki-made souvenirs and support independent makers. The energy is relaxed and welcoming — New Plymouth's artsy side at its most accessible.
review_summary: A pleasant area to explore with some excellent galleries. First Friday events are popular. The shops are varied and interesting. A good complement to the Len Lye Centre and Govett-Brewster.

name: Egmont Road Forest Walk (Goblin Forest)
short: A mossy, epiphyte-draped 'goblin forest' on Mount Taranaki's lower slopes — ethereal and otherworldly.
commentary: The goblin forests on Mount Taranaki's lower slopes are among the most atmospheric landscapes in New Zealand. The Egmont Road track enters a world where every branch and trunk is sheathed in thick moss, hung with filmy ferns and dripping with moisture. The trees are stunted and contorted by altitude and wind, creating an otherworldly canopy. In fog — which is frequent — the forest becomes genuinely eerie. Several short loops are accessible from Egmont Road, making this an easy half-day walk that feels like stepping into a fantasy landscape. The forest is also home to rifleman and whitehead.
review_summary: One of the most atmospheric forest walks in New Zealand. Best in misty conditions for the full effect. Short and accessible. Photography opportunities are exceptional.

name: Nelson Art & Craft Trail
short: Drive between open studios and galleries across the Nelson-Tasman region — one of New Zealand's densest concentrations of working artists.
commentary: Nelson-Tasman has more working artists per capita than anywhere else in New Zealand, and the Art & Craft Trail connects dozens of open studios and galleries scattered across the region. Expect glass-blowers, potters, jewellers, printmakers, sculptors and painters — many international-calibre — working in studios you can visit. Highlights include Höglund Art Glass in Richmond, the textile artists of Moutere, and the potters' studios along the Great Taste Trail route. Many studios welcome drop-in visitors, but a few work by appointment. The trail brochure is available at the Nelson i-SITE.
review_summary: A wonderful way to discover local artists. The quality is consistently high. Some studios are appointment-only so check ahead. The trail brochure is essential for navigation. Allow a full day.

name: Canaan Downs Campsite & Walks
short: A free DOC campsite in an elevated karst meadow on Tākaka Hill — stunning geology and the trailhead for Harwoods Hole.
commentary: Canaan Downs sits in a wide, grassy basin high on Tākaka Hill, surrounded by marble outcrops and karst formations. The free DOC campsite is basic but beautifully positioned, and several walking tracks radiate from it — including the route to Harwoods Hole and various loops through the marble landscape. The downs themselves are an unusual ecosystem: tussock meadow on marble bedrock, dotted with sinkholes and disappearing streams. On clear nights the stargazing is exceptional, with minimal light pollution. It's one of those places that's worth visiting for the landscape alone, even without the walks.
review_summary: A beautiful and unique camping spot. The geological landscape is fascinating. Free and rarely crowded. Basic facilities only. The access road is narrow but manageable.

name: Cook's Cove & Hole in the Wall Walk
short: Walk through farmland and bush to a secluded cove with a natural rock arch — one of the East Coast's most scenic short walks.
commentary: The Cook's Cove Walkway starts near Tolaga Bay and crosses private farmland before dropping through native bush to a sheltered cove where Captain Cook anchored in 1769. The highlight is the dramatic 'Hole in the Wall' — a natural sea arch carved through a rocky headland, visible from a clifftop lookout. The walk takes about 2.5 hours return and offers coastal views along the way. The cove itself is pristine and sheltered, perfect for swimming if conditions allow. The combination of pastoral landscape, native bush, dramatic geology and maritime history makes this one of the most rewarding short walks on the East Coast.
review_summary: A beautiful walk with a dramatic payoff. The Hole in the Wall is impressive. The farmland sections are pleasant. Allow time to explore the cove. Sturdy footwear recommended.

name: Tikitere (Hell's Gate) Mud Concert Pools
short: The Kakahi Falls at Tikitere — the Southern Hemisphere's largest hot waterfall — plus therapeutic mud bathing in volcanic clay.
commentary: Hell's Gate (Tikitere) is Rotorua's most active geothermal park, and its Kakahi Falls are the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere, cascading 8 metres of steaming water over a series of terraces. The park is unique in offering genuine therapeutic mud bathing — you coat yourself in volcanic mud, let it dry, then rinse off in the sulphur spa. The experience is gloriously messy and oddly invigorating. The geothermal walk through the park reveals violently boiling pools, steaming craters and the distinctive 'concert' of bubbling mud that gives the area its character. Tikitere has been used therapeutically by local Māori for 700 years.
review_summary: The mud bathing is a unique and memorable experience. The geothermal activity is impressively violent. The hot waterfall is a highlight. Some find the smell strong. Allow at least 2 hours.

name: Dolomite Point & Truman Track, Punakaiki
short: A short bush track near Punakaiki to a dramatic coastal viewpoint over limestone cliffs and crashing Tasman surf.
commentary: Dolomite Point and the Truman Track offer a complementary experience to the Pancake Rocks just down the road. The Truman Track drops through subtropical coastal bush — nikau palms, cabbage trees and tree ferns — to emerge at a dramatic coastal platform where limestone cliffs meet the Tasman Sea. On wild days the surf is spectacular, and on calm days the rock pools are fascinating. The walk takes only 15 minutes each way but the coast here is raw and beautiful. Combined with the Pororari River Track and Pancake Rocks, it forms a trio of walks that showcase the best of the Punakaiki coast.
review_summary: A short but rewarding walk to a dramatic coastal viewpoint. The bush is beautiful. Best combined with the Pancake Rocks visit. Watch for rough seas — stay back from the cliff edge.

name: Oamaru Victorian Heritage Walking Tour
short: Walk through New Zealand's most complete Victorian streetscape — grand whitestone buildings, steampunk art, and a thriving creative community.
commentary: Oamaru's Victorian Precinct is New Zealand's most architecturally complete 19th-century streetscape, built from local Oamaru whitestone during the 1880s boom years. The grand warehouses and commercial buildings now house galleries, cafés, vintage shops and studios, including the famous Steampunk HQ with its industrial-age art installations. A self-guided heritage walk traces the rise and fall and renaissance of the precinct through interpretive panels and QR codes. The Harbour-Tyne area comes alive on Heritage Sunday events with period costumes, penny-farthing rides and live music. The contrast between the grand architecture and the creative community that inhabits it makes Oamaru unique.
review_summary: A beautifully preserved Victorian streetscape. The creative community adds life. Steampunk HQ is a highlight. The whitestone architecture is stunning. Allow time to explore the galleries and shops.

name: Macraes Flat — Grand & Otago Skink Conservation
short: The last stronghold of two of New Zealand's rarest reptiles — the grand skink and Otago skink — in dramatic tussock-covered schist country.
commentary: Macraes Flat in eastern Otago is the primary remaining habitat for the critically endangered grand skink and Otago skink — two large, striking reptiles found nowhere else on earth. DOC's conservation programme here includes predator control, habitat protection and a public viewing enclosure where you can reliably see both species basking on schist rock outcrops. The surrounding landscape of tussock-covered hills and exposed schist tor formations is hauntingly beautiful in itself. The skinks are most active on warm, sunny days — arrive mid-morning for best viewing. A small car park and interpretation panel mark the viewing site.
review_summary: A unique wildlife experience for reptile enthusiasts. The viewing enclosure is well-designed. Best on warm sunny days when skinks are basking. The landscape is dramatic. Worth the detour from SH85.

name: Fiordland Crested Penguin / Tawaki Viewing
short: See the rare tawaki (Fiordland crested penguin) — one of the world's rarest penguin species — on the remote southwest coast.
commentary: The tawaki or Fiordland crested penguin is the third rarest penguin species on earth, breeding only on the remote southwest coast of the South Island and a few offshore islands. Breeding pairs arrive from July and can be seen at several coastal sites, with Monro Beach near Lake Moeraki being the most accessible viewing location. The penguins nest in coastal rainforest and can be spotted crossing the beach at dawn and dusk during the breeding season (July-November). Approach quietly, stay low, and maintain at least 10 metres distance. Seeing these striking, yellow-crested birds in their wild rainforest habitat is one of New Zealand's most exclusive wildlife encounters.
review_summary: A rare and special wildlife experience. Sightings are seasonal and not guaranteed. Dawn and dusk visits are essential. The remote location adds to the magic. Patience and quiet are rewarded.

name: Wairaurahiri Jet, Tuatapere
short: A jet-boat adventure from Tuatapere into the wilderness rivers at the edge of Fiordland — deep into pristine rainforest.
commentary: The Wairaurahiri Jet takes visitors from Tuatapere into the roadless wilderness at the southern edge of Fiordland National Park, navigating rivers that cut through virgin podocarp rainforest beneath mountains. The journey passes through gorges, across rapids, and into country that is virtually unchanged since before human arrival. Your driver-guide shares natural history and stories of the southern pioneers who attempted to settle this impossibly remote coast. The experience is raw and exhilarating — genuine wilderness access without the multi-day tramp. The return by helicopter (on some tours) adds a dramatic aerial perspective.
review_summary: An extraordinary wilderness jet-boat experience. The remoteness is genuine. Guides are passionate about the environment. Weather-dependent — be flexible. One of the most thrilling activities in Southland.

name: Quail Island / Ōtamahua Day Trip
short: An island in Lyttelton Harbour with walking tracks, swimming beaches, and layered history from Māori settlement to Antarctic expeditions.
commentary: Quail Island (Ōtamahua) sits in the middle of Lyttelton Harbour, accessible by a short ferry from Lyttelton. The island's history spans centuries — it was a Māori food-gathering site, a quarantine station for animals and people, and a depot for Robert Falcon Scott's Antarctic expeditions. Today walking tracks loop through regenerating bush and across grassland to swimming beaches and historic sites. DOC has been restoring the native ecology, and birdlife is improving. The island makes an excellent day trip from Christchurch — pack a picnic, catch the morning ferry, and spend the day walking, swimming and exploring.
review_summary: A pleasant day trip from Lyttelton. The history is fascinating. The walks are easy and scenic. Good swimming beaches in calm weather. Ferry timetable can be limited — check ahead.

name: Dark Sky Project Stargazing, Lake Tekapo
short: World-class stargazing in the Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve — among the darkest skies on earth.
commentary: The Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve is the largest dark sky reserve in the world, and the Dark Sky Project at Lake Tekapo offers guided stargazing tours that take full advantage of the extraordinary conditions. Using powerful telescopes and expert guides, tours reveal nebulae, star clusters, planets and galaxies invisible to the naked eye. On clear nights the Milky Way is so bright it casts shadows, and the Magellanic Clouds — satellite galaxies of our own — are clearly visible. Sessions run year-round but winter offers the darkest, clearest skies. The experience is genuinely awe-inspiring and consistently rated as one of New Zealand's best activities.
review_summary: An extraordinary stargazing experience. The darkness and clarity are unmatched. Guides are excellent. Weather-dependent — cloudy nights lead to cancellations. Book early and stay flexible.

name: Ōpito Bay Snorkelling & Rock Pools
short: A sheltered bay with excellent snorkelling over reef and rock pools — clear water and abundant marine life.
commentary: Ōpito Bay is a small, sheltered cove on the eastern Coromandel coast that offers some of the best snorkelling on the peninsula. The rocky headlands at either end of the bay create a natural aquarium of clear water over reef, kelp and sand, teeming with small fish, starfish, anemones and occasional octopus. At low tide, the rock pools are fascinating for children. The beach is backed by pōhutukawa trees and the vibe is relaxed and local. Access is via a narrow road from Kuaotunu — check tides for best snorkelling conditions. A snorkel and mask are all you need.
review_summary: Excellent snorkelling in clear water. The rock pools are great for kids. A beautiful, quiet beach. Access road is narrow. Best at mid to low tide.

name: Pelorus River Kayaking (Hobbit Filming Location)
short: Kayak down the crystal-clear Pelorus River — a stunning forest river made famous as a filming location for The Hobbit.
commentary: The Pelorus River near Havelock achieved international fame as the filming location for the barrel-riding scene in The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug. The river is genuinely beautiful — crystal-clear water flowing through beech-forested gorges with deep swimming holes and rapids. Several operators offer guided kayak trips ranging from gentle half-day floats to full-day adventures including the Hobbit filming section. The swimming holes are superb on warm days — the water is cold but clean and invigorating. The surrounding Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve adds excellent walking options. Film fan or not, the river is stunning.
review_summary: A beautiful river kayaking experience. The water clarity is incredible. Hobbit fans love the film location. Guides are skilled. The swimming holes are refreshing. Best in summer when water levels are manageable.

name: Glenorchy to Paradise Road — Film Locations Drive
short: The road from Glenorchy to Paradise through some of New Zealand's most dramatic scenery — a film location for Lord of the Rings and Narnia.
commentary: The road from Glenorchy to the aptly named settlement of Paradise passes through some of the most spectacular scenery in the South Island. Towering beech-clad mountains, braided rivers, and wide glacial valleys provided the backdrop for multiple scenes in The Lord of the Rings (Isengard, Lothlórien, Ithilien) and The Chronicles of Narnia. The road becomes gravel beyond Glenorchy and crosses several shallow fords, so a 4WD or high-clearance vehicle is recommended. Even without the film connections, this landscape is extraordinary — primordial, vast, and humbling. The end of the road at the Dart River is a fitting reward.
review_summary: Jaw-dropping scenery that lives up to its fantasy film heritage. The drive is scenic but the road is rough — take it slowly. A 4WD is recommended. Allow time for stops and photos. Paradise is worth reaching.

name: Hawke's Bay Winery Cycle Trail
short: Cycle between cellar doors on flat, vineyard-lined trails — one of New Zealand's best ways to combine wine tasting with exercise.
commentary: The Hawke's Bay wine region is perfectly suited to cycling — flat terrain, warm climate, and cellar doors conveniently spaced along dedicated cycle trails. Several loop options connect top wineries including Mission Estate (NZ's oldest winery), Church Road, Black Barn and dozens of boutique producers. Bike hire is widely available and many companies offer guided tours with support vehicles. The Landscapes Ride and the Water Ride are the most popular routes, weaving through vineyards, orchards and olive groves. It's a civilised way to taste your way through one of New Zealand's finest wine regions without worrying about driving.
review_summary: The perfect way to explore the wine region. Flat terrain makes it easy. Many excellent cellar doors to visit. Guided tours handle logistics beautifully. Book bike hire ahead in peak season.

name: Russell Heritage Walking Trail
short: Walk through New Zealand's first European settlement — once known as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific,' now a charming harbour village.
commentary: Russell (originally Kororāreka) was New Zealand's first European settlement and briefly its capital, earning a reputation as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific' for its lawless whaling days. The heritage walk traces this wild history through the town's surviving buildings: Christ Church (NZ's oldest surviving church, with musket holes from the 1845 battle), Pompallier Mission (a French Catholic printing house), and the Duke of Marlborough Hotel (one of NZ's oldest licenced hotels). The contrast between Russell's violent past and its current genteel harbour-village atmosphere is striking. The walk takes about 90 minutes and is self-guided with excellent information panels.
review_summary: A fascinating walk through New Zealand's earliest European history. The heritage buildings are well-preserved. The information panels are excellent. The harbour setting is beautiful. Ferry from Paihia adds to the experience.

name: Ōhakune Old Coach Road
short: A heritage cycle and walking trail between Ōhakune and Horopito — cobblestone roads, bush tunnels and a magnificent 17-metre viaduct.
commentary: The Old Coach Road follows the original 1906 route that connected Ōhakune to the Main Trunk Railway, threading through native bush, across cobblestone surfaces, and over the spectacular Hapuawhenua Viaduct — a graceful steel structure spanning a bush-filled gorge. The trail is 15 km one way and is best done on a mountain bike or e-bike, with shuttle services running between the two ends. The engineering heritage — viaducts, cuttings, retaining walls — is impressive, and the bush sections are atmospheric, with moss-draped trees forming natural tunnels. It's one of the North Island's best half-day cycling experiences.
review_summary: A superb cycling trail with varied terrain. The viaduct is a highlight. The bush sections are beautiful. Shuttle services make logistics easy. Some cobblestone sections are rough on bikes.

name: Waiheke Island Food & Wine Trail
short: A day of cellar-door wine tasting, olive oil sampling and vineyard dining on Waiheke Island — Auckland's Mediterranean escape.
commentary: Waiheke Island is a 40-minute ferry from Auckland but feels like a different country. Over 30 boutique wineries produce excellent Bordeaux-style reds, along with olive groves, artisan food producers and several world-class restaurants with vineyard views. A self-drive or guided tour connecting cellar doors — Stonyridge, Cable Bay, Man O' War, Te Motu, Mudbrick — is one of Auckland's finest day-out options. Between tastings, swim at one of the island's golden-sand beaches. The combination of wine, food, beaches and a 40-minute commute from central Auckland makes Waiheke one of the world's great wine islands.
review_summary: A world-class food and wine experience. The wineries are excellent. The island setting is stunning. The ferry ride adds to the journey. Book restaurants ahead in summer. Guided tours handle transport well.

name: Rakaia Gorge Walkway
short: A riverside walk through a spectacular schist gorge where the Rakaia River narrows into turquoise rapids — excellent swimming holes.
commentary: The Rakaia Gorge Walkway follows the river through a deep gorge of ancient schist rock, where the mighty Rakaia narrows from its braided plains into a series of rapids and deep pools. The turquoise water is ice-melt from the Southern Alps and the colour is extraordinary. The walk is easy — about 30 minutes each way along a well-formed track — and the swimming holes are popular in summer (the water is cold but refreshing). The gorge bridge on SH77 offers dramatic views from above. It's an easy stop between Christchurch and Methven, and a popular local picnic and swimming spot.
review_summary: The gorge scenery is dramatic and the water colour extraordinary. Good swimming in summer despite cold water. Easy walking. A convenient stop en route to the mountains. Popular with locals on weekends.

name: Otago Central Rail Trail — Gold Towns to Vineyards
short: New Zealand's original Great Ride — 152 km of car-free cycling through Central Otago's gold-town heritage and dramatic tussock landscapes.
commentary: The Otago Central Rail Trail was New Zealand's first Great Ride and remains one of its best — 152 km of gentle, off-road cycling along the former Otago Central Railway between Middlemarch and Clyde. The trail passes through some of the most dramatic and sparsely populated landscape in the country: tussock-covered ranges, schist gorges, gold-rush towns, and the sun-drenched vineyards of Bannockburn. Most riders take 3-4 days, staying in characterful country pubs and B&Bs along the way. The trail is flat to gently graded (it was a railway) and suitable for most fitness levels. E-bike hire makes it accessible to all.
review_summary: One of New Zealand's great cycling experiences. The landscape is stunning and the heritage fascinating. Country pubs along the way are excellent. E-bikes make it accessible to all. Book accommodation ahead in summer.

name: Stewart Island Wild Kiwi Spotting Tour
short: The only place in New Zealand where kiwi are regularly seen in the wild during daylight — a guided encounter on Stewart Island's beaches.
commentary: Stewart Island (Rakiura) is home to an estimated 13,000 Southern Tokoeka kiwi — more kiwi than people. Unlike elsewhere in New Zealand, these kiwi sometimes forage on the beaches during daylight, especially in autumn and winter, making Stewart Island the best place in the country for a wild kiwi encounter. Guided evening tours take small groups by boat to remote beaches at dusk, waiting quietly for the birds to emerge from the bush. Sighting rates are high but not guaranteed — patience is essential. Seeing a kiwi foraging on a wild beach, with only the sound of the sea and the forest, is one of New Zealand's most profound wildlife experiences.
review_summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience. Sighting rates are good but not guaranteed. The guides are passionate and knowledgeable. The remote setting adds magic. Best in autumn/winter. Dress warmly.

name: Ōpārara Basin Walks & Limestone Arches
short: Ancient rainforest sheltering massive limestone arches and cave systems near Karamea — one of the West Coast's most dramatic natural wonders.
commentary: The Ōpārara Basin hides one of New Zealand's most extraordinary natural landscapes deep in the Kahurangi National Park near Karamea. Walking tracks thread through primeval rainforest to reach the Ōpārara Arch — a massive limestone bridge spanning 43 metres over the river — and the smaller Moria Gate Arch. The forest is ancient and dripping, home to large carnivorous snails, powelliphanta, and a network of cave systems containing rare blind cave spiders and ancient invertebrates. The basin is reached by a 16 km gravel road from Karamea and feels genuinely remote. The arches are awe-inspiring in scale and the forest has a prehistoric atmosphere unlike anywhere else in New Zealand.
review_summary: A genuinely awe-inspiring natural wonder. The arches are massive and beautiful. The ancient forest is atmospheric. The access road is long and gravel but manageable. Worth the detour to Karamea.

name: Wildwire Via Ferrata, Wanaka
short: Climb alongside (and through) waterfalls on iron rungs and cable bridges — the world's highest waterfall via ferrata near Wanaka.
commentary: Wildwire's via ferrata near Wanaka is billed as the world's highest waterfall cable climb, ascending the cliff faces alongside Twin Falls on iron rungs, cable bridges and catwalks fixed into the rock. The experience combines rock climbing, waterfall spray and jaw-dropping exposure in a managed adventure that requires no climbing experience — guides clip you in and talk you up. Multiple levels range from beginner-friendly to genuinely demanding. The Climb & Canyon option adds abseiling and a zipline descent. The combination of vertical adventure, waterfall immersion and alpine scenery makes this one of the most exhilarating activities in the Queenstown-Wanaka region.
review_summary: An extraordinary adventure experience. The waterfall setting is spectacular. Guides are excellent and safety-focused. Suitable for beginners on lower levels. The higher levels provide genuine thrills.

name: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary Walk
short: A predator-fenced sanctuary on Nelson's doorstep where native birds thrive — South Island robin, bellbird, kākā and more.
commentary: Brook Waimārama Sanctuary is one of New Zealand's largest predator-fenced mainland sanctuaries, protecting 700 hectares of native forest just minutes from central Nelson. Inside the fence, native bird populations are recovering rapidly — South Island robin, bellbird, tūī, kererū and the recently reintroduced South Island kākā are all present. Walking tracks range from 30-minute loops to half-day adventures through mature beech and podocarp forest. The birds here are notably bold and curious, especially the robins, which will approach to within arm's reach. Night tours offer encounters with little spotted kiwi and morepork.
review_summary: The birdsong inside the fence is remarkable. Robins are extremely friendly and photogenic. Well-maintained tracks. A great introduction to native conservation. Night tours for kiwi are excellent.

name: Deep Canyon — Wanaka Canyoning
short: Abseil waterfalls, slide down natural chutes and leap into crystal-clear pools — world-class canyoning in the Wanaka backcountry.
commentary: Wanaka offers some of the best canyoning in New Zealand, with operators like Deep Canyon taking groups through spectacular alpine canyons involving abseiling down waterfalls, sliding down natural rock chutes, jumping into deep pools and navigating through narrow rock channels. No prior experience is needed — guides provide all equipment and instruction. The canyon environments are stunning: polished schist walls, crystal-clear pools, cascading waterfalls and pockets of native bush. Multiple trip options range from half-day introductions to full-day expeditions in remote backcountry. It's one of the most immersive ways to experience the South Island's landscape.
review_summary: An exhilarating adventure in stunning scenery. Guides are professional and encouraging. Suitable for beginners. The water is cold but wetsuits keep you warm. One of the best activities in the Wanaka area.

name: Jerusalem / Hiruhārama — James K Baxter Heritage
short: The riverside settlement where poet James K Baxter established his famous commune in 1969 — a pilgrimage for New Zealand literature lovers.
commentary: Jerusalem (Hiruhārama) is a tiny settlement on the Whanganui River Road where New Zealand's most celebrated poet, James K Baxter, established a commune in 1969. Baxter lived here until his death in 1972, writing some of his finest poetry and advocating for a simpler, more communal life. The settlement includes the historic Catholic mission and convent established by Mother Aubert in the 1880s. Today it's a place of pilgrimage for literature lovers — Baxter's grave is on the hillside above the river. The setting is achingly beautiful: the river curving through steep bush-clad hills, with the sounds of birdsong and flowing water.
review_summary: A moving literary and historical site. The setting is beautiful. Baxter's grave is peaceful. The convent heritage adds depth. The river road drive is an experience in itself.

name: Whanganui Arts & Glass Quarter
short: A thriving arts precinct in heritage buildings — Whanganui has attracted artists with cheap studio space and is now one of NZ's most creative small cities.
commentary: Whanganui has quietly become one of New Zealand's most vibrant arts communities, attracting painters, glass artists, sculptors and writers with affordable studio space in atmospheric heritage buildings. The creative quarter centres on Taupo Quay and the riverfront, with highlights including the New Zealand Glassworks (the country's only public-access glass-blowing facility), the Sarjeant Gallery (a magnificent 1919 building), and numerous independent galleries and studios. The Whanganui glass art scene is internationally recognised, and visitors can watch glass-blowers at work and purchase directly from the artists. Open Studios events run periodically.
review_summary: An underrated arts destination with genuine creative energy. The Glassworks is fascinating. The heritage buildings are beautiful. Artists are welcoming. A pleasant surprise for most visitors.

name: Ōhakune — Carrot Capital & Mountain Town
short: A mountain town with a giant carrot, excellent restaurants, and gateway status to Tongariro — quirky Kiwi character at altitude.
commentary: Ōhakune sits at the southern foot of Mount Ruapehu and serves as the main gateway to the Tongariro National Park from the south. The town is famous for two things: the 7.5-metre giant carrot on its main street (celebrating the surrounding vegetable-growing industry) and its transformation from sleepy railway town to ski-season party town. Outside winter, Ōhakune is an excellent base for walking, mountain biking and exploring the national park. The restaurant scene has improved dramatically in recent years, with several excellent options punching well above the town's weight. The giant carrot photograph is obligatory.
review_summary: A fun mountain town with real character. The giant carrot is a classic photo stop. Good restaurants for a small town. Excellent base for Tongariro. Quiet outside ski season but pleasant year-round.

name: Wynyard Quarter & Silo Park
short: Auckland's revitalised waterfront precinct — restaurants, markets, playgrounds and harbour views on former industrial wharves.
commentary: Wynyard Quarter is Auckland's most successful urban regeneration project, transforming former working wharves into a vibrant waterfront precinct. The area now hosts restaurants, bars, the Auckland Fish Market, ASB Waterfront Theatre, and Silo Park — an outdoor events space built around converted industrial silos that hosts night markets, outdoor cinema and live music. The playground is one of the best in the city, and the promenade offers excellent harbour walking with views to the Harbour Bridge and Rangitoto. Weekend mornings see a bustling farmer's market, and the evening dining scene rivals anywhere in Auckland.
review_summary: A vibrant waterfront area with excellent dining. The Fish Market is a highlight. Silo Park events are fun. The playground is great for families. Parking can be challenging — consider walking or using the ferry.

name: Great Barrier Island / Aotea Nature Experience
short: Auckland's wild frontier — a roadless, off-grid island with hot springs, pristine bush, dark skies, and a self-sufficient community.
commentary: Great Barrier Island (Aotea) is just 90 km from Auckland but feels like another country. The island has no mains electricity, no street lights, and no reticulated water — the community is entirely self-sufficient. This makes it one of the best dark sky sites in the North Island. The bush-clad interior shelters excellent walking tracks, including the summit of Mount Hobson (Hirakimata) through ancient kauri forest. The natural hot springs at Kaitoke are a highlight — soaking in naturally heated pools surrounded by bush. Beaches are wild and beautiful, the fishing is excellent, and the pace of life is refreshingly slow. Access is by ferry (4.5 hours) or small plane (30 minutes).
review_summary: A genuinely wild island experience close to Auckland. The hot springs are wonderful. The dark skies are stunning. Come prepared — facilities are basic. The community is warm and welcoming.

name: Whakaari / White Island Volcano
short: New Zealand's most active marine volcano — a smouldering island visible from the Bay of Plenty coast, now viewed by scenic flight.
commentary: Whakaari (White Island) is New Zealand's most continuously active volcano, sitting 49 km offshore in the Bay of Plenty. The island is a dramatic sight — steaming, stained with sulphur, and ringed by turquoise sea. Following the tragic 2019 eruption that killed 22 people, island landings have been suspended, but scenic flights and boat tours circle the volcano, offering dramatic aerial views of the active crater, steaming vents and sulphur-yellow deposits. The island remains a powerful reminder of New Zealand's position on the Pacific Ring of Fire. Views from the mainland coast at Whakatāne and Ōhope are possible on clear days.
review_summary: A dramatic sight even from a distance. Scenic flights offer excellent views. Landing tours are currently suspended. The 2019 eruption adds a sombre dimension. Check weather for best viewing conditions.

name: Whakatāne Heads & Pōhatu Pā Walk
short: Walk to a dramatic headland pā site above Whakatāne — where the Mataatua waka first landed, with sweeping views of the Bay of Plenty.
commentary: The Whakatāne Heads walk climbs to one of the most dramatically positioned pā sites in the Bay of Plenty. The headland was the landing place of the Mataatua waka, one of the great migration canoes, and remains deeply significant to local iwi. The walk passes through coastal bush and along cliff tops to viewpoints overlooking the river mouth, the town, White Island, and the sweep of the Bay of Plenty. Interpretation panels tell the story of the Mataatua landing and the establishment of the settlement. The combination of dramatic scenery, cultural significance and accessible walking makes this one of the best short walks in the eastern Bay.
review_summary: A rewarding coastal walk with great views and important cultural history. The pā site is dramatic. Interpretation panels are well-done. Some steep sections. Best in good weather for the views.

name: Akaroa Cooking School
short: Hands-on cooking classes using local Banks Peninsula produce — channelling Akaroa's French culinary heritage.
commentary: The Akaroa Cooking School draws on the town's French heritage and the peninsula's excellent local produce to offer hands-on cooking classes for visitors. Classes range from half-day sessions focused on French-inspired techniques to demonstrations using seasonal ingredients sourced from local farms, the harbour and the bush. The school operates from a beautiful kitchen with views over Akaroa Harbour, and the results of your labour become lunch or dinner. It's a wonderful way to engage with the food culture of Banks Peninsula and take home skills that outlast any souvenir. Book ahead — classes are small and popular.
review_summary: A delightful hands-on food experience. The instructors are skilled and fun. Local produce is excellent. The harbour views are a bonus. Book well in advance — classes fill quickly.

name: Farewell Spit Bird Sanctuary Tour
short: A guided tour of one of the world's great bird sanctuaries — a 34 km sand spit hosting 90+ migratory and wetland bird species.
commentary: Farewell Spit (Onetahua) is a 34 km arc of sand extending from the northwestern tip of the South Island, forming one of the most important wetland bird habitats in the Southern Hemisphere. Access beyond the public walking area is by guided tour only, with operators running 4WD trips along the spit to the lighthouse and bird hides. Over 90 species have been recorded, with bar-tailed godwits, knots, oystercatchers and numerous tern and gull species present in vast numbers. The spit is particularly spectacular from September to March when Arctic migrants swell the populations. The gannet colony at the tip is the only mainland colony in the South Island.
review_summary: An extraordinary birding experience. The guided tours are essential for access. The scale of the bird populations is impressive. Best during migration season. The landscape itself is hauntingly beautiful.

name: Curio Bay Petrified Forest & Beach Walk
short: Walk across a 180-million-year-old petrified forest exposed on a wave-cut platform — one of the world's finest examples of Jurassic fossils.
commentary: Curio Bay's petrified forest is a geological marvel — the fossilised stumps, logs and foliage of a 180-million-year-old Jurassic forest, exposed on a wave-cut platform at the southeastern tip of the South Island. At low tide, the tree stumps and fallen trunks are clearly visible in the rock, some still showing growth rings and bark patterns. The interpretive panels explain the geological processes that preserved the forest and the ancient environment it reveals. In the evening, yellow-eyed penguins often come ashore on the adjacent beach, creating an improbable combination of Jurassic geology and living wildlife. Visit at low tide for best fossil viewing.
review_summary: A remarkable geological site. The fossils are clearly visible at low tide. The penguin viewing is a bonus. Interpretation panels are excellent. Some visitors find it modest compared to expectations — manage accordingly.

name: Queen Charlotte Track Day Walk
short: Walk a section of one of New Zealand's finest coastal tracks — ridgeline views over the Marlborough Sounds on a day trip from Picton.
commentary: The Queen Charlotte Track stretches 70 km through the Marlborough Sounds, but day walkers can enjoy its finest sections by combining water taxi transfers with walks between bays. The most popular day walk runs from Ship Cove to Furneaux Lodge (15 km, 4-5 hours), following a ridgeline with alternating views over Queen Charlotte and Kenepuru Sounds — deep blue water, bush-clad peninsulas, and distant mountain ranges. Water taxis from Picton handle the logistics, dropping you at the start and collecting from the finish. The track is well-maintained and graded moderate, suitable for anyone with reasonable fitness.
review_summary: One of New Zealand's most scenic day walks. The ridgeline views are spectacular. Water taxi logistics work seamlessly. Well-maintained track. Book water taxis ahead in summer.

name: Dart River FunYak Safari
short: Paddle inflatable kayaks down the crystal-clear Dart River through Lord of the Rings landscapes — accessible wilderness adventure.
commentary: The Dart River FunYak Safari combines a jet-boat ride up the braided Dart River with a gentle paddle downstream in inflatable kayaks (FunYaks). The river flows through ancient beech forest, past towering mountains and through country used as filming locations for The Lord of the Rings. No kayaking experience is needed — the FunYaks are stable and the river is gentle, with guides managing all the logistics. The scenery is jaw-dropping: Tolkien-esque forests, snow-capped peaks reflected in glacial pools, and a profound silence broken only by birdsong and the river's murmur. It's one of the best ways to experience the Glenorchy wilderness without a multi-day tramp.
review_summary: An outstanding wilderness experience accessible to all fitness levels. The scenery is breathtaking. Guides are excellent. No kayaking experience needed. Book ahead in summer.

name: Boundary Stream Mainland Island — Kōkako Trail
short: A DOC-managed 'mainland island' in the Kaweka Range where intensive predator control has brought kōkako and other rare birds back from the brink.
commentary: Boundary Stream Mainland Island is a 800-hectare area of native forest in the Kaweka Range where intensive pest control has produced remarkable results. The North Island kōkako — a striking blue-wattled bird with a haunting organ-like call — has been reintroduced and is breeding successfully. Dawn visits offer the best chance of hearing or seeing kōkako, along with North Island robin, whitehead, and falcon. Several walking tracks explore the forest, from short loops to longer tramping routes. The Shine Falls track leads to an impressive 58-metre waterfall. It's a powerful demonstration of what predator control can achieve for native wildlife.
review_summary: An excellent birding destination. Dawn is best for kōkako. The forest is beautiful and well-maintained. Shine Falls is a bonus attraction. Some tracks are muddy after rain.

name: Lake Rotokare Kiwi Night Walk
short: Listen for wild kiwi calls on a guided night walk around a predator-fenced lake sanctuary in south Taranaki.
commentary: Lake Rotokare Scenic Reserve is a community-led conservation triumph — a predator-fenced sanctuary surrounding a bush-fringed lake near Eltham. The reserve has reintroduced North Island brown kiwi, and guided night walks offer an excellent chance of hearing their piercing calls echoing through the forest. The lake loop walk takes about an hour and passes through some of the finest native bush in the region. Daytime visits are equally rewarding — the birdsong is extraordinary, with tūī, bellbird, kererū, and fantail all abundant. The project is entirely community-funded and volunteer-run, making a visit both a wildlife experience and a celebration of grassroots conservation.
review_summary: A wonderful community conservation project. Night walks for kiwi are popular — book ahead. The daytime bush walk is equally rewarding. The lake setting is peaceful. A heartening conservation success story.

name: The Pinnacles Sunrise Hike (Kauaeranga Valley)
short: An overnight hike to the Pinnacles summit for sunrise over the Coromandel — 360-degree views from the volcanic spine of the peninsula.
commentary: The Pinnacles Track in the Kauaeranga Valley climbs through regenerating kauri forest to the rocky Pinnacles summit at 759 metres. The experience is at its best as an overnight trip — stay at the DOC Pinnacles Hut (book ahead) and climb to the summit for sunrise, when the 360-degree views over the Coromandel Peninsula, Hauraki Gulf and Bay of Plenty are bathed in golden light. The track passes through forest rich in history — kauri logging heritage is visible in old dams, tramways and the remains of a vast timber industry. The summit scramble involves fixed chains on exposed rock but is manageable for most. A rite of passage for Auckland and Waikato trampers.
review_summary: The sunrise from the summit is unforgettable. The hut is well-maintained. The forest walk is beautiful. Book the hut well ahead. The final summit scramble is steep but exciting.

name: Blue Lake / Tikitapu Walkway
short: A bush-fringed walk around one of Rotorua's most beautiful volcanic lakes — stunning blue water, excellent swimming, and forest birdsong.
commentary: The Blue Lake (Tikitapu) walkway is a 5.5 km loop around one of Rotorua's prettiest volcanic lakes. The track threads through mature native bush with frequent views over the intensely blue water — the colour comes from the lake's volcanic origin and exceptional clarity. Several access points allow swimming from sandy beaches and rocky ledges. The walk is undulating rather than flat, with some short climbs, but is well-maintained and accessible to most walkers. The birdsong in the bush is excellent — tūī, bellbird and fantail are common companions. On calm mornings the lake's mirror-like surface reflects the surrounding forest perfectly.
review_summary: A beautiful walk around a stunning lake. The blue water is remarkable. Swimming spots are excellent. Well-maintained track with some hills. Can be busy on weekends but rarely crowded.

name: Spa Thermal Park Hot Stream Walk
short: A free natural hot stream where geothermal water meets cold river water — create your perfect temperature in the bush above Taupō.
commentary: Spa Thermal Park is one of Taupō's best free experiences. A short bush walk from the car park leads to a stream where naturally heated geothermal water flows into a cold stream, creating a stretch of river where you can find your perfect bathing temperature by shifting position. Move upstream for hotter water, downstream for cooler. The setting is beautiful — native bush, birdsong, and steam rising from the water. Locals swim here year-round, and on winter mornings the steam creates a magical atmosphere. It's an authentic, uncommericalised hot spring experience right on Taupō's doorstep. Free and accessible 24 hours.
review_summary: A wonderful free hot spring experience. Finding the right temperature spot is part of the fun. Beautiful bush setting. Can get busy on summer weekends. Bring a towel and water shoes.

name: E Hayes & Sons / Burt Munro Heritage
short: A hardware store housing a world-famous motorcycle collection — including Burt Munro's original 'World's Fastest Indian' record-breaking bike.
commentary: E Hayes & Sons is ostensibly a hardware store in central Invercargill, but it houses one of the most significant motorcycle collections in the Southern Hemisphere. The centrepiece is Burt Munro's original Indian Scout — the motorcycle he rode to a world speed record at Bonneville Salt Flats in 1967, immortalised in the film The World's Fastest Indian starring Anthony Hopkins. The bike sits alongside other vintage and racing motorcycles, all within a working hardware shop that's been trading since 1932. The combination of retail hardware, world-class motorcycles and genuine Southland hospitality is uniquely New Zealand. Free to view.
review_summary: A unique and charming experience. Burt Munro's bike is genuinely thrilling to see. The hardware store setting is part of the appeal. Free and friendly. A must-visit in Invercargill.

name: West Coast Wilderness Trail — Great Ride
short: A 136 km Great Ride from Greymouth to Hokitika through rainforest, river valleys and gold-rush heritage — one of New Zealand's finest cycling trails.
commentary: The West Coast Wilderness Trail is a 136 km Great Ride connecting Greymouth to Hokitika via Ross, threading through some of the most dramatic landscapes on the coast. The trail passes through ancient podocarp rainforest, across swingbridges over wild rivers, along old tramway routes and through gold-rush settlements. Most riders take 3-4 days, staying in small-town accommodation along the way. The trail surface is generally good but includes some technical sections through bush. E-bike hire is available and highly recommended for less experienced riders. The combination of rainforest, rivers, heritage and genuine West Coast remoteness makes this one of the country's most atmospheric cycling experiences.
review_summary: An outstanding multi-day cycling experience. The rainforest sections are stunning. Heritage adds depth. Some sections are technically challenging. E-bikes are recommended. Book accommodation ahead.

name: Gisborne Chardonnay Wine Trail
short: Explore New Zealand's 'Chardonnay Capital' — a circuit of boutique wineries producing exceptional cool-climate wines on the East Cape.
commentary: Gisborne (Tairāwhiti) is New Zealand's third-largest wine region and holds the unofficial title of 'Chardonnay Capital of New Zealand.' The warm climate and alluvial soils produce rich, complex chardonnays alongside excellent gewürztraminer, viognier, and other white varietals. A self-drive cellar-door circuit connects wineries including Bushmere Estate, Matawhero (site of the first commercial chardonnay planting in NZ), Wright's Vineyard, and several boutique producers. The cellar doors are intimate and unhurried — you're likely to be tasting with the winemaker. The region remains genuinely underrated, which means excellent wines without the crowds of Marlborough or Hawke's Bay.
review_summary: Excellent chardonnay in an uncrowded, authentic setting. The winemakers are welcoming and accessible. The region is underrated. Great value compared to more famous wine regions. Self-drive is best.

name: Māpua Wharf Dining Precinct
short: A cluster of excellent eateries on a restored estuary wharf — smoked fish, craft beer, ice cream and waterfront dining at its best.
commentary: Māpua Wharf has been transformed from a derelict commercial wharf into one of the Nelson-Tasman region's best dining destinations. The standout is The Smokehouse, where fish is smoked on-site over mānuka sawdust and served with views over the Waimea Estuary. Alongside it you'll find craft beer at Golden Bear Brewing, excellent gelato, a produce store, and several cafés and restaurants. The wharf sits at the start of the Great Taste Trail, making it the perfect refuelling stop for cyclists. At high tide, the estuary laps at the wharf's edge; at low tide, the mudflats host wading birds. It's a quintessential Nelson-Tasman afternoon.
review_summary: The Smokehouse is a highlight. The waterfront setting is lovely. Great variety of food options. Popular on weekends. A perfect lunch stop on the Great Taste Trail.
